┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         TERRAFORM S3 BACKEND                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  Developer Machine             AWS Cloud                                     │
│  ──────────────────           ─────────────────────────────────────────     │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │ terraform apply │──────────►    S3 Bucket (State Storage)        │       │
│  │                 │          │    ─────────────────────────────     │       │
│  │ 1. Lock state   │◄─────────│    • terraform.tfstate              │       │
│  │ 2. Read state   │          │    • Versioning enabled             │       │
│  │ 3. Plan changes │          │    • Encryption (SSE-S3/KMS)        │       │
│  │ 4. Apply changes│          │    • Access logging                 │       │
│  │ 5. Write state  │          └─────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│  │ 6. Unlock       │                         │                               │
│  └─────────────────┘                         │                               │
│                                              │                               │
│                               ┌──────────────▼──────────────┐               │
│                               │  DynamoDB Table (Locking)   │               │
│                               │  ────────────────────────   │               │
│                               │  • LockID (primary key)     │               │
│                               │  • Prevents concurrent runs │               │
│                               │  • Auto-release on completion│              │
│                               └─────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️

Backend Block Limitation

Backend configuration cannot use variables or expressions. Values must be literal strings. Use partial configuration (terraform init -backend-config=...) for dynamic values.

4.10 Import Blocks (Terraform 1.5+)

NEW IN 1.5 GAME CHANGER
🎯

What You're Doing Here

Import blocks revolutionize importing existing resources. Instead of the old terraform import command, you can now declaratively define imports in your configuration. Terraform 1.5+ can even generate the resource configuration automatically!

4.10.1 import Block Syntax

HCL Import Block Examples 
# ============================================================================
# imports.tf - Declarative Resource Imports (Terraform 1.5+)
# ============================================================================

# Import an existing VPC
import {
  to = aws_vpc.main
  id = "vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
}

# Import an existing S3 bucket
import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.data
  id = "my-existing-bucket-name"
}

# Import an existing EC2 instance
import {
  to = aws_instance.web
  id = "i-0abc123def456789"
}

# Import with for_each (import multiple resources)
import {
  for_each = toset(["sg-111111", "sg-222222", "sg-333333"])
  to       = aws_security_group.imported[each.key]
  id       = each.key
}
ℹ️

Import Block vs terraform import Command

Import block: Declarative, version-controlled, can generate config. terraform import: Imperative, one-time operation, requires pre-written config.

4.10.2 Generating Configuration from Import

Terraform 1.5+ can generate resource configurations automatically:

Bash Generate Config from Import 
# Step 1: Add import block (without the resource block)
cat > imports.tf << 'EOF'
import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.existing
  id = "my-production-bucket"
}
EOF

# Step 2: Run plan with -generate-config-out flag
terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated_resources.tf

# Step 3: Review the generated configuration
cat generated_resources.tf
# Terraform creates the full aws_s3_bucket resource block!

# Step 4: Apply to complete the import
terraform apply

# Step 5: Remove import block (optional - it's idempotent)
# The import block can stay or be removed after successful import
1️⃣ Add Import Block

Define what to import with import { to = ..., id = ... }

2️⃣ Generate Config

Run terraform plan -generate-config-out=file.tf

3️⃣ Review & Adjust

Check generated config, add variables, fix any issues

4️⃣ Apply Import

Run terraform apply to import to state

4.10.3 Import Block vs terraform import Command

Feature import Block (1.5+) terraform import Command
Declarative ✅ In .tf files ❌ CLI command
Version Control ✅ Committed to Git ❌ Not tracked
Config Generation ✅ -generate-config-out ❌ Must write manually
Bulk Import ✅ for_each support ❌ One at a time
Plan Preview ✅ Shows in plan ❌ Immediate execution
CI/CD Friendly ✅ Automated pipelines ❌ Manual intervention

📋 Chapter 4 Summary

Core Files
  • main.tf — Resources, data sources, modules
  • variables.tf — Input variable declarations
  • outputs.tf — Output value definitions
  • providers.tf — Provider configurations
Supporting Files
  • terraform.tf — Version constraints & backend
  • locals.tf — Computed local values
  • data.tf — Data source organization
  • backend.tf — Remote state config
Key Takeaways
  • ✅ Consistent file organization improves readability
  • ✅ Use provider aliases for multi-region deployments
  • ✅ Pin versions with ~> constraint
  • ✅ Import blocks (1.5+) enable declarative imports
← Previous: Chapter 3 - HCL Deep Dive Next: Chapter 5 - Providers →
🔌 ☁️ 🔗 🔐 🌐
Chapter 5

Providers - Deep Dive

The Gateway to Cloud Infrastructure

3000+ Available Providers
10 Major Topics
AWS/Azure/GCP Cloud Deep Dives
Critical Exam Relevance

Chapter 5: Providers - Deep Dive

Providers are the heart of Terraform's extensibility. They translate your HCL configurations into API calls that provision real infrastructure across thousands of platforms. This chapter provides comprehensive coverage of provider architecture, configuration patterns, authentication strategies, and deep dives into the three major cloud providers.

🎯 Learning Objectives

🔌 Understand provider architecture and plugin system
⚙️ Master provider configuration and aliases
🔐 Implement secure authentication patterns
☁️ Deep dive into AWS, Azure, and GCP providers
🌐 Design multi-cloud architectures
🔧 Troubleshoot provider issues effectively

📑 In This Chapter

5.1 Provider Architecture 5.2 Provider Configuration 5.3 Provider Aliases 5.4 Provider Requirements 5.5 Authentication Patterns 5.6 AWS Provider Deep Dive 5.7 Azure Provider Deep Dive 5.8 GCP Provider Deep Dive 5.9 Multi-Cloud Patterns 5.10 Provider Troubleshooting
🔌
"Providers are Terraform's superpowers—they connect your code to every cloud, service, and platform imaginable. Understanding providers deeply is essential for building production-grade infrastructure."

5.1 Provider Architecture

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC

5.1.1 How Providers Work

🔧 Terraform Provider Plugin Architecture 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          TERRAFORM PLUGIN ARCHITECTURE                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│    │                        TERRAFORM CORE                                 │     │
│    │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                │     │
│    │  │   HCL Parser │  │    Graph     │  │    State     │                │     │
│    │  │              │  │   Builder    │  │   Manager    │                │     │
│    │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                │     │
│    └──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                   │                                              │
│                      ┌────────────▼────────────┐                                │
│                      │    Go RPC Protocol      │                                │
│                      │   (gRPC over loopback)  │                                │
│                      └────────────┬────────────┘                                │
│                                   │                                              │
│    ┌──────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┐              │
│    │                              │                               │              │
│    ▼                              ▼                               ▼              │
│  ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐           │
│  │   AWS PROVIDER  │     │  AZURE PROVIDER │     │   GCP PROVIDER  │           │
│  │ ─────────────── │     │ ─────────────── │     │ ─────────────── │           │
│  │ terraform-      │     │ terraform-      │     │ terraform-      │           │
│  │ provider-aws    │     │ provider-azurerm│     │ provider-google │           │
│  │                 │     │                 │     │                 │           │
│  │ • Resources     │     │ • Resources     │     │ • Resources     │           │
│  │ • Data Sources  │     │ • Data Sources  │     │ • Data Sources  │           │
│  │ • SDK Calls     │     │ • SDK Calls     │     │ • SDK Calls     │           │
│  └────────┬────────┘     └────────┬────────┘     └────────┬────────┘           │
│           │                       │                       │                     │
│           ▼                       ▼                       ▼                     │
│       AWS APIs              Azure APIs              Google APIs                 │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Key Concepts
  • Providers are plugins — Separate executables from Terraform core
  • RPC communication — Terraform talks to providers via gRPC
  • Independent versioning — Provider versions separate from Terraform
  • Platform-agnostic core — Same Terraform CLI works with any provider

5.1.2 Provider Registry

🏠
Official Providers

Maintained by HashiCorp: AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, etc. Highest quality & support.

🤝
Partner Providers

Maintained by technology partners: MongoDB, Datadog, PagerDuty, etc.

👥
Community Providers

Community-maintained for niche use cases and custom integrations.

ℹ️

Terraform Registry

Browse providers at registry.terraform.io/browse/providers — over 3,000+ providers available!

5.1.3 Provider Installation

Bash terraform init - Provider Download 
# terraform init downloads and installs providers
$ terraform init

Initializing the backend...

Initializing provider plugins...
- Finding hashicorp/aws versions matching "~> 5.31"...
- Finding hashicorp/random versions matching "~> 3.6"...
- Installing hashicorp/aws v5.31.0...
- Installed hashicorp/aws v5.31.0 (signed by HashiCorp)
- Installing hashicorp/random v3.6.0...
- Installed hashicorp/random v3.6.0 (signed by HashiCorp)

# Providers stored in .terraform/providers/
$ ls -la .terraform/providers/registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/
aws/     random/

5.1.4 Dependency Lock File (.terraform.lock.hcl)

HCL .terraform.lock.hcl Example 
# This file is maintained automatically by "terraform init".
# Manual edits may be lost in future updates.

provider "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws" {
  version     = "5.31.0"
  constraints = "~> 5.31"
  hashes = [
    "h1:ltYPMnw3RMtLzriT7Wz8f2HPdR3P5kB4Gp0P/wCa7WY=",
    "zh:0cdb9c2083bf0902442384f7309367791e4640581652dda456f2d6d7abf0de8d",
    "zh:2fe4884cb9642f48a5889f8dff8f5f511418a18ee7204699d5bef4d7fcb0c0dd",
    # ... additional hashes for different platforms
  ]
}

provider "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random" {
  version     = "3.6.0"
  constraints = "~> 3.6"
  hashes = [
    "h1:R5qdQjKzOU16TziCN1vR3Exr/B+8WGK80glLTT4ZCPk=",
    # ... platform hashes
  ]
}
⚠️

Commit .terraform.lock.hcl to Version Control!

This file ensures all team members and CI/CD pipelines use identical provider versions. Never add it to .gitignore.

5.1.5 Provider Caching

Bash Provider Cache Configuration 
# Configure global provider cache (speeds up terraform init)
export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"

# Create cache directory
mkdir -p $TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR

# Or set in .terraformrc / terraform.rc
cat > ~/.terraformrc << 'EOF'
plugin_cache_dir = "$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
EOF

# Now providers are downloaded once and shared across projects
# Saves bandwidth and speeds up initialization

5.1.6 Provider Source Addresses

Source Type Format Example
Official (HashiCorp) hashicorp/<name> hashicorp/aws
Third-Party <namespace>/<name> integrations/github
Private Registry <hostname>/<namespace>/<name> app.terraform.io/company/custom
Local Filesystem terraform.io/builtin/<name> terraform.io/builtin/terraform

5.2 Provider Configuration

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC

5.2.1 Basic Provider Block

HCL providers.tf - Basic Configuration 
# ============================================================================
# providers.tf - Provider Configuration
# ============================================================================

# AWS Provider - Basic Configuration
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"

  # Tags applied to all resources (AWS provider 4.0+)
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = "production"
      Project     = "terraform-demo"
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  }
}

# Azure Provider
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}  # Required, even if empty
  
  subscription_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
}

# Google Cloud Provider
provider "google" {
  project = "my-gcp-project"
  region  = "us-central1"
}

5.2.2 Provider Configuration Sources

1️⃣ Provider Block Arguments

Explicit values in your .tf files (least recommended for credentials)

2️⃣ Environment Variables

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, ARM_CLIENT_ID

3️⃣ Shared Credentials Files

~/.aws/credentials, ~/.azure/, ~/.config/gcloud/

4️⃣ Instance Profiles / Managed Identity

IAM roles, Azure MSI, GCP service accounts (RECOMMENDED)

✅

Best Practice: Use Instance Profiles

For production, use IAM roles attached to your compute (EC2, Lambda, ECS) rather than static credentials. This eliminates credential management and rotation.

5.2.3 Provider Configuration with Variables

HCL Dynamic Provider Configuration 
# variables.tf
variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region for resources"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment name"
  type        = string
}

# providers.tf
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  }
}

# Use with: terraform apply -var="environment=production"

5.2.4 Assume Role Configuration

HCL Cross-Account Access with Assume Role 
# Access another AWS account via assume role
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"

  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::TARGET_ACCOUNT_ID:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "TerraformSession"
    external_id  = "terraform-external-id"  # Optional but recommended
  }
}

# Common pattern: Base credentials + assume role
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "management"
  region = "us-east-1"
  # Uses default credentials (instance profile)
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "workload"
  region = "us-east-1"

  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/TerraformDeployRole"
  }
}
🔑 Cross-Account Assume Role Flow 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         CROSS-ACCOUNT ASSUME ROLE                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   MANAGEMENT ACCOUNT (111111111111)         WORKLOAD ACCOUNT (222222222222) │
│   ─────────────────────────────────         ─────────────────────────────── │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌─────────────────────────┐              ┌─────────────────────────┐      │
│   │  CI/CD Pipeline         │              │  TerraformDeployRole    │      │
│   │  (or Developer)         │─── STS ─────►│  ─────────────────────  │      │
│   │  ─────────────────────  │   AssumeRole │  Trust: account 111111  │      │
│   │  Has IAM Role/User with │◄─────────────│  Permissions:           │      │
│   │  sts:AssumeRole         │  Temporary   │    - ec2:*              │      │
│   │                         │  Credentials │    - s3:*               │      │
│   └─────────────────────────┘              │    - rds:*              │      │
│                                            └─────────────────────────┘      │
│            │                                           │                    │
│            │ 1. Use base credentials                   │ 3. Deploy resources│
│            │ 2. Call sts:AssumeRole                    ▼                    │
│            │                               ┌─────────────────────────┐      │
│            └──────────────────────────────►│    AWS Resources        │      │
│                                            │    - VPC, EC2, RDS      │      │
│                                            └─────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

5.3 Provider Aliases

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

Provider aliases enable multiple configurations of the same provider. This is essential for multi-region deployments, cross-account access, or using different credentials for specific resources.

5.3.1 Multi-Region Deployment

HCL Multi-Region Provider Configuration 
# ============================================================================
# providers.tf - Multi-Region Configuration
# ============================================================================

# Default provider (us-east-1)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  default_tags {
    tags = { Region = "us-east-1" }
  }
}

# Secondary region provider
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "west"
  region = "us-west-2"
  
  default_tags {
    tags = { Region = "us-west-2" }
  }
}

# EU region provider
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "eu"
  region = "eu-west-1"
  
  default_tags {
    tags = { Region = "eu-west-1" }
  }
}

5.3.2 Using Provider Aliases in Resources

HCL Resources with Provider Selection 
# main.tf - Using aliased providers

# Uses default provider (us-east-1)
resource "aws_vpc" "primary" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  
  tags = { Name = "primary-vpc" }
}

# Uses west region provider
resource "aws_vpc" "secondary" {
  provider   = aws.west  # Explicit provider selection
  cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"
  
  tags = { Name = "secondary-vpc" }
}

# Uses EU provider
resource "aws_vpc" "eu" {
  provider   = aws.eu
  cidr_block = "10.2.0.0/16"
  
  tags = { Name = "eu-vpc" }
}

# Global resources (CloudFront, Route53) typically go in us-east-1
resource "aws_acm_certificate" "global" {
  # ACM certs for CloudFront must be in us-east-1
  provider          = aws  # Default (us-east-1)
  domain_name       = "example.com"
  validation_method = "DNS"
}

5.3.3 Provider Aliases in Modules

HCL Passing Providers to Modules 
# Root module - main.tf

# Deploy VPC module to primary region
module "vpc_primary" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  name       = "primary"
  
  # No providers block = uses default provider
}

# Deploy VPC module to secondary region
module "vpc_secondary" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"
  name       = "secondary"
  
  # Explicitly pass aliased provider
  providers = {
    aws = aws.west
  }
}

# Deploy to EU with explicit provider
module "vpc_eu" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  cidr_block = "10.2.0.0/16"
  name       = "eu"
  
  providers = {
    aws = aws.eu
  }
}
HCL Module Provider Requirements 
# modules/vpc/providers.tf

terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
      # configuration_aliases allows module to accept different provider configs
    }
  }
}

5.3.4 Cross-Account with Aliases

HCL Multi-Account Architecture 
# ============================================================================
# Multi-Account Provider Configuration
# ============================================================================

# Shared services account (networking, security)
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "shared"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

# Development account
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "dev"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

# Production account
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "prod"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::333333333333:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

# Deploy Transit Gateway in shared account
resource "aws_ec2_transit_gateway" "main" {
  provider = aws.shared
  
  description = "Central Transit Gateway"
}

# Accept TGW attachment in dev account
resource "aws_ec2_transit_gateway_vpc_attachment" "dev" {
  provider           = aws.dev
  transit_gateway_id = aws_ec2_transit_gateway.main.id
  vpc_id             = module.vpc_dev.vpc_id
  subnet_ids         = module.vpc_dev.private_subnet_ids
}
🏗️ Multi-Account Architecture Pattern 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         AWS MULTI-ACCOUNT PATTERN                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│     SHARED SERVICES (111111111111)                                              │
│     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│     │                    Transit Gateway                          │             │
│     │                         │                                   │             │
│     │    ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐             │             │
│     │    │                    │                    │             │             │
│     └────┼────────────────────┼────────────────────┼─────────────┘             │
│          │                    │                    │                            │
│          ▼                    ▼                    ▼                            │
│     ┌─────────┐          ┌─────────┐          ┌─────────┐                      │
│     │   DEV   │          │ STAGING │          │  PROD   │                      │
│     │222222222│          │333333333│          │444444444│                      │
│     ├─────────┤          ├─────────┤          ├─────────┤                      │
│     │  VPC    │          │  VPC    │          │  VPC    │                      │
│     │10.1.0.0 │          │10.2.0.0 │          │10.3.0.0 │                      │
│     └─────────┘          └─────────┘          └─────────┘                      │
│                                                                                  │
│     Terraform Provider Aliases:                                                  │
│     • aws.shared → Account 111111111111                                         │
│     • aws.dev    → Account 222222222222                                         │
│     • aws.staging→ Account 333333333333                                         │
│     • aws.prod   → Account 444444444444                                         │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

5.3.5 Endpoints Configuration (LocalStack, Custom)

Override default AWS endpoints for testing with LocalStack or custom API endpoints.

HCL LocalStack Configuration 
# LocalStack provider for local development/testing
provider "aws" {
  region                      = "us-east-1"
  access_key                  = "test"
  secret_key                  = "test"
  skip_credentials_validation = true
  skip_metadata_api_check     = true
  skip_requesting_account_id  = true

  endpoints {
    s3             = "http://localhost:4566"
    dynamodb       = "http://localhost:4566"
    lambda         = "http://localhost:4566"
    sqs            = "http://localhost:4566"
    sns            = "http://localhost:4566"
    iam            = "http://localhost:4566"
    sts            = "http://localhost:4566"
    secretsmanager = "http://localhost:4566"
    ssm            = "http://localhost:4566"
    ec2            = "http://localhost:4566"
  }
}

# Custom VPC endpoint configuration
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "private"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  endpoints {
    s3  = "https://bucket.vpce-abc123.s3.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com"
    ec2 = "https://vpce-xyz789.ec2.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com"
  }
}
💡

LocalStack Setup

Run LocalStack with: docker run -d -p 4566:4566 localstack/localstack

All AWS services are available on port 4566.

5.3.6 Retry Configuration

Configure automatic retries for transient API failures.

HCL Retry Settings 
# AWS provider with retry configuration
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-west-2"
  
  # Retry settings for API throttling
  retry_mode = "standard"  # Options: legacy, standard, adaptive
  
  # Maximum retry attempts (default: 25 for standard mode)
  max_retries = 10
}

# Adaptive retry mode for high-volume operations
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "high_volume"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  retry_mode = "adaptive"  # Adjusts based on API response
  max_retries = 50
  
  # Default tags for all resources
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      ManagedBy = "terraform"
    }
  }
}
📋

Retry Modes Comparison

Mode Behavior Use Case
legacy Fixed 3 retries with exponential backoff Backward compatibility
standard Up to 3 retries with jitter Most workloads (default)
adaptive Dynamic retry based on API feedback High-volume, batch operations

5.3.7 Skip Metadata API Check

Control EC2 instance metadata service (IMDS) behavior for non-EC2 environments.

HCL Skip Options for Non-AWS Environments 
# Running Terraform outside AWS (CI/CD, local dev)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-west-2"
  
  # Skip IMDS check - faster startup when not on EC2
  skip_metadata_api_check = true
  
  # Skip credential validation (useful for offline planning)
  skip_credentials_validation = true
  
  # Skip requesting Account ID via STS
  skip_requesting_account_id = true
  
  # Skip region validation (for custom/gov regions)
  skip_region_validation = true
}

# Minimal configuration for terraform plan without AWS access
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "offline"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  skip_metadata_api_check     = true
  skip_credentials_validation = true
  skip_requesting_account_id  = true
  
  # Dummy credentials for offline planning
  access_key = "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
  secret_key = "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
}
⚠️

When to Use Skip Options

  • skip_metadata_api_check: Running outside EC2/ECS (GitHub Actions, local)
  • skip_credentials_validation: Offline planning, testing provider config
  • skip_requesting_account_id: When credentials lack STS permissions

5.4 Provider Requirements

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC

5.4.1 required_providers Block

HCL Complete required_providers Example 
# terraform.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    # Official HashiCorp provider
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.31"
    }
    
    # Partner provider
    datadog = {
      source  = "DataDog/datadog"
      version = "~> 3.30"
    }
    
    # Community provider
    github = {
      source  = "integrations/github"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
    
    # Provider with local name different from type
    postgres = {
      source  = "cyrilgdn/postgresql"
      version = "~> 1.21"
    }
  }
}

5.4.2 Version Constraint Syntax

Operator Example Meaning Allowed Versions
= = 5.31.0 Exact version Only 5.31.0
!= != 5.30.0 Exclude version Any except 5.30.0
>, >=, <, <= >= 5.0 Comparison 5.0.0 and above
~> ~> 5.31 Pessimistic (allow rightmost) 5.31.x but not 5.32+
~> ~> 5.31.0 Patch updates only 5.31.x (same minor)
Combined >= 5.0, < 6.0 Range constraint 5.x.x only
💡

Recommended: Use ~> for Stability

~> 5.31 allows 5.31.x, 5.32.x... but not 6.0. This gives you security patches while avoiding breaking changes.

5.4.3 Upgrading Provider Versions

Bash Provider Upgrade Commands 
# Check current provider versions
terraform version

# Look for updates available
terraform init -upgrade

# Update the lock file to latest allowed versions
terraform init -upgrade

# If you change version constraints in terraform.tf:
# 1. Update the constraint: version = "~> 5.40" (previously ~> 5.31)
# 2. Run terraform init -upgrade
# 3. Review changes in .terraform.lock.hcl
# 4. Commit the updated lock file

# List providers in state (shows what was used last apply)
terraform providers

# Provider mirror for air-gapped environments
terraform providers mirror /path/to/mirror

5.4.4 Module Provider Requirements

HCL Module terraform.tf with configuration_aliases 
# modules/multi-region-vpc/terraform.tf

terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
      
      # This module expects multiple AWS provider configurations
      configuration_aliases = [
        aws.primary,
        aws.secondary,
      ]
    }
  }
}

# Module usage from root
module "multi_region_vpc" {
  source = "./modules/multi-region-vpc"
  
  providers = {
    aws.primary   = aws           # Default provider
    aws.secondary = aws.west      # Aliased provider
  }
}

5.5 Authentication Patterns

ESSENTIAL PRODUCTION
🔐

Security-First Authentication

Never hardcode credentials in Terraform files. This section covers secure authentication patterns for AWS, Azure, and GCP providers.

5.5.1 AWS Authentication Methods

🏆 Best: Instance Profile / IAM Role

Automatic credentials on EC2, ECS, Lambda. No secrets to manage!

✅ Good: Environment Variables

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

✅ Good: Shared Credentials File

~/.aws/credentials with named profiles

⚠️ Avoid: Hardcoded in Provider Block

Credentials visible in code and state file

HCL AWS Authentication Examples 
# ✅ BEST: No explicit credentials (uses instance profile or env vars)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

# ✅ GOOD: Using named profile from ~/.aws/credentials
provider "aws" {
  region  = "us-east-1"
  profile = "production"
}

# ✅ GOOD: Assume role (cross-account access)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::TARGET_ACCT:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

# ⚠️ AVOID: Hardcoded credentials (security risk!)
provider "aws" {
  region     = "us-east-1"
  access_key = "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"      # DON'T DO THIS!
  secret_key = "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG..."  # DON'T DO THIS!
}

5.5.2 Azure Authentication Methods

HCL Azure Provider Authentication 
# ✅ BEST: Azure CLI authentication (for development)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
}
# Requires: az login

# ✅ BEST: Managed Identity (for Azure VMs, App Service, Functions)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  use_msi = true
}

# ✅ GOOD: Service Principal via environment variables
# Set: ARM_CLIENT_ID, ARM_CLIENT_SECRET, ARM_TENANT_ID, ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
}

# ✅ GOOD: Service Principal explicit (avoid secrets in code)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  
  client_id       = var.arm_client_id
  client_secret   = var.arm_client_secret
  tenant_id       = var.arm_tenant_id
  subscription_id = var.arm_subscription_id
}

# ✅ GOOD: OIDC authentication (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  use_oidc = true
}

5.5.3 GCP Authentication Methods

HCL GCP Provider Authentication 
# ✅ BEST: Application Default Credentials
# Uses: gcloud auth application-default login (dev)
#   or: Service account on GCE, Cloud Run, GKE
provider "google" {
  project = "my-project-id"
  region  = "us-central1"
}

# ✅ GOOD: Service account key file (CI/CD)
provider "google" {
  project     = "my-project-id"
  region      = "us-central1"
  credentials = file("service-account.json")
}

# ✅ BETTER: Service account key via environment
# Set: GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/path/to/key.json
provider "google" {
  project = "my-project-id"
  region  = "us-central1"
}

# ✅ GOOD: Workload Identity (GKE)
provider "google" {
  project = "my-project-id"
  region  = "us-central1"
  # Automatically uses workload identity on GKE
}

# ✅ GOOD: Impersonation (recommended for elevated access)
provider "google" {
  project = "my-project-id"
  region  = "us-central1"
  
  impersonate_service_account = "terraform@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
}

5.5.4 CI/CD Authentication Patterns

YAML GitHub Actions with OIDC (AWS) 
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml
name: Terraform

permissions:
  id-token: write   # Required for OIDC
  contents: read

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure AWS credentials (OIDC)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/GitHubActionsRole
          aws-region: us-east-1
      
      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init
      
      - name: Terraform Plan
        run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
      
      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: terraform apply tfplan
✅

OIDC is the Gold Standard

OpenID Connect (OIDC) eliminates long-lived secrets. GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps all support OIDC federation with AWS, Azure, and GCP.

5.5.5 Secrets Management Integration

HCL Vault Provider for Dynamic Secrets 
# Configure Vault provider
provider "vault" {
  address = "https://vault.company.com:8200"
  # Auth via VAULT_TOKEN env var or instance metadata
}

# Get dynamic AWS credentials from Vault
data "vault_aws_access_credentials" "creds" {
  backend = "aws"
  role    = "terraform-role"
  type    = "sts"
}

# Use Vault-generated credentials for AWS provider
provider "aws" {
  region     = "us-east-1"
  access_key = data.vault_aws_access_credentials.creds.access_key
  secret_key = data.vault_aws_access_credentials.creds.secret_key
  token      = data.vault_aws_access_credentials.creds.security_token
}

5.6 AWS Provider Deep Dive

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
☁️

Amazon Web Services Provider

The AWS provider is the most widely used Terraform provider, with support for 1000+ resource types across all AWS services.

5.6.1 AWS Provider Configuration

HCL Complete AWS Provider Configuration 
# terraform.tf
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.31"
    }
  }
}

# providers.tf
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  # Default tags applied to ALL resources
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = var.environment
      Project     = var.project_name
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Owner       = "platform-team"
    }
  }
  
  # Ignore specific tag changes (useful for auto-tagging)
  ignore_tags {
    key_prefixes = ["kubernetes.io/", "aws:"]
  }
  
  # Custom retry configuration
  retry_mode  = "standard"
  max_retries = 3
}

5.6.2 Default Tags (AWS Provider 4.0+)

HCL Default Tags Pattern 
# Default tags apply to ALL resources automatically
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = "production"
      Team        = "platform"
      CostCenter  = "CC-12345"
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  }
}

# Resource-specific tags MERGE with default tags
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
    Role = "frontend"
    # Results in: Environment, Team, CostCenter, ManagedBy, Name, Role
  }
}

# Resource tags can OVERRIDE default tags
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "override_example" {
  bucket = "my-bucket"
  
  tags = {
    Environment = "staging"  # Overrides default "production"
    Name        = "staging-bucket"
  }
}
ℹ️

Tag Precedence

Resource-level tags override default_tags when keys match. Use this for environment-specific overrides while maintaining baseline tags.

5.6.3 AWS Provider Endpoints

HCL Custom Endpoints (LocalStack, VPC Endpoints) 
# LocalStack for local development/testing
provider "aws" {
  region                      = "us-east-1"
  access_key                  = "test"
  secret_key                  = "test"
  skip_credentials_validation = true
  skip_metadata_api_check     = true
  skip_requesting_account_id  = true
  
  endpoints {
    s3       = "http://localhost:4566"
    dynamodb = "http://localhost:4566"
    lambda   = "http://localhost:4566"
    sqs      = "http://localhost:4566"
    sns      = "http://localhost:4566"
    iam      = "http://localhost:4566"
    sts      = "http://localhost:4566"
  }
}

# VPC Endpoints (PrivateLink) for security
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "private"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  endpoints {
    s3  = "https://bucket.vpce-xxx.s3.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com"
    ec2 = "https://vpce-xxx.ec2.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com"
  }
}

5.6.4 Common AWS Resources

Category Resource Type Common Use
Compute aws_instance EC2 instances
Compute aws_lambda_function Serverless functions
Networking aws_vpc Virtual Private Cloud
Networking aws_subnet VPC subnets
Storage aws_s3_bucket Object storage
Database aws_db_instance RDS databases
Security aws_security_group Firewall rules
IAM aws_iam_role IAM roles

5.6.5 AWS Data Sources

HCL Common AWS Data Sources 
# Get current AWS account info
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

# Get current region
data "aws_region" "current" {}

# Get available AZs
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# Find latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]
  
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]
  }
}

# Look up existing VPC
data "aws_vpc" "default" {
  default = true
}

# Look up existing subnets
data "aws_subnets" "private" {
  filter {
    name   = "vpc-id"
    values = [data.aws_vpc.default.id]
  }
  
  tags = {
    Tier = "private"
  }
}

# Use in resources
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = data.aws_subnets.private.ids[0]
}

5.6.6 AWS Provider Best Practices

🏷️ Use Default Tags

Set organization-wide tags in the provider block for consistent resource tagging.

🔒 Use IAM Roles

Prefer instance profiles and assume roles over static credentials.

🌍 Multi-Region

Use provider aliases for multi-region deployments and DR patterns.

📌 Pin Versions

Always pin provider versions with ~> constraints.

5.6.7 HashiCorp Vault Integration

Integrate HashiCorp Vault for secure secrets management in AWS deployments.

HCL Vault Provider with AWS Secrets 
# Configure Vault provider
provider "vault" {
  address = "https://vault.example.com:8200"
  
  # Auth via AWS IAM
  auth_login {
    path = "auth/aws/login"
    
    parameters = {
      role = "terraform-role"
    }
  }
}

# Read AWS credentials from Vault
data "vault_aws_access_credentials" "creds" {
  backend = "aws"
  role    = "terraform-deployment"
  type    = "sts"  # Use STS for temporary credentials
}

# Use Vault-provided credentials
provider "aws" {
  region     = "us-west-2"
  access_key = data.vault_aws_access_credentials.creds.access_key
  secret_key = data.vault_aws_access_credentials.creds.secret_key
  token      = data.vault_aws_access_credentials.creds.security_token
}

# Read database credentials from Vault
data "vault_generic_secret" "db" {
  path = "secret/data/production/database"
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = "postgres"
  instance_class = "db.t3.medium"
  
  username = data.vault_generic_secret.db.data["username"]
  password = data.vault_generic_secret.db.data["password"]
}

Vault Integration Architecture

Terraform
vault provider
aws provider
→ auth
HashiCorp Vault
AWS Secrets Engine
KV Secrets Engine
→ creds
AWS
STS
Resources
💡

Benefits of Vault Integration

  • Dynamic Secrets: Short-lived credentials that auto-expire
  • Audit Logging: All secret access is logged
  • Centralized Management: Single source of truth for secrets
  • No Hardcoded Credentials: Secrets never stored in code or state

5.7 Azure Provider Deep Dive

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC

5.7.1 Azure Provider Configuration

HCL Complete Azure Provider Configuration 
# terraform.tf
terraform {
  required_providers {
    azurerm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"
      version = "~> 3.85"
    }
    azuread = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azuread"
      version = "~> 2.47"
    }
  }
}

# providers.tf
provider "azurerm" {
  features {
    # Key Vault configuration
    key_vault {
      purge_soft_delete_on_destroy    = false
      recover_soft_deleted_key_vaults = true
    }
    
    # Resource Group configuration
    resource_group {
      prevent_deletion_if_contains_resources = true
    }
    
    # Virtual Machine configuration
    virtual_machine {
      delete_os_disk_on_deletion     = true
      graceful_shutdown              = false
      skip_shutdown_and_force_delete = false
    }
  }
  
  # Optional: Specify subscription
  subscription_id = var.subscription_id
}

5.7.2 Features Block (Required)

⚠️

Features Block is Mandatory

The Azure provider requires a features {} block, even if empty. This is unique to the AzureRM provider.

HCL Minimal vs Full Features Block 
# Minimal (still valid)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
}

# Production-ready with safety features
provider "azurerm" {
  features {
    key_vault {
      purge_soft_delete_on_destroy = false  # Safety: don't purge deleted vaults
    }
    
    resource_group {
      prevent_deletion_if_contains_resources = true  # Safety: prevent accidental deletion
    }
    
    log_analytics_workspace {
      permanently_delete_on_destroy = false
    }
  }
}

5.7.3 Common Azure Resources

Category Resource Type Common Use
Foundation azurerm_resource_group Logical container for resources
Compute azurerm_linux_virtual_machine Linux VMs
Compute azurerm_function_app Serverless functions
Networking azurerm_virtual_network Virtual Network (VNet)
Storage azurerm_storage_account Blob, File, Queue, Table storage
Database azurerm_mssql_database Azure SQL Database
Security azurerm_key_vault Secrets management
Containers azurerm_kubernetes_cluster AKS clusters

5.7.4 Azure Data Sources

HCL Common Azure Data Sources 
# Get current subscription info
data "azurerm_subscription" "current" {}

# Get current client configuration
data "azurerm_client_config" "current" {}

# Look up existing resource group
data "azurerm_resource_group" "existing" {
  name = "existing-rg"
}

# Look up existing Key Vault
data "azurerm_key_vault" "secrets" {
  name                = "company-secrets"
  resource_group_name = "security-rg"
}

# Get secret from Key Vault
data "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "db_password" {
  name         = "database-password"
  key_vault_id = data.azurerm_key_vault.secrets.id
}

# Look up existing Virtual Network
data "azurerm_virtual_network" "hub" {
  name                = "hub-vnet"
  resource_group_name = "networking-rg"
}

5.7.5 Multi-Subscription Deployment

HCL Multiple Azure Subscriptions 
# Default subscription (Connectivity)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  subscription_id = "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111"
}

# Workload subscription
provider "azurerm" {
  alias           = "workload"
  features {}
  subscription_id = "22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222"
}

# Deploy hub VNet in connectivity subscription
resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "hub" {
  name                = "hub-vnet"
  location            = "eastus"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.hub.name
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
}

# Deploy spoke VNet in workload subscription
resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "spoke" {
  provider            = azurerm.workload
  name                = "spoke-vnet"
  location            = "eastus"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.spoke.name
  address_space       = ["10.1.0.0/16"]
}

5.7.6 Azure Provider Best Practices

🔧 Configure Features

Use features block to enable safety behaviors like soft-delete protection.

🏷️ Use Tags

Apply consistent tagging for cost tracking and governance.

🔑 Managed Identity

Use system/user-assigned managed identity instead of service principals when possible.

📍 Location Management

Use location variable to ensure consistent region deployment.

5.8 GCP Provider Deep Dive

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC

5.8.1 Google Provider Configuration

HCL Complete GCP Provider Configuration 
# terraform.tf
terraform {
  required_providers {
    google = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google"
      version = "~> 5.10"
    }
    google-beta = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google-beta"
      version = "~> 5.10"
    }
  }
}

# providers.tf
provider "google" {
  project = var.project_id
  region  = var.region
  zone    = var.zone
  
  # Impersonation for enhanced security
  impersonate_service_account = var.terraform_service_account
  
  # Request batching
  batching {
    enable_batching = true
    send_after      = "10s"
  }
}

# Beta provider for preview features
provider "google-beta" {
  project = var.project_id
  region  = var.region
}

5.8.2 Google vs Google-Beta Provider

Aspect google Provider google-beta Provider
Stability GA (Generally Available) Beta + GA features
API Support Stable APIs only Beta and stable APIs
Use Case Production workloads Preview features, early adopters
Risk Level Low Medium - features may change

5.8.3 Common GCP Resources

Category Resource Type Common Use
Compute google_compute_instance Compute Engine VMs
Compute google_cloudfunctions_function Cloud Functions
Networking google_compute_network VPC networks
Storage google_storage_bucket Cloud Storage buckets
Database google_sql_database_instance Cloud SQL
Containers google_container_cluster GKE clusters
IAM google_project_iam_member IAM bindings

5.8.4 GCP Data Sources

HCL Common GCP Data Sources 
# Get current project info
data "google_project" "current" {}

# Get current client configuration
data "google_client_config" "current" {}

# Get available zones in region
data "google_compute_zones" "available" {
  region = var.region
}

# Look up existing VPC
data "google_compute_network" "shared" {
  name    = "shared-vpc"
  project = var.host_project_id
}

# Get latest container-optimized OS image
data "google_compute_image" "cos" {
  family  = "cos-stable"
  project = "cos-cloud"
}

5.8.5 Multi-Project Deployment

HCL Multi-Project Configuration 
# Host project (Shared VPC)
provider "google" {
  alias   = "host"
  project = var.host_project_id
  region  = var.region
}

# Service project
provider "google" {
  alias   = "service"
  project = var.service_project_id
  region  = var.region
}

# Create Shared VPC in host project
resource "google_compute_network" "shared_vpc" {
  provider                = google.host
  name                    = "shared-vpc"
  auto_create_subnetworks = false
}

# Create service in service project using shared VPC
resource "google_cloud_run_service" "app" {
  provider = google.service
  name     = "my-app"
  location = var.region
  # ...
}

5.8.6 GCP Provider Best Practices

👤 Impersonation

Use service account impersonation instead of key files.

🏷️ Labels

Apply consistent labels for cost allocation and organization.

📦 Projects

Organize resources into projects for isolation and billing.

🔑 Workload Identity

Use Workload Identity for GKE service account binding.

5.9 Multi-Cloud Patterns

ADVANCED PRODUCTION

5.9.1 Multi-Cloud Provider Configuration

HCL Multi-Cloud Setup 
# terraform.tf - Multi-Cloud Requirements
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.31"
    }
    azurerm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"
      version = "~> 3.85"
    }
    google = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google"
      version = "~> 5.10"
    }
  }
}

# providers.tf - Configure all three
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
}

provider "google" {
  project = "my-gcp-project"
  region  = "us-central1"
}

5.9.2 Multi-Cloud Architecture Example

🌐 Multi-Cloud DR Architecture 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         MULTI-CLOUD DR ARCHITECTURE                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                              Global Load Balancer                               │
│                                     │                                            │
│                    ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                          │
│                    │                │                │                          │
│                    ▼                ▼                ▼                          │
│        ┌───────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐                 │
│        │     AWS       │  │    Azure      │  │     GCP       │                 │
│        │  (Primary)    │  │  (Secondary)  │  │(DR/Analytics) │                 │
│        │───────────────│  │───────────────│  │───────────────│                 │
│        │ • EKS Cluster │  │ • AKS Cluster │  │ • GKE Cluster │                 │
│        │ • RDS Primary │  │ • SQL Replica │  │ • BigQuery    │                 │
│        │ • S3 Storage  │  │ • Blob Storage│  │ • GCS Storage │                 │
│        │ • CloudFront  │  │ • Front Door  │  │ • Cloud CDN   │                 │
│        └───────────────┘  └───────────────┘  └───────────────┘                 │
│                    │                │                │                          │
│                    └────────────────┴────────────────┘                          │
│                              │                                                   │
│                     Cross-Cloud Data Sync                                       │
│                     (Real-time replication)                                     │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

5.9.3 Multi-Cloud Considerations

Consideration Challenge Solution
State Management Where to store state? Terraform Cloud or primary cloud's storage
Authentication Multiple credential sets OIDC federation from single identity provider
Networking Cross-cloud connectivity VPN tunnels, interconnects, or overlay networks
Naming Inconsistent conventions Locals with cloud-specific prefixes
Complexity Managing multiple providers Modular architecture, separate workspaces

5.9.4 Archived Providers

Some providers have been archived and are no longer actively maintained. Understanding how to handle these is important for long-term infrastructure management.

📋

Notable Archived Providers

Archived Provider Status Replacement/Alternative
terraform-provider-template Deprecated Use templatefile() function
terraform-provider-null Active (minimal) Built-in terraform_data resource
terraform-provider-opc Archived Use oci provider for Oracle Cloud
terraform-provider-packet Archived Use equinix provider
terraform-provider-scaffolding Template only Not for production use
HCL Migrating from Archived Providers 
# OLD: Using deprecated template provider
data "template_file" "user_data" {
  template = file("${path.module}/user-data.tpl")
  vars = {
    hostname = var.hostname
    region   = var.region
  }
}

# NEW: Using built-in templatefile() function
locals {
  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/user-data.tpl", {
    hostname = var.hostname
    region   = var.region
  })
}

# OLD: Using null_resource for triggers
resource "null_resource" "example" {
  triggers = {
    config_hash = sha256(jsonencode(var.config))
  }
  
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "echo 'Config changed'"
  }
}

# NEW: Using terraform_data (Terraform 1.4+)
resource "terraform_data" "example" {
  triggers_replace = [
    sha256(jsonencode(var.config))
  ]
  
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "echo 'Config changed'"
  }
}
⚠️

Working with Archived Providers

  • Check registry status: Look for "Archived" badge on registry.terraform.io
  • Plan migration: Archived providers may still work but won't receive updates
  • Security risk: No patches for vulnerabilities
  • State migration: Use terraform state mv when switching providers

5.10 Provider Troubleshooting

ESSENTIAL PRODUCTION

5.10.1 Common Provider Errors

Text Error: Provider Not Found 
Error: Failed to query available provider packages

Could not retrieve the list of available versions for provider
hashicorp/awss: provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/awss was not found

# SOLUTION: Check provider name spelling
# Wrong: hashicorp/awss
# Right: hashicorp/aws
Text Error: Version Constraints 
Error: Failed to query available provider packages

No available releases match the given constraints ~> 99.0 for provider
hashicorp/aws

# SOLUTION: Use valid version constraint
# Check latest version at registry.terraform.io

5.10.2 Authentication Errors

Text AWS Authentication Error 
Error: error configuring Terraform AWS Provider: no valid credential sources found

# SOLUTIONS:
# 1. Check environment variables
echo $AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
echo $AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

# 2. Check ~/.aws/credentials file exists and is valid

# 3. If using assume role, verify you can assume it
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/MyRole \
  --role-session-name test

# 4. If on EC2, verify instance profile is attached
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/

5.10.3 Enabling Debug Logging

Bash Debug Provider Issues 
# Enable detailed logging
export TF_LOG=DEBUG
export TF_LOG_PATH="./terraform.log"

# Run Terraform
terraform plan

# Check the log file for provider communication details
grep -i "provider" terraform.log

# Provider-specific logging (AWS)
export AWS_SDK_LOAD_CONFIG=1

# Log levels: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR
# TRACE is most verbose, includes full HTTP requests/responses

5.10.4 Lock File Issues

Bash Resolving Lock File Problems 
# Error: provider checksums don't match lock file
# Usually happens when switching between platforms (Mac/Linux/Windows)

# Solution 1: Update lock file for all platforms
terraform providers lock \
  -platform=windows_amd64 \
  -platform=linux_amd64 \
  -platform=darwin_amd64 \
  -platform=darwin_arm64

# Solution 2: Reinitialize with upgrade
terraform init -upgrade

# Solution 3: Delete lock file and reinitialize (last resort)
rm .terraform.lock.hcl
terraform init

5.10.5 Provider Troubleshooting Checklist

1️⃣ Check Provider Configuration

Verify required_providers block and version constraints

2️⃣ Verify Authentication

Test credentials outside Terraform using CLI tools

3️⃣ Check Network Connectivity

Ensure registry.terraform.io and cloud APIs are reachable

4️⃣ Enable Debug Logging

Set TF_LOG=DEBUG to capture detailed information

5️⃣ Check Provider Documentation

Review changelog for breaking changes in newer versions

📋 Chapter 5 Summary

Provider Fundamentals
  • Providers are plugins that connect to APIs
  • Use required_providers for version control
  • Commit .terraform.lock.hcl to Git
Authentication
  • Prefer instance profiles/managed identity
  • Use OIDC for CI/CD pipelines
  • Never hardcode credentials
Key Takeaways
  • ✅ Use provider aliases for multi-region/account
  • ✅ Pin versions with ~> constraint
  • ✅ Configure default_tags (AWS) / features (Azure)
  • ✅ Enable debug logging for troubleshooting
← Previous: Chapter 4 - Configuration Files Next: Chapter 6 - Resources →
🏗️ ⚡ 🔄 🎯 🛡️
Chapter 6

Resources - Complete Guide

Building Infrastructure Block by Block

⚠️ CRITICAL For Exam
8 Major Topics
Core Concept Importance
Must Master Priority

Chapter 6: Resources - Complete Guide

Resources are the heart of Terraform. They represent real infrastructure components—servers, databases, networks, DNS records, and everything in between. This chapter covers every aspect of resource management, from basic syntax to advanced patterns like lifecycle management and provisioners.

⚠️

CRITICAL FOR TERRAFORM CERTIFICATION

This chapter covers concepts that make up 20-30% of the HashiCorp Terraform Associate exam. Pay special attention to resource lifecycle, meta-arguments, and dependency management.

🎯 Learning Objectives

🏗️ Master resource block syntax and arguments
🔁 Understand resource lifecycle (create, update, destroy)
🔀 Use meta-arguments (count, for_each, depends_on)
⚙️ Control behavior with lifecycle blocks
📥 Import existing resources and manage drift
🛠️ Understand provisioners and when to use them

📑 In This Chapter

6.1 Resource Basics 6.2 Resource Lifecycle 6.3 Meta-Arguments 6.4 Lifecycle Block 6.5 Resource Dependencies 6.6 Provisioners 6.7 Resource Import 6.8 Resource Patterns

6.1 Resource Basics

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning the most fundamental building block of Terraform—resources. Every piece of infrastructure you manage with Terraform (servers, databases, networks, DNS records, IAM policies) is defined as a resource. Understanding resource syntax is like learning to write sentences in a new language—once you master it, you can express any infrastructure configuration. This section teaches you the anatomy of resource blocks, how to read provider documentation to find available resources and their arguments, and how resources connect to each other through references.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Understand resource block structure (type, name, arguments, nested blocks)
  • Know the difference between required, optional, and computed arguments
  • Be able to reference one resource's attributes from another resource
  • Understand resource addressing with count and for_each indices
  • Know what read-only attributes are and when they become available
🏗️

🧱 Resources: The Heart of Terraform

Resources are the primary way you tell Terraform what infrastructure to create and manage. Each resource block describes one or more infrastructure objects—like an EC2 instance, a VPC, or an S3 bucket.

  • Declarative: You describe the desired end state, not the steps to get there
  • Provider-specific: Each resource type (like aws_instance) comes from a provider
  • Identifiable: Each resource has a unique address for referencing
  • Stateful: Terraform tracks resource state to determine changes needed

6.1.1 Resource Block Syntax

📝 Understanding the Code Below

This example shows the complete anatomy of a resource block. The resource keyword starts the block, followed by the resource type ("aws_instance") which determines what kind of infrastructure to create, and your chosen local name ("web_server") which you'll use to reference this resource elsewhere. Inside the block, you provide arguments that configure the resource—some required (like ami), some optional, and some are nested blocks for complex configurations like storage.

HCL Resource Block Anatomy 
# Resource block structure:
# resource "PROVIDER_TYPE" "LOCAL_NAME" {
#   ARGUMENT = VALUE
# }

resource "aws_instance" "web_server" {
  # Required arguments
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  # Optional arguments
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.main.id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web.id]
  
  # Nested block arguments
  root_block_device {
    volume_size = 20
    volume_type = "gp3"
    encrypted   = true
  }
  
  # Tags (map argument)
  tags = {
    Name        = "web-server"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}
Resource Block Components
  • resource — Keyword that starts the block
  • PROVIDER_TYPE — Resource type (e.g., aws_instance, azurerm_virtual_network)
  • LOCAL_NAME — Your chosen identifier (must be unique within module)
  • Arguments — Configuration for the resource

6.1.2 Resource Addressing

🔗 Understanding Resource References

Resource addressing is how you connect resources together. When you create an EC2 instance that needs to be in a specific VPC subnet, you reference the subnet's ID using its address. The format is always resource_type.local_name.attribute. This creates an implicit dependency—Terraform automatically knows to create the referenced resource first. Understanding references is crucial because infrastructure components rarely exist in isolation; they form an interconnected graph of dependencies.

HCL Resource Addresses and References 
# Resource address format: <resource_type>.<local_name>
# Full reference: <resource_type>.<local_name>.<attribute>

resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  # Reference another resource's attribute
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id           # Full reference
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
  
  # Available after creation (read-only attributes)
  # aws_subnet.public.id
  # aws_subnet.public.arn
  # aws_subnet.public.availability_zone
}

# Reference in output
output "subnet_id" {
  value = aws_subnet.public.id
}

# Reference with count
# aws_instance.web[0].id
# aws_instance.web[1].id

# Reference with for_each
# aws_instance.web["app1"].id
# aws_instance.web["app2"].id

6.1.3 Argument Types

📋 Why Argument Types Matter

Every resource has different types of arguments. Required arguments must be provided or Terraform will fail—like specifying which AMI to use for an EC2 instance. Optional arguments have sensible defaults if you don't specify them. Computed arguments are read-only values that Terraform gets from the provider after creating the resource—like the instance ID or public IP. Nested blocks configure complex sub-components like storage volumes. Understanding these categories helps you read provider documentation effectively and know what you must specify vs. what you can rely on defaults for.

Argument Type Description Example
Required Must be provided ami = "ami-xxx"
Optional Has default value associate_public_ip = true
Computed Set by provider after creation id, arn
Nested Block Sub-configuration block root_block_device { }
Meta-Argument Special Terraform arguments count, for_each, depends_on

6.1.4 Read-Only Attributes

📖 Understanding Computed Attributes

Read-only (computed) attributes are values that only exist after Terraform creates the resource. You can't set them in your configuration—they're determined by the cloud provider. For example, when you create an EC2 instance, AWS assigns it an ID, private IP, and possibly a public IP. These values aren't known until the instance actually exists. You can reference these computed attributes in other resources (like associating an Elastic IP with an instance using its ID) or export them via outputs so other configurations or humans can use them. This deferred evaluation is why terraform plan sometimes shows (known after apply).

HCL Computed/Read-Only Attributes 
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# These attributes are computed AFTER the resource is created:
# - aws_instance.web.id                  → Instance ID
# - aws_instance.web.arn                 → Amazon Resource Name
# - aws_instance.web.public_ip           → Public IP (if assigned)
# - aws_instance.web.private_ip          → Private IP
# - aws_instance.web.public_dns          → Public DNS name
# - aws_instance.web.availability_zone   → AZ where deployed

# Use computed attributes in other resources
resource "aws_eip" "web" {
  instance = aws_instance.web.id  # Uses computed 'id'
  domain   = "vpc"
}

# Or in outputs
output "instance_details" {
  value = {
    id         = aws_instance.web.id
    public_ip  = aws_instance.web.public_ip
    private_ip = aws_instance.web.private_ip
  }
}

6.2 Resource Lifecycle

ESSENTIAL 📝 CRITICAL EXAM
🔄

Understanding Resource Lifecycle

Every resource goes through a lifecycle: creation, reading, updating, and deletion. Understanding how Terraform manages this lifecycle is fundamental to predicting behavior.

6.2.1 Create, Update, Delete Operations

🔄 The CRUD Lifecycle Explained

Terraform manages resources through a Create-Read-Update-Delete (CRUD) lifecycle. When you run terraform apply for the first time, resources are created. On subsequent runs, Terraform first reads (refreshes) the current state of your infrastructure, then compares it to your configuration to determine what changes are needed. If attributes differ, it performs an update (in-place if possible, or replace if required). When you remove a resource from your configuration or run terraform destroy, it deletes the resource. Understanding this lifecycle helps you predict what Terraform will do and avoid surprises.

🔄 Resource Lifecycle Flow 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         TERRAFORM RESOURCE LIFECYCLE                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐                 │
│   │   CREATE    │        │    READ     │        │   UPDATE    │                 │
│   │ ─────────── │        │ ─────────── │        │ ─────────── │                 │
│   │ terraform   │        │ terraform   │        │ terraform   │                 │
│   │ apply       │───────►│ refresh     │───────►│ apply       │                 │
│   │             │        │ (implicit)  │        │             │                 │
│   │ + resource  │        │             │        │ ~ resource  │                 │
│   └─────────────┘        └─────────────┘        └─────────────┘                 │
│          │                      │                      │                         │
│          │                      │                      │                         │
│          │                      ▼                      │                         │
│          │              ┌─────────────┐               │                         │
│          │              │   COMPARE   │               │                         │
│          │              │ ─────────── │               │                         │
│          │              │ Config vs   │◄──────────────┘                         │
│          │              │ State vs    │                                         │
│          │              │ Real World  │                                         │
│          │              └──────┬──────┘                                         │
│          │                     │                                                 │
│          │                     │ (if deleted from config)                       │
│          │                     ▼                                                 │
│          │              ┌─────────────┐                                         │
│          │              │   DELETE    │                                         │
│          └─────────────►│ ─────────── │                                         │
│                         │ terraform   │                                         │
│                         │ destroy     │                                         │
│                         │             │                                         │
│                         │ - resource  │                                         │
│                         └─────────────┘                                         │
│                                                                                  │
│   Plan Symbols:  + create   ~ update (in-place)   -/+ replace   - destroy      │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

6.2.2 In-Place Update vs Replace

⚠️ Critical Distinction: Update vs Replace

This is one of the most important concepts to understand. When you change a resource's configuration, Terraform determines whether it can update in-place (modify the existing resource without downtime) or must replace (destroy and recreate). Changing an EC2 instance's tags? In-place, no problem. Changing its AMI? That's an immutable attribute—Terraform must destroy the old instance and create a new one, causing downtime. Always run terraform plan and look for # forces replacement warnings to understand the impact before applying.

Operation Symbol When It Happens Impact
In-Place Update ~ Modifying tags, security groups, etc. 🟢 No downtime
Replace (destroy/create) -/+ Changing immutable attributes (AMI, disk type) 🔴 Causes downtime
Replace (create/destroy) +/- With create_before_destroy lifecycle 🟡 Minimizes downtime
Text terraform plan Output Examples 
# In-place update (not disruptive)
  ~ resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      ~ tags = {
          + "NewTag" = "value"
        }
    }

# Replace - destroy then create (DISRUPTIVE!)
-/+ resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      ~ ami = "ami-old" -> "ami-new" # forces replacement
    }

# Replace - create then destroy (less disruptive)
+/- resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      ~ ami = "ami-old" -> "ami-new" # forces replacement
      # (with create_before_destroy = true)
    }
⚠️

Watch for "forces replacement"

Always look for # forces replacement in plan output. This means the resource will be destroyed and recreated, potentially causing downtime!

6.2.3 Resource State

💾 The State File: Terraform's Memory

Terraform stores information about your managed infrastructure in a state file (terraform.tfstate). This JSON file maps your configuration to real-world resources—it knows that aws_instance.web in your code corresponds to instance i-0abc123def456789 in AWS. Without state, Terraform wouldn't know what already exists. The state file also caches attribute values (like IPs and ARNs) to improve performance. Never edit state files manually—use terraform state commands. For team environments, always use remote state backends (S3, Terraform Cloud) with locking to prevent conflicts.

JSON Resource in State File 
{
  "resources": [
    {
      "mode": "managed",
      "type": "aws_instance",
      "name": "web",
      "provider": "provider[\"registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws\"]",
      "instances": [
        {
          "schema_version": 1,
          "attributes": {
            "id": "i-0abc123def456789",
            "ami": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0",
            "instance_type": "t3.micro",
            "public_ip": "54.123.45.67",
            "tags": {
              "Name": "web-server"
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

6.2.4 Drift Detection

🔍 What is Configuration Drift?

Drift occurs when the actual infrastructure differs from what Terraform expects. This happens when someone manually changes a resource through the AWS Console, CLI, or another tool. For example, if someone manually adds a tag to an EC2 instance that Terraform manages, the real infrastructure is now "drifted" from your Terraform state. Terraform detects drift during the refresh phase of plan and apply. You can either accept the real-world changes (-refresh-only) or let Terraform revert them to match your configuration. Regular drift detection is essential for maintaining infrastructure-as-code integrity.

Bash Detecting and Handling Drift 
# Refresh state to detect drift
terraform refresh  # Deprecated in favor of:
terraform apply -refresh-only

# Plan shows differences between real infrastructure and state
terraform plan

# Example drift output:
# Note: Objects have changed outside of Terraform

#   ~ resource "aws_instance" "web" {
#       ~ tags = {
#           - "Environment" = "production" -> null
#           + "Environment" = "staging"
#         }
#     }

# Options to handle drift:
# 1. Accept real-world state: terraform apply -refresh-only
# 2. Revert to config: terraform apply (will change real resource)

6.2.5 lifecycle

⚙️ Fine-Tuning Resource Behavior

The lifecycle meta-argument gives you precise control over how Terraform handles resource changes. Need zero-downtime deployments? Use create_before_destroy so Terraform creates the replacement before destroying the old resource. Want to protect a database from accidental deletion? prevent_destroy will make Terraform refuse to destroy it. Have an auto-scaling group where desired_capacity changes externally? ignore_changes tells Terraform to leave that attribute alone. These lifecycle rules are essential for production environments where you need to balance infrastructure-as-code automation with real-world operational requirements.

The lifecycle meta-argument controls how Terraform handles resource creation, updates, and deletion.

HCL lifecycle Block Options 
# Complete lifecycle configuration
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  lifecycle {
    # Create new resource before destroying old one
    create_before_destroy = true
    
    # Prevent accidental destruction
    prevent_destroy = true
    
    # Ignore changes to specific attributes
    ignore_changes = [
      tags,
      user_data,
    ]
    
    # Replace resource when these change (Terraform 1.2+)
    replace_triggered_by = [
      aws_security_group.web.id
    ]
  }
}

# Conditional ignore_changes
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "app" {
  name                = "app-asg"
  min_size            = 2
  max_size            = 10
  desired_capacity    = 4  # Set initially
  
  lifecycle {
    # Don't revert manual scaling changes
    ignore_changes = [desired_capacity]
  }
}
📋

lifecycle Attributes

Attribute Purpose Use Case
create_before_destroy Create replacement before destroying original Zero-downtime updates
prevent_destroy Block resource destruction Protect critical resources
ignore_changes Ignore external modifications Auto-scaling, external updates
replace_triggered_by Force replacement when referenced resource changes Cascading updates
precondition Validate before apply Guard rails
postcondition Validate after apply Verify state

6.3 Meta-Arguments

ESSENTIAL 📝 CRITICAL EXAM
🎯

What Are Meta-Arguments?

Meta-arguments are special arguments that work with all resources, regardless of type. They control how Terraform manages resources rather than what is configured.

6.3.1 count - Create Multiple Instances

🔢 Creating Multiple Resources with count

The count meta-argument lets you create multiple instances of a resource from a single block. Instead of writing three separate aws_instance blocks for three web servers, you write one block with count = 3. Inside the block, count.index gives you the current iteration number (0, 1, 2) so you can differentiate instances with unique names or configurations. The catch: resources are addressed by index (aws_instance.web[0]), so if you remove an item from the middle, all subsequent indices shift, causing unwanted resource recreation. For most use cases, for_each is preferred.

HCL count Meta-Argument 
# Create 3 identical instances
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count = 3

  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  tags = {
    Name = "web-server-${count.index}"  # web-server-0, web-server-1, web-server-2
  }
}

# Access individual instances
output "first_instance_id" {
  value = aws_instance.web[0].id
}

# Access all instance IDs
output "all_instance_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].id  # Splat expression
}

# Conditional creation (0 = don't create)
variable "create_bastion" {
  type    = bool
  default = false
}

resource "aws_instance" "bastion" {
  count = var.create_bastion ? 1 : 0  # Creates only if true

  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}
⚠️

count Index Problem

Removing an item from the middle of a count list causes all subsequent resources to be recreated. Prefer for_each for resources that may be individually added/removed.

6.3.2 for_each - Create Named Instances

🏷️ The Power of Named Resources

for_each is the modern, preferred way to create multiple resources. Unlike count which uses numeric indices, for_each creates resources with named keys. If you have buckets ["logs", "backups", "artifacts"], you get aws_s3_bucket.buckets["logs"], not aws_s3_bucket.buckets[0]. The huge advantage? Remove "backups" from your set and only that bucket is destroyed—"logs" and "artifacts" remain untouched with their same addresses. each.key gives you the current key and each.value gives the value (same as key for sets, or the value for maps). Use for_each whenever resources have logical identities beyond just being "the third one."

HCL for_each Meta-Argument 
# for_each with set of strings
variable "bucket_names" {
  type    = set(string)
  default = ["logs", "backups", "artifacts"]
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "buckets" {
  for_each = var.bucket_names

  bucket = "${var.project}-${each.value}"

  tags = {
    Name = each.value  # "logs", "backups", "artifacts"
  }
}

# for_each with map
variable "instances" {
  type = map(object({
    instance_type = string
    ami           = string
  }))
  default = {
    web = {
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      ami           = "ami-web123"
    }
    api = {
      instance_type = "t3.small"
      ami           = "ami-api456"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "servers" {
  for_each = var.instances

  ami           = each.value.ami
  instance_type = each.value.instance_type

  tags = {
    Name = each.key  # "web", "api"
  }
}

# Access specific instance
output "web_instance_id" {
  value = aws_instance.servers["web"].id
}

# Access all
output "all_ids" {
  value = { for k, v in aws_instance.servers : k => v.id }
}
Feature count for_each
Addressing resource[0], resource[1] resource["name"]
Input Type Number Set or Map
Removal Behavior Index shift causes recreation Only removed item destroyed
Use Case Identical resources Uniquely identified resources
Recommendation Simple cases, conditional creation Most cases - preferred

6.3.3 depends_on - Explicit Dependencies

🔗 When Automatic Dependencies Aren't Enough

Terraform automatically creates dependencies when one resource references another's attribute (e.g., vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id). But sometimes dependencies exist that aren't visible in references. For example, an EKS cluster might need an IAM role to exist first, but if the role ARN comes from a variable rather than a reference, Terraform doesn't know to create the role first. depends_on explicitly declares these "hidden" dependencies. Use it sparingly—if you find yourself using it frequently, your configuration might benefit from restructuring to make dependencies explicit through references instead.
