HCL depends_on Meta-Argument 
# Implicit dependency (automatic via references)
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id  # Implicit dependency on VPC
  # Terraform automatically creates VPC first
}

# Explicit dependency (when no reference exists)
resource "aws_iam_role" "eks" {
  name = "eks-cluster-role"
  # ... role configuration
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "eks_policy" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.eks.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSClusterPolicy"
}

resource "aws_eks_cluster" "main" {
  name     = "my-cluster"
  role_arn = aws_iam_role.eks.arn

  # Explicit dependency - wait for policy attachment
  # Even though there's no direct reference to the attachment
  depends_on = [
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.eks_policy
  ]
}
💡

When to Use depends_on

Only use depends_on when there's a hidden dependency that Terraform can't detect from references. Overusing it can slow down operations.

6.3.4 provider - Specify Non-Default Provider

HCL provider Meta-Argument 
# Multiple provider configurations
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"  # Default
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "west"
  region = "us-west-2"
}

# Use default provider
resource "aws_instance" "east" {
  ami           = "ami-east"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# Use aliased provider
resource "aws_instance" "west" {
  provider = aws.west  # Specifies which provider to use

  ami           = "ami-west"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

6.4 Lifecycle Block

ESSENTIAL 📝 CRITICAL EXAM
⚙️

Controlling Resource Behavior

The lifecycle block customizes how Terraform creates, updates, and deletes resources. This is critical for production safety and zero-downtime deployments.

6.4.1 create_before_destroy

HCL Zero-Downtime Replacement 
# Default behavior: destroy, then create (causes downtime)
# With create_before_destroy: create new, then destroy old

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

# Common use case: Launch configurations
resource "aws_launch_configuration" "web" {
  name_prefix   = "web-"
  image_id      = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

# Required for resources that must have unique names
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name_prefix = "web-sg-"  # Use name_prefix instead of name
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

6.4.2 prevent_destroy

HCL Protect Critical Resources 
# Prevent accidental deletion of critical resources
resource "aws_db_instance" "production" {
  identifier     = "production-database"
  engine         = "postgres"
  instance_class = "db.t3.large"
  
  # Production safety
  deletion_protection = true  # AWS-level protection

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true  # Terraform-level protection
  }
}

# terraform destroy will fail with:
# Error: Instance cannot be destroyed
#
# Resource aws_db_instance.production has lifecycle.prevent_destroy
# set, but the plan calls for this resource to be destroyed.

# To actually destroy: first set prevent_destroy = false
⚠️

prevent_destroy Limitation

prevent_destroy only prevents destruction via Terraform. If you remove the resource block entirely, Terraform will still try to destroy it. Use with AWS deletion protection for full safety.

6.4.3 ignore_changes

HCL Ignore External Changes 
# Ignore specific attributes (useful when external systems modify resources)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [
      tags,        # Ignore tag changes (maybe auto-tagged by AWS)
      user_data,   # Ignore user_data changes
    ]
  }
}

# Ignore ALL changes (make resource read-only after creation)
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "web" {
  name                = "web-asg"
  min_size            = 2
  max_size            = 10
  desired_capacity    = 4  # ASG might scale this

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = all  # Never update after creation
  }
}

# Common pattern: EKS node groups (AWS manages capacity)
resource "aws_eks_node_group" "workers" {
  cluster_name    = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  node_group_name = "workers"
  
  scaling_config {
    desired_size = 3
    min_size     = 1
    max_size     = 10
  }

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [scaling_config[0].desired_size]
  }
}

6.4.4 replace_triggered_by (Terraform 1.2+)

HCL Force Replacement on Trigger 
# Replace resource when another resource changes
resource "aws_appautoscaling_target" "ecs" {
  # ... configuration
}

resource "aws_ecs_service" "web" {
  name            = "web-service"
  cluster         = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id
  task_definition = aws_ecs_task_definition.web.arn
  desired_count   = 3

  lifecycle {
    replace_triggered_by = [
      # Recreate service if autoscaling target changes
      aws_appautoscaling_target.ecs
    ]
  }
}

# Replace when a variable changes
resource "null_resource" "deploy" {
  triggers = {
    version = var.app_version
  }

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "deploy.sh"
  }

  lifecycle {
    replace_triggered_by = [
      null_resource.config_change.id
    ]
  }
}

6.4.5 precondition and postcondition (Terraform 1.2+)

HCL Resource Validation 
# Validate before resource creation
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  lifecycle {
    # Check BEFORE creation
    precondition {
      condition     = contains(["t3.micro", "t3.small", "t3.medium"], var.instance_type)
      error_message = "Instance type must be t3.micro, t3.small, or t3.medium."
    }

    # Check AFTER creation  
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.public_ip != null
      error_message = "Instance must have a public IP address."
    }
  }
}

# Validate data sources too
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]  # Canonical

  lifecycle {
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.architecture == "x86_64"
      error_message = "AMI must be x86_64 architecture."
    }
  }
}

6.5 Resource Dependencies

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

Why Dependencies Matter

Infrastructure resources are interconnected—a subnet needs a VPC to exist first, an EC2 instance needs a subnet and security group, a load balancer needs target groups. Terraform builds a dependency graph to determine the correct order to create, update, and destroy resources. Most dependencies are detected automatically when you reference one resource's attribute in another. Understanding both implicit and explicit dependencies helps you structure your code correctly and troubleshoot ordering issues when they occur.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Understand how Terraform builds and uses the dependency graph
  • Know the difference between implicit and explicit dependencies
  • Be able to visualize your dependency graph with terraform graph
  • Configure custom timeouts for slow-creating resources

6.5.1 Implicit Dependencies

HCL Automatic Dependency Detection 
# Terraform automatically detects dependencies from references

resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id  # Creates implicit dependency
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
}

resource "aws_internet_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id  # Depends on VPC
}

resource "aws_route_table" "public" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  route {
    cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
    gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.main.id  # Depends on IGW
  }
}

# Terraform builds a dependency graph:
# VPC -> Subnet
# VPC -> IGW
# VPC -> Route Table -> (also IGW)
📊 Dependency Graph Visualization 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         TERRAFORM DEPENDENCY GRAPH                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                              ┌─────────────┐                                    │
│                              │   aws_vpc   │                                    │
│                              │    main     │                                    │
│                              └──────┬──────┘                                    │
│                    ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                          │
│                    │                │                │                          │
│                    ▼                ▼                ▼                          │
│            ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                   │
│            │ aws_subnet  │  │   aws_igw   │  │aws_route_tbl│                   │
│            │   public    │  │    main     │  │   public    │                   │
│            └─────────────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘                   │
│                                    │                │                          │
│                                    └────────┬───────┘                          │
│                                             │                                   │
│                                             ▼                                   │
│                                    ┌─────────────┐                              │
│                                    │ aws_route   │                              │
│                                    │   (inline)  │                              │
│                                    └─────────────┘                              │
│                                                                                  │
│   View graph: terraform graph | dot -Tpng > graph.png                           │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

6.5.2 Explicit Dependencies (depends_on)

HCL When to Use depends_on 
# Scenario: IAM policy must be attached before creating resources that use it

resource "aws_iam_role" "lambda" {
  name = "lambda-execution-role"
  
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "lambda.amazonaws.com"
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "lambda_vpc" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.lambda.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole"
}

resource "aws_lambda_function" "main" {
  function_name = "my-function"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "nodejs18.x"
  filename      = "lambda.zip"

  vpc_config {
    subnet_ids         = [aws_subnet.private.id]
    security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.lambda.id]
  }

  # Without depends_on, Lambda might be created before policy attachment
  # This would cause "InvalidParameterValueException" errors
  depends_on = [
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.lambda_vpc
  ]
}
💡

depends_on Best Practice

Use depends_on sparingly. If possible, create an implicit dependency by referencing an attribute. Explicit dependencies can't be automatically optimized.

6.5.3 Dependency Graph Commands

Bash Visualizing Dependencies 
# Generate dependency graph in DOT format
terraform graph

# Generate and visualize (requires Graphviz)
terraform graph | dot -Tpng > dependency-graph.png

# Generate only plan graph (what will change)
terraform graph -type=plan

# Generate only apply graph
terraform graph -type=apply

# Generate refresh graph (drift detection)
terraform graph -type=refresh

# Example output (DOT format):
digraph {
  compound = "true"
  "aws_vpc.main" -> "aws_subnet.public"
  "aws_vpc.main" -> "aws_internet_gateway.main"
  "aws_internet_gateway.main" -> "aws_route_table.public"
}

6.5.4 Custom Timeout Configuration

Many resources support custom timeouts for create, update, and delete operations.

HCL Timeout Block Examples 
# Database with extended timeouts
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  identifier        = "production-db"
  engine            = "postgres"
  engine_version    = "14.6"
  instance_class    = "db.r5.xlarge"
  allocated_storage = 500

  timeouts {
    create = "60m"   # Allow up to 60 minutes for creation
    update = "90m"   # Large databases take time to modify
    delete = "30m"   # Deletion with final snapshot
  }
}

# EKS cluster with custom timeouts
resource "aws_eks_cluster" "main" {
  name     = "production-cluster"
  role_arn = aws_iam_role.cluster.arn

  vpc_config {
    subnet_ids = var.subnet_ids
  }

  timeouts {
    create = "45m"   # EKS clusters take 20-30 mins typically
    update = "60m"   # Node group updates can be slow
    delete = "30m"   # Clean deletion
  }
}

# Lambda function (shorter timeouts)
resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" {
  function_name = "api-handler"
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "nodejs18.x"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  filename      = "function.zip"

  timeouts {
    create = "10m"   # Lambda deploys quickly
    update = "10m"
  }
}
💡

Timeout Best Practices

  • Default timeouts: Providers have sensible defaults; only override when needed
  • CI/CD consideration: Extend timeouts for resources that may take longer in shared environments
  • Not all resources: Check provider docs - not every resource supports timeouts

6.6 Provisioners

ADVANCED 📝 EXAM TOPIC
⚠️

When Nothing Else Works

Provisioners execute scripts or commands on a local or remote machine after a resource is created. They're useful when you need to bootstrap software on a server that cloud-init can't handle. However, HashiCorp explicitly recommends provisioners as a "last resort" because they break Terraform's declarative model—Terraform can't track what provisioners did, and failures cause unpredictable state. In nearly all cases, you should use cloud-init/user_data, pre-baked AMIs (Packer), or configuration management tools (Ansible, Chef, Puppet) instead.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Understand when provisioners might be appropriate (rarely!)
  • Know the three provisioner types: local-exec, remote-exec, file
  • Understand provisioner failure behavior and the taint mechanism
  • Know better alternatives for most provisioner use cases

6.6.1 local-exec Provisioner

HCL local-exec Examples 
# Runs commands on the machine running Terraform (not the resource)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  # After instance is created, run local command
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "echo ${self.public_ip} >> ip_addresses.txt"
  }
}

# With working directory and environment
resource "null_resource" "deploy" {
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command     = "./deploy.sh"
    working_dir = "${path.module}/scripts"
    
    environment = {
      ENVIRONMENT = var.environment
      VERSION     = var.app_version
    }
  }
}

# Choosing interpreter
resource "null_resource" "powershell" {
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command     = "Get-Date; Write-Host 'Deployment complete'"
    interpreter = ["PowerShell", "-Command"]
  }
}

# Using when = destroy for cleanup
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    when    = destroy
    command = "echo 'Instance ${self.id} is being destroyed' >> destroy.log"
  }
}

6.6.2 remote-exec Provisioner

HCL remote-exec Examples 
# Runs commands ON the remote resource via SSH or WinRM
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  key_name      = aws_key_pair.deployer.key_name

  # Connection block (required for remote-exec)
  connection {
    type        = "ssh"
    user        = "ec2-user"
    private_key = file("~/.ssh/id_rsa")
    host        = self.public_ip
  }

  # Run inline commands
  provisioner "remote-exec" {
    inline = [
      "sudo yum update -y",
      "sudo yum install -y nginx",
      "sudo systemctl start nginx",
      "sudo systemctl enable nginx"
    ]
  }
}

# Run a script file
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  key_name      = aws_key_pair.deployer.key_name

  connection {
    type        = "ssh"
    user        = "ubuntu"
    private_key = file(var.private_key_path)
    host        = self.public_ip
  }

  # Upload and execute script
  provisioner "file" {
    source      = "scripts/setup.sh"
    destination = "/tmp/setup.sh"
  }

  provisioner "remote-exec" {
    inline = [
      "chmod +x /tmp/setup.sh",
      "/tmp/setup.sh"
    ]
  }
}

6.6.3 file Provisioner

HCL file Provisioner Examples 
# Copy a single file
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  connection {
    type        = "ssh"
    user        = "ec2-user"
    private_key = file("~/.ssh/id_rsa")
    host        = self.public_ip
  }

  provisioner "file" {
    source      = "config/app.conf"
    destination = "/etc/myapp/app.conf"
  }
}

# Copy a directory
provisioner "file" {
  source      = "scripts/"      # Trailing slash copies contents
  destination = "/opt/scripts"
}

# Using content instead of source
provisioner "file" {
  content     = templatefile("${path.module}/config.tftpl", {
    db_host = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
    db_name = var.db_name
  })
  destination = "/etc/myapp/config.json"
}

6.6.4 Provisioner Failure Behavior

HCL Handling Provisioner Failures 
# Default: on_failure = fail (terraform apply fails)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command    = "exit 1"  # This will fail
    on_failure = continue  # Continue despite failure
  }
}

# Options:
# on_failure = fail     → Stop and mark resource as tainted (default)
# on_failure = continue → Ignore errors and continue

# Resource gets TAINTED on provisioner failure
# Next apply will destroy and recreate the resource
# Unless you run: terraform untaint aws_instance.web
⚠️

Provisioner Alternatives

Preferred alternatives: cloud-init/user_data, Packer images, Ansible/Chef/Puppet, AWS SSM Run Command. These tools are more reliable and don't require network access during apply.

6.6.5 Provisioner Triggers

Control when provisioners run using triggers with null_resource or terraform_data.

HCL Triggering Provisioners Conditionally 
# Using null_resource with triggers (legacy approach)
resource "null_resource" "deploy" {
  triggers = {
    # Re-run when these values change
    config_hash   = sha256(file("${path.module}/config.yml"))
    instance_id   = aws_instance.web.id
    always_run    = timestamp()  # Run on every apply
  }

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "ansible-playbook -i '${aws_instance.web.public_ip},' deploy.yml"
  }
}

# Using terraform_data (Terraform 1.4+ - recommended)
resource "terraform_data" "deploy" {
  triggers_replace = [
    sha256(file("${path.module}/config.yml")),
    aws_instance.web.id,
  ]

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "./deploy.sh ${aws_instance.web.public_ip}"
  }
}

# Trigger only on specific changes
resource "terraform_data" "config_update" {
  triggers_replace = [
    sha256(jsonencode(var.app_config))
  ]

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "curl -X POST https://api.example.com/refresh-config"
  }
}

6.6.6 on_failure Behavior

📋

on_failure Options

Setting Behavior Use Case
fail Stop apply, taint resource Critical provisioner (default)
continue Log error, continue apply Non-critical operations
HCL on_failure Usage Patterns 
# Critical setup must succeed
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  provisioner "remote-exec" {
    inline = [
      "sudo apt-get update",
      "sudo apt-get install -y nginx",
    ]
    
    on_failure = fail  # Default - apply fails if nginx install fails
  }
}

# Notification is optional - continue even if it fails
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  # Critical: must succeed
  provisioner "remote-exec" {
    inline = ["sudo bootstrap.sh"]
    on_failure = fail
  }

  # Optional: notification can fail
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command    = "curl -X POST https://slack.webhook/notify"
    on_failure = continue  # Don't fail if Slack is down
  }
}

6.6.7 Why Provisioners are Last Resort

⚠️

HashiCorp's Official Stance

Provisioners are a "last resort" because they break Terraform's declarative model and introduce unpredictability.

📋

Provisioner Problems vs Alternatives

Problem Why It's Bad Better Alternative
Only runs on create/destroy Configuration drift not detected Configuration management (Ansible)
Requires network access SSH/WinRM must be open at apply time Cloud-init, AMI baking (Packer)
No state tracking Terraform can't detect provisioner drift AWS SSM, EC2 Image Builder
Failure taints resource May recreate working infrastructure user_data with cloud-init
Not idempotent Running twice may break things Pre-built images with Packer
HCL Better: Using user_data Instead 
# BETTER: Cloud-init via user_data (no provisioner needed)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  user_data = <<-EOF
    #!/bin/bash
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y nginx
    systemctl enable nginx
    systemctl start nginx
  EOF

  user_data_replace_on_change = true
}

# BEST: Pre-baked AMI with Packer
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-yourprebaked123"  # Packer-built AMI
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  # No provisioner needed - everything pre-installed
}

6.7 Resource Import

ESSENTIAL PRODUCTION
🎯

Bringing Existing Infrastructure Under Terraform Control

Real-world projects rarely start from scratch. You'll often need to manage infrastructure that was created manually through the AWS Console, CLI, or other tools. Resource import lets you bring these existing resources under Terraform management without recreating them. Terraform 1.5+ introduced the import block with -generate-config-out which automatically generates HCL configuration—a massive improvement over the legacy terraform import command. The moved block helps you refactor resource names or reorganize into modules without destroying infrastructure.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Import existing resources using the modern import block (Terraform 1.5+)
  • Use -generate-config-out to auto-generate HCL from imports
  • Understand the legacy terraform import command and when you might still need it
  • Refactor resource names and module structures with moved blocks

6.7.1 terraform import Command (Legacy)

Bash terraform import Command 
# Step 1: Write the resource block first (import doesn't generate it)
# main.tf
resource "aws_instance" "imported" {
  # Minimum required arguments (fill in after import)
}

# Step 2: Run terraform import
terraform import aws_instance.imported i-0abc123def456789

# Step 3: View imported state
terraform state show aws_instance.imported

# Step 4: Copy attributes from state to your config
# Update main.tf with actual values from state show output

# Step 5: Run plan to verify no changes
terraform plan
# Should show: "No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration."

# Import other resource types:
terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-0123456789
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.data my-bucket-name
terraform import azurerm_virtual_network.main /subscriptions/xxx/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myVnet
terraform import google_compute_instance.vm projects/my-project/zones/us-central1-a/instances/my-instance

6.7.2 import Block (Terraform 1.5+) - Recommended

HCL Declarative Import with Config Generation 
# imports.tf - Declare what to import

import {
  to = aws_instance.existing_web
  id = "i-0abc123def456789"
}

import {
  to = aws_vpc.existing
  id = "vpc-0123456789abcdef"
}

import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.existing
  id = "my-existing-bucket"
}
Bash Generate Configuration from Import 
# Generate HCL configuration automatically!
terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf

# Review the generated configuration
cat generated.tf

# Apply to complete the import
terraform apply

# Clean up: Remove import blocks after successful import
# (They're idempotent but unnecessary after import)

6.7.3 Bulk Import Pattern

HCL Import Multiple Resources with for_each 
# List of existing resources to import
locals {
  existing_buckets = {
    logs      = "company-logs-bucket"
    backups   = "company-backups-bucket"
    artifacts = "company-artifacts-bucket"
  }
}

# Import blocks with for_each
import {
  for_each = local.existing_buckets
  to       = aws_s3_bucket.imported[each.key]
  id       = each.value
}

# Resource blocks
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "imported" {
  for_each = local.existing_buckets
  bucket   = each.value
}

# Run: terraform plan -generate-config-out=imported_buckets.tf

6.7.4 Multiple Moves

Use moved blocks to refactor resource names or move resources between modules without recreation.

HCL Moved Block Examples 
# Rename a resource
moved {
  from = aws_instance.web
  to   = aws_instance.web_server
}

# Move to module
moved {
  from = aws_instance.web_server
  to   = module.compute.aws_instance.web
}

# Move from count to for_each
moved {
  from = aws_instance.web[0]
  to   = aws_instance.web["primary"]
}

moved {
  from = aws_instance.web[1]
  to   = aws_instance.web["secondary"]
}

# Move between modules
moved {
  from = module.old_network.aws_vpc.main
  to   = module.new_network.aws_vpc.main
}

# Refactoring for_each keys
moved {
  from = aws_s3_bucket.data["old_name"]
  to   = aws_s3_bucket.data["new_name"]
}
💡

Moved Block Best Practices

  • Keep for history: Leave moved blocks in code for other team members running older state
  • Clean up later: Safe to remove after all users have applied
  • Chain moves: Multiple moves can be chained for complex refactoring
  • No recreation: State is updated without destroying resources
HCL Multiple Moves in One File 
# moves.tf - Refactoring multiple resources
# Moving from flat structure to module

moved {
  from = aws_vpc.main
  to   = module.network.aws_vpc.this
}

moved {
  from = aws_subnet.public
  to   = module.network.aws_subnet.public
}

moved {
  from = aws_subnet.private
  to   = module.network.aws_subnet.private
}

moved {
  from = aws_internet_gateway.main
  to   = module.network.aws_internet_gateway.this
}

moved {
  from = aws_nat_gateway.main
  to   = module.network.aws_nat_gateway.this
}

# After terraform apply:
# - State is updated with new addresses
# - No infrastructure changes
# - Plan shows: "No changes"

6.8 Resource Patterns

ADVANCED PRODUCTION
🎯

Battle-Tested Resource Strategies

These patterns solve real-world problems you'll encounter in production Terraform. Conditional resource creation lets you enable/disable features per environment (like deploying a bastion host only in prod). Dynamic blocks generate repeated nested configuration based on variables—essential for security group rules or EBS volumes. Naming conventions ensure consistent, predictable resource names that include environment, project, and component information. These patterns separate junior Terraform users from senior practitioners who write maintainable, scalable infrastructure code.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Conditionally create resources using count with ternary expressions
  • Generate dynamic nested blocks from variable data
  • Implement consistent naming conventions across all resources
  • Combine patterns for flexible, environment-aware configurations

6.8.1 Conditional Resource Creation

HCL Conditional Patterns 
# Pattern 1: count with condition
variable "create_bastion" {
  type    = bool
  default = false
}

resource "aws_instance" "bastion" {
  count = var.create_bastion ? 1 : 0  # Creates 0 or 1

  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# Pattern 2: Environment-based
variable "environment" {
  type = string
}

resource "aws_instance" "debug" {
  count = var.environment == "development" ? 1 : 0
  # Only in dev environment
}

# Pattern 3: Feature flags
variable "features" {
  type = object({
    enable_monitoring = bool
    enable_backup     = bool
  })
}

resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "cpu" {
  count = var.features.enable_monitoring ? 1 : 0
  # ...
}

# Safe reference (handles count = 0)
output "bastion_ip" {
  value = var.create_bastion ? aws_instance.bastion[0].public_ip : null
}

6.8.2 Dynamic Blocks

HCL Dynamic Block for Repeated Nested Blocks 
# Instead of repeating ingress blocks manually:
variable "ingress_rules" {
  type = list(object({
    port        = number
    protocol    = string
    cidr_blocks = list(string)
    description = string
  }))
  default = [
    { port = 80,  protocol = "tcp", cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"], description = "HTTP" },
    { port = 443, protocol = "tcp", cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"], description = "HTTPS" },
    { port = 22,  protocol = "tcp", cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/8"], description = "SSH" }
  ]
}

resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name   = "web-sg"
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  # Dynamic block generates multiple ingress blocks
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.ingress_rules
    content {
      from_port   = ingress.value.port
      to_port     = ingress.value.port
      protocol    = ingress.value.protocol
      cidr_blocks = ingress.value.cidr_blocks
      description = ingress.value.description
    }
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}

6.8.3 Resource Naming Patterns

HCL Consistent Naming with Locals 
# locals.tf - Centralized naming convention
locals {
  # Naming components
  name_prefix = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  
  # Resource names
  names = {
    vpc           = "${local.name_prefix}-vpc"
    public_subnet = "${local.name_prefix}-public"
    private_subnet= "${local.name_prefix}-private"
    web_sg        = "${local.name_prefix}-web-sg"
    db_sg         = "${local.name_prefix}-db-sg"
  }
  
  # Common tags
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    Owner       = var.owner
  }
}

# Usage
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"

  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = local.names.vpc
  })
}

resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name   = local.names.web_sg
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = local.names.web_sg
  })
}

📋 Chapter 6 Summary

Resource Fundamentals
  • Resources are the core building blocks
  • Address format: type.name
  • Computed attributes available after creation
Meta-Arguments
  • count - Create multiple identical
  • for_each - Create named instances
  • depends_on - Explicit dependencies
Key Lifecycle Settings
  • create_before_destroy - Zero downtime
  • prevent_destroy - Safety protection
  • ignore_changes - External modifications
Exam Tips
  • ✅ Understand ~> vs -/+ replacement
  • ✅ Know when to use count vs for_each
  • ✅ Import blocks (1.5+) over commands
  • ✅ Provisioners are last resort
← Previous: Chapter 5 - Providers Next: Chapter 7 - Data Sources →
CHAPTER 7

Data Sources

Query and Reference Existing Infrastructure

🔍
6 Sections
~20 Minutes
Read-Only Operations

🔍 Data Sources: Query External Data

📎 Learning Objectives

  • Understand the difference between resources and data sources
  • Query existing infrastructure without creating new resources
  • Filter data sources to find specific resources
  • Use data sources for dynamic configuration lookups

📑 Chapter Contents

  • 7.1 What Are Data Sources?
  • 7.2 Data Source Syntax
  • 7.3 Common Data Sources
  • 7.4 Filtering Data Sources
  • 7.5 Data Source vs Resource
  • 7.6 Data Source Patterns

7.1 Data Sources Explained

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning how to read information from existing infrastructure without creating or modifying anything. Data sources are the "lookup" mechanism in Terraform—they let you query your cloud provider for resources that already exist. Maybe you need the latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI ID, the list of available availability zones, or the ID of a VPC that another team created. Instead of hard-coding these values (which would break when they change), data sources dynamically retrieve them at plan/apply time. This is critical for writing portable, maintainable infrastructure code.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Understand why data sources are read-only (never create/update/delete)
  • Know the difference between resources (manage) and data sources (query)
  • Recognize common use cases: AMI lookup, AZ lists, existing VPC references
  • Understand how data sources refresh on every terraform plan

7.1.1 Purpose of Data Sources

💡 Common Use Cases for Data Sources
  • AMI Lookup: Find latest Amazon Machine Image by filters
  • Availability Zones: Get list of AZs in a region
  • Existing VPC: Reference a pre-existing VPC by ID or name
  • IAM Policy: Get AWS managed policy ARN
  • Account Info: Get current AWS account ID or region

7.1.2 Read-Only Infrastructure Queries

📊 Data Sources Are Read-Only 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        DATA SOURCE CHARACTERISTICS                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│   │                    DATA SOURCE OPERATIONS                       │           │
│   ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤           │
│   │  ✅ READ existing data from external sources                    │           │
│   │  ✅ QUERY cloud provider APIs                                   │           │
│   │  ✅ FILTER resources to find specific items                     │           │
│   │  ✅ REFERENCE attributes in other resources                     │           │
│   │  ⚡ REFRESH on every plan (not just apply)                      │           │
│   ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤           │
│   │  ❌ Cannot CREATE new resources                                 │           │
│   │  ❌ Cannot UPDATE existing resources                            │           │
│   │  ❌ Cannot DELETE resources                                     │           │
│   │  ❌ Cannot manage lifecycle                                     │           │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

7.1.3 Data Source vs Resource

📊 Data Sources vs Resources Comparison 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        DATA SOURCES vs RESOURCES                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────────────────┐        │
│   │      DATA SOURCE            │        │         RESOURCE            │        │
│   │      (data block)           │        │      (resource block)       │        │
│   ├─────────────────────────────┤        ├─────────────────────────────┤        │
│   │  ✅ READ existing data      │        │  ✅ CREATE new resources    │        │
│   │  ❌ Cannot create           │        │  ✅ UPDATE resources        │        │
│   │  ❌ Cannot modify           │        │  ✅ DELETE resources        │        │
│   │  ❌ Cannot delete           │        │  ✅ Track in state          │        │
│   │  ⚡ Queries on every plan   │        │  📋 Full lifecycle          │        │
│   └─────────────────────────────┘        └─────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                                  │
│   Use Case:                              Use Case:                               │
│   "I need the ID of an existing VPC"     "I need to create a new VPC"           │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Aspect Data Source Resource
Block keyword data resource
Creates infrastructure? ❌ No ✅ Yes
Reference format data.type.name.attr type.name.attr
Tracked in state? Cached only Full tracking

7.2 Data Source Block Syntax

ESSENTIAL
🎯

Learning the Data Block Structure

The data block is your way of telling Terraform "go find this existing thing." It follows a similar structure to resource blocks: data "provider_type" "name" { ... }. Inside the block, you provide arguments that tell Terraform how to find what you're looking for (filters, IDs, tags). The key difference from resources: you reference them with the data. prefix: data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id instead of aws_ami.ubuntu.id. You can also use meta-arguments like count and for_each to query multiple instances of the same data source type.

7.2.1 Data Block Structure

HCL Data Source Block Structure 
# Syntax
data "<PROVIDER_TYPE>" "<NAME>" {
  # Query arguments (filters, IDs, names)
  argument = value
}

# Reference format
data.<TYPE>.<NAME>.<ATTRIBUTE>

# Example: Query an existing VPC
data "aws_vpc" "existing" {
  id = "vpc-0123456789abcdef"
}

# Use the data
resource "aws_subnet" "new" {
  vpc_id     = data.aws_vpc.existing.id   # Reference the data source
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
}

7.2.2 Data Arguments and Attributes

HCL Arguments (Input) vs Attributes (Output) 
# Arguments are the INPUTS you provide to query
data "aws_vpc" "by_name" {
  tags = {                    # ← Argument (query filter)
    Name = "production-vpc"
  }
}

# Attributes are the OUTPUTS returned by the query
output "vpc_cidr" {
  value = data.aws_vpc.by_name.cidr_block   # ← Attribute (returned data)
}

output "vpc_id" {
  value = data.aws_vpc.by_name.id           # ← Attribute
}

output "vpc_arn" {
  value = data.aws_vpc.by_name.arn          # ← Attribute
}
Aspect Resource Block Data Block
Keyword resource data
Reference aws_vpc.main.id data.aws_vpc.main.id
Creates? ✅ Yes ❌ No
In State? Full tracking Cached query results
When Queried During apply During plan AND apply

7.2.3 Multiple Data Instances

HCL Using count and for_each with Data Sources 
# Using for_each with data sources
variable "subnet_names" {
  default = ["subnet-a", "subnet-b", "subnet-c"]
}

data "aws_subnet" "selected" {
  for_each = toset(var.subnet_names)
  
  filter {
    name   = "tag:Name"
    values = [each.value]
  }
}

# Reference specific instance
output "subnet_a_id" {
  value = data.aws_subnet.selected["subnet-a"].id
}

# Using count
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

data "aws_subnet" "az_subnets" {
  count = length(data.aws_availability_zones.available.names)
  
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  vpc_id            = data.aws_vpc.main.id
}

7.3 Common AWS Data Sources

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

The Data Sources You'll Use Every Day

These are the workhorses of Terraform AWS development. aws_ami finds AMIs by owner, name pattern, and architecture—essential for always deploying the latest patched image. aws_availability_zones returns the AZs available in your region—required for multi-AZ deployments. aws_caller_identity gets your AWS account ID. aws_iam_policy_document generates proper JSON policies without manual JSON writing. aws_secretsmanager_secret_version pulls secrets at plan time. Learn these 10 data sources well—they appear in nearly every production Terraform configuration.

7.3.1 aws_ami

HCL Find AMI Images 
# Find latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id  # Always latest!
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

7.3.2 aws_availability_zones

HCL Get Available AZs 
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

output "azs" {
  value = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names
  # ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
}

7.3.3 aws_caller_identity

HCL Get Current Account Info 
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

output "account_id" {
  value = data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id
}

output "caller_arn" {
  value = data.aws_caller_identity.current.arn
}

7.3.4 aws_region

HCL Get Current Region 
data "aws_region" "current" {}

output "region" {
  value = data.aws_region.current.name
}

output "region_description" {
  value = data.aws_region.current.description
}

7.3.5 aws_vpc

HCL Query Existing VPC 
# By ID
data "aws_vpc" "by_id" {
  id = "vpc-0123456789abcdef"
}

# By tag
data "aws_vpc" "by_name" {
  tags = {
    Name = "production-vpc"
  }
}

output "vpc_cidr" {
  value = data.aws_vpc.by_name.cidr_block
}

7.3.6 aws_subnet

HCL Query Subnets 
# Single subnet by ID
data "aws_subnet" "selected" {
  id = "subnet-0123456789abcdef"
}

# Multiple subnets by filter
data "aws_subnets" "private" {
  filter {
    name   = "vpc-id"
    values = [data.aws_vpc.main.id]
  }
  tags = {
    Tier = "private"
  }
}

7.3.7 aws_security_group

HCL Query Security Groups 
data "aws_security_group" "default" {
  name   = "default"
  vpc_id = data.aws_vpc.main.id
}

data "aws_security_groups" "web" {
  tags = {
    Application = "web"
  }
}

7.3.8 aws_iam_policy_document

HCL Generate IAM Policy JSON 
# Generate policy JSON programmatically
data "aws_iam_policy_document" "s3_read" {
  statement {
    effect = "Allow"
    actions = [
      "s3:GetObject",
      "s3:ListBucket"
    ]
    resources = [
      "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket",
      "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"
    ]
  }
}

resource "aws_iam_policy" "s3_read" {
  name   = "s3-read-policy"
  policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.s3_read.json
}

7.3.9 aws_secretsmanager_secret_version

HCL Read Secrets 
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "db_creds" {
  name = "production/db/credentials"
}

data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_creds" {
  secret_id = data.aws_secretsmanager_secret.db_creds.id
}

# Use in resource (value is JSON)
locals {
  db_creds = jsondecode(data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_creds.secret_string)
}

7.3.10 aws_ssm_parameter

HCL Read SSM Parameters 
data "aws_ssm_parameter" "db_host" {
  name = "/production/database/host"
}

# For SecureString parameters
data "aws_ssm_parameter" "db_password" {
  name            = "/production/database/password"
  with_decryption = true
}

output "db_endpoint" {
  value     = data.aws_ssm_parameter.db_host.value
  sensitive = false
}

7.4 Filtering Data Sources

ADVANCED 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

Precision Queries with Filters

Filter blocks let you narrow your data source queries to find exactly what you need. Each filter block acts as an AND condition—all filters must match. Within a single filter's values list, it's OR—any value can match. You can also use tags = { ... } for tag-based filtering. Mastering filter patterns is essential for production use cases: finding the latest AMI that matches your naming convention, locating all subnets in a specific tier, or querying security groups by application tag.

7.4.1 Filter Blocks

HCL Filtering with Multiple Criteria 
# Find AMI with multiple filter conditions
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]  # Canonical's AWS account

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-*-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "root-device-type"
    values = ["ebs"]
  }
  
  filter {
    name   = "architecture"
    values = ["x86_64"]
  }
}

# Filter VPCs by multiple tags
data "aws_vpcs" "production" {
  filter {
    name   = "tag:Environment"
    values = ["production", "prod"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "state"
    values = ["available"]
  }
}

output "production_vpc_ids" {
  value = data.aws_vpcs.production.ids
}

7.4.2 Tag Filtering

HCL Query by Tags 
# Find VPC by Name tag
data "aws_vpc" "main" {
  tags = {
    Name = "main-vpc"
  }
}

# Find subnet by multiple tags
data "aws_subnet" "private" {
  tags = {
    Name        = "private-subnet"
    Environment = var.environment
    Tier        = "private"
  }
}

# Find all subnets with specific tag
data "aws_subnets" "private" {
  filter {
    name   = "vpc-id"
    values = [data.aws_vpc.main.id]
  }

  tags = {
    Tier = "private"
  }
}

# Create EC2 instances in each private subnet
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  for_each = toset(data.aws_subnets.private.ids)
  
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = each.value
}

7.4.3 Multiple Filters

Combine multiple filter blocks for precise resource selection. Each filter acts as an AND condition.

HCL Combining Multiple Filters 
# Complex multi-filter AMI lookup
data "aws_ami" "custom" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["self"]

  # Filter 1: Name pattern
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["myapp-*"]
  }

  # Filter 2: Architecture
  filter {
    name   = "architecture"
    values = ["x86_64"]
  }

  # Filter 3: State
  filter {
    name   = "state"
    values = ["available"]
  }

  # Filter 4: Virtualization type
  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# Multi-filter subnet lookup
data "aws_subnet" "selected" {
  filter {
    name   = "vpc-id"
    values = [data.aws_vpc.main.id]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "availability-zone"
    values = ["us-west-2a"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "cidr-block"
    values = ["10.0.1.0/24"]
  }
}
📋

Filter Combination Rules

Pattern Behavior Use Case
Multiple filter blocks AND logic - all must match Precise resource selection
Multiple values in one filter OR logic - any can match Flexible matching
filter + tags AND logic together Tag-aware filtering
HCL OR Logic Within Filters 
# Find security groups matching multiple patterns
data "aws_security_groups" "web" {
  # OR: matches groups with ANY of these name patterns
  filter {
    name   = "group-name"
    values = ["web-*", "frontend-*", "nginx-*"]
  }

  # AND: must also be in this VPC
  filter {
    name   = "vpc-id"
    values = [data.aws_vpc.main.id]
  }
}

# Result: Groups named web-* OR frontend-* OR nginx-*
#         AND in the specified VPC

7.5 Special Data Sources

ADVANCED PRODUCTION
🎯

Beyond Cloud Provider Queries

Terraform includes built-in data sources that aren't tied to any cloud provider. local_file reads files from disk (SSH keys, certificates). http makes GET requests to APIs. external executes scripts and captures JSON output. terraform_remote_state reads outputs from other Terraform configurations—essential for splitting infrastructure across multiple state files. These data sources bridge the gap between Terraform and the outside world.

Beyond provider-specific data sources, Terraform includes built-in data sources for reading files, executing scripts, making HTTP requests, and accessing remote state. These are essential for integrating external data into your configurations.

7.5.1 local_file

The local_file data source reads the contents of a file from the local filesystem.

HCL Reading Local Files 
# Read a text file
data "local_file" "ssh_key" {
  filename = "${path.module}/keys/deploy_key.pub"
}

# Use the file content
resource "aws_key_pair" "deploy" {
  key_name   = "deploy-key"
  public_key = data.local_file.ssh_key.content
}

# Read a configuration file
data "local_file" "config" {
  filename = "${path.module}/config/app-config.json"
}

# Parse JSON content
locals {
  app_config = jsondecode(data.local_file.config.content)
}

resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "config" {
  name  = "/app/config"
  type  = "String"
  value = data.local_file.config.content
}
📋

local_file Attributes

Attribute Type Description
content String Raw file contents as UTF-8 string
content_base64 String Base64 encoded file contents
content_md5 String MD5 hash of file content
content_sha1 String SHA1 hash of file content
content_sha256 String SHA256 hash of file content

7.5.2 external (Script Execution)

The external data source executes an external program and captures its JSON output. The program must read a JSON object from stdin and write a JSON object to stdout.

HCL External Data Source Usage 
# Execute a shell script
data "external" "git_info" {
  program = ["bash", "${path.module}/scripts/git-info.sh"]
}

# Access the returned data
locals {
  git_commit = data.external.git_info.result.commit
  git_branch = data.external.git_info.result.branch
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = {
    GitCommit = local.git_commit
    GitBranch = local.git_branch
  }
}

# Pass query parameters to script
data "external" "lookup" {
  program = ["python3", "${path.module}/scripts/lookup.py"]
  
  query = {
    environment = var.environment
    region      = var.region
  }
}
Bash scripts/git-info.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# Read stdin (not used in this example)
read -r INPUT

# Output must be valid JSON
cat <<EOF
{
  "commit": "$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)",
  "branch": "$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)",
  "dirty": "$(git diff --quiet && echo 'false' || echo 'true')"
}
EOF
⚠️

External Data Source Limitations

  • Program must be deterministic - same input, same output
  • No side effects - read-only operations only
  • Output must be valid JSON object with string values only
  • Runs on every terraform plan and apply

7.5.3 http

The http data source makes HTTP GET requests to retrieve data from web endpoints.

HCL HTTP Data Source Examples 
# Get current public IP
data "http" "my_ip" {
  url = "https://api.ipify.org?format=json"
}

locals {
  my_public_ip = jsondecode(data.http.my_ip.response_body).ip
}

# Create security group allowing your IP
resource "aws_security_group" "admin" {
  name        = "admin-access"
  description = "Admin access from my IP"
  vpc_id      = data.aws_vpc.main.id

  ingress {
    from_port   = 22
    to_port     = 22
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["${local.my_public_ip}/32"]
    description = "SSH from my IP"
  }
}

# Fetch configuration from API
data "http" "config" {
  url = "https://config.example.com/api/v1/settings"
  
  request_headers = {
    Accept        = "application/json"
    Authorization = "Bearer ${var.api_token}"
  }
}

# With request timeout
data "http" "external_api" {
  url = "https://api.example.com/data"
  
  request_timeout_ms = 5000  # 5 seconds
  
  retry {
    attempts     = 3
    min_delay_ms = 1000
  }
}
📋

http Data Source Attributes

Attribute Description
response_body Response body as string
response_headers Map of response headers
status_code HTTP status code (e.g., 200)
response_body_base64 Base64-encoded response body

7.5.4 terraform_remote_state

The terraform_remote_state data source retrieves output values from another Terraform workspace's state file.

HCL Remote State Access Patterns 
# Access S3-stored remote state
data "terraform_remote_state" "network" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "my-terraform-state"
    key    = "network/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-west-2"
  }
}

# Use outputs from network workspace
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.private_subnet_ids[0]
  
  vpc_security_group_ids = [
    data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.app_security_group_id
  ]
}

# Access local state file
data "terraform_remote_state" "local" {
  backend = "local"
  config = {
    path = "../shared/terraform.tfstate"
  }
}

# Access Azure-stored remote state  
data "terraform_remote_state" "azure" {
  backend = "azurerm"
  config = {
    resource_group_name  = "terraform-state-rg"
    storage_account_name = "tfstateaccount"
    container_name       = "tfstate"
    key                  = "prod.terraform.tfstate"
  }
}
💡

Data Source vs Resource Decision

Use Data Source: "I need to READ information about something that exists"

Use Resource: "I need to CREATE and MANAGE something"

When another Terraform workspace manages the infrastructure, use terraform_remote_state to access its outputs.

Remote State Architecture

Network Workspace
VPC
Subnets
Security Groups
outputs →
S3 State Bucket
network/terraform.tfstate
Application Workspace
terraform_remote_state
↓ reads
EC2 Instances

7.6 Data Source Patterns

ADVANCED PRODUCTION
🎯

Production Data Source Strategies

These patterns combine data sources with other Terraform features to solve real-world problems. Cross-account references use terraform_remote_state to share VPC IDs and subnet lists between workspaces. Dynamic AMI selection ensures you always deploy the latest patched image. Environment-specific configurations use data sources with locals to look up values per environment. These patterns are the building blocks of enterprise Terraform architectures.

7.6.1 Cross-Account References

HCL Share Data Across Workspaces 
# In the networking workspace (exports outputs)
# network/outputs.tf
output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

# In the application workspace (imports data)
# app/main.tf
data "terraform_remote_state" "network" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "terraform-state-bucket"
    key    = "network/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-west-2"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  # Use output from network workspace
  subnet_id = data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.private_subnet_ids[0]
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.app.id]
}

7.6.2 Dynamic AMI Selection

HCL Always Use Latest AMI Pattern 
# variables.tf
variable "ami_config" {
  type = map(object({
    owner  = string
    filter = string
  }))
  default = {
    amazon-linux = {
      owner  = "amazon"
      filter = "amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"
    }
    ubuntu = {
      owner  = "099720109477"
      filter = "ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-*-22.04-amd64-server-*"
    }
  }
}

variable "os_type" {
  type    = string
  default = "amazon-linux"
}

# data.tf - Dynamic AMI lookup
data "aws_ami" "selected" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = [var.ami_config[var.os_type].owner]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = [var.ami_config[var.os_type].filter]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# main.tf
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.selected.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name    = "web-server"
    AMI     = data.aws_ami.selected.name
    OS      = var.os_type
  }
}

7.6.3 Availability Zone Distribution

HCL Distribute Resources Across AZs 
# Get available AZs
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
  
  # Exclude Local Zones
  filter {
    name   = "opt-in-status"
    values = ["opt-in-not-required"]
  }
}

# Create subnets in each AZ
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count = length(data.aws_availability_zones.available.names)

  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(aws_vpc.main.cidr_block, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]

  tags = {
    Name = "private-${data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]}"
    Tier = "private"
  }
}

# Create instances distributed across AZs
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  count = 3

  ami               = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type     = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id         = aws_subnet.private[count.index % length(aws_subnet.private)].id
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index % length(data.aws_availability_zones.available.names)]
}

📋 Chapter 7 Summary

Data Source Basics
  • data block for read-only queries
  • Reference: data.type.name.attr
  • Queries run on every plan
Common Data Sources
  • aws_ami - Find AMI images
  • aws_availability_zones - List AZs
  • aws_caller_identity - Account info
Key Patterns
  • Filter blocks for complex queries
  • terraform_remote_state for cross-workspace
  • Dynamic AMI selection
Exam Tips
  • ✅ Data sources are READ-ONLY
  • ✅ data. prefix in references
  • ✅ most_recent = true for AMIs
  • ✅ Queries execute during plan phase
← Previous: Chapter 6 - Resources Next: Chapter 8 - Variables →
CHAPTER 8

Input Variables

Parameterizing Your Infrastructure Code

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM CRITICAL ⏱️ 35 min read
🎛️

What Are Input Variables?

Input variables are the parameters of your Terraform configuration. They allow you to customize your infrastructure without modifying the underlying code, making configurations reusable, maintainable, and environment-agnostic.

🎯 Learning Objectives

✅ Declare and use input variables
✅ Master all variable types (primitive & complex)
✅ Implement validation rules
✅ Understand variable precedence
✅ Work with tfvars files
✅ Handle sensitive variables securely

📖 Quick Reference

  • 8.1 Variable Declaration
  • 8.2 Variable Types (CRITICAL FOR EXAM)
  • 8.3 Variable Default Values
  • 8.4 Variable Descriptions
  • 8.5 Variable Validation Rules
  • 8.6 Sensitive Variables
  • 8.7 Nullable Variables
  • 8.8 Variable Precedence Order (EXAM)
  • 8.9 Methods to Provide Variable Values
  • 8.10 Variable Files (.tfvars)

8.1 Variable Declaration

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM TOPIC
📋

The Variable Block

Variables are declared using the variable block. Each variable must have a unique name within a module and can have several optional arguments to control its behavior.

8.1.1 Basic Variable Syntax

HCL Basic Variable Declaration 
# Minimal variable declaration
variable "region" {}  # No type, description, or default

# Simple variable with default
variable "instance_type" {
  default = "t3.micro"
}

# Complete variable declaration
variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment (dev, staging, prod)"
  default     = "dev"
}

# Using variables in resources
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = var.instance_type  # Reference with var. prefix
  
  tags = {
    Environment = var.environment
  }
}
💡

Variable Reference Syntax

Always reference variables using the var. prefix: var.variable_name

8.1.2 Variable Block Components

Variable Block Anatomy

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    VARIABLE BLOCK STRUCTURE                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│   variable "database_config" {                                  │
│     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│     │ type = object({         ← TYPE CONSTRAINT         │      │
│     │   engine  = string                                │      │
│     │   version = string                                │      │
│     │   size    = number                                │      │
│     │ })                                                │      │
│     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│     │ description = "..."     ← DOCUMENTATION           │      │
│     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│     │ default = { ... }       ← DEFAULT VALUE           │      │
│     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│     │ validation { ... }      ← VALIDATION RULES        │      │
│     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│     │ sensitive = true        ← SENSITIVE FLAG          │      │
│     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│     │ nullable = false        ← NULLABLE FLAG           │      │
│     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│   }                                                             │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📋

Variable Block Arguments

Argument Required Purpose
type No Constrains accepted values (string, number, bool, list, map, object, tuple)
description No Documents the variable's purpose
default No Value used if none provided
validation No Custom validation rules
sensitive No Redacts value from logs (default: false)
nullable No Allows null value (default: true)

8.1.3 Variable Documentation

HCL Well-Documented Variables (variables.tf) 
# =============================================================================
# REQUIRED VARIABLES - Must be set by the user
# =============================================================================

variable "project_name" {
  type        = string
  description = <<-EOT
    The name of the project. Used as a prefix for all resources.
    Must be lowercase, alphanumeric, and between 3-20 characters.
    Example: "mywebapp"
  EOT

  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z][a-z0-9]{2,19}$", var.project_name))
    error_message = "Project name must be lowercase, start with a letter, and be 3-20 characters."
  }
}

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  type        = string
  description = "CIDR block for the VPC. Example: 10.0.0.0/16"
}

# =============================================================================
# OPTIONAL VARIABLES - Have sensible defaults
# =============================================================================

variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment (dev, staging, prod)"
  default     = "dev"

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type        = bool
  description = "Enable CloudWatch detailed monitoring for EC2 instances"
  default     = false
}
⚠️

Required vs Optional Variables

Variables without a default value are required. Terraform will prompt for them interactively if not provided via other methods.

8.2 Variable Types (CRITICAL FOR EXAM)

ESSENTIAL 📝 CRITICAL EXAM
🎯

Master the Type System

Terraform's type system validates incoming variable values before any infrastructure changes are made. Understanding types prevents runtime errors and catches configuration mistakes early. Primitive types (string, number, bool) are the building blocks. Complex types (list, map, set) hold collections. Structural types (object, tuple) define precise shapes with mixed attribute types. The exam heavily tests list vs set vs map distinctions and object type definitions.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know all primitive types and when to use each
  • Understand list vs set vs map and their key differences
  • Define complex object types with nested attributes
  • Know when to use any and its risks

8.2.1 Primitive Types

Terraform Type System

PRIMITIVE TYPES
string
"hello"
number
42, 3.14
bool
true, false
COMPLEX TYPES
list(type)
["a", "b"]
map(type)
{a = 1}
set(type)
unique values
STRUCTURAL TYPES
object({...})
named attrs
tuple([...])
fixed sequence
HCL Primitive Type Examples 
# STRING - Text values
variable "instance_name" {
  type    = string
  default = "web-server"
}

# NUMBER - Integers and floating point
variable "instance_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 3
}

variable "cpu_threshold" {
  type    = number
  default = 80.5  # Decimal allowed
}

# BOOL - True or false
variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

# Type conversion (automatic)
# "123" → 123 (string to number)
# true  → "true" (bool to string)

8.2.2 Complex Types

HCL List, Map, and Set Types 
# LIST - Ordered collection of same type
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
}

# Access: var.availability_zones[0] → "us-west-2a"

# MAP - Key-value pairs with same value type
variable "instance_types" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    dev     = "t3.micro"
    staging = "t3.small"
    prod    = "t3.large"
  }
}

# Access: var.instance_types["prod"] → "t3.large"
# Or:     var.instance_types.prod    → "t3.large"

# SET - Unordered unique values (no duplicates)
variable "allowed_ports" {
  type    = set(number)
  default = [80, 443, 22, 80]  # Duplicates removed → [22, 80, 443]
}

# Nested complex types
variable "subnet_configs" {
  type = list(map(string))
  default = [
    { name = "public-1",  cidr = "10.0.1.0/24" },
    { name = "private-1", cidr = "10.0.2.0/24" },
  ]
}
📋

List vs Set vs Map

Type Ordered Unique Access Method Use Case
list ✅ Yes ❌ No [index] Ordered items, duplicates OK
set ❌ No ✅ Yes Iteration only Unique values, for_each
map ❌ No Keys unique ["key"] or .key Key-value lookups

8.2.3 any Type

HCL Using the any Constraint 
# `any` allows any type - Terraform infers at runtime
variable "flexible_value" {
  type = any  # Accepts string, number, bool, list, map, etc.
}

# Common use: list/map of any type
variable "tags" {
  type    = map(any)  # Values can be any type
  default = {
    Name        = "web-server"   # string
    Environment = "production"   # string  
    Count       = 5              # number
    Active      = true           # bool
  }
}

# List of any type
variable "parameters" {
  type    = list(any)
  default = ["hello", 42, true]  # Mixed types allowed
}

# CAUTION: `any` bypasses type checking
# Prefer specific types when possible for better validation
⚠️

When to Use any

Use any sparingly. It bypasses type checking and can lead to runtime errors. Prefer specific types for better validation and documentation.

8.2.4 Optional Object Attributes (Terraform 1.3+)

HCL Object with Optional Attributes 
# Object type with required and optional attributes
variable "database_config" {
  type = object({
    engine         = string                    # Required
    engine_version = string                    # Required
    instance_class = optional(string, "db.t3.micro")  # Optional with default
    storage        = optional(number, 20)     # Optional with default
    multi_az       = optional(bool)           # Optional, defaults to null
    tags           = optional(map(string), {}) # Optional with empty map default
  })

  default = {
    engine         = "mysql"
    engine_version = "8.0"
    # Other attributes use their defaults
  }
}

# Usage
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine            = var.database_config.engine
  engine_version    = var.database_config.engine_version
  instance_class    = var.database_config.instance_class  # "db.t3.micro"
  allocated_storage = var.database_config.storage         # 20
  multi_az          = var.database_config.multi_az        # null
}

# With partial override
# terraform.tfvars:
# database_config = {
#   engine         = "postgres"
#   engine_version = "14.0"
#   instance_class = "db.r5.large"  # Override default
#   # storage uses default (20)
# }

8.2.5 Type Constraints Best Practices

✅ Always Specify Types

Explicit types catch errors early and serve as documentation.

✅ Use Object for Complex Data

Objects provide named attributes with individual type constraints.

✅ Prefer list(string) over list

Specific element types provide better validation.

⚠️ Avoid any in Modules

Published modules should have specific types for clarity.

8.3 Variable Default Values

ESSENTIAL
🎛️

Making Variables Optional

Default values determine whether a variable is required or optional. Variables without defaults force users to provide values—useful for environment-specific settings like region or project name. Variables with sensible defaults reduce configuration burden and provide consistent secure baselines. The pattern of empty defaults ({} or []) lets users optionally extend configurations without breaking code that expects collections.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know when to use defaults vs requiring explicit values
  • Set appropriate defaults for all variable types
  • Use empty collection defaults for optional extensions
  • Merge user-provided values with sensible defaults

8.3.1 Setting Defaults

HCL Default Value Patterns 
# Simple defaults
variable "region" {
  type    = string
  default = "us-west-2"
}

variable "instance_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 1
}

variable "enable_deletion_protection" {
  type    = bool
  default = false
}

# Complex type defaults
variable "vpc_config" {
  type = object({
    cidr_block           = string
    enable_dns_hostnames = bool
    enable_dns_support   = bool
  })
  
  default = {
    cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
    enable_dns_hostnames = true
    enable_dns_support   = true
  }
}

# List default
variable "security_group_ports" {
  type    = list(number)
  default = [80, 443]
}

# Empty defaults (for optional collections)
variable "additional_tags" {
  type    = map(string)
  default = {}  # Empty map - no additional tags by default
}

variable "extra_subnets" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = []  # Empty list
}

8.3.2 When to Use Defaults

📋

Default Value Guidelines

Scenario Use Default? Reasoning
Environment-agnostic settings ✅ Yes Same value works everywhere
Sensible security defaults ✅ Yes Secure by default
Passwords/Secrets ❌ No Never hardcode secrets
Environment-specific values ❌ No Force explicit setting
Resource names ⚠️ Depends Unique names may conflict

8.3.3 Defaults for Complex Types

HCL Merging Defaults with User Input 
# Define defaults in locals
locals {
  default_tags = {
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
  
  # Merge user tags with defaults (user tags override)
  all_tags = merge(local.default_tags, var.additional_tags)
}

# Variable for user-provided tags
variable "additional_tags" {
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
  description = "Additional tags to apply to all resources"
}

# Usage - default tags + any user additions
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = local.all_tags
}

# User can add tags via terraform.tfvars:
# additional_tags = {
#   CostCenter = "engineering"
#   Owner      = "platform-team"
# }

8.4 Variable Descriptions

BEST PRACTICE
📝

Self-Documenting Infrastructure

Descriptions are the first line of documentation for your Terraform modules. They appear in terraform plan prompts, registry documentation, and IDE tooltips. Well-written descriptions explain what the variable controls, valid values it accepts, and examples of proper usage. This investment pays dividends when other team members (or future you) need to understand and modify configurations.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Write clear, actionable variable descriptions
  • Include examples and valid value ranges
  • Use heredoc syntax for multi-line documentation
  • Structure descriptions for registry and IDE display

8.4.1 Writing Good Descriptions

HCL Effective Variable Descriptions 
# BAD: Vague description
variable "size" {
  description = "The size"  # What size? Of what?
}

# GOOD: Clear and specific
variable "instance_type" {
  type        = string
  description = "EC2 instance type for the web servers. Larger types provide more CPU and memory. Examples: t3.micro (free tier), t3.small, t3.medium"
  default     = "t3.micro"
}

# GOOD: Multi-line description with examples
variable "cidr_block" {
  type = string
  description = <<-EOT
    CIDR block for the VPC. Must be a valid IPv4 CIDR.
    
    Examples:
      - 10.0.0.0/16 (65,536 IPs) - Recommended for production
      - 10.0.0.0/20 (4,096 IPs) - Development/testing
      - 172.16.0.0/16 (65,536 IPs) - Alternative private range
    
    Note: AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet for internal use.
  EOT
}

# GOOD: Include constraints in description
variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment. Must be one of: dev, staging, prod"
  
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

8.4.2 Description Best Practices

📝 Be Specific

State exactly what the variable controls and its impact.

📋 Include Examples

Show valid values to help users understand expected input.

⚠️ Note Constraints

Document any limits, formats, or validation rules.

🔗 Reference Docs

Link to AWS/provider docs for complex settings.

8.5 Variable Validation Rules

✅

Catching Bad Input Before It Hurts

Validation rules are your first line of defense against misconfiguration. They run during terraform plan—before any infrastructure changes—and provide clear, actionable error messages when input doesn't meet requirements. Use validation for enforcing naming conventions, restricting values to approved lists, validating CIDR formats, checking string patterns, and ensuring numeric ranges. Multiple validation blocks per variable let you build layered checks with specific error messages for each failure mode.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Write validation blocks with condition and error_message
  • Use contains(), regex, length checks, and can() for validations
  • Understand the scope limitation (can only reference its own variable)
  • Create multiple validation blocks for complex requirements
EXAM TOPIC Variable validation is heavily tested. Know syntax, condition expressions, and error_message formatting!

8.5.1 validation Block Syntax

Validation Block Structure 
# Basic validation syntax
variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["t2.micro", "t2.small", "t3.micro"], var.instance_type)
    error_message = "Instance type must be t2.micro, t2.small, or t3.micro."
  }
}
🔍 Validation Block Anatomy
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     VALIDATION BLOCK STRUCTURE                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│   validation {                                                      │
│     condition     = <boolean_expression>   ◄── Must evaluate TRUE   │
│     error_message = "<user_message>"       ◄── Shown when FALSE     │
│   }                                                                 │
│                                                                     │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  CONDITION REQUIREMENTS:                                    │   │
│   │  • Must return true or false                                │   │
│   │  • Can only reference var.<name> (same variable)           │   │
│   │  • Cannot reference other variables or resources            │   │
│   │  • Evaluated during terraform plan                          │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

8.5.2 condition Expression

💡 Condition Must Reference Only Its Variable

The condition expression can ONLY reference var.<name> where name is the current variable. It cannot access other variables, locals, or resources.

Common Condition Patterns 
# String length validation
variable "username" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = length(var.username) >= 3 && length(var.username) <= 20
    error_message = "Username must be between 3 and 20 characters."
  }
}

# List of valid values
variable "environment" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

# Number range
variable "instance_count" {
  type = number
  validation {
    condition     = var.instance_count >= 1 && var.instance_count <= 10
    error_message = "Instance count must be between 1 and 10."
  }
}

# Null check
variable "optional_value" {
  type    = string
  default = null
  validation {
    condition     = var.optional_value == null || length(var.optional_value) > 0
    error_message = "If provided, value cannot be empty."
  }
}

8.5.3 error_message

Requirement Description Example
String Type Must be a string literal "Must be valid."
Sentence Case Start with capital letter "Value must be..."
End with Period Proper punctuation "...valid format."
No Interpolation Cannot use ${} syntax ❌ "Got ${var.x}"
Terraform 1.9+ CAN use string templates! ✅ "Got ${var.x}"
⚠️ Version Difference!

Before Terraform 1.9: error_message cannot contain template expressions
Terraform 1.9+: error_message CAN include ${var.name} to show actual value

Terraform 1.9+ Dynamic Error Messages 
# Terraform 1.9+ allows variable references in error_message
variable "region" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = startswith(var.region, "us-")
    error_message = "Region '${var.region}' must start with 'us-'. Got: ${var.region}"
  }
}

8.5.4 Multiple Validation Rules

Variables can have multiple validation blocks. Each is evaluated independently!

Multiple Validation Blocks 
variable "s3_bucket_name" {
  description = "Name of S3 bucket"
  type        = string

  # Rule 1: Length check
  validation {
    condition     = length(var.s3_bucket_name) >= 3 && length(var.s3_bucket_name) <= 63
    error_message = "Bucket name must be between 3 and 63 characters."
  }

  # Rule 2: Start with letter or number
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z0-9]", var.s3_bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name must start with lowercase letter or number."
  }

  # Rule 3: Only valid characters
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9.-]*[a-z0-9]$", var.s3_bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name can only contain lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens, and periods."
  }

  # Rule 4: No consecutive periods
  validation {
    condition     = !can(regex("\\.\\.", var.s3_bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name cannot contain consecutive periods."
  }
}
📊 Validation Execution Flow
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTIPLE VALIDATION EXECUTION                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
