    dev     = "t3.micro"
    staging = "t3.small"
    prod    = "t3.large"
  }
}

# Access by key
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  instance_type = var.instance_types[var.environment]  # Key access
}
🔧

Structural Types

Type Characteristics Use Case
object({...}) Named attributes, each with its own type Complex configuration structures
tuple([...]) Fixed-length sequence, each element with its own type Heterogeneous ordered data
Structural Type Examples 
# Object - named attributes with specific types
variable "database_config" {
  type = object({
    engine         = string
    engine_version = string
    instance_class = string
    allocated_storage = number
    multi_az       = bool
    backup_retention_period = number
  })
  
  default = {
    engine         = "postgres"
    engine_version = "14.7"
    instance_class = "db.t3.medium"
    allocated_storage = 100
    multi_az       = true
    backup_retention_period = 7
  }
}

# Using object attributes
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = var.database_config.engine
  engine_version = var.database_config.engine_version
  instance_class = var.database_config.instance_class
  allocated_storage = var.database_config.allocated_storage
  multi_az       = var.database_config.multi_az
  backup_retention_period = var.database_config.backup_retention_period
}

# Tuple - fixed-length mixed types
variable "server_spec" {
  type = tuple([string, number, bool])
  default = ["t3.micro", 3, true]  # [instance_type, count, monitoring]
}

# Complex nested structure
variable "vpc_config" {
  type = object({
    cidr_block = string
    subnets = list(object({
      cidr_block = string
      az         = string
      public     = bool
    }))
    tags = map(string)
  })
  
  default = {
    cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
    subnets = [
      { cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24", az = "us-west-2a", public = true },
      { cidr_block = "10.0.2.0/24", az = "us-west-2b", public = true },
      { cidr_block = "10.0.10.0/24", az = "us-west-2a", public = false },
    ]
    tags = {
      Environment = "prod"
      Project     = "myapp"
    }
  }
}
📝

EXAM TIP: list vs set vs map

Know when to use each:

  • list: When order matters or you need duplicates
  • set: When you need unique values (often used with for_each)
  • map: When you need to look up values by key

11.1.3 Null Value

The null value represents the absence of a value. It's used differently than an empty string or zero.

Null Value Usage 
# Variable that can be null
variable "customer_gateway_ip" {
  type        = string
  default     = null  # No default value
  description = "Customer gateway IP for VPN (optional)"
}

# Conditional resource creation
resource "aws_vpn_connection" "main" {
  count = var.customer_gateway_ip != null ? 1 : 0
  
  customer_gateway_id = aws_customer_gateway.main[0].id
  vpn_gateway_id      = aws_vpn_gateway.main.id
  type                = "ipsec.1"
}

# Using null in conditional expressions
locals {
  # Returns null if condition is false
  vpc_id = var.create_vpc ? aws_vpc.main[0].id : null
}

# Null coalescing with try()
locals {
  instance_id = try(aws_instance.web[0].id, null)
}

# Omitting optional arguments with null
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  
  # key_name is only set if var.key_name is not null
  key_name = var.key_name  # If null, attribute is omitted
}

# Explicit null assignment
variable "optional_tag_value" {
  type    = string
  default = null
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "web-server"
    # This tag is only added if value is not null
    CustomTag   = var.optional_tag_value
  }
}
⚠️

null vs empty string vs zero

Value Type Boolean Evaluation Use Case
null no type Causes error if tested Absence of value, omit attribute
"" string false in conditions Empty string value
0 number false in conditions Zero numeric value
[] list false (empty) Empty list

11.1.4 Type Conversion

Terraform automatically converts values between compatible types when needed. Understanding these conversions helps avoid errors.

📊 Automatic Type Conversions
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      AUTOMATIC TYPE CONVERSIONS                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   string ←──────────────────────────────────────────────→ number            │
│           "42" converts to 42    │    42 converts to "42"                   │
│                                                                              │
│   string ←──────────────────────────────────────────────→ bool              │
│           "true" converts to true│    true converts to "true"               │
│           "false" converts to false│  false converts to "false"             │
│                                                                              │
│   list(any) ←────────────────────────────────────────────→ set(any)         │
│              toset([]) converts   │    tolist(set) converts                 │
│                                                                              │
│   list(any) ←────────────────────────────────────────────→ tuple([...])     │
│              Same element types   │    Compatible conversion                │
│                                                                              │
│   map(any)  ←────────────────────────────────────────────→ object({...})    │
│              Keys match attributes│    Attributes become keys               │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Type Conversion Examples 
# Explicit type conversion functions
locals {
  # String to number
  port_number = tonumber("8080")  # → 8080
  
  # Number to string  
  port_string = tostring(8080)    # → "8080"
  
  # String to bool
  is_enabled = tobool("true")     # → true
  
  # List to set (removes duplicates)
  unique_ports = toset([80, 443, 80])  # → {80, 443}
  
  # Set to list
  port_list = tolist(toset([80, 443])) # → [80, 443]
  
  # Map to object (if structure matches)
  config_obj = tomap({
    name = "myapp"
    port = 8080
  })
}

# Implicit conversion in expressions
variable "port" {
  type    = string
  default = "8080"
}

resource "aws_security_group_rule" "web" {
  # Terraform converts string "8080" to number automatically
  from_port   = var.port  # Works! String converted to number
  to_port     = var.port
  protocol    = "tcp"
  type        = "ingress"
  cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.main.id
}

# Type conversion with try() for safety
locals {
  # Safely convert with fallback
  safe_port = try(tonumber(var.port), 80)
  
  # Complex conversion with validation
  config = try(
    {
      name = tostring(var.app_config.name)
      port = tonumber(var.app_config.port)
    },
    {
      name = "default"
      port = 8080
    }
  )
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Type Conversion Functions

Know these conversion functions:

  • tostring(), tonumber(), tobool() - primitive conversions
  • tolist(), toset(), tomap() - collection conversions
  • try() - safe conversion with fallback
  • can() - test if conversion is possible

11.2 String Interpolation and Templates

📝

Building Dynamic Strings in Terraform

String interpolation with ${...} embeds expressions inside strings—the most common way to build dynamic values. Template directives %{for...} and %{if...} create complex multi-line strings. The tilde ~ strip marker controls whitespace. These features turn static strings into powerful templates for user_data scripts, policy documents, and configuration files.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Embed expressions in strings with ${...}
  • Generate text loops with %{for...}
  • Add conditional text with %{if...}
  • Control whitespace with strip markers

11.2.1 ${...} Interpolation

The ${...} sequence embeds the result of an expression into a string. This is the primary way to construct dynamic values.

Basic String Interpolation 
# Simple variable reference
locals {
  greeting = "Hello, ${var.user_name}!"
}

# Multiple interpolations
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-web"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project
    FQDN        = "${var.hostname}.${var.domain}"
  }
}

# Expression interpolation
locals {
  instance_count_message = "Creating ${var.instance_count} instances"
  total_storage          = "Total storage: ${var.disk_size * var.instance_count} GB"
}

# Resource attribute interpolation
output "connection_string" {
  value = "postgresql://${aws_db_instance.main.username}:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/${aws_db_instance.main.db_name}"
}

# Conditional in interpolation
locals {
  env_suffix = "Environment: ${var.environment == "prod" ? "PRODUCTION" : var.environment}"
}

# Nested interpolation (avoid when possible)
locals {
  complex_name = "${var.prefix}-${var.environment == "prod" ? "prd" : var.environment}-${var.suffix}"
}
💡

Interpolation Best Practices

  • Don't wrap simple references: Use var.name not "${var.name}" for single values
  • Keep expressions simple: Complex logic belongs in locals
  • Escape literal $: Use $$ for literal dollar signs
Escaping Dollar Signs 
# Use $$ to produce a literal $
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  user_data = <<-EOF
    #!/bin/bash
    # Use $$ for bash variables (not Terraform interpolation)
    export LOG_FILE="/var/log/app.log"
    for i in $$(seq 1 5); do
      echo "Iteration $$i" >> $$LOG_FILE
    done
    
    # This IS Terraform interpolation
    echo "Environment: ${var.environment}"
  EOF
}

11.2.2 %{for ...} Directive

The %{for} directive generates repeated text within a string template. It's perfect for generating configuration files with dynamic content.

For Directive Syntax 
# Basic for directive
locals {
  servers = ["web1", "web2", "web3"]
  
  hosts_content = <<-EOF
    # /etc/hosts additions
    %{ for server in local.servers ~}
    10.0.1.${index(local.servers, server) + 10}  ${server}.example.com
    %{ endfor ~}
  EOF
}

# With index
variable "subnets" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
}

locals {
  subnet_config = <<-YAML
    subnets:
    %{ for idx, cidr in var.subnets ~}
      - name: subnet-${idx}
        cidr: ${cidr}
        az: ${element(["a", "b", "c"], idx)}
    %{ endfor ~}
  YAML
}

# Generating nginx upstream config
variable "backend_servers" {
  type = list(object({
    name   = string
    ip     = string
    port   = number
    weight = number
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "server1", ip = "10.0.1.10", port = 8080, weight = 5 },
    { name = "server2", ip = "10.0.1.11", port = 8080, weight = 3 },
    { name = "server3", ip = "10.0.1.12", port = 8080, weight = 2 },
  ]
}

locals {
  nginx_upstream = <<-NGINX
    upstream backend {
    %{ for server in var.backend_servers ~}
        server ${server.ip}:${server.port} weight=${server.weight};  # ${server.name}
    %{ endfor ~}
    }
  NGINX
}
📊 For Directive Flow
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         FOR DIRECTIVE SYNTAX                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   %{ for <ITEM> in <COLLECTION> }                                          │
│     ... repeated content using ${<ITEM>} ...                                │
│   %{ endfor }                                                               │
│                                                                              │
│   %{ for <INDEX>, <ITEM> in <COLLECTION> }                                 │
│     ... repeated content using ${<INDEX>} and ${<ITEM>} ...                │
│   %{ endfor }                                                               │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  INPUT: ["a", "b", "c"]                                             │   │
│   │                                               │                      │   │
│   │  %{ for item in list }                       ▼                      │   │
│   │  - ${item}                          OUTPUT:  - a                    │   │
│   │  %{ endfor }                                 - b                    │   │
│   │                                              - c                    │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

11.2.3 %{if ...} Directive

The %{if} directive includes text conditionally within a string template.

If Directive Examples 
# Basic if directive
variable "enable_ssl" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

locals {
  nginx_server = <<-NGINX
    server {
        listen 80;
        server_name ${var.domain};
        
        %{ if var.enable_ssl ~}
        listen 443 ssl;
        ssl_certificate /etc/nginx/ssl/cert.pem;
        ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/ssl/key.pem;
        %{ endif ~}
        
        location / {
            proxy_pass http://backend;
        }
    }
  NGINX
}

# If-else directive
variable "environment" {
  type    = string
  default = "dev"
}

locals {
  app_config = <<-YAML
    application:
      name: myapp
      %{ if var.environment == "prod" ~}
      log_level: WARN
      debug: false
      replicas: 3
      %{ else ~}
      log_level: DEBUG
      debug: true
      replicas: 1
      %{ endif ~}
  YAML
}

# Combined for and if
variable "services" {
  type = list(object({
    name    = string
    enabled = bool
    port    = number
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "api", enabled = true, port = 8080 },
    { name = "admin", enabled = false, port = 8081 },
    { name = "metrics", enabled = true, port = 9090 },
  ]
}

locals {
  docker_compose = <<-YAML
    version: '3.8'
    services:
    %{ for svc in var.services ~}
    %{ if svc.enabled ~}
      ${svc.name}:
        image: myapp/${svc.name}:latest
        ports:
          - "${svc.port}:${svc.port}"
    %{ endif ~}
    %{ endfor ~}
  YAML
}

11.2.4 Strip Markers (~)

The tilde ~ character removes whitespace adjacent to template directives, giving you precise control over output formatting.

Strip Marker Examples 
# Without strip markers (includes newlines)
locals {
  without_strip = <<-EOF
    items:
    %{ for item in ["a", "b", "c"] }
    - ${item}
    %{ endfor }
  EOF
  # Result has extra blank lines around each item
}

# With strip markers (clean output)
locals {
  with_strip = <<-EOF
    items:
    %{~ for item in ["a", "b", "c"] ~}
    - ${item}
    %{~ endfor ~}
  EOF
  # Result:
  # items:
  # - a
  # - b
  # - c
}

# Strip marker positions
#   %{~ ... }   - strips BEFORE the directive
#   %{ ... ~}   - strips AFTER the directive
#   %{~ ... ~}  - strips BOTH before and after

# Practical example - JSON array
variable "allowed_ips" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["10.0.0.1", "10.0.0.2", "192.168.1.1"]
}

locals {
  ip_policy = <<-JSON
    {
      "Version": "2012-10-17",
      "Statement": [{
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Condition": {
          "IpAddress": {
            "aws:SourceIp": [
              %{~ for i, ip in var.allowed_ips ~}
              "${ip}"${ i < length(var.allowed_ips) - 1 ? "," : ""}
              %{~ endfor ~}
            ]
          }
        }
      }]
    }
  JSON
}
📋

Strip Marker Reference

Syntax Effect Use Case
%{~ for Strip whitespace before for Remove leading blank line
endfor ~} Strip whitespace after endfor Remove trailing blank line
%{~ if Strip whitespace before if Inline conditional
${~ expr ~} Strip around interpolation Compact output

11.3 References to Named Values

🔗

Accessing Data Throughout Your Configuration

Terraform provides several ways to reference values: var.name for input variables, local.name for locals, resource_type.name.attribute for resources, data.type.name.attribute for data sources, and module.name.output for module outputs. Special paths like path.module, path.root, and terraform.workspace provide context about the current execution environment.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Reference all named value types correctly
  • Navigate resource attributes and nested blocks
  • Use path references for file operations
  • Access workspace information dynamically
📊 Named Value Reference Patterns
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      NAMED VALUE REFERENCES                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  INPUT VARIABLES          var.<name>                                  │ │
│   │  ─────────────────────────────────────────                            │ │
│   │  variable "region" {}  →  var.region                                  │ │
│   │  variable "tags" {}    →  var.tags["Environment"]                     │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  LOCAL VALUES             local.<name>                                │ │
│   │  ─────────────────────────────────────────                            │ │
│   │  locals { x = 1 }      →  local.x                                     │ │
│   │  NOTE: "local" not "locals"!                                          │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  RESOURCES                <type>.<name>.<attribute>                   │ │
│   │  ─────────────────────────────────────────                            │ │
│   │  aws_instance.web      →  aws_instance.web.id                         │ │
│   │                        →  aws_instance.web.private_ip                 │ │
│   │  With count:           →  aws_instance.web[0].id                      │ │
│   │  With for_each:        →  aws_instance.web["key"].id                  │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  DATA SOURCES             data.<type>.<name>.<attribute>              │ │
│   │  ─────────────────────────────────────────                            │ │
│   │  data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" →  data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id                    │ │
│   │                          →  data.aws_ami.ubuntu.image_id              │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  MODULE OUTPUTS           module.<name>.<output>                      │ │
│   │  ─────────────────────────────────────────                            │ │
│   │  module "vpc" {}       →  module.vpc.vpc_id                           │ │
│   │                        →  module.vpc.private_subnet_ids               │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

11.3.1 Resource Attributes (resource_type.name.attribute)

Resource Attribute References 
# Basic resource attribute reference
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id          # Reference VPC's id attribute
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
}

# Multiple attributes from same resource
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public.id
}

output "instance_info" {
  value = {
    id         = aws_instance.web.id
    public_ip  = aws_instance.web.public_ip
    private_ip = aws_instance.web.private_ip
    az         = aws_instance.web.availability_zone
    arn        = aws_instance.web.arn
  }
}

# With count - index reference
resource "aws_instance" "cluster" {
  count         = 3
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# Reference specific instance
output "first_instance_id" {
  value = aws_instance.cluster[0].id
}

# Reference all instances (splat)
output "all_instance_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.cluster[*].id
}

# With for_each - key reference  
resource "aws_instance" "servers" {
  for_each      = toset(["web", "api", "worker"])
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  tags = {
    Name = each.key
  }
}

# Reference specific instance by key
output "web_server_id" {
  value = aws_instance.servers["web"].id
}

11.3.2 Input Variables (var.name)

Variable References 
# Simple variable reference
variable "instance_type" {
  type    = string
  default = "t3.micro"
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  instance_type = var.instance_type  # Direct reference
}

# Map variable with key access
variable "instance_types" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    dev  = "t3.micro"
    prod = "t3.large"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  instance_type = var.instance_types[var.environment]  # Map lookup
}

# Object variable with attribute access
variable "database" {
  type = object({
    engine   = string
    version  = string
    instance = string
  })
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = var.database.engine    # Object attribute
  engine_version = var.database.version
  instance_class = var.database.instance
}

# List variable with index access
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]  # Index access
}

11.3.3 Local Values (local.name)

Local Value References 
# IMPORTANT: Use "local" (singular) not "locals" when referencing!

locals {
  # Simple local
  project_name = "myapp"
  
  # Computed local
  name_prefix = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  
  # Complex local
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  # Reference locals (singular!)
  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${local.name_prefix}-web"  # Using local.name_prefix
  })
}

# ❌ WRONG - Common mistake
# tags = locals.common_tags  # ERROR! "locals" is not valid

# ✅ CORRECT
# tags = local.common_tags   # Use "local" (singular)
⚠️

Common Mistake: locals vs local

Define with locals { } (plural) — Reference with local.name (singular)

11.3.4 Data Sources (data.type.name.attribute)

Data Source References 
# Data source definition and reference
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]  # Canonical
  
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-*-amd64-server-*"]
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id  # data.<type>.<name>.<attribute>
}

# Multiple data source attributes
output "ami_details" {
  value = {
    id           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
    name         = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.name
    architecture = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.architecture
    owner_id     = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.owner_id
  }
}

# Data source with count
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = length(data.aws_availability_zones.available.names)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
}

11.3.5 Module Outputs (module.name.output)

Module Output References 
# Module definition
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  azs        = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
}

# Reference module outputs
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = module.vpc.public_subnet_ids[0]  # module.<name>.<output>
  
  vpc_security_group_ids = [module.vpc.default_security_group_id]
}

# Multiple modules
module "database" {
  source = "./modules/rds"
  
  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id           # Reference another module's output
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
}

output "db_endpoint" {
  value = module.database.endpoint         # Reference module output
}

11.3.6 path.module, path.root, path.cwd

Path References 
# path.module - Directory of the current module
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  # Read file relative to the module
  user_data = file("${path.module}/scripts/bootstrap.sh")
}

# path.root - Directory of the root module
# Useful when calling from child modules
locals {
  shared_config = file("${path.root}/shared/config.json")
}

# path.cwd - Current working directory (where terraform runs)
# Usually same as path.root, but different in some cases

# Common pattern: Module with local files
# modules/lambda/main.tf
resource "aws_lambda_function" "handler" {
  function_name = var.function_name
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "python3.9"
  
  # Package code from module directory
  filename         = "${path.module}/lambda_package.zip"
  source_code_hash = filebase64sha256("${path.module}/lambda_package.zip")
}

# Path comparison
output "paths" {
  value = {
    module = path.module  # /project/modules/vpc
    root   = path.root    # /project
    cwd    = path.cwd     # /project (usually)
  }
}
📋

Path Reference Summary

Reference Description Use Case
path.module Directory containing current .tf file Module-relative files
path.root Directory of the root module Shared files from root
path.cwd Current working directory Rare - CI/CD paths

11.3.7 terraform.workspace

Workspace Reference 
# terraform.workspace returns the current workspace name
# Default workspace is named "default"

# Use workspace for environment-specific configuration
locals {
  environment = terraform.workspace
  
  instance_type = {
    default = "t3.micro"
    dev     = "t3.micro"
    staging = "t3.small"
    prod    = "t3.large"
  }[terraform.workspace]
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = local.instance_type
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "web-${terraform.workspace}"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
  }
}

# Conditional based on workspace
locals {
  is_production = terraform.workspace == "prod"
  
  db_config = {
    instance_class = local.is_production ? "db.r5.large" : "db.t3.micro"
    multi_az       = local.is_production
    backup_days    = local.is_production ? 30 : 7
  }
}

# State path includes workspace
# terraform.tfstate.d/<workspace>/terraform.tfstate
📝

EXAM TIP: terraform.workspace

The terraform.workspace expression returns the name of the currently selected workspace. The default workspace is named "default". This is commonly used for environment-specific configurations.

11.4 Operators

🔢

Performing Operations on Values

Terraform supports arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %), comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=), and logical operators (&&, ||, !). These are essential for conditional logic, calculating values, and building dynamic configurations.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Use arithmetic operators for calculations
  • Compare values with equality and comparison operators
  • Combine conditions with logical operators

11.4.1 Arithmetic Operators

🔢

Arithmetic Operators

Operator Description Example Result
+ Addition 3 + 2 5
- Subtraction 5 - 2 3
* Multiplication 4 * 3 12
/ Division 10 / 3 3.333...
% Modulo (remainder) 10 % 3 1
- (unary) Negation -5 -5
Arithmetic Examples 
# Basic arithmetic
locals {
  total_memory_gb    = var.instances * var.memory_per_instance
  average_allocation = var.total_budget / var.department_count
  remaining_capacity = var.max_instances - var.current_instances
}

# CIDR calculations
variable "cidr_prefix" {
  default = 8  # /24 = 256-8 = 248+ addresses
}

locals {
  # Calculate number of available hosts
  # 2^(32-prefix) - 2 (network + broadcast)
  available_hosts = pow(2, 32 - var.cidr_prefix) - 2
}

# Modulo for round-robin distribution
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count             = 6
  availability_zone = element(var.azs, count.index % length(var.azs))
  # count.index: 0,1,2,3,4,5
  # % 3:         0,1,2,0,1,2 (distributes across 3 AZs)
}

# Division for even distribution
locals {
  instances_per_az = floor(var.instance_count / length(var.azs))
}

11.4.2 Comparison Operators

⚖️

Comparison Operators

Operator Description Example Result
== Equal "a" == "a" true
!= Not equal "a" != "b" true
< Less than 3 < 5 true
> Greater than 5 > 3 true
<= Less than or equal 3 <= 3 true
>= Greater than or equal 5 >= 3 true
Comparison Examples 
# Environment checks
locals {
  is_production = var.environment == "prod"
  is_not_dev    = var.environment != "dev"
}

# Capacity checks
locals {
  needs_scaling = var.current_instances < var.min_instances
  at_capacity   = var.current_instances >= var.max_instances
}

# Resource sizing decisions
locals {
  instance_type = var.cpu_cores > 4 ? "m5.xlarge" : "m5.large"
  storage_tier  = var.data_size_gb >= 1000 ? "provisioned" : "standard"
}

# String comparisons (case-sensitive!)
locals {
  is_us_region = var.region == "us-east-1" || var.region == "us-west-2"
}

11.4.3 Logical Operators

🔀

Logical Operators

Operator Description Example Result
&& AND - both must be true true && true true
|| OR - at least one true true || false true
! NOT - inverts boolean !false true
Logical Operator Examples 
# AND conditions
locals {
  # Both conditions must be true
  enable_ha = var.environment == "prod" && var.enable_redundancy
  
  create_backup = var.is_critical && var.backup_enabled
}

# OR conditions
locals {
  # At least one condition must be true
  needs_monitoring = var.environment == "prod" || var.environment == "staging"
  
  is_compute_heavy = var.cpu_cores > 8 || var.memory_gb > 32
}

# NOT operator
locals {
  # Invert a boolean
  skip_tests = !var.run_tests
  
  is_not_production = !local.is_production
}

# Combined logical operations
locals {
  # Complex condition with grouping
  create_alerts = (var.environment == "prod" || var.is_critical) && var.alerting_enabled
  
  enable_debug = var.debug_mode && !var.is_production
  
  # Validation condition
  valid_config = var.instance_count > 0 && var.instance_count <= 10
}

# Use in count for conditional resources
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "cpu" {
  count = (var.environment == "prod" && var.enable_alarms) ? 1 : 0
  # ... alarm configuration
}
📊 Logical Operator Truth Tables
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        LOGICAL OPERATOR TRUTH TABLES                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │
│  │     AND (&&)         │  │      OR (||)         │  │     NOT (!)      │   │
│  ├──────────────────────┤  ├──────────────────────┤  ├──────────────────┤   │
│  │  A     B    A && B   │  │  A     B    A || B   │  │  A       !A      │   │
│  │ true  true   true    │  │ true  true   true    │  │ true    false    │   │
│  │ true  false  false   │  │ true  false  true    │  │ false   true     │   │
│  │ false true   false   │  │ false true   true    │  └──────────────────┘   │
│  │ false false  false   │  │ false false  false   │                         │
│  └──────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────┘                         │
│                                                                              │
│  PRECEDENCE (highest to lowest):                                            │
│  1. !   (NOT)                                                               │
│  2. &&  (AND)                                                               │
│  3. ||  (OR)                                                                │
│                                                                              │
│  Example: !a || b && c  =  (!a) || (b && c)                                 │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

11.5 Conditional Expressions EXAM TOPIC

❓

Making Decisions in Configuration

The conditional expression condition ? true_val : false_val (ternary operator) selects a value based on a boolean condition. Combined with count, it enables conditionally creating or omitting entire resources. This is one of the most frequently used expressions in real-world Terraform configurations.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Write conditional expressions with proper syntax
  • Use conditionals for resource creation
  • Handle null values in conditionals
  • Apply conditional patterns for common scenarios

11.5.1 Syntax: condition ? true_val : false_val

📊 Conditional Expression Anatomy
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      CONDITIONAL EXPRESSION SYNTAX                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│       condition   ?   true_value   :   false_value                          │
│           │       │        │       │        │                               │
│           │       │        │       │        └── Returned when false         │
│           │       │        │       └── Separator                            │
│           │       │        └── Returned when true                           │
│           │       └── Ternary operator                                      │
│           └── Boolean expression                                            │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  var.environment == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"                │   │
│   │       └─────────┬─────────┘   └────┬───┘   └────┬───┘               │   │
│   │           condition          if true      if false                  │   │
│   │                                                                     │   │
│   │  If environment is "prod", use "t3.large", otherwise "t3.micro"    │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Basic Conditional Syntax 
# Basic conditional expression
locals {
  instance_type = var.environment == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"
  #                    condition           ?  true_val  :  false_val
}

# Multiple conditionals
locals {
  # Environment-based sizing
  instance_type = var.environment == "prod" ? "m5.xlarge" : (
                  var.environment == "staging" ? "m5.large" : "t3.micro"
                  )
  
  # Simpler with lookup
  instance_types = {
    prod    = "m5.xlarge"
    staging = "m5.large"
    dev     = "t3.micro"
  }
  instance_type_v2 = local.instance_types[var.environment]
}

# Boolean variable conditionals
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami                         = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type               = local.instance_type
  associate_public_ip_address = var.is_public ? true : false
  # Note: The above is redundant since var.is_public is already boolean!
  # Better: associate_public_ip_address = var.is_public
}

# Conditional resource naming
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  bucket = var.bucket_name != "" ? var.bucket_name : "${var.project}-${var.environment}-data"
}

11.5.2 With count for Conditional Resource Creation

Conditional Resource Creation 
# THE PATTERN: count = condition ? 1 : 0

# Create resource only in production
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "cpu_high" {
  count = var.environment == "prod" ? 1 : 0
  
  alarm_name          = "cpu-high-${var.project}"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  namespace           = "AWS/EC2"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = 80
}

# Create based on boolean variable
variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = false
}

resource "aws_sns_topic" "alerts" {
  count = var.enable_monitoring ? 1 : 0
  name  = "${var.project}-alerts"
}

# Referencing conditional resources
# Must use [0] index and handle empty list
output "sns_topic_arn" {
  value = var.enable_monitoring ? aws_sns_topic.alerts[0].arn : null
}

# Better pattern with try()
output "sns_topic_arn_safe" {
  value = try(aws_sns_topic.alerts[0].arn, "")
}

# Multiple conditions for creation
resource "aws_elasticache_cluster" "cache" {
  count = var.enable_caching && var.environment != "dev" ? 1 : 0
  
  cluster_id           = "${var.project}-cache"
  engine               = "redis"
  node_type            = "cache.t3.micro"
  num_cache_nodes      = 1
  parameter_group_name = "default.redis6.x"
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Conditional Resource Pattern

count = var.create_resource ? 1 : 0 is the standard pattern for conditionally creating resources. When count is 0, the resource is not created. When count is 1, exactly one instance is created.

11.5.3 Null Handling in Conditionals

Working with Null Values 
# Null omits optional arguments
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  
  # Only set key_name if provided (null omits the argument)
  key_name = var.key_name != "" ? var.key_name : null
  
  # Only set monitoring if explicitly enabled
  monitoring = var.enable_monitoring ? true : null
}

# Using null with optional variables
variable "custom_ami" {
  type    = string
  default = null  # null means "not provided"
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  # Use custom AMI if provided, otherwise use data source
  ami           = var.custom_ami != null ? var.custom_ami : data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# Coalesce for null handling
# Returns first non-null value
locals {
  selected_ami = coalesce(var.custom_ami, data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id)
}

# Null in nested blocks (optional configuration)
variable "root_block_device" {
  type = object({
    volume_size = number
    volume_type = string
  })
  default = null
}

resource "aws_instance" "configurable" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  dynamic "root_block_device" {
    for_each = var.root_block_device != null ? [var.root_block_device] : []
    content {
      volume_size = root_block_device.value.volume_size
      volume_type = root_block_device.value.volume_type
    }
  }
}

11.5.4 Common Conditional Patterns

Conditional Patterns Collection 
# Pattern 1: Default value if empty
locals {
  bucket_name = var.bucket_name != "" ? var.bucket_name : "${var.project}-default"
}

# Pattern 2: Environment-specific values
locals {
  multi_az = var.environment == "prod" ? true : false
  
  replicas = {
    dev     = 1
    staging = 2
    prod    = 3
  }[var.environment]
}

# Pattern 3: Optional feature toggle
resource "aws_waf_web_acl" "main" {
  count = var.enable_waf ? 1 : 0
  name  = "${var.project}-waf"
  # ...
}

# Pattern 4: Conditional tags
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = merge(
    var.common_tags,
    var.environment == "prod" ? { "CriticalResource" = "true" } : {}
  )
}

# Pattern 5: Conditional block content
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name = "${var.project}-web-sg"
  
  # Always allow HTTPS
  ingress {
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
  
  # SSH only in non-prod
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.environment != "prod" ? [1] : []
    content {
      from_port   = 22
      to_port     = 22
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = var.admin_cidrs
    }
  }
}

# Pattern 6: One-of-many selection
variable "storage_type" {
  type    = string
  default = "standard"  # standard, ssd, provisioned
}

locals {
  iops = var.storage_type == "provisioned" ? 3000 : null
  volume_type = (
    var.storage_type == "ssd" ? "gp3" :
    var.storage_type == "provisioned" ? "io1" :
    "gp2"
  )
}

11.6 For Expressions EXAM TOPIC

🔄

Transforming Collections

For expressions transform collections by iterating over each element and producing a new collection. [for x in list : expression] produces a list, while {for k, v in map : key => value} produces a map. Add if condition to filter elements. These expressions are powerful tools for data manipulation.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Transform lists with [for ...] syntax
  • Create maps with {for ...} syntax
  • Filter elements with if clauses
  • Handle nested data structures

11.6.1 [for x in list : expr] to Produce a List

📊 For Expression Output Types
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       FOR EXPRESSION SYNTAX                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   LIST OUTPUT: [ ... ]                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  [for item in list : expression]                                    │   │
│   │  [for idx, item in list : expression]                               │   │
│   │  [for item in list : expression if condition]                       │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
│   MAP OUTPUT: { ... }                                                        │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  {for item in list : key_expr => value_expr}                        │   │
│   │  {for key, val in map : new_key => new_val}                         │   │
│   │  {for item in list : key => value if condition}                     │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
│   The BRACKETS determine output type:                                        │
│     [ ] = list output                                                        │
│     { } = map output                                                         │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
For Expression - List Output 
# Basic list transformation
variable "names" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["alice", "bob", "charlie"]
}

locals {
  # Transform each element
  upper_names = [for name in var.names : upper(name)]
  # Result: ["ALICE", "BOB", "CHARLIE"]
  
  # Add prefix to each element
  prefixed = [for name in var.names : "user-${name}"]
  # Result: ["user-alice", "user-bob", "user-charlie"]
}

# With index
locals {
  indexed_names = [for i, name in var.names : "${i}: ${name}"]
  # Result: ["0: alice", "1: bob", "2: charlie"]
}

# Practical example: Generate subnet CIDRs
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
}

locals {
  subnet_cidrs = [for i, az in var.availability_zones : cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, i)]
  # Result: ["10.0.0.0/24", "10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
}

# Extract attributes from objects
variable "instances" {
  type = list(object({
    name = string
    type = string
    az   = string
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "web-1", type = "t3.micro", az = "us-west-2a" },
    { name = "web-2", type = "t3.small", az = "us-west-2b" },
  ]
}

locals {
  instance_names = [for inst in var.instances : inst.name]
  # Result: ["web-1", "web-2"]
}

11.6.2 {for k,v in map : key => value} to Produce a Map

For Expression - Map Output 
# Transform map values
variable "instance_config" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    web    = "t3.micro"
    api    = "t3.small"
    worker = "t3.medium"
  }
}

locals {
  # Add prefix to map keys
  prefixed_config = {for k, v in var.instance_config : "app-${k}" => v}
  # Result: { "app-web" = "t3.micro", "app-api" = "t3.small", ... }
  
  # Transform values
  instance_families = {for k, v in var.instance_config : k => split(".", v)[0]}
  # Result: { "web" = "t3", "api" = "t3", "worker" = "t3" }
}

# List to map conversion
variable "users" {
  type = list(object({
    name  = string
    email = string
    role  = string
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "alice", email = "alice@example.com", role = "admin" },
    { name = "bob", email = "bob@example.com", role = "user" },
  ]
}

locals {
  # Convert list to map using name as key
  users_by_name = {for user in var.users : user.name => user}
  # Result: { "alice" = { name = "alice", email = "...", role = "admin" }, ... }
  
  # Create email lookup map
  user_emails = {for user in var.users : user.name => user.email}
  # Result: { "alice" = "alice@example.com", "bob" = "bob@example.com" }
}

# Practical: Create tags map from list
variable "tag_list" {
  type = list(object({
    key   = string
    value = string
  }))
  default = [
    { key = "Environment", value = "prod" },
    { key = "Project", value = "myapp" },
  ]
}

locals {
  tags_map = {for tag in var.tag_list : tag.key => tag.value}
  # Result: { "Environment" = "prod", "Project" = "myapp" }
}

11.6.3 Filtering with if

For Expression with Filtering 
# Filter list elements
variable "servers" {
  type = list(object({
    name    = string
    env     = string
    enabled = bool
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "web-prod", env = "prod", enabled = true },
    { name = "api-prod", env = "prod", enabled = true },
    { name = "web-dev", env = "dev", enabled = true },
    { name = "old-server", env = "prod", enabled = false },
  ]
}

locals {
  # Filter to only prod servers
  prod_servers = [for s in var.servers : s.name if s.env == "prod"]
  # Result: ["web-prod", "api-prod", "old-server"]
  
  # Filter to enabled prod servers
  active_prod_servers = [for s in var.servers : s.name 
                         if s.env == "prod" && s.enabled]
  # Result: ["web-prod", "api-prod"]
  
  # Get enabled servers as map
  enabled_map = {for s in var.servers : s.name => s.env if s.enabled}
  # Result: { "web-prod" = "prod", "api-prod" = "prod", "web-dev" = "dev" }
}

# Practical: Filter subnets by type
variable "subnets" {
  type = list(object({
    cidr   = string
    type   = string  # "public" or "private"
    az     = string
  }))
  default = [
    { cidr = "10.0.1.0/24", type = "public", az = "us-west-2a" },
    { cidr = "10.0.2.0/24", type = "public", az = "us-west-2b" },
    { cidr = "10.0.10.0/24", type = "private", az = "us-west-2a" },
    { cidr = "10.0.11.0/24", type = "private", az = "us-west-2b" },
  ]
}

locals {
  public_cidrs  = [for s in var.subnets : s.cidr if s.type == "public"]
  private_cidrs = [for s in var.subnets : s.cidr if s.type == "private"]
}
📝

EXAM TIP: For Expression Filter Syntax

The if clause comes AFTER the value expression: [for x in list : x.name if x.enabled]. This filters which elements are included in the result.

11.6.4 Grouping Results with ...

Grouping with Ellipsis 
# Problem: Duplicate keys cause error
variable "instances" {
  type = list(object({
    name = string
    az   = string
    type = string
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "web-1", az = "us-west-2a", type = "t3.micro" },
    { name = "web-2", az = "us-west-2a", type = "t3.micro" },  # Same AZ!
    { name = "api-1", az = "us-west-2b", type = "t3.small" },
  ]
}

# This would ERROR - duplicate key "us-west-2a"
# BAD: {for inst in var.instances : inst.az => inst.name}

# Solution: Use ... to group values into a list
locals {
  instances_by_az = {for inst in var.instances : inst.az => inst.name...}
  # Result: {
  #   "us-west-2a" = ["web-1", "web-2"]
  #   "us-west-2b" = ["api-1"]
  # }
}

# Group objects by attribute
locals {
  full_instances_by_az = {for inst in var.instances : inst.az => inst...}
  # Result: {
  #   "us-west-2a" = [
  #     { name = "web-1", az = "us-west-2a", type = "t3.micro" },
  #     { name = "web-2", az = "us-west-2a", type = "t3.micro" }
  #   ],
  #   "us-west-2b" = [
  #     { name = "api-1", az = "us-west-2b", type = "t3.small" }
  #   ]
  # }
}

# Practical: Group security group rules by port
variable "sg_rules" {
  type = list(object({
    port        = number
    cidr        = string
    description = string
  }))
  default = [
    { port = 443, cidr = "10.0.0.0/8", description = "Internal HTTPS" },
    { port = 443, cidr = "192.168.0.0/16", description = "VPN HTTPS" },
    { port = 22, cidr = "10.0.0.0/8", description = "Internal SSH" },
  ]
}

locals {
  rules_by_port = {for rule in var.sg_rules : rule.port => rule.cidr...}
  # Result: { 443 = ["10.0.0.0/8", "192.168.0.0/16"], 22 = ["10.0.0.0/8"] }
}

11.6.5 Nested For Expressions

Nested For Expressions 
# Flatten nested structures with nested for
variable "vpc_config" {
  type = map(object({
    cidr    = string
    subnets = list(string)
  }))
  default = {
    production = {
      cidr    = "10.0.0.0/16"
      subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
    }
    staging = {
      cidr    = "10.1.0.0/16"
      subnets = ["10.1.1.0/24"]
    }
  }
}

# Nested for to flatten
locals {
  all_subnets = flatten([
    for vpc_name, vpc in var.vpc_config : [
      for subnet_cidr in vpc.subnets : {
        vpc_name = vpc_name
        vpc_cidr = vpc.cidr
        subnet   = subnet_cidr
      }
    ]
  ])
  # Result: [
  #   { vpc_name = "production", vpc_cidr = "10.0.0.0/16", subnet = "10.0.1.0/24" },
  #   { vpc_name = "production", vpc_cidr = "10.0.0.0/16", subnet = "10.0.2.0/24" },
  #   { vpc_name = "staging", vpc_cidr = "10.1.0.0/16", subnet = "10.1.1.0/24" }
  # ]
}

# Cartesian product (all combinations)
variable "environments" {
  default = ["dev", "staging", "prod"]
}

variable "regions" {
  default = ["us-east-1", "us-west-2"]
}

locals {
  env_region_combos = flatten([
    for env in var.environments : [
      for region in var.regions : {
        env    = env
        region = region
        name   = "${env}-${region}"
      }
    ]
  ])
  # Result: 6 combinations (3 envs × 2 regions)
}

11.6.6 Using for_each with For Expressions

For Expressions with for_each 
# Convert list to map for for_each
variable "bucket_names" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["logs", "data", "backups"]
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "buckets" {
  for_each = toset(var.bucket_names)
  bucket   = "${var.project}-${each.key}"
}

# Transform list of objects for for_each
variable "users" {
  type = list(object({
    name  = string
    email = string
    admin = bool
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "alice", email = "alice@example.com", admin = true },
    { name = "bob", email = "bob@example.com", admin = false },
  ]
}

resource "aws_iam_user" "users" {
  # Transform list to map keyed by name
  for_each = {for user in var.users : user.name => user}
  
  name = each.key
  tags = {
    Email = each.value.email
    Admin = each.value.admin
  }
}

# Filter and transform for for_each
resource "aws_iam_user_policy_attachment" "admin" {
  # Only create for admin users
  for_each = {
    for user in var.users : user.name => user
    if user.admin
  }
  
  user       = aws_iam_user.users[each.key].name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess"
}

# Flattened data with composite keys
locals {
  subnet_configs = flatten([
    for vpc_name, vpc in var.vpc_config : [
      for i, cidr in vpc.subnets : {
        key      = "${vpc_name}-${i}"
        vpc_name = vpc_name
        cidr     = cidr
      }
    ]
  ])
}

resource "aws_subnet" "all" {
  for_each = {for s in local.subnet_configs : s.key => s}
  
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.vpcs[each.value.vpc_name].id
  cidr_block = each.value.cidr
}

11.7 Splat Expressions EXAM TOPIC

✨

Extracting Attributes from Collections

Splat expressions provide a concise syntax for extracting a single attribute from all elements in a list. resource[*].attribute is shorthand for [for x in resource : x.attribute]. The generalized splat resource[*] works with lists and sets, while resource.* is the legacy syntax.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Use [*] splat to extract attributes
  • Understand how splat works with count
  • Choose between splat and for expressions

11.7.1 [*] Generalized Splat

📊 Splat Expression Comparison
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       SPLAT EXPRESSION SYNTAX                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   SPLAT EXPRESSION:                                                          │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  resource[*].attribute                                                │ │
│   │  aws_instance.web[*].id                                               │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│   EQUIVALENT FOR EXPRESSION:                                                 │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │  [for x in resource : x.attribute]                                    │ │
│   │  [for x in aws_instance.web : x.id]                                   │ │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│   INPUT:   [{ id = "i-111" }, { id = "i-222" }, { id = "i-333" }]           │
│   OUTPUT:  ["i-111", "i-222", "i-333"]                                       │
│                                                                              │
│   WHEN TO USE SPLAT:                                                         │
│   • Simple attribute extraction from list                                    │
│   • Clean, readable code for single attribute                                │
│   • Works with resources created with count                                  │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Splat Expression Examples 
# Resources with count
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count         = 3
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "web-${count.index + 1}"
  }
}

# Splat to get all IDs
output "instance_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].id
  # Result: ["i-abc123", "i-def456", "i-ghi789"]
}

# Splat for other attributes
output "public_ips" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].public_ip
}

output "private_ips" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].private_ip
}

output "availability_zones" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].availability_zone
}

# Splat with nested attributes
output "instance_names" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].tags.Name
  # Result: ["web-1", "web-2", "web-3"]
}

# Chaining splat operations
# First get root_block_device list, then get volume_id from each
output "volume_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].root_block_device[0].volume_id
}

11.7.2 Legacy .* Attribute Splat

Legacy Splat Syntax 
# Legacy syntax (still works but [*] is preferred)

# Legacy attribute-only splat
output "instance_ids_legacy" {
  value = aws_instance.web.*.id
  # Same as: aws_instance.web[*].id
}

# Limitations of legacy syntax:
# - Only works at the top level
# - Cannot access nested attributes deeply
# - Less consistent behavior

# Comparison:
# Modern:  aws_instance.web[*].tags.Name
# Legacy:  aws_instance.web.*.tags.Name  # Also works

# The [*] syntax is more explicit and recommended
💡

Best Practice: Use [*] Splat

Always prefer [*] over .* syntax. The generalized splat [*] is more explicit, consistent, and works in more contexts including nested structures.

11.7.3 Splat vs For Expressions

Choosing Between Splat and For 
# USE SPLAT: Simple attribute extraction
output "all_ids" {
  # Splat is cleaner for single attribute
  value = aws_instance.web[*].id
}

# EQUIVALENT FOR (more verbose)
output "all_ids_for" {
  value = [for inst in aws_instance.web : inst.id]
}

# USE FOR: Transformations
output "formatted_ips" {
  # For is needed when transforming values
  value = [for inst in aws_instance.web : "IP: ${inst.private_ip}"]
}

# USE FOR: Filtering
output "large_instances" {
  # For is needed for filtering
  value = [for inst in aws_instance.web : inst.id 
           if inst.instance_type == "t3.large"]
}

# USE FOR: Creating maps
output "instance_map" {
  # For is needed for map output
  value = {for inst in aws_instance.web : inst.tags.Name => inst.id}
}

# USE FOR: Multiple attributes
output "instance_details" {
  # For when you need multiple attributes per element
  value = [for inst in aws_instance.web : {
    id = inst.id
    ip = inst.private_ip
    az = inst.availability_zone
  }]
}

# SPLAT with for_each resources
resource "aws_instance" "servers" {
  for_each      = toset(["web", "api", "worker"])
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# Cannot use splat with for_each - must use values()
output "for_each_ids" {
  # This extracts the instance objects, then splat gets ids
  value = values(aws_instance.servers)[*].id
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Splat Expression Rules

  • [*] only works with lists, tuples, and sets (not maps)
  • For resources with for_each, use values(resource)[*].attr
  • Splat cannot filter—use for expressions for conditional logic

11.8 Dynamic Blocks EXAM TOPIC

🔄

Generating Repeated Nested Blocks

Dynamic blocks generate repeated nested blocks within a resource, data source, provider, or provisioner. Unlike count or for_each which create multiple resource instances, dynamic creates multiple nested configuration blocks within a single resource—perfect for security group rules, IAM policy statements, or any repeating block structure.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Write dynamic blocks with proper syntax
  • Access iterator values with content
  • Use custom iterator names for clarity
  • Nest dynamic blocks for complex structures
  • Apply best practices for readability

11.8.1 The dynamic Block Syntax

📊 Dynamic Block Anatomy
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        DYNAMIC BLOCK STRUCTURE                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   dynamic "<BLOCK_NAME>" {                                                  │
│     for_each = <COLLECTION>                                                 │
│     iterator = <OPTIONAL_NAME>    # defaults to block name                  │
│     labels   = <OPTIONAL_LABELS>  # for blocks with labels                  │
│                                                                              │
│     content {                                                                │
│       # Attributes of the generated block                                    │
│       # Access current item with <iterator>.value                           │
│       # Access current key with <iterator>.key                              │
│     }                                                                        │
│   }                                                                          │
│                                                                              │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  EXAMPLE TRANSFORMATION:                                            │   │
│   │                                                                     │   │
│   │  for_each = [{port=80}, {port=443}]   →   ingress { port = 80 }    │   │
│   │                                            ingress { port = 443 }   │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Basic Dynamic Block 
# Security group with dynamic ingress rules
variable "ingress_rules" {
  type = list(object({
    port        = number
    protocol    = string
    cidr_blocks = list(string)
    description = string
  }))
  default = [
    { port = 80,  protocol = "tcp", cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"], description = "HTTP" },
    { port = 443, protocol = "tcp", cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"], description = "HTTPS" },
    { port = 22,  protocol = "tcp", cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/8"], description = "SSH" },
  ]
}

resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name        = "web-sg"
  description = "Web server security group"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  # Dynamic block generates one ingress block per item
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.ingress_rules
    
    content {
      from_port   = ingress.value.port
      to_port     = ingress.value.port
      protocol    = ingress.value.protocol
      cidr_blocks = ingress.value.cidr_blocks
      description = ingress.value.description
    }
  }
  
  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}

# This generates:
# ingress { from_port = 80, to_port = 80, ... }
# ingress { from_port = 443, to_port = 443, ... }
# ingress { from_port = 22, to_port = 22, ... }

11.8.2 The content Block

Content Block Details 
# The content block defines what goes inside each generated block
# Access values using <block_name>.value and <block_name>.key

resource "aws_lb_listener" "https" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.main.arn
  port              = 443
  protocol          = "HTTPS"
  ssl_policy        = "ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08"
  certificate_arn   = var.certificate_arn
  
  default_action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.main.arn
  }
}

# Add additional certificates dynamically
variable "additional_certs" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = []
}

resource "aws_lb_listener_certificate" "extra" {
  for_each        = toset(var.additional_certs)
  listener_arn    = aws_lb_listener.https.arn
  certificate_arn = each.value
}

# Using dynamic with complex objects
variable "target_groups" {
  type = list(object({
    name     = string
    port     = number
    protocol = string
    health_check = object({
      path     = string
      interval = number
    })
  }))
}

resource "aws_lb_target_group" "dynamic" {
  for_each = {for tg in var.target_groups : tg.name => tg}
  
  name     = each.value.name
  port     = each.value.port
  protocol = each.value.protocol
  vpc_id   = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  dynamic "health_check" {
    for_each = [each.value.health_check]  # Single item list to create ONE block
    
    content {
      path     = health_check.value.path
      interval = health_check.value.interval
    }
  }
}

11.8.3 Custom Iterator Names

Using iterator Argument 
# The iterator argument allows custom names for clarity

resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  name = "app-sg"
  
  # Using custom iterator name "rule" instead of "ingress"
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.ingress_rules
    iterator = rule  # Custom name for readability
    
    content {
      from_port   = rule.value.port  # Use custom name
      to_port     = rule.value.port
      protocol    = rule.value.protocol
      cidr_blocks = rule.value.cidr_blocks
      description = rule.value.description
    }
  }
}

# Helpful when the block name is confusing or clashes
variable "policies" {
  type = list(object({
    name   = string
    effect = string
    actions = list(string)
    resources = list(string)
  }))
}

resource "aws_iam_policy" "custom" {
  name = "custom-policy"
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      for policy in var.policies : {
        Sid      = policy.name
        Effect   = policy.effect
        Action   = policy.actions
        Resource = policy.resources
      }
    ]
  })
}

11.8.4 Nested Dynamic Blocks

Nested Dynamic Blocks 
# Nested dynamic blocks for complex structures

variable "listener_rules" {
  type = list(object({
    priority = number
    conditions = list(object({
      type   = string  # "path" or "host"
      values = list(string)
    }))
    target_group_key = string
  }))
}

resource "aws_lb_listener_rule" "rules" {
  for_each     = {for rule in var.listener_rules : rule.priority => rule}
  listener_arn = aws_lb_listener.main.arn
  priority     = each.key
  
  action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.groups[each.value.target_group_key].arn
  }
  
  # Outer dynamic for condition blocks
  dynamic "condition" {
    for_each = each.value.conditions
    iterator = cond
    
    content {
      # Inner dynamic based on condition type
      dynamic "path_pattern" {
        for_each = cond.value.type == "path" ? [cond.value.values] : []
        content {
          values = path_pattern.value
        }
      }
      
      dynamic "host_header" {
        for_each = cond.value.type == "host" ? [cond.value.values] : []
        content {
          values = host_header.value
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# Another example: EC2 with optional blocks
resource "aws_instance" "configurable" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  
  # Only include if EBS volumes specified
  dynamic "ebs_block_device" {
    for_each = var.additional_ebs_volumes
    iterator = volume
    
    content {
      device_name = volume.value.device_name
      volume_size = volume.value.size
      volume_type = volume.value.type
      encrypted   = true
    }
  }
}

11.8.5 Best Practices and Overuse Warning

Dynamic Block Best Practices 
# ❌ AVOID: Dynamic blocks can reduce readability

# Hard to understand at a glance
resource "aws_security_group" "complex" {
  name = "complex-sg"
  
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = flatten([
      for env, config in var.environments : [
        for rule in config.rules : {
          port = rule.port
          cidr = config.cidr
          env  = env
        }
      ]
    ])
    content {
      from_port   = ingress.value.port
      to_port     = ingress.value.port
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = [ingress.value.cidr]
      description = "Allow from ${ingress.value.env}"
    }
  }
}

# ✅ BETTER: Break into smaller, clearer pieces
locals {
  # Prepare data separately
  all_ingress_rules = flatten([
    for env, config in var.environments : [
      for rule in config.rules : {
        port        = rule.port
        cidr_blocks = [config.cidr]
        description = "Allow ${rule.name} from ${env}"
      }
    ]
  ])
}

resource "aws_security_group" "cleaner" {
  name = "cleaner-sg"
  
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = local.all_ingress_rules
    iterator = rule
    
    content {
      from_port   = rule.value.port
      to_port     = rule.value.port
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = rule.value.cidr_blocks
      description = rule.value.description
    }
  }
}

# WHEN TO USE DYNAMIC BLOCKS:
# ✅ Security group ingress/egress rules
# ✅ IAM policy statements
# ✅ Load balancer listener rules
# ✅ EBS volumes on instances
# ✅ Tags (when complex)

# WHEN TO AVOID:
# ❌ When static blocks are clearer
# ❌ When only 2-3 blocks needed
# ❌ Complex nested conditions
⚠️

HashiCorp's Warning on Dynamic Blocks

From the official documentation: "Overuse of dynamic blocks can make configuration hard to read and maintain, so we recommend using them only when you need to hide details in order to build a clean user interface for a re-usable module."

📝

EXAM TIP: Dynamic Block Requirements

  • Must have for_each argument
  • Must have content block
  • Block name must match a valid nested block type
  • Iterator defaults to block name if not specified

11.9 Type Constraints

🔒

Defining and Enforcing Value Types

Type constraints specify what kinds of values variables accept. Beyond primitive types, Terraform supports complex types: list(type), set(type), map(type), object({...}), and tuple([...]). The any keyword allows mixed types, while optional() marks object attributes as optional with defaults.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Define complex type constraints
  • Use optional() for flexible objects
  • Understand type inference with any

11.9.1 Complex Type Constructors

📊 Type Constraint Reference
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      TYPE CONSTRAINT CONSTRUCTORS                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  COLLECTION TYPES:                                                           │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  list(type)    - Ordered, indexed, duplicates allowed                  │ │
│  │  set(type)     - Unordered, unique elements only                       │ │
│  │  map(type)     - Key-value pairs (string keys)                         │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│  STRUCTURAL TYPES:                                                           │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  object({      - Named attributes with types                           │ │
│  │    attr = type                                                         │ │
│  │  })                                                                    │ │
│  │                                                                        │ │
│  │  tuple([       - Ordered list with specific type per position          │ │
│  │    type1,                                                              │ │
│  │    type2                                                               │ │
│  │  ])                                                                    │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│  SPECIAL:                                                                    │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  any           - Accepts any type (determined at runtime)              │ │
│  │  optional(t)   - Object attribute that can be omitted                  │ │
│  │  optional(t,d) - Optional with default value d                         │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Complex Type Examples 
# List of strings
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
}

# Set of numbers (unique values)
variable "allowed_ports" {
  type    = set(number)
  default = [80, 443, 8080]
}

# Map of strings
variable "ami_ids" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    "us-east-1" = "ami-abc123"
    "us-west-2" = "ami-def456"
  }
}

# Object with specific attributes
variable "database_config" {
  type = object({
    engine         = string
    engine_version = string
    instance_class = string
    allocated_storage = number
    multi_az       = bool
  })
  default = {
    engine            = "postgres"
    engine_version    = "14.0"
    instance_class    = "db.t3.micro"
    allocated_storage = 20
    multi_az          = false
  }
}

# Tuple with specific types per position
variable "network_range" {
  type = tuple([string, number, number])
  # [CIDR, start_port, end_port]
  default = ["10.0.0.0/8", 1024, 65535]
}

# Nested complex types
variable "server_config" {
  type = map(object({
    instance_type = string
    disk_size     = number
    tags          = map(string)
  }))
  default = {
    web = {
      instance_type = "t3.small"
      disk_size     = 50
      tags          = { role = "web" }
    }
    api = {
      instance_type = "t3.medium"
      disk_size     = 100
      tags          = { role = "api" }
    }
  }
}

11.9.2 optional() for Object Attributes

Optional Attributes (Terraform 1.3+) 
# optional() marks attributes that can be omitted

variable "instance_config" {
  type = object({
    name          = string                           # Required
    instance_type = optional(string, "t3.micro")     # Optional with default
    ami_id        = optional(string)                 # Optional, defaults to null
    monitoring    = optional(bool, false)            # Optional bool with default
    tags          = optional(map(string), {})        # Optional map with default
  })
}

# Usage - only name is required
instance_config = {
  name = "my-server"
  # All other attributes use defaults
}

# Or with some optional values
instance_config = {
  name          = "my-server"
  instance_type = "t3.large"
  monitoring    = true
}

# Complex example with nested optionals
variable "database" {
  type = object({
    identifier = string
    engine     = string
    
    # Optional performance config
    performance = optional(object({
      iops               = optional(number)
      storage_type       = optional(string, "gp3")
      performance_insights = optional(bool, false)
    }), {})
    
    # Optional backup config
    backup = optional(object({
      retention_period = optional(number, 7)
      window          = optional(string, "03:00-04:00")
    }))
  })
}

# Minimal usage
database = {
  identifier = "mydb"
  engine     = "postgres"
}

# With optional nested objects
database = {
  identifier = "mydb"
  engine     = "postgres"
  performance = {
    iops = 3000
    performance_insights = true
  }
  backup = {
    retention_period = 30
  }
}

11.9.3 The any Keyword

Using any for Dynamic Types 
# any allows any type - determined at runtime

# Common use: map with any value type
variable "tags" {
  type    = map(any)
  default = {}
}

# This accepts:
tags = {
  Name        = "server"   # string
  Environment = "prod"     # string
  Count       = 3          # would be converted to string
}

# List of any
variable "mixed_list" {
  type    = list(any)
  default = []
}

# Any at top level (rarely used)
variable "config" {
  type    = any
  default = null
}

# CAUTION: any loses type safety
# Better to use specific types when structure is known
variable "better_tags" {
  type = map(string)  # Explicit is better
}

# When any IS useful:
# - Module inputs that pass through to providers
# - Configuration that varies by use case
# - Wrapper modules

variable "extra_config" {
  type        = any
  default     = {}
  description = "Provider-specific configuration passed through"
}
💡

Type Constraint Best Practices

  • Be as specific as possible with types
  • Use optional() instead of any when structure is known
  • Document expected formats for any types
  • Validate complex inputs with validation blocks

Chapter 12 Built-in Functions

Terraform includes a rich library of built-in functions for transforming and combining values in expressions. Functions are called with parentheses: function(arg1, arg2). Terraform does not support user-defined functions—only the built-in ones are available. This chapter covers the most important function categories: numeric, string, collection, encoding, filesystem, date/time, hash/crypto, IP network, and type conversion functions.

🎯

Learning Objectives

📊 Numeric Functions

  • Use min(), max(), abs() for calculations
  • Round numbers with floor(), ceil(), round()

📝 String Functions

  • Format strings with format(), join(), split()
  • Transform with upper(), lower(), trim()
  • Use regex() for pattern matching

📦 Collection Functions

  • Query with length(), contains(), lookup()
  • Transform with flatten(), merge(), zipmap()
  • Select with element(), slice(), keys(), values()

🔧 Utility Functions

  • Read files with file(), fileexists(), templatefile()
  • Encode/decode with jsonencode(), base64encode(), yamlencode()
  • Network calculations with cidrsubnet(), cidrhost()

📑 In This Chapter

12.1 Numeric Functions
12.2 String Functions
12.3 Collection Functions
12.4 Encoding Functions
12.5 Filesystem Functions
12.6 Date and Time Functions
12.7 Hash and Crypto Functions
12.8 IP Network Functions
12.9 Type Conversion Functions

12.1 Numeric Functions

🔢

Mathematical Operations on Numbers

Numeric functions perform mathematical operations on numbers. Use abs() for absolute values, min()/max() for finding extremes, ceil()/floor() for rounding, and pow()/log() for exponential calculations. These are essential for CIDR calculations, capacity planning, and dynamic scaling.

12.1.1 abs, ceil, floor, round

🔢

Rounding Functions

Function Description Example Result
abs(n) Absolute value abs(-5) 5
ceil(n) Round up to nearest integer ceil(4.1) 5
floor(n) Round down to nearest integer floor(4.9) 4
round(n) Round to nearest integer round(4.5) 5
Rounding Functions in Practice 
# Calculate number of instances needed (always round up)
variable "total_capacity_needed" {
  default = 1000
}

variable "capacity_per_instance" {
  default = 150
}

locals {
  # Round UP to ensure enough capacity
  instances_needed = ceil(var.total_capacity_needed / var.capacity_per_instance)
  # 1000 / 150 = 6.67 → ceil = 7 instances
}

# Calculate storage allocation (round down for safety)
locals {
  # Don't over-allocate
  safe_storage_gb = floor(var.total_budget / var.cost_per_gb)
}
