# Handle negative differences
locals {
  capacity_difference = var.current_capacity - var.required_capacity
  shortage           = abs(capacity_difference)  # Always positive
}

# Round to specific precision (using multiplication/division)
locals {
  price_rounded = round(var.calculated_price * 100) / 100  # 2 decimal places
}

12.1.2 min, max

Min and Max Functions 
# min() returns smallest value
# max() returns largest value

# Basic usage
locals {
  smallest = min(5, 12, 9, 3, 7)   # Result: 3
  largest  = max(5, 12, 9, 3, 7)   # Result: 12
}

# With variables
variable "requested_instances" {
  type    = number
  default = 10
}

variable "max_allowed_instances" {
  type    = number
  default = 5
}

variable "min_required_instances" {
  type    = number
  default = 2
}

locals {
  # Clamp value between min and max
  actual_instances = min(
    max(var.requested_instances, var.min_required_instances),
    var.max_allowed_instances
  )
  # If requested = 10, min = 2, max = 5: Result = 5
  # If requested = 1, min = 2, max = 5: Result = 2
}

# Using with lists via expansion
variable "memory_allocations" {
  type    = list(number)
  default = [512, 1024, 256, 2048]
}

locals {
  max_memory = max(var.memory_allocations...)  # ... expands list
  min_memory = min(var.memory_allocations...)
}

# Practical: Ensure reasonable timeouts
variable "user_timeout" {
  default = 3600
}

locals {
  safe_timeout = min(max(var.user_timeout, 60), 7200)  # Between 60-7200
}

12.1.3 pow, log, signum

Exponential and Other Math Functions 
# pow(base, exponent) - Raises base to the power of exponent
locals {
  squared    = pow(2, 2)    # 4
  cubed      = pow(2, 3)    # 8
  kilobyte   = pow(2, 10)   # 1024
  megabyte   = pow(2, 20)   # 1048576
}

# Practical: Calculate available hosts in CIDR
variable "subnet_prefix" {
  default = 24  # /24 network
}

locals {
  # /24 = 32 - 24 = 8 bits for hosts = 2^8 - 2 = 254 hosts
  available_hosts = pow(2, 32 - var.subnet_prefix) - 2
}

# log(number, base) - Logarithm
locals {
  log_base_2  = log(256, 2)    # 8 (2^8 = 256)
  log_base_10 = log(1000, 10)  # 3 (10^3 = 1000)
}

# Calculate required subnet bits
variable "required_hosts" {
  default = 500
}

locals {
  # Find smallest power of 2 >= required_hosts
  host_bits_needed  = ceil(log(var.required_hosts + 2, 2))
  recommended_cidr  = 32 - local.host_bits_needed
  # 500 hosts → log(502, 2) = 8.97 → ceil = 9 → /23 (32-9=23)
}

# signum(number) - Returns -1, 0, or 1 based on sign
locals {
  sign_positive = signum(5)    # 1
  sign_negative = signum(-3)   # -1
  sign_zero     = signum(0)    # 0
}

12.2 String Functions EXAM TOPIC

📝

Manipulating Text Data

String functions are among the most frequently used in Terraform. Use format() for printf-style formatting, join()/split() for list-string conversion, replace() for substitution, trim*() for whitespace cleanup, and regex() for pattern matching. These are essential for generating resource names, parsing identifiers, and building connection strings.

12.2.1 format, formatlist

Format Functions 
# format(spec, values...) - Printf-style string formatting
locals {
  # %s = string, %d = integer, %f = float, %v = default format
  name_tag = format("%s-%s-%03d", var.project, var.environment, 1)
  # "myproject-prod-001"
  
  # Padding and width
  padded_number = format("%05d", 42)     # "00042"
  right_aligned = format("%10s", "hi")   # "        hi"
  left_aligned  = format("%-10s", "hi")  # "hi        "
  
  # Float precision
  price = format("%.2f", 19.9999)  # "20.00"
  
  # Multiple values
  connection = format("host=%s port=%d dbname=%s", 
                      var.db_host, var.db_port, var.db_name)
}

# formatlist(spec, list...) - Format each element
variable "server_numbers" {
  default = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
}

locals {
  server_names = formatlist("server-%02d", var.server_numbers)
  # ["server-01", "server-02", "server-03", "server-04", "server-05"]
  
  # Multiple lists (zipped together)
  hostnames = formatlist("%s.%s", 
                         ["web", "api", "db"],
                         ["example.com", "example.com", "internal"])
  # ["web.example.com", "api.example.com", "db.internal"]
}

12.2.2 join, split

Join and Split Functions 
# join(separator, list) - Combine list into string
variable "path_parts" {
  default = ["var", "log", "app"]
}

locals {
  unix_path    = join("/", var.path_parts)    # "var/log/app"
  windows_path = join("\\", var.path_parts)   # "var\log\app"
  csv_line     = join(",", ["a", "b", "c"])   # "a,b,c"
  no_separator = join("", ["a", "b", "c"])    # "abc"
}

# split(separator, string) - Split string into list
variable "csv_input" {
  default = "apple,banana,cherry"
}

locals {
  fruits = split(",", var.csv_input)  # ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
}

# Practical: Parse ARN
variable "arn" {
  default = "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/prefix/file.txt"
}

locals {
  arn_parts    = split(":", var.arn)
  service      = local.arn_parts[2]           # "s3"
  resource     = local.arn_parts[5]           # "my-bucket/prefix/file.txt"
  bucket       = split("/", local.resource)[0] # "my-bucket"
}

# Practical: Build tag string
variable "tags" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    Environment = "prod"
    Project     = "myapp"
  }
}

locals {
  tag_string = join(",", [for k, v in var.tags : "${k}=${v}"])
  # "Environment=prod,Project=myapp"
}

12.2.3 upper, lower, title

Case Conversion Functions 
# upper(string) - Convert to uppercase
# lower(string) - Convert to lowercase  
# title(string) - Capitalize first letter of each word

locals {
  upper_env = upper(var.environment)  # "PROD"
  lower_env = lower(var.environment)  # "prod"
  title_env = title(var.environment)  # "Prod"
}

# Practical: Normalize input
variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment name (case-insensitive)"
  type        = string
}

locals {
  # Always use lowercase internally
  normalized_env = lower(var.environment)
  
  # Use in resource names (ensures consistency)
  bucket_name = "myapp-${local.normalized_env}-data"
}

# Validation with normalization
variable "region_input" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["us-east-1", "us-west-2"], lower(var.region_input))
    error_message = "Region must be us-east-1 or us-west-2."
  }
}

12.2.4 trim, trimprefix, trimsuffix, trimspace

Trim Functions 
# trim(string, chars) - Remove specified chars from start/end
# trimprefix(string, prefix) - Remove prefix if present
# trimsuffix(string, suffix) - Remove suffix if present
# trimspace(string) - Remove whitespace from start/end

locals {
  trimmed       = trim("???hello???", "?")        # "hello"
  trim_slashes  = trim("/path/to/file/", "/")     # "path/to/file"
  no_prefix     = trimprefix("prefix-name", "prefix-")  # "name"
  no_suffix     = trimsuffix("file.tf.txt", ".txt")     # "file.tf"
  clean_input   = trimspace("  hello world  ")    # "hello world"
}

# Practical: Clean up S3 keys
variable "s3_key" {
  default = "/uploads//file.txt"
}

locals {
  clean_key = trim(var.s3_key, "/")  # "uploads//file.txt"
}

# Practical: Remove known suffix
variable "hostname" {
  default = "web-prod.example.com"
}

locals {
  short_name = trimsuffix(var.hostname, ".example.com")  # "web-prod"
}

# Practical: Handle environment prefix
variable "full_name" {
  default = "env-prod-app-server"
}

locals {
  # Remove environment prefix
  app_name = trimprefix(
    trimprefix(var.full_name, "env-prod-"),
    "env-dev-"
  )  # "app-server"
}

12.2.5 replace, regex, regexall

Replace and Regex Functions 
# replace(string, search, replace) - String substitution
locals {
  # Simple replacement
  dashed = replace("hello_world_test", "_", "-")  # "hello-world-test"
  
  # Remove characters
  no_spaces = replace("hello world", " ", "")  # "helloworld"
  
  # Replace with regex (wrap pattern in //)
  no_digits = replace("abc123def", "/[0-9]+/", "")  # "abcdef"
  clean_name = replace("My App Name!", "/[^a-zA-Z0-9-]/", "-")  # "My-App-Name-"
}

# regex(pattern, string) - Extract first match
variable "arn" {
  default = "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket-123"
}

locals {
  # Extract bucket name from S3 ARN
  bucket_name = regex("arn:aws:s3:::([a-z0-9-]+)", var.arn)[0]
  # Capture group [0] = "my-bucket-123"
}

# regexall(pattern, string) - Extract all matches
variable "log_line" {
  default = "Error at 10.0.1.1, retry from 10.0.2.2, gateway 10.0.0.1"
}

locals {
  # Find all IP addresses
  ip_addresses = regexall("\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+", var.log_line)
  # ["10.0.1.1", "10.0.2.2", "10.0.0.1"]
}

# Practical: Extract domain from URL
variable "url" {
  default = "https://api.example.com:8080/v1/users"
}

locals {
  domain = regex("https?://([^:/]+)", var.url)[0]  # "api.example.com"
}

# Safe regex with try()
locals {
  maybe_match = try(regex("pattern", var.input), null)
}
📝

EXAM TIP: String Function Essentials

  • join(",",[a,b]) = "a,b" — List to string
  • split(",","a,b") = ["a","b"] — String to list
  • format("%s-%d",x,1) — Printf-style formatting
  • replace(str,"/regex/","") — Use // for regex patterns

12.2.6 substr, startswith, endswith, strcontains

String Inspection and Extraction 
# substr(string, offset, length) - Extract substring
locals {
  full_string = "Hello, World!"
  first_five  = substr(local.full_string, 0, 5)   # "Hello"
  from_seven  = substr(local.full_string, 7, -1)  # "World!" (-1 = rest)
}

# startswith(string, prefix) - Check if starts with prefix (Terraform 1.3+)
# endswith(string, suffix) - Check if ends with suffix (Terraform 1.3+)
# strcontains(string, search) - Check if contains substring (Terraform 1.5+)

variable "filename" {
  default = "config.json"
}

locals {
  is_json   = endswith(var.filename, ".json")      # true
  is_config = startswith(var.filename, "config")   # true
  has_dot   = strcontains(var.filename, ".")       # true
}

# Practical: Route based on file type
variable "upload_path" {
  default = "data/reports/quarterly.csv"
}

locals {
  is_csv  = endswith(var.upload_path, ".csv")
  is_json = endswith(var.upload_path, ".json")
  is_xml  = endswith(var.upload_path, ".xml")
  
  file_type = (
    local.is_csv  ? "csv" :
    local.is_json ? "json" :
    local.is_xml  ? "xml" :
    "unknown"
  )
}

# Practical: Validate resource names
variable "bucket_name" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = !startswith(var.bucket_name, "xn--") && !endswith(var.bucket_name, "-s3alias")
    error_message = "Bucket name cannot start with 'xn--' or end with '-s3alias'."
  }
}

12.3 Collection Functions EXAM TOPIC

📦

Working with Lists, Maps, and Sets

Collection functions manipulate lists, maps, and sets. length() counts elements, contains() checks membership, lookup() safely accesses map values, merge() combines maps, flatten() unpacks nested lists, and keys()/values() extract map components. These are critical for dynamic configurations and data transformations.

12.3.1 length, contains, index

Basic Collection Functions 
# length(collection) - Count elements
locals {
  list_length   = length(["a", "b", "c"])         # 3
  map_length    = length({a=1, b=2})              # 2
  string_length = length("hello")                  # 5
  empty_length  = length([])                       # 0
}

# contains(list, value) - Check if value exists
locals {
  has_prod = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], "prod")  # true
  has_qa   = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], "qa")    # false
}

# index(list, value) - Find position of value (errors if not found!)
locals {
  prod_index = index(["dev", "staging", "prod"], "prod")  # 2
}

# Practical: Conditional resource creation
variable "enabled_features" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["logging", "monitoring"]
}

resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "app" {
  count = contains(var.enabled_features, "logging") ? 1 : 0
  name  = "/app/logs"
}

# Practical: Dynamic subnet count
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count = length(var.availability_zones)
  
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]
}

12.3.2 element, lookup, coalesce

Safe Access Functions 
# element(list, index) - Wraps around for out-of-bounds index!
variable "azs" {
  default = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
}

locals {
  az_0 = element(var.azs, 0)  # "us-west-2a"
  az_3 = element(var.azs, 3)  # "us-west-2a" (wraps! 3 % 3 = 0)
  az_5 = element(var.azs, 5)  # "us-west-2c" (5 % 3 = 2)
}

# Round-robin distribution
resource "aws_instance" "servers" {
  count             = 5
  availability_zone = element(var.azs, count.index)
  # Results: 2a, 2b, 2c, 2a, 2b
}

# lookup(map, key, default) - Safe map access with default
variable "instance_sizes" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    dev  = "t3.micro"
    prod = "t3.large"
  }
}

locals {
  # Returns default if key not found
  dev_size     = lookup(var.instance_sizes, "dev", "t3.micro")    # "t3.micro"
  staging_size = lookup(var.instance_sizes, "staging", "t3.small") # "t3.small"
}

# coalesce(values...) - First non-null, non-empty value
variable "custom_name" {
  default = ""
}

locals {
  # Falls through empty/null values
  final_name = coalesce(var.custom_name, var.default_name, "unnamed")
  
  # With null
  first_valid = coalesce(null, "", "hello", "world")  # "hello"
}

# coalescelist(lists...) - First non-empty list
variable "user_cidrs" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = []
}

locals {
  allowed_cidrs = coalescelist(var.user_cidrs, ["10.0.0.0/8"])
}

12.3.3 keys, values, merge

Map Functions 
# keys(map) - List of map keys (sorted alphabetically)
# values(map) - List of map values (in key order)
variable "servers" {
  type = map(object({
    type = string
    az   = string
  }))
  default = {
    web    = { type = "t3.small", az = "us-west-2a" }
    api    = { type = "t3.medium", az = "us-west-2b" }
    worker = { type = "t3.large", az = "us-west-2c" }
  }
}

locals {
  server_names = keys(var.servers)    # ["api", "web", "worker"]
  server_configs = values(var.servers)
}

# merge(maps...) - Combine maps (later values override)
variable "default_tags" {
  default = {
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    Environment = "default"
  }
}

variable "custom_tags" {
  default = {
    Environment = "production"  # Overrides default
    Project     = "myapp"       # New key
  }
}

locals {
  all_tags = merge(var.default_tags, var.custom_tags)
  # {
  #   ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
  #   Environment = "production"  # Overridden
  #   Project     = "myapp"       # Added
  # }
}

# Practical: Build instance tags
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = merge(
    var.common_tags,
    {
      Name = "web-server"
      Role = "frontend"
    }
  )
}

12.3.4 flatten, concat, distinct

List Manipulation Functions 
# flatten(list) - Flatten nested lists into single list
locals {
  nested = [["a", "b"], ["c"], ["d", "e", "f"]]
  flat   = flatten(local.nested)  # ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"]
}

# concat(lists...) - Combine lists into one
locals {
  list1 = ["a", "b"]
  list2 = ["c", "d"]
  combined = concat(local.list1, local.list2)  # ["a", "b", "c", "d"]
}

# distinct(list) - Remove duplicates (preserves order)
locals {
  with_dupes = ["a", "b", "a", "c", "b", "d"]
  unique     = distinct(local.with_dupes)  # ["a", "b", "c", "d"]
}

# Practical: Collect all CIDRs from nested structure
variable "network_config" {
  default = {
    public = {
      subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
    }
    private = {
      subnets = ["10.0.10.0/24", "10.0.11.0/24"]
    }
  }
}

locals {
  all_subnet_cidrs = flatten([
    for network in var.network_config : network.subnets
  ])
  # ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.10.0/24", "10.0.11.0/24"]
}

# Practical: Combine security group IDs
locals {
  all_sg_ids = distinct(concat(
    var.base_security_groups,
    var.app_security_groups,
    var.optional_security_groups
  ))
}

12.3.5 setproduct, setunion, setintersection

Set Operation Functions 
# setproduct(sets...) - Cartesian product (all combinations)
variable "environments" {
  default = ["dev", "prod"]
}

variable "regions" {
  default = ["us-east-1", "us-west-2"]
}

locals {
  env_region_combos = setproduct(var.environments, var.regions)
  # [
  #   ["dev", "us-east-1"],
  #   ["dev", "us-west-2"],
  #   ["prod", "us-east-1"],
  #   ["prod", "us-west-2"]
  # ]
}

# setunion(sets...) - Combine sets (unique values)
locals {
  set_a = toset(["a", "b", "c"])
  set_b = toset(["b", "c", "d"])
  union = setunion(local.set_a, local.set_b)  # toset(["a", "b", "c", "d"])
}

# setintersection(sets...) - Common elements
locals {
  common = setintersection(local.set_a, local.set_b)  # toset(["b", "c"])
}

# setsubtract(set_a, set_b) - Elements in a but not in b
locals {
  only_in_a = setsubtract(local.set_a, local.set_b)  # toset(["a"])
}

# Practical: Create resources for all combinations
locals {
  deployments = [
    for combo in local.env_region_combos : {
      environment = combo[0]
      region      = combo[1]
      name        = "${combo[0]}-${combo[1]}"
    }
  ]
}

12.3.6 zipmap, slice, reverse, sort

List Transformation Functions 
# zipmap(keys, values) - Create map from two lists
variable "server_names" {
  default = ["web", "api", "db"]
}

variable "server_ips" {
  default = ["10.0.1.1", "10.0.1.2", "10.0.1.3"]
}

locals {
  servers = zipmap(var.server_names, var.server_ips)
  # { web = "10.0.1.1", api = "10.0.1.2", db = "10.0.1.3" }
}

# slice(list, start, end) - Extract portion of list
locals {
  full_list = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e"]
  subset    = slice(local.full_list, 1, 4)  # ["b", "c", "d"]
}

# reverse(list) - Reverse list order
locals {
  reversed = reverse([1, 2, 3])  # [3, 2, 1]
}

# sort(list) - Sort strings lexicographically
locals {
  sorted = sort(["banana", "apple", "cherry"])  # ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
}

# Practical: Create lookup from resource outputs
resource "aws_instance" "servers" {
  count = 3
  # ...
}

locals {
  server_ids = aws_instance.servers[*].id
  server_ips = aws_instance.servers[*].private_ip
  server_map = zipmap(local.server_ids, local.server_ips)
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Collection Function Essentials

  • length([a,b,c]) = 3 — Works on lists, maps, strings
  • element(list, i) — Wraps around (mod behavior)
  • lookup(map, key, default) — Safe map access
  • merge(map1, map2) — Later maps override earlier
  • flatten([[a],[b]]) = [a,b] — Unpacks nested lists

12.4 Encoding Functions

🔐

Encoding and Serialization

Encoding functions convert between data formats. jsonencode()/jsondecode() handle JSON, yamlencode()/yamldecode() process YAML, base64encode()/base64decode() handle binary data, and urlencode() makes strings URL-safe. These are essential for API integrations, configuration files, and cloud-init scripts.

12.4.1 jsonencode, jsondecode

JSON Functions 
# jsonencode(value) - Convert HCL to JSON string
locals {
  policy_document = {
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect   = "Allow"
        Action   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
        Resource = ["arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket", "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"]
      }
    ]
  }
  
  policy_json = jsonencode(local.policy_document)
  # '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[...]}'
}

# Use in IAM policies
resource "aws_iam_policy" "s3_read" {
  name   = "s3-read-policy"
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["s3:GetObject"]
      Resource = "*"
    }]
  })
}

# jsondecode(string) - Parse JSON string to HCL
variable "config_json" {
  default = <<EOF
{
  "database": {
    "host": "localhost",
    "port": 5432
  },
  "features": ["logging", "monitoring"]
}
EOF
}

locals {
  config  = jsondecode(var.config_json)
  db_host = local.config.database.host    # "localhost"
  db_port = local.config.database.port    # 5432
}

# Practical: Parse AWS API response
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db" {
  secret_id = aws_secretsmanager_secret.db.id
}

locals {
  db_creds = jsondecode(data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db.secret_string)
  username = local.db_creds.username
  password = local.db_creds.password
}

12.4.2 yamlencode, yamldecode

YAML Functions 
# yamlencode(value) - Convert HCL to YAML string
locals {
  k8s_config = {
    apiVersion = "v1"
    kind       = "ConfigMap"
    metadata = {
      name      = "app-config"
      namespace = "default"
    }
    data = {
      "app.properties" = "debug=false"
    }
  }
  
  yaml_output = yamlencode(local.k8s_config)
  # apiVersion: v1
  # kind: ConfigMap
  # metadata:
  #   name: app-config
  # ...
}

# Write YAML config file
resource "local_file" "k8s_manifest" {
  filename = "${path.module}/config.yaml"
  content  = yamlencode({
    version = "1.0"
    settings = {
      debug    = var.debug_mode
      log_level = var.log_level
    }
  })
}

# yamldecode(string) - Parse YAML to HCL
variable "yaml_config" {
  default = <<EOF
database:
  host: db.example.com
  port: 5432
  ssl: true
features:
  - logging
  - monitoring
  - caching
EOF
}

locals {
  parsed   = yamldecode(var.yaml_config)
  db_host  = local.parsed.database.host      # "db.example.com"
  features = local.parsed.features           # ["logging", "monitoring", "caching"]
}

# Practical: Load external YAML config
locals {
  app_config = yamldecode(file("${path.module}/config.yaml"))
}

12.4.3 base64encode, base64decode, base64gzip

Base64 Functions 
# base64encode(string) - Encode to Base64
locals {
  plain_text   = "Hello, World!"
  encoded_text = base64encode(local.plain_text)  
  # "SGVsbG8sIFdvcmxkIQ=="
}

# base64decode(string) - Decode from Base64
locals {
  decoded = base64decode("SGVsbG8sIFdvcmxkIQ==")  
  # "Hello, World!"
}

# Practical: EC2 User Data
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  # AWS expects user_data to be Base64 encoded
  user_data = base64encode(<<-EOF
    #!/bin/bash
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y nginx
    systemctl start nginx
  EOF
  )
}

# base64gzip(string) - Gzip then Base64 (for large scripts)
locals {
  large_script = file("${path.module}/bootstrap.sh")
  compressed   = base64gzip(local.large_script)
}

# Practical: Lambda function code
resource "aws_lambda_function" "inline" {
  function_name = "my-function"
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "python3.9"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  
  # For small inline functions
  filename = null
  source_code_hash = null
  
  # Base64 encoded ZIP (advanced use case)
}

# textencodebase64 / textdecodebase64 - With specific encoding
locals {
  utf16_encoded = textencodebase64("Hello", "UTF-16LE")
}

12.4.4 urlencode, csvdecode

URL and CSV Functions 
# urlencode(string) - URL-safe encoding
locals {
  search_term   = "hello world & test"
  encoded_term  = urlencode(local.search_term)
  # "hello+world+%26+test"
  
  # Build URL with parameters
  api_url = "https://api.example.com/search?q=${urlencode(var.query)}"
}

# csvdecode(string) - Parse CSV to list of maps
variable "servers_csv" {
  default = <<EOF
name,type,zone
web,t3.small,us-west-2a
api,t3.medium,us-west-2b
db,r5.large,us-west-2c
EOF
}

locals {
  servers = csvdecode(var.servers_csv)
  # [
  #   { name = "web", type = "t3.small", zone = "us-west-2a" },
  #   { name = "api", type = "t3.medium", zone = "us-west-2b" },
  #   { name = "db", type = "r5.large", zone = "us-west-2c" }
  # ]
}

# Create resources from CSV data
resource "aws_instance" "from_csv" {
  for_each = { for s in local.servers : s.name => s }
  
  ami               = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type     = each.value.type
  availability_zone = each.value.zone
  
  tags = { Name = each.key }
}

# Practical: Load from CSV file
locals {
  team_members = csvdecode(file("${path.module}/team.csv"))
}
💡

Encoding Functions Quick Reference

  • jsonencode() / jsondecode() — HCL ↔ JSON
  • yamlencode() / yamldecode() — HCL ↔ YAML
  • base64encode() / base64decode() — Binary safe
  • csvdecode() — CSV → List of maps (no encode!)

12.5 Filesystem Functions

📂

File Operations

Filesystem functions read external files and compute directory paths. file() reads text files, filebase64() reads binary files, templatefile() processes templates with variables, fileset() finds files matching patterns, and path references (path.module, path.root) help locate files reliably.

12.5.1 file, fileexists, filebase64

File Reading Functions 
# file(path) - Read file as string (UTF-8)
locals {
  # Read configuration file
  config_content = file("${path.module}/config.json")
  
  # Read SSH public key
  public_key = file("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
  
  # Read shell script
  bootstrap_script = file("${path.module}/scripts/bootstrap.sh")
}

# Practical: Load IAM policy from file
resource "aws_iam_policy" "custom" {
  name   = "custom-policy"
  policy = file("${path.module}/policies/custom-policy.json")
}

# fileexists(path) - Check if file exists (Terraform 0.12+)
locals {
  has_custom_config = fileexists("${path.module}/custom.json")
  
  config = local.has_custom_config ? 
    file("${path.module}/custom.json") : 
    file("${path.module}/default.json")
}

# filebase64(path) - Read binary file as Base64
locals {
  # For binary files (images, certificates, etc.)
  logo_base64 = filebase64("${path.module}/assets/logo.png")
  cert_base64 = filebase64("${path.module}/certs/server.crt")
}

# Practical: Upload binary file to S3
resource "aws_s3_object" "logo" {
  bucket         = aws_s3_bucket.assets.id
  key            = "images/logo.png"
  content_base64 = filebase64("${path.module}/assets/logo.png")
  content_type   = "image/png"
}

12.5.2 templatefile

Template File Function 
# templatefile(path, vars) - Render template with variables

# Template file: templates/userdata.sh.tpl
# #!/bin/bash
# echo "Environment: ${environment}"
# echo "Region: ${region}"
# %{ for pkg in packages ~}
# apt-get install -y ${pkg}
# %{ endfor ~}

locals {
  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/userdata.sh.tpl", {
    environment = var.environment
    region      = var.aws_region
    packages    = ["nginx", "docker", "curl"]
  })
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  user_data     = local.user_data
}

# Template file: templates/nginx.conf.tpl
# server {
#     listen ${port};
#     server_name ${server_name};
#     
#     %{ for upstream in upstreams ~}
#     upstream ${upstream.name} {
#         server ${upstream.address};
#     }
#     %{ endfor ~}
# }

locals {
  nginx_config = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/nginx.conf.tpl", {
    port        = 80
    server_name = "example.com"
    upstreams = [
      { name = "api", address = "10.0.1.1:8080" },
      { name = "web", address = "10.0.1.2:3000" }
    ]
  })
}

# Conditional in template
# %{ if enable_ssl ~}
# ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/cert.pem;
# %{ endif ~}

# With complex objects
locals {
  k8s_manifest = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/deployment.yaml.tpl", {
    app_name    = var.app_name
    replicas    = var.replica_count
    image       = "${var.registry}/${var.image}:${var.tag}"
    environment = var.environment_vars
  })
}
⚠️

templatefile vs file

  • file() — Reads file as-is, no variable substitution
  • templatefile() — Processes ${...} and %{...} syntax
  • Use $${} and %%{} for literal ${} and %{}

12.5.3 fileset, pathexpand

File Discovery Functions 
# fileset(path, pattern) - Find files matching glob pattern
locals {
  # Find all .tf files
  tf_files = fileset(path.module, "*.tf")
  # {"main.tf", "variables.tf", "outputs.tf"}
  
  # Find all JSON files in subdirectory
  policy_files = fileset(path.module, "policies/*.json")
  # {"policies/read.json", "policies/write.json"}
  
  # Recursive search
  all_yaml = fileset(path.module, "**/*.yaml")
  # {"config.yaml", "envs/dev.yaml", "envs/prod.yaml"}
}

# Practical: Upload multiple files
resource "aws_s3_object" "configs" {
  for_each = fileset("${path.module}/configs", "**/*.json")
  
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.configs.id
  key    = each.value
  source = "${path.module}/configs/${each.value}"
  etag   = filemd5("${path.module}/configs/${each.value}")
}

# Practical: Create IAM policies from files
resource "aws_iam_policy" "policies" {
  for_each = fileset("${path.module}/policies", "*.json")
  
  name   = trimsuffix(each.value, ".json")
  policy = file("${path.module}/policies/${each.value}")
}

# pathexpand(path) - Expand ~ to home directory
locals {
  ssh_key_path = pathexpand("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
  # "/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa.pub"
}

resource "aws_key_pair" "deployer" {
  key_name   = "deployer-key"
  public_key = file(pathexpand("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub"))
}

# dirname and basename
locals {
  full_path = "/var/log/app/server.log"
  dir_name  = dirname(local.full_path)   # "/var/log/app"
  base_name = basename(local.full_path)  # "server.log"
}

12.5.4 abspath, path references

Path Functions and References 
# Path references (not functions, but essential for file operations)
# path.module  - Directory of current module
# path.root    - Directory of root module
# path.cwd     - Current working directory

locals {
  # Relative to current module
  module_config = file("${path.module}/config.json")
  
  # Relative to root module
  root_config = file("${path.root}/global-config.json")
  
  # Scripts directory
  scripts_dir = "${path.module}/scripts"
}

# abspath(path) - Convert to absolute path
locals {
  relative_path = "../shared/config.json"
  absolute_path = abspath(local.relative_path)
  # "/home/user/terraform/shared/config.json"
}

# Practical: Module file structure
# modules/
#   web-server/
#     main.tf
#     templates/
#       userdata.sh.tpl

module "web_server" {
  source = "./modules/web-server"
  # Inside module, path.module = "./modules/web-server"
}

# Inside web-server module:
locals {
  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/userdata.sh.tpl", {
    # variables...
  })
}

# filemd5 and filesha256 - Hash file contents
resource "aws_s3_object" "app" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.deploy.id
  key    = "app.zip"
  source = "${path.module}/dist/app.zip"
  
  # Trigger update when file changes
  etag = filemd5("${path.module}/dist/app.zip")
}

resource "aws_lambda_function" "app" {
  filename         = "${path.module}/dist/app.zip"
  source_code_hash = filebase64sha256("${path.module}/dist/app.zip")
  # ...
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Filesystem Functions

  • file() — Read text, filebase64() — Read binary
  • templatefile() — Processes templates with ${} and %{}
  • fileset() — Glob pattern matching for multiple files
  • path.module vs path.root — Current vs root module

12.6 Date and Time Functions

⏰

Working with Timestamps

Date and time functions handle timestamps and duration calculations. timestamp() returns current UTC time, formatdate() formats timestamps into custom strings, timeadd() calculates future/past times, and timecmp() compares dates. These are useful for expiration dates, scheduling, and audit trails.

12.6.1 timestamp, plantimestamp

Current Time Functions 
# timestamp() - Current UTC time in RFC 3339 format
# WARNING: Changes every run, can cause drift!
locals {
  current_time = timestamp()
  # "2024-03-15T14:30:00Z"
}

# Use carefully - causes resource updates every apply
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  # ...
  tags = {
    # BAD: Changes every apply
    LastUpdated = timestamp()
  }
}

# plantimestamp() - Time when plan was created (Terraform 1.5+)
# More stable than timestamp() - same value during plan/apply
locals {
  plan_time = plantimestamp()
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  tags = {
    # BETTER: Stable during plan/apply cycle
    PlannedAt = plantimestamp()
  }
  
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [tags["PlannedAt"]]
  }
}

# Practical: Tag with deployment date (with ignore_changes)
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "app-server"
    DeployedAt  = timestamp()
  }
  
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [tags["DeployedAt"]]
  }
}

12.6.2 formatdate

Date Formatting Function 
# formatdate(spec, timestamp) - Format timestamp to string
locals {
  now = timestamp()  # "2024-03-15T14:30:45Z"
  
  # Format specifiers
  year_month_day = formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", local.now)      # "2024-03-15"
  time_only      = formatdate("HH:mm:ss", local.now)        # "14:30:45"
  full_datetime  = formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD'T'HH:mm:ssZ", local.now)
  
  # Human-readable
  readable = formatdate("EEEE, MMMM D, YYYY", local.now)
  # "Friday, March 15, 2024"
  
  # For resource naming (no special chars)
  safe_timestamp = formatdate("YYYYMMDDHHmmss", local.now)  # "20240315143045"
}

# Format specifiers reference:
# YYYY = 4-digit year       YY = 2-digit year
# MMMM = Month name         MMM = Abbreviated month
# MM = 2-digit month        M = 1-digit month
# DD = 2-digit day          D = 1-digit day
# EEEE = Weekday name       EEE = Abbreviated weekday
# HH = 24-hour              hh = 12-hour
# mm = minutes              ss = seconds
# Z = timezone offset       ZZZ = timezone name
# AA = AM/PM                aa = am/pm

# Practical: Version naming
locals {
  version = formatdate("YYYY.MM.DD", timestamp())
  # "2024.03.15"
}

# Practical: Log file naming
output "log_filename" {
  value = "app-${formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", timestamp())}.log"
  # "app-2024-03-15.log"
}

12.6.3 timeadd, timecmp

Time Calculation Functions 
# timeadd(timestamp, duration) - Add/subtract time
# Duration format: "XhYmZs" (hours, minutes, seconds)
locals {
  now = timestamp()
  
  # Add time
  one_hour_later   = timeadd(local.now, "1h")
  one_day_later    = timeadd(local.now, "24h")
  one_week_later   = timeadd(local.now, "168h")  # 24 * 7
  
  # Subtract time (negative duration)
  one_hour_ago = timeadd(local.now, "-1h")
  one_day_ago  = timeadd(local.now, "-24h")
  
  # Complex duration
  offset_time = timeadd(local.now, "2h30m15s")
}

# Practical: Certificate expiration
resource "tls_self_signed_cert" "example" {
  validity_period_hours = 8760  # 1 year
  
  # Alternative: Calculate end time
  # not_after = timeadd(timestamp(), "8760h")
}

# timecmp(timestamp_a, timestamp_b) - Compare timestamps
# Returns: -1 (a < b), 0 (a == b), 1 (a > b)
locals {
  is_expired = timecmp(
    timestamp(),                           # now
    "2024-12-31T23:59:59Z"                # deadline
  ) > 0  # true if now > deadline
}

# Practical: Conditional based on time
variable "maintenance_end" {
  default = "2024-04-01T00:00:00Z"
}

locals {
  maintenance_over = timecmp(timestamp(), var.maintenance_end) >= 0
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count = local.maintenance_over ? 3 : 0
  # Only create after maintenance period
}
💡

Time Function Tips

  • Use plantimestamp() (1.5+) for stable timestamps
  • Always use ignore_changes with timestamp() in tags
  • Duration format: hours (h), minutes (m), seconds (s)
  • All timestamps are UTC — no timezone conversions!

12.7 Hash and Cryptographic Functions

🔒

Hashing and Checksums

Hash functions generate fixed-size checksums for integrity verification and change detection. md5(), sha256(), and sha512() hash strings, while filemd5(), filesha256() hash file contents. uuid() generates unique identifiers. These are essential for ETags, source_code_hash, and deduplication.

12.7.1 md5, sha1, sha256, sha512

String Hash Functions 
# md5(string) - MD5 hash (32 hex chars)
# sha1(string) - SHA-1 hash (40 hex chars)
# sha256(string) - SHA-256 hash (64 hex chars)
# sha512(string) - SHA-512 hash (128 hex chars)

locals {
  input = "Hello, World!"
  
  md5_hash    = md5(local.input)     # "65a8e27d8879283831b664bd8b7f0ad4"
  sha1_hash   = sha1(local.input)    # "0a0a9f2a6772942557ab5355d76af442f8f65e01"
  sha256_hash = sha256(local.input)  # "dffd6021bb2bd5b0af676290809ec3a53191dd81c7f70a4b28688a362182986f"
}

# base64sha256, base64sha512 - Hash and Base64 encode
locals {
  b64_hash = base64sha256("mystring")
  # For Lambda source_code_hash
}

# bcrypt(string, cost) - Password hashing (10 = default cost)
locals {
  hashed_password = bcrypt("mysecretpassword")
  # Cost factor can be adjusted for security vs performance
}

# Practical: Generate unique IDs from inputs
locals {
  # Create deterministic ID from multiple values
  resource_id = substr(sha256("${var.project}-${var.environment}"), 0, 8)
  # Always same output for same inputs
}

# Practical: Content hash for cache busting
locals {
  config_hash = md5(jsonencode(var.app_config))
  # Changes when config changes
}

resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "config" {
  name  = "/app/config"
  type  = "String"
  value = jsonencode(var.app_config)
  
  tags = {
    ContentHash = local.config_hash
  }
}

12.7.2 filemd5, filesha256, filesha512

File Hash Functions 
# filemd5(path) - MD5 hash of file contents
# filesha256(path) - SHA-256 hash of file contents
# filesha512(path) - SHA-512 hash of file contents

locals {
  script_hash = filemd5("${path.module}/scripts/bootstrap.sh")
  config_hash = filesha256("${path.module}/config.json")
}

# Practical: S3 object ETag (detects changes)
resource "aws_s3_object" "app_config" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.configs.id
  key    = "app/config.json"
  source = "${path.module}/config.json"
  
  # ETag triggers update when file changes
  etag = filemd5("${path.module}/config.json")
}

# filebase64sha256, filebase64sha512 - Hash file, Base64 encode
locals {
  lambda_hash = filebase64sha256("${path.module}/lambda.zip")
}

# Practical: Lambda source code hash
resource "aws_lambda_function" "processor" {
  function_name = "data-processor"
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "python3.11"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  
  filename         = "${path.module}/lambda.zip"
  source_code_hash = filebase64sha256("${path.module}/lambda.zip")
  # Triggers redeployment when ZIP changes
}

# Practical: Archive with hash
data "archive_file" "lambda" {
  type        = "zip"
  source_dir  = "${path.module}/src"
  output_path = "${path.module}/lambda.zip"
}

resource "aws_lambda_function" "app" {
  filename         = data.archive_file.lambda.output_path
  source_code_hash = data.archive_file.lambda.output_base64sha256
  # ...
}

12.7.3 uuid, uuidv5

UUID Functions 
# uuid() - Generate random UUID v4
# WARNING: New value every run, can cause drift!
locals {
  random_id = uuid()
  # "b5ee72a3-54dd-c4b8-90c6-d4e55d4b3c8a"
}

# Use with care - changes every apply
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  tags = {
    # BAD: Changes every apply
    UniqueId = uuid()
  }
  
  lifecycle {
    # Use ignore_changes to prevent constant updates
    ignore_changes = [tags["UniqueId"]]
  }
}

# uuidv5(namespace, name) - Deterministic UUID v5
# Namespace UUIDs: dns, url, oid, x500
locals {
  # Always same output for same inputs
  deterministic_id = uuidv5("dns", "example.com")
  # "cfbff0d1-9375-5685-968c-48ce8b15ae17"
  
  # Custom namespace (must be valid UUID)
  custom_ns = "a6e4a9b0-7c93-11eb-9439-0242ac130002"
  app_id    = uuidv5(local.custom_ns, "myapp-prod")
}

# Practical: Stable unique IDs
locals {
  # Create deterministic ID from multiple values
  deployment_id = uuidv5("dns", "${var.project}-${var.environment}-${var.region}")
  # Same inputs = same UUID, every time
}

# Practical: Resource naming with stable IDs
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "app" {
  name = "app-${substr(uuidv5("dns", var.app_name), 0, 8)}"
  # Stable name: "app-cfbff0d1"
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Hash Function Usage

  • filemd5() — S3 ETags, change detection
  • filebase64sha256() — Lambda source_code_hash
  • uuid() — Random, changes every run!
  • uuidv5() — Deterministic, same input = same output

12.8 IP Network Functions EXAM TOPIC

🌐

Network Address Calculations

IP network functions calculate CIDR blocks and network addresses. cidrsubnet() divides networks into smaller subnets, cidrhost() calculates specific host IPs, cidrnetmask() extracts subnet masks, and cidrsubnets() creates multiple subnets at once. These are essential for VPC and subnet design.

12.8.1 cidrsubnet

CIDR Subnet Calculation 
# cidrsubnet(prefix, newbits, netnum) - Calculate subnet from CIDR
# prefix  = Base CIDR block
# newbits = Additional bits for subnet mask (increases prefix length)
# netnum  = Subnet number (0-indexed)

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  default = "10.0.0.0/16"  # 65,536 IPs
}

locals {
  # Create /24 subnets (16 + 8 = 24)
  subnet_0 = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, 0)   # "10.0.0.0/24"
  subnet_1 = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, 1)   # "10.0.1.0/24"
  subnet_2 = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, 2)   # "10.0.2.0/24"
  
  # Create /20 subnets (16 + 4 = 20)
  large_subnet_0 = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 4, 0)   # "10.0.0.0/20"
  large_subnet_1 = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 4, 1)   # "10.0.16.0/20"
}

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │                    CIDRSUBNET VISUAL BREAKDOWN                              │
# ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
# │  VPC: 10.0.0.0/16 (65,536 IPs)                                              │
# │                                                                             │
# │  cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, n) → /24 subnets (256 IPs each)              │
# │                                                                             │
# │  n=0: 10.0.0.0/24    n=1: 10.0.1.0/24    n=2: 10.0.2.0/24                  │
# │  n=3: 10.0.3.0/24    ...                 n=255: 10.0.255.0/24              │
# │                                                                             │
# │  Total: 256 possible /24 subnets from a /16                                │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Practical: Create subnets dynamically
variable "availability_zones" {
  default = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count = length(var.availability_zones)
  
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  # Creates: 10.0.0.0/24, 10.0.1.0/24, 10.0.2.0/24
}

resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count = length(var.availability_zones)
  
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index + 100)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  # Creates: 10.0.100.0/24, 10.0.101.0/24, 10.0.102.0/24
}

12.8.2 cidrsubnets

Multiple Subnets at Once 
# cidrsubnets(prefix, newbits...) - Create multiple contiguous subnets
# Takes variable number of newbits arguments

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  default = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

locals {
  # Create different sized subnets
  subnets = cidrsubnets(var.vpc_cidr, 4, 4, 8, 8, 8)
  # [
  #   "10.0.0.0/20",    # +4 bits = /20 (4,096 IPs) - for EKS
  #   "10.0.16.0/20",   # +4 bits = /20 (4,096 IPs) - for RDS
  #   "10.0.32.0/24",   # +8 bits = /24 (256 IPs) - public-a
  #   "10.0.33.0/24",   # +8 bits = /24 (256 IPs) - public-b
  #   "10.0.34.0/24"    # +8 bits = /24 (256 IPs) - public-c
  # ]
}

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │                         CIDRSUBNETS ALLOCATION                              │
# ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
# │  10.0.0.0/16 (65,536 IPs)                                                  │
# │  ├── cidrsubnets(vpc, 4, 4, 8, 8, 8)                                       │
# │  │                                                                          │
# │  │   [0] 10.0.0.0/20   (4,096 IPs)  ████████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░       │
# │  │   [1] 10.0.16.0/20  (4,096 IPs)  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░████████████████       │
# │  │   [2] 10.0.32.0/24  (256 IPs)    ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░█      │
# │  │   [3] 10.0.33.0/24  (256 IPs)    ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░█     │
# │  │   [4] 10.0.34.0/24  (256 IPs)    ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░█    │
# │  │                                                                          │
# │  └── Subnets are allocated contiguously, no overlap                        │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Practical: Named subnet allocation
locals {
  subnet_newbits = {
    eks_nodes    = 4   # /20 for EKS
    database     = 4   # /20 for RDS
    public_a     = 8   # /24 for public
    public_b     = 8
    public_c     = 8
    private_a    = 8   # /24 for private
    private_b    = 8
    private_c    = 8
  }
  
  subnet_cidrs = cidrsubnets(var.vpc_cidr, [for k, v in local.subnet_newbits : v]...)
  # Creates list of CIDRs
  
  subnets_map = zipmap(keys(local.subnet_newbits), local.subnet_cidrs)
  # { eks_nodes = "10.0.0.0/20", database = "10.0.16.0/20", ... }
}

12.8.3 cidrhost, cidrnetmask

Host and Netmask Functions 
# cidrhost(prefix, hostnum) - Get specific host IP in CIDR
# hostnum = Host number (0 = network, last = broadcast)

variable "subnet_cidr" {
  default = "10.0.1.0/24"
}

locals {
  # Get specific hosts
  network_addr = cidrhost(var.subnet_cidr, 0)    # "10.0.1.0" (network)
  first_host   = cidrhost(var.subnet_cidr, 1)    # "10.0.1.1"
  gateway      = cidrhost(var.subnet_cidr, 1)    # "10.0.1.1" (AWS default)
  last_host    = cidrhost(var.subnet_cidr, 254)  # "10.0.1.254"
  broadcast    = cidrhost(var.subnet_cidr, 255)  # "10.0.1.255"
  
  # Negative numbers count from end
  second_last = cidrhost(var.subnet_cidr, -2)    # "10.0.1.254"
}

# Practical: Static IP assignments
resource "aws_instance" "bastion" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public.id
  
  private_ip = cidrhost(aws_subnet.public.cidr_block, 10)
  # Assigns .10 address in subnet
}

# cidrnetmask(prefix) - Get netmask from CIDR
locals {
  netmask_24 = cidrnetmask("10.0.0.0/24")  # "255.255.255.0"
  netmask_16 = cidrnetmask("10.0.0.0/16")  # "255.255.0.0"
  netmask_20 = cidrnetmask("10.0.0.0/20")  # "255.255.240.0"
}

# cidrcontains(prefix, address) - Check if IP is in CIDR (Terraform 1.7+)
locals {
  in_range = cidrcontains("10.0.0.0/16", "10.0.5.100")  # true
  outside  = cidrcontains("10.0.0.0/16", "192.168.1.1") # false
}

# Practical: Validate input IP
variable "allowed_ip" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = cidrcontains("10.0.0.0/8", var.allowed_ip)
    error_message = "IP must be in 10.0.0.0/8 range."
  }
}
📝

EXAM TIP: CIDR Functions

  • cidrsubnet(cidr, newbits, num) — Single subnet calculation
  • cidrsubnets(cidr, bits...) — Multiple contiguous subnets
  • cidrhost(cidr, num) — Specific IP in range
  • newbits increases mask: /16 + 8 = /24

12.9 Type Conversion Functions

🔄

Explicit Type Conversion

Type conversion functions explicitly convert values between types. tostring(), tonumber(), tobool() convert primitives; tolist(), toset(), tomap() convert collections. try() provides fallback for conversions that might fail, and can() tests if an expression succeeds.

12.9.1 tostring, tonumber, tobool

Primitive Type Conversions 
# tostring(value) - Convert to string
locals {
  num_to_str  = tostring(42)        # "42"
  bool_to_str = tostring(true)      # "true"
  null_to_str = tostring(null)      # Error! null can't convert
}

# tonumber(value) - Convert to number
locals {
  str_to_num   = tonumber("42")     # 42
  float_to_num = tonumber("3.14")   # 3.14
  bad_number   = tonumber("hello")  # Error!
}

# tobool(value) - Convert to boolean
locals {
  str_true  = tobool("true")   # true
  str_false = tobool("false")  # false
  # Only "true" and "false" strings work!
}

# Practical: Handle string input as number
variable "port" {
  type        = string
  description = "Port number (accepts string input)"
  default     = "8080"
}

resource "aws_security_group_rule" "app" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = tonumber(var.port)
  to_port           = tonumber(var.port)
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.app.id
}

# Practical: Boolean from string env var
variable "enable_debug" {
  type    = string
  default = "false"
}

locals {
  debug_enabled = tobool(var.enable_debug)
}

12.9.2 tolist, toset, tomap

Collection Type Conversions 
# tolist(value) - Convert to list (preserves order, allows duplicates)
locals {
  set_to_list = tolist(toset(["c", "b", "a"]))  # ["a", "b", "c"]
  # Note: set sorts, then converts to list
}

# toset(value) - Convert to set (sorted, unique values)
locals {
  list_to_set = toset(["a", "b", "a", "c"])  # toset(["a", "b", "c"])
  # Duplicates removed, sorted
}

# tomap(value) - Convert to map
locals {
  obj_to_map = tomap({
    name = "myapp"
    env  = "prod"
  })
}

# Practical: for_each requires set of strings
variable "users" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["alice", "bob", "alice", "charlie"]  # Has duplicate!
}

resource "aws_iam_user" "users" {
  for_each = toset(var.users)  # Removes duplicate "alice"
  name     = each.key
}

# Practical: Convert tuple to list
locals {
  # Tuples from for expressions become lists
  names = [for u in var.users : upper(u)]  # tuple
  names_list = tolist(local.names)         # list
}

# Practical: Map for resource iteration
variable "instances" {
  type = list(object({
    name = string
    type = string
  }))
}

locals {
  instances_map = { for i in var.instances : i.name => i }
  # Can now use for_each
}

resource "aws_instance" "servers" {
  for_each      = local.instances_map
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = each.value.type
  tags          = { Name = each.key }
}

12.9.3 try, can

Error Handling Functions 
# try(expressions...) - Return first successful expression
# Evaluates expressions left to right, returns first that succeeds

locals {
  # Safe access with fallback
  maybe_value = try(var.config.nested.value, "default")
  
  # Multiple fallbacks
  final_value = try(
    var.user_input,
    data.aws_ssm_parameter.config.value,
    "hardcoded-default"
  )
}

# Practical: Safe JSON parsing
variable "json_input" {
  type    = string
  default = "invalid json"
}

locals {
  parsed = try(jsondecode(var.json_input), {})
  # Returns {} if parsing fails
}

# Practical: Safe regex extraction
locals {
  domain = try(
    regex("https?://([^/]+)", var.url)[0],
    "unknown"
  )
}

# can(expression) - Test if expression succeeds (returns bool)
locals {
  is_valid_json = can(jsondecode(var.json_input))  # true/false
  has_key       = can(var.config.nested.key)       # true/false
}

# Practical: Validation with can()
variable "email" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z]+@[a-z]+\\.[a-z]+$", var.email))
    error_message = "Invalid email format."
  }
}

# Practical: Optional nested access
variable "config" {
  type = any
  default = {
    database = {}  # Empty, no host key
  }
}

locals {
  db_host = try(var.config.database.host, "localhost")
  # Returns "localhost" since host doesn't exist
  
  has_db_config = can(var.config.database.host)
  # false
}

# Combining try and can
locals {
  # Check first, then access
  final_host = can(var.config.database) ? (
    try(var.config.database.host, "localhost")
  ) : "localhost"
}

12.9.4 sensitive, nonsensitive

Sensitive Value Functions 
# sensitive(value) - Mark value as sensitive (hidden in output)
locals {
  api_key = sensitive("sk-abc123def456")
  # Value hidden in plan/apply output
}

output "api_key" {
  value     = local.api_key
  sensitive = true  # Required when outputting sensitive values
}

# nonsensitive(value) - Remove sensitive marking
# USE WITH CAUTION - exposes secret values!

locals {
  secret_value = sensitive("hidden")
  
  # Sometimes needed for operations that don't support sensitive
  # Only use when you're sure it's safe!
  exposed = nonsensitive(local.secret_value)
}

# Practical: Debug (temporary!)
output "debug_secret" {
  value = nonsensitive(data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db.secret_string)
  # WARNING: Exposes secret in output!
}

# Practical: Extract non-sensitive part
locals {
  db_secret = jsondecode(
    nonsensitive(data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db.secret_string)
  )
  # Can now access db_secret.host, db_secret.port
  # But db_secret.password should stay sensitive
  
  db_host = local.db_secret.host           # Non-sensitive
  db_pass = sensitive(local.db_secret.password)  # Re-mark sensitive
}

# type() - Get type of value (useful for debugging)
locals {
  my_type = type(var.input)  # Returns type description
  # "string", "number", "list(string)", "map(any)", etc.
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Type Conversion Essentials

  • toset() — Required for for_each with lists
  • try(expr, default) — Safe access with fallback
  • can(expr) — Test without error (returns bool)
  • sensitive() — Hides values in plan output
💾 📊 🔒 🗄️
13

Terraform State - Fundamentals

Understanding Terraform's Source of Truth

7 Sections
28 Subsections
15+ Exam Tips

Chapter 13Terraform State - Fundamentals

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Understand what Terraform state is and why it's essential
2 Master the JSON structure of terraform.tfstate files
3 Know default state file locations and backup behavior
4 Understand the four critical purposes of state (EXAM)
5 Learn how state locking prevents corruption (EXAM)
6 Implement state file security best practices
7 Use terraform show and state commands to inspect state

📑 In This Chapter

13.1 What is Terraform State? 13.2 State File Structure 13.3 State File Location 13.4 Why State is Critical 📝 13.5 State Locking 📝 13.6 State File Security 13.7 Inspecting State

13.1 What is Terraform State?

💾

The Foundation of Terraform Operations

Terraform state is the mechanism by which Terraform knows what infrastructure exists in the real world. Without state, Terraform would have no way to know if a resource already exists, what its current configuration is, or whether it needs to be created, updated, or destroyed. Understanding state is absolutely fundamental to working with Terraform effectively.

💾

🎯 Terraform State Definition

Terraform state is a JSON file that stores the mapping between your Terraform configuration and the real-world resources it manages. It's Terraform's database of what exists, enabling it to determine what changes need to be made.

  • Maps configuration to real infrastructure
  • Tracks resource IDs, attributes, and metadata
  • Enables plan calculation and change detection
  • Stored locally by default as terraform.tfstate
🎯

13.1.1 Purpose of State

🔑 Why Terraform Needs State

State serves four critical purposes that make Terraform's declarative model possible.

PURPOSE 1
🗺️

Mapping to Real World

State maps resource addresses (like aws_instance.web) to real resource IDs (like i-0abc123def).

PURPOSE 2
📋

Storing Metadata

State stores resource dependencies, allowing Terraform to determine the correct order for create/destroy operations.

PURPOSE 3
⚡

Performance

State caches resource attribute values, avoiding costly API calls to refresh every resource on every plan.

PURPOSE 4
👥

Team Sync

Remote state enables teams to share a single source of truth about infrastructure.

📝

EXAM TIP: Four Purposes of State

The exam frequently asks about why Terraform needs state. Remember the four purposes: Mapping (config to real resources), Metadata (dependencies), Performance (caching), and Syncing (team collaboration).

📖

13.1.2 State as Source of Truth

📊 State as the Source of Truth

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      TERRAFORM'S SOURCE OF TRUTH                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│    ┌──────────────────┐                      ┌──────────────────┐               │
│    │  Configuration   │                      │   Real World     │               │
│    │    (.tf files)   │                      │  Infrastructure  │               │
│    │                  │                      │                  │               │
│    │  resource "aws_  │                      │  EC2 Instance    │               │
│    │  instance" "web" │                      │  i-0abc123def    │               │
│    │  { ... }         │                      │  t3.micro        │               │
│    └────────┬─────────┘                      └────────┬─────────┘               │
│             │                                         │                          │
│             │    ┌─────────────────────────┐         │                          │
│             │    │    TERRAFORM STATE      │         │                          │
│             └───►│   (Source of Truth)     │◄────────┘                          │
│                  │                         │                                     │
│                  │  • Resource mappings    │                                     │
│                  │  • Current attributes   │                                     │
│                  │  • Dependencies         │                                     │
│                  │  • Provider info        │                                     │
│                  └─────────────────────────┘                                     │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              ▼                                                   │
│                  ┌─────────────────────────┐                                     │
│                  │    terraform plan       │                                     │
│                  │  Compares config ↔ state│                                     │
│                  │  to determine changes   │                                     │
│                  └─────────────────────────┘                                     │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

When you run terraform plan, Terraform compares your configuration against the state file to determine what changes are needed. The state file, not the cloud provider, is what Terraform considers the "current" state of your infrastructure.

⚖️

13.1.3 State vs Real Infrastructure

📋

State vs Reality Scenarios

Scenario State Says Reality Result
Normal Instance exists Instance exists ✅ No changes
Drift Instance type: t3.micro Instance type: t3.small ⚠️ Detected on refresh
Manual Delete Instance exists Instance deleted 🔄 Will recreate
Out of Band Create Instance not tracked Instance exists ❌ Unknown to Terraform
⚠️

Configuration Drift

If someone manually changes infrastructure outside of Terraform (e.g., via AWS Console), the state becomes "stale." Running terraform refresh or terraform plan -refresh=true updates state to match reality.

📄

13.1.4 State File Format (JSON)

terraform.tfstate (JSON Format) 
{
  "version": 4,
  "terraform_version": "1.7.0",
  "serial": 42,
  "lineage": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
  "outputs": {
    "instance_ip": {
      "value": "54.123.45.67",
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "resources": [
    {
      "mode": "managed",
      "type": "aws_instance",
      "name": "web",
      "provider": "provider[\"registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws\"]",
      "instances": [
        {
          "schema_version": 1,
          "attributes": {
            "id": "i-0abc123def456789",
            "ami": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0",
            "instance_type": "t3.micro",
            "tags": {
              "Name": "web-server"
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
💡

State File Format

State is stored as JSON, making it human-readable but should never be manually edited. Always use terraform state commands for modifications. The current format version is 4 (as of Terraform 0.12+).

13.2 State File Structure

🔍

Anatomy of terraform.tfstate

Understanding the internal structure of the state file helps you troubleshoot issues, understand Terraform's behavior, and work with state safely. While you should never manually edit state, knowing what each section contains is valuable knowledge.

📋

13.2.1 terraform.tfstate Anatomy

📊 State File Structure Overview

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        terraform.tfstate ANATOMY                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  {                                                                               │
│    "version": 4,                    ◄── State format version                     │
│    "terraform_version": "1.7.0",    ◄── Terraform version that wrote state       │
│    "serial": 42,                    ◄── Incremented on each state change         │
│    "lineage": "uuid-string",        ◄── Unique ID for this state's history       │
│                                                                                  │
│    "outputs": {                     ◄── Output values from configuration         │
│      "instance_ip": {                                                            │
│        "value": "54.123.45.67",                                                  │
│        "type": "string",                                                         │
│        "sensitive": false                                                        │
│      }                                                                           │
│    },                                                                            │
│                                                                                  │
│    "resources": [                   ◄── All managed resources                    │
│      {                                                                           │
│        "mode": "managed",           ◄── managed or data                          │
│        "type": "aws_instance",      ◄── Resource type                            │
│        "name": "web",               ◄── Resource name                            │
│        "provider": "...",           ◄── Provider address                         │
│        "instances": [...]           ◄── Resource instances                       │
│      }                                                                           │
│    ]                                                                             │
│  }                                                                               │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📌

13.2.2 Version Information

State Version Field 
{
  "version": 4
}

// State format versions:
// Version 1: Terraform 0.6.x and earlier
// Version 2: Terraform 0.7.x - 0.8.x
// Version 3: Terraform 0.9.x - 0.11.x
// Version 4: Terraform 0.12.x and later (current)
💡

State Format Version

Version 4 is the current state format, introduced in Terraform 0.12. Terraform automatically upgrades older state formats when needed. You cannot downgrade state versions.

🔢

13.2.3 Terraform Version in State

Terraform Version Field 
{
  "terraform_version": "1.7.0"
}

// This records which Terraform version last modified the state
// Used for compatibility warnings when using older/newer versions
⚠️

Version Compatibility

Running an older Terraform version on state created by a newer version may cause issues. Terraform 1.x can generally read state from older 1.x and 0.14+ versions, but downgrades aren't guaranteed to work.

🔄

13.2.4 Serial Number

Serial Number Field 
{
  "serial": 42
}

// Serial increments with EVERY state modification:
// - After terraform apply
// - After terraform state mv
// - After terraform state rm
// - After terraform import

// Example progression:
// Initial creation:  serial: 1
// First apply:       serial: 2
// Resource update:   serial: 3
// State mv command:  serial: 4
📝

EXAM TIP: Serial Number Purpose

The serial number prevents state conflicts in team environments. When using remote state with locking, Terraform uses the serial to detect if the state has been modified since it was read, preventing lost updates.

🔗

13.2.5 Lineage

Lineage Field 
{
  "lineage": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890"
}

// Lineage is a UUID generated when state is first created
// It NEVER changes for the lifetime of that state
// Used to prevent accidentally overwriting unrelated state

// Example: If you try to push state with different lineage
// to a remote backend, Terraform will refuse (safety check)

Lineage Protection: The lineage UUID ensures you don't accidentally overwrite state from a completely different Terraform project. If lineages don't match during terraform state push, you must use -force (dangerous!).

📦

13.2.6 Resources Array

Resources Array Structure 
{
  "resources": [
    {
      "mode": "managed",
      "type": "aws_instance",
      "name": "web",
      "provider": "provider[\"registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws\"]",
      "instances": [
        {
          "schema_version": 1,
          "attributes": {
            "id": "i-0abc123def456789",
            "ami": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0",
            "instance_type": "t3.micro",
            "availability_zone": "us-west-2a",
            "private_ip": "10.0.1.100",
            "public_ip": "54.123.45.67",
            "subnet_id": "subnet-12345678",
            "vpc_security_group_ids": ["sg-12345678"],
            "tags": {
              "Name": "web-server",
              "Environment": "production"
            },
            "tags_all": {
              "Name": "web-server",
              "Environment": "production"
            }
          },
          "sensitive_attributes": [],
          "private": "base64-encoded-provider-metadata",
          "dependencies": [
            "aws_subnet.main",
            "aws_security_group.web"
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "mode": "data",
      "type": "aws_ami",
      "name": "ubuntu",
      "provider": "provider[\"registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws\"]",
      "instances": [
        {
          "schema_version": 0,
          "attributes": {
            "id": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0",
            "name": "ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-focal-20.04-amd64-server-*"
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
📋

Resource Entry Fields

Field Description Example
mode managed (resources) or data (data sources) "managed"
type Resource type from provider "aws_instance"
name Local name in configuration "web"
provider Full provider address "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"
instances Array of resource instances (for count/for_each) [{...}]
dependencies Explicit and implicit dependencies ["aws_subnet.main"]
📤

13.2.7 Outputs in State

Outputs Section 
{
  "outputs": {
    "instance_ip": {
      "value": "54.123.45.67",
      "type": "string",
      "sensitive": false
    },
    "instance_ids": {
      "value": ["i-abc123", "i-def456", "i-ghi789"],
      "type": ["tuple", ["string", "string", "string"]],
      "sensitive": false
    },
    "database_password": {
      "value": "super-secret-password",
      "type": "string",
      "sensitive": true
    }
  }
}
⚠️

Sensitive Outputs in State

Even when marked as sensitive = true, output values are stored in plain text in the state file. The sensitive flag only affects display in terraform output and plan. This is why state file security is critical!

13.3 State File Location

📁

Where State Lives

By default, Terraform stores state locally in your working directory. Understanding where state files are located and how backup files work is essential for troubleshooting and disaster recovery scenarios.

📂

13.3.1 Default Local State

📊 Default Local State Location

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     TERRAFORM PROJECT DIRECTORY                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  my-terraform-project/                                                           │
│  │                                                                               │
│  ├── main.tf                    # Your configuration files                       │
│  ├── variables.tf               # Variable definitions                           │
│  ├── outputs.tf                 # Output definitions                             │
│  ├── terraform.tfvars           # Variable values                                │
│  │                                                                               │
│  ├── terraform.tfstate          # ◄── MAIN STATE FILE (auto-created)            │
│  ├── terraform.tfstate.backup   # ◄── PREVIOUS STATE (auto-created)             │
│  │                                                                               │
│  └── .terraform/                # ◄── TERRAFORM WORKING DIRECTORY               │
│      ├── providers/             #     Downloaded provider plugins                │
│      ├── modules/               #     Downloaded modules                         │
│      └── terraform.tfstate      #     Backend state (if configured)              │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Check State Location 
# List files in project directory
ls -la

# Output:
# -rw-r--r--  main.tf
# -rw-r--r--  variables.tf
# -rw-r--r--  terraform.tfstate         # State file
# -rw-r--r--  terraform.tfstate.backup  # Backup
# drwxr-xr-x  .terraform/               # Working directory
💾

13.3.2 terraform.tfstate.backup

🔄 Automatic Backup Behavior

Before writing a new state file, Terraform automatically copies the existing state to terraform.tfstate.backup. This provides a simple recovery mechanism for accidental state corruption.

Backup File Lifecycle 
# Before terraform apply:
# terraform.tfstate        → current state (serial: 5)
# terraform.tfstate.backup → previous state (serial: 4)

# After terraform apply:
# terraform.tfstate        → new state (serial: 6)
# terraform.tfstate.backup → previous state (serial: 5)

# Recovery from backup:
cp terraform.tfstate.backup terraform.tfstate

# Disable backup (not recommended):
terraform apply -backup=-
💡

Backup Limitations

The backup file only contains the previous state—not a full history. For comprehensive state versioning, use remote backends with versioning enabled (like S3 with versioning).

⚙️

13.3.3 State in .terraform Directory

.terraform Directory Contents 
# The .terraform directory structure:
.terraform/
├── providers/
│   └── registry.terraform.io/
│       └── hashicorp/
│           └── aws/
│               └── 5.0.0/
│                   └── linux_amd64/
│                       └── terraform-provider-aws_v5.0.0
├── modules/
│   └── modules.json              # Module source mappings
└── terraform.tfstate             # Backend configuration state

# This terraform.tfstate in .terraform/ stores:
# - Which backend is configured
# - Backend configuration (bucket, key, etc.)
# - NOT your actual infrastructure state!
⚠️

Don't Confuse These Files!

.terraform/terraform.tfstate is NOT your infrastructure state. It only stores backend configuration metadata. Your actual state is in terraform.tfstate (local) or your configured remote backend.

📋

State File Locations Compared

File Location Contains
terraform.tfstate Project root Actual infrastructure state
terraform.tfstate.backup Project root Previous infrastructure state
.terraform/terraform.tfstate .terraform folder Backend configuration only
Remote state S3, GCS, Azure, etc. Actual infrastructure state (remote)

13.4 Why State is Critical 📝 EXAM TOPIC

⚠️

Exam-Critical Knowledge

The HashiCorp Terraform certification exam heavily tests your understanding of why Terraform needs state. This section covers the four key reasons that state is essential: mapping, metadata, performance, and synchronization.

🗺️

13.4.1 Mapping Configuration to Real Resources

📊 Configuration to Resource Mapping

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATE: THE MAPPING LAYER                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│    CONFIGURATION                  STATE                    REAL WORLD           │
│    ═════════════                  ═════                    ══════════           │
│                                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐       │
│  │ resource "aws_   │      │ aws_instance.web │      │ EC2 Instance      │       │
│  │ instance" "web"  │─────►│ =                │─────►│ i-0abc123def      │       │
│  │ { ... }          │      │ i-0abc123def     │      │                    │       │
│  └──────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘       │
│                                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐       │
│  │ resource "aws_   │      │ aws_s3_bucket.   │      │ S3 Bucket         │       │
│  │ s3_bucket"       │─────►│ data =           │─────►│ my-data-bucket    │       │
│  │ "data" { ... }   │      │ my-data-bucket   │      │                    │       │
│  └──────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘       │
│                                                                                  │
│  Without state, Terraform can't know which real-world resource corresponds       │
│  to which resource block in your configuration!                                  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝

EXAM TIP: Why Mapping Matters

The exam may ask: "Why does Terraform need state?" or "What happens without state?" Key answer: Without state, Terraform cannot map configuration resource addresses to real-world resource IDs. It would have no way to know that aws_instance.web corresponds to i-0abc123def.

📋

13.4.2 Metadata Storage

📦 What Metadata State Stores

State stores critical metadata that Terraform needs to manage resources correctly, including dependencies that determine operation order.

METADATA
🔗

Dependencies

Both implicit (from references) and explicit (depends_on) dependencies are stored so Terraform can destroy resources in the correct order.

METADATA
🏷️

Resource Attributes

All computed attributes from the provider (IDs, ARNs, IPs, etc.) are stored for use in other resources and outputs.

METADATA
🔌

Provider Information

Which provider (and version) manages each resource, enabling multi-provider configurations.

METADATA
📐

Schema Version

Resource schema versions help providers handle schema upgrades across Terraform versions.

Dependency Tracking Example 
# In your configuration:
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "web" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id  # Implicit dependency
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
}

# In state, the subnet records its dependency:
# "dependencies": ["aws_vpc.main"]

# This ensures:
# CREATE: VPC first, then Subnet
# DESTROY: Subnet first, then VPC
⚡

13.4.3 Performance Optimization

📊 State Caching for Performance

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    WITHOUT STATE CACHE                                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   terraform plan                                                                 │
│        │                                                                         │
│        ▼                                                                         │
│   ┌─────────────┐    API Call    ┌─────────────┐                                │
│   │ Resource 1  │───────────────►│ AWS API     │  ⏱️ 500ms                       │
│   └─────────────┘                └─────────────┘                                │
│   ┌─────────────┐    API Call    ┌─────────────┐                                │
│   │ Resource 2  │───────────────►│ AWS API     │  ⏱️ 500ms                       │
│   └─────────────┘                └─────────────┘                                │
│   ...                                                                            │
│   ┌─────────────┐    API Call    ┌─────────────┐                                │
│   │ Resource N  │───────────────►│ AWS API     │  ⏱️ 500ms                       │
│   └─────────────┘                └─────────────┘                                │
│                                                                                  │
│   Total: N × 500ms = SLOW! (100 resources = 50 seconds just to plan)            │
│                                                                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                    WITH STATE CACHE                                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   terraform plan                                                                 │
│        │                                                                         │
│        ▼                                                                         │
│   ┌─────────────────────────┐                                                    │
│   │    Read State File      │  ⏱️ 10ms (local disk read)                         │
│   │    (All N resources)    │                                                    │
│   └─────────────────────────┘                                                    │
│                                                                                  │
│   Total: ~10ms = FAST! (regardless of resource count)                           │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡

Refresh Behavior

By default, terraform plan and apply refresh state by querying providers. Use -refresh=false to skip this for faster operations (but risk operating on stale data). In Terraform 1.10+, speculative plans use -refresh=false by default.

👥

13.4.4 Syncing Within Teams

📊 Team Synchronization via Remote State

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TEAM SYNCHRONIZATION                                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│    Developer A                                    Developer B                    │
│    ═══════════                                    ═══════════                    │
│                                                                                  │
