│  ┌───────────────┐                              ┌───────────────┐               │
│  │ terraform     │                              │ terraform     │               │
│  │ apply         │                              │ plan          │               │
│  └───────┬───────┘                              └───────┬───────┘               │
│          │                                              │                        │
│          │         ┌─────────────────────┐             │                        │
│          │         │    REMOTE STATE     │             │                        │
│          └────────►│    (S3 Bucket)      │◄────────────┘                        │
│                    │                     │                                       │
│                    │  • Single source    │                                       │
│                    │    of truth         │                                       │
│                    │  • Locked during    │                                       │
│                    │    operations       │                                       │
│                    │  • Versioned        │                                       │
│                    └─────────────────────┘                                       │
│                                                                                  │
│  Without remote state: Each developer has local state = conflicts!              │
│  With remote state: Everyone works from same state = synchronized!              │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝

EXAM TIP: Team Collaboration

The exam tests understanding of why remote state is necessary for teams. Key points: Local state doesn't sync between team members, leading to conflicts and duplicate resources. Remote state with locking ensures only one person can modify infrastructure at a time.

13.5 State Locking 📝 EXAM TOPIC

🔒

Preventing Concurrent Modifications

State locking prevents multiple users from modifying state simultaneously, which could lead to state corruption or conflicting infrastructure changes. This is a critical exam topic and essential for production Terraform usage.

🔐

13.5.1 What is State Locking?

🔒 State Locking Explained

State locking is a mechanism that prevents concurrent state modifications. When Terraform begins an operation that could modify state, it acquires a lock. The lock is released when the operation completes.

📊 State Locking in Action

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATE LOCKING MECHANISM                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  Time ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►    │
│                                                                                  │
│  User A: terraform apply                                                         │
│          │                                                                       │
│          ├── Acquire Lock ──────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│          │                                                          │            │
│          │   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │            │
│          │   │              LOCK HELD BY USER A                │   │            │
│          │   │                                                 │   │            │
│          │   │  User B: terraform apply                        │   │            │
│          │   │           │                                     │   │            │
│          │   │           └── ❌ BLOCKED! Lock already held     │   │            │
│          │   │               Error: state locked               │   │            │
│          │   │                                                 │   │            │
│          │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │            │
│          │                                                          │            │
│          └── Release Lock ──────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│          │                                                                       │
│          ▼                                                                       │
│          Complete                                                                │
│                                                                                  │
│  User B: Can now acquire lock and proceed                                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Lock Error Example 
# When someone else holds the lock:
$ terraform apply

Error: Error acquiring the state lock

Error message: ConditionalCheckFailedException: The conditional request failed
Lock Info:
  ID:        a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
  Path:      terraform-state/project/terraform.tfstate
  Operation: OperationTypeApply
  Who:       alice@company.com
  Version:   1.7.0
  Created:   2024-03-15 10:30:00.000000000 +0000 UTC
  Info:      

Terraform acquires a state lock to protect the state from being
written by multiple users at the same time. Please resolve the
issue above and try again.
📋

13.5.2 Lock Information

Lock Information Fields 
// Lock information stored in DynamoDB (S3 backend):
{
  "LockID": "terraform-state/project/terraform.tfstate",
  "Info": {
    "ID": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
    "Operation": "OperationTypeApply",
    "Who": "alice@company.com",
    "Version": "1.7.0",
    "Created": "2024-03-15T10:30:00Z",
    "Path": "terraform-state/project/terraform.tfstate"
  }
}
📋

Lock Information Fields Explained

Field Description
ID Unique identifier for this lock instance
Operation What operation acquired the lock (Apply, Plan, etc.)
Who User@hostname who holds the lock
Version Terraform version in use
Created When the lock was acquired
Path Path to the state file being locked
🔓

13.5.3 Force Unlock

Force Unlock Command 
# Force release a stuck lock (use with caution!)
terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID

# Example:
terraform force-unlock a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

# You'll see a confirmation prompt:
# Do you really want to force-unlock?
#   Terraform will remove the lock on the remote state.
#   This will allow local Terraform commands to modify this state,
#   even though it may still be in use.
#
#   Only 'yes' will be accepted to confirm.
#
#   Enter a value: yes

# To skip confirmation (for automation - dangerous!):
terraform force-unlock -force LOCK_ID
⚠️

When to Use Force Unlock

Only use force-unlock when you are certain no Terraform process is running. Common scenarios:

  • CI/CD pipeline crashed during apply
  • User's machine lost network during operation
  • Terraform process was killed (Ctrl+C during critical operation)

Never force-unlock if someone is actively running Terraform!

🗄️

13.5.4 Backends with Locking Support

📋

Backend Locking Support

Backend Locking Lock Mechanism
local ✅ Yes File system lock (.terraform.tfstate.lock.info)
s3 ✅ Yes* DynamoDB table (use_lockfile in 1.10+)
azurerm ✅ Yes Azure Blob lease
gcs ✅ Yes GCS object locking
consul ✅ Yes Consul sessions
cloud (HCP) ✅ Yes HCP Terraform built-in
http ⚠️ Optional Depends on server implementation
pg ✅ Yes PostgreSQL advisory locks
📝

EXAM TIP: S3 Backend Locking

The exam may ask about S3 backend locking. Key point: S3 backend requires a separate DynamoDB table for locking (configured via dynamodb_table argument). S3 itself doesn't provide locking—DynamoDB does. As of Terraform 1.10+, you can use use_lockfile = true for S3-native locking.

S3 Backend with DynamoDB Locking 
# S3 backend with DynamoDB locking
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "project/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-west-2"
    encrypt        = true
    
    # DynamoDB table for locking
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
  }
}

# The DynamoDB table must have:
# - Primary key named "LockID" (String type)
# - Created before terraform init

13.6 State File Security

🛡️

Protecting Sensitive Data

State files contain sensitive information including resource IDs, secrets, passwords, and API keys. Protecting state is critical for security and compliance.

⚠️

13.6.1 Sensitive Data in State

⚠️

CRITICAL: State Contains Secrets!

Even if you mark variables as sensitive = true, the actual values are stored in plain text in the state file. Terraform redacts values in console output, but NOT in state.

📊 What Sensitive Data Lives in State

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SENSITIVE DATA IN STATE                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                         STATE FILE (terraform.tfstate)                  │    │
│  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    │
│  │                                                                         │    │
│  │  🔑 DATABASE PASSWORDS                                                  │    │
│  │     aws_db_instance.main: "password": "MySecretP@ss123"               │    │
│  │                                                                         │    │
│  │  🔑 API KEYS                                                            │    │
│  │     provider "datadog": "api_key": "abc123def456..."                    │    │
│  │                                                                         │    │
│  │  🔑 TLS PRIVATE KEYS                                                    │    │
│  │     tls_private_key.main: "private_key_pem": "-----BEGIN RSA..."       │    │
│  │                                                                         │    │
│  │  🔑 ACCESS CREDENTIALS                                                  │    │
│  │     aws_iam_access_key.admin: "secret": "wJalrXUtnFEMI..."             │    │
│  │                                                                         │    │
│  │  🔑 SENSITIVE OUTPUTS                                                   │    │
│  │     outputs.admin_password: "value": "admin123"                         │    │
│  │                                                                         │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                                  │
│  ALL of this is in PLAIN TEXT unless you encrypt at rest!                       │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔐

13.6.2 Encryption at Rest

AWS
🔐

S3 Server-Side Encryption

encrypt = true

Uses AES-256 encryption via KMS

AZURE
🔐

Azure Storage Encryption

Automatically encrypted at rest

GCP
🔐

GCS Encryption

encryption_key

Default or customer-managed keys

HCP
🔐

HCP Terraform

Encrypted by default at rest and in transit

S3 Backend with KMS Encryption 
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "project/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-west-2"
    
    # Enable server-side encryption
    encrypt        = true
    
    # Use custom KMS key (optional, defaults to aws/s3)
    kms_key_id     = "arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:123456789:key/mrk-abc123"
    
    # Locking
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
  }
}
🚫

13.6.3 Never Commit to Version Control

🚨

CRITICAL: Never Commit State Files!

State files should never be committed to Git or any version control system. They contain secrets in plain text and will be exposed in your repository history forever.

Essential .gitignore for Terraform 
# Terraform state files
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*
*.tfstate.backup
.terraform.tfstate.lock.info

# Terraform directory (providers, modules)
.terraform/

# Sensitive variable files
*.tfvars
!example.tfvars
terraform.tfvars

# Crash logs
crash.log
crash.*.log

# Override files
override.tf
override.tf.json
*_override.tf
*_override.tf.json

# CLI configuration
.terraformrc
terraform.rc
🔒

13.6.4 Access Control Best Practices

🛡️

State Security Best Practices

Practice Implementation
Use Remote State Store state in remote backends (S3, Azure, GCS, HCP), never locally in production
Enable Encryption Always enable encryption at rest with encrypt = true
Enable Versioning Enable bucket versioning to recover from corruption/mistakes
Restrict Access IAM policies to limit who can read/write state
Enable Logging CloudTrail/audit logging to track state access
Use Separate States One state per environment (dev/staging/prod)
S3 Bucket Policy for State Security 
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "EnforceTLS",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "s3:*",
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-terraform-state",
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-terraform-state/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "Bool": {
          "aws:SecureTransport": "false"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "EnforceEncryption",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "s3:PutObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-terraform-state/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringNotEquals": {
          "s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption": "aws:kms"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

13.7 Inspecting State

🔍

Examining State Safely

Terraform provides several commands to inspect state without modifying it. These commands are essential for debugging, auditing, and understanding your infrastructure.

📋

13.7.1 terraform state list

List All Resources in State 
# List all resources
$ terraform state list
aws_instance.web
aws_s3_bucket.logs
aws_security_group.web_sg
aws_vpc.main

# Filter by resource type
$ terraform state list aws_instance.*
aws_instance.web

# List resources in a module
$ terraform state list module.vpc
module.vpc.aws_vpc.this
module.vpc.aws_subnet.public[0]
module.vpc.aws_subnet.public[1]

# List resources with ID filtering
$ terraform state list -id=i-0abc123def456
aws_instance.web
🔎

13.7.2 terraform state show

Show Detailed Resource State 
# Show single resource details
$ terraform state show aws_instance.web

# Sample output:
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
    ami                          = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
    arn                          = "arn:aws:ec2:us-west-2:123456789:instance/i-0abc123"
    associate_public_ip_address  = true
    availability_zone            = "us-west-2a"
    cpu_core_count               = 1
    cpu_threads_per_core         = 1
    disable_api_termination      = false
    ebs_optimized                = false
    get_password_data            = false
    hibernation                  = false
    id                           = "i-0abc123def456"
    instance_state               = "running"
    instance_type                = "t3.micro"
    monitoring                   = false
    primary_network_interface_id = "eni-0123456789abcdef"
    private_dns                  = "ip-10-0-1-50.us-west-2.compute.internal"
    private_ip                   = "10.0.1.50"
    public_dns                   = "ec2-54-123-45-67.us-west-2.compute.amazonaws.com"
    public_ip                    = "54.123.45.67"
    security_groups              = []
    source_dest_check            = true
    subnet_id                    = "subnet-0abc123"
    tags                         = {
        "Name" = "web-server"
    }
    tenancy                      = "default"
    vpc_security_group_ids       = [
        "sg-0abc123",
    ]
}
📊

13.7.3 terraform show

Show Entire State or Plan 
# Show human-readable state
$ terraform show

# Show JSON output (useful for scripting)
$ terraform show -json

# Show a saved plan file
$ terraform show plan.tfplan

# Show plan as JSON
$ terraform show -json plan.tfplan

# Extract specific values with jq
$ terraform show -json | jq '.values.root_module.resources[]'
💡

terraform show vs terraform state show

  • terraform show - Shows the entire state or a plan file
  • terraform state show <resource> - Shows a single resource
🖥️

13.7.4 terraform console

🖥️ Interactive State Exploration

terraform console provides an interactive shell to query state using Terraform expressions and functions.

Interactive Console Examples 
# Start interactive console
$ terraform console

# Query a resource attribute
> aws_instance.web.public_ip
"54.123.45.67"

# Query all instance IDs
> aws_instance.web[*].id
["i-0abc123", "i-0def456", "i-0ghi789"]

# Use functions
> length(aws_instance.web)
3

# Check conditionals
> aws_instance.web.instance_type == "t3.micro"
true

# Query module outputs
> module.vpc.vpc_id
"vpc-0abc123"

# Exit console
> exit
📝

EXAM TIP: State Inspection Commands

Know the difference between state inspection commands:

  • terraform state list - List resource addresses
  • terraform state show <resource> - Show single resource
  • terraform show - Show entire state or plan
  • terraform console - Interactive expression evaluation

📋 Chapter 13 Summary: Terraform State Fundamentals

💾

State Purpose

  • Maps configuration to real-world resources
  • Stores metadata and dependency info
  • Optimizes performance by caching
  • Enables team synchronization
📁

State Structure

  • JSON format in terraform.tfstate
  • Contains version, serial, lineage
  • Resources array with all managed objects
  • Outputs section for exported values
🔒

State Locking

  • Prevents concurrent modifications
  • Automatic with supported backends
  • -lock=false to disable (risky)
  • force-unlock for stuck locks
🛡️

State Security

  • Contains sensitive data in plain text
  • Never commit to version control
  • Always encrypt at rest
  • Restrict access permissions
☁️ 🔧 🔗 🛡️
14

Remote State & Backends

Store, Secure, and Share Infrastructure State

8 Sections
36+ Subsections
12+ Exam Tips

Chapter 14Remote State & Backends

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Understand why remote state is essential for team workflows
2 Configure backend blocks for remote state storage
3 Set up S3 backend with DynamoDB locking (AWS)
4 Configure cloud backend for HCP Terraform
5 Compare and select appropriate backend options
6 Implement partial backend configuration for security
7 Migrate state between different backends safely

📑 In This Chapter

14.1 Why Remote State? 14.2 Backend Configuration 14.3 S3 Backend (AWS) 14.4 Cloud Backend (HCP) 14.5 Other Backend Options 14.6 Backend Authentication 14.7 Migrating State 14.8 Partial Configuration

14.1 Why Remote State?

☁️

Beyond Local State

By default, Terraform stores state locally as terraform.tfstate in your working directory. This works fine when you're learning or working alone on small projects. But the moment a second person touches that infrastructure—or you need disaster recovery—local state becomes a serious liability.

The Problems With Local State:
• No collaboration — Each developer has their own state file; they'll overwrite each other's changes
• No locking — Two people running apply simultaneously can corrupt infrastructure
• Security risk — State contains secrets (passwords, keys) in plain text on developer laptops
• Single point of failure — If the laptop dies, so does your only record of what Terraform manages

Remote state solves all of these by storing state in a secure, shared location with built-in locking and versioning. For any production use of Terraform, remote state isn't optional—it's essential.

👥

14.1.1 Team Collaboration

📊 Local vs Remote State for Teams

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    LOCAL STATE CHAOS                                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Developer A                Developer B                Developer C              │
│   ═══════════                ═══════════                ═══════════              │
│   ┌─────────┐               ┌─────────┐               ┌─────────┐               │
│   │ State A │               │ State B │               │ State C │               │
│   │ (local) │               │ (local) │               │ (local) │               │
│   └────┬────┘               └────┬────┘               └────┬────┘               │
│        │                         │                         │                     │
│        ▼                         ▼                         ▼                     │
│   terraform                 terraform                 terraform                  │
│   apply                     apply                     apply                      │
│        │                         │                         │                     │
│        └─────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘                     │
│                                  │                                               │
│                                  ▼                                               │
│                         ┌───────────────┐                                        │
│                         │   AWS/Azure   │                                        │
│                         │   💥 CHAOS!   │                                        │
│                         │ Conflicting   │                                        │
│                         │   changes     │                                        │
│                         └───────────────┘                                        │
│                                                                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                    REMOTE STATE ORDER                                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Developer A    Developer B    Developer C                                      │
│        │              │              │                                           │
│        └──────────────┼──────────────┘                                           │
│                       │                                                          │
│                       ▼                                                          │
│              ┌─────────────────┐                                                 │
│              │  Remote State   │  ← Single source of truth                       │
│              │  (S3 / HCP)     │  ← Locking prevents conflicts                   │
│              │    🔒 Locked    │  ← Encrypted at rest                            │
│              └────────┬────────┘                                                 │
│                       │                                                          │
│                       ▼                                                          │
│              ┌───────────────┐                                                   │
│              │   AWS/Azure   │                                                   │
│              │   ✅ ORDER!   │                                                   │
│              └───────────────┘                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔒

14.1.2 State Locking

🔒 Automatic State Locking

Remote backends that support locking automatically prevent concurrent modifications. When one user runs terraform apply, others are blocked until the operation completes.

AWS
🗄️

S3 + DynamoDB

DynamoDB provides consistent locking for S3-stored state

AZURE
📦

Azure Blob Lease

Built-in blob lease mechanism for locking

GCP
🪣

GCS Locking

Native GCS object generation for consistency

HCP
☁️

HCP Terraform

Built-in run queuing and state locking

🛡️

14.1.3 Security Benefits

🛡️

Remote State Security Advantages

Security Feature Local State Remote State
Encryption at Rest ❌ Plain text on disk ✅ AES-256 / KMS encryption
Encryption in Transit N/A ✅ TLS/HTTPS
Access Control ❌ File system permissions only ✅ IAM/RBAC policies
Audit Logging ❌ None ✅ CloudTrail, etc.
Versioning ❌ Manual backups ✅ Automatic versioning
Disaster Recovery ❌ Laptop loss = state loss ✅ Replicated, backed up
📤

14.1.4 Remote State Data Source

📤 Sharing State Between Configurations

The terraform_remote_state data source allows one Terraform configuration to read outputs from another, enabling infrastructure composition across teams.

Remote State Data Source Example 
# networking/outputs.tf - Team A manages networking
output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

# application/main.tf - Team B consumes networking outputs
data "terraform_remote_state" "networking" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "terraform-state"
    key    = "networking/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-west-2"
  }
}

# Use outputs from the networking state
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  # Reference values from remote state
  subnet_id     = data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.private_subnet_ids[0]
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.app.id]
}

resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  vpc_id = data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.vpc_id
  # ...
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Remote State Data Source

The exam may ask about sharing data between configurations. Key points:

  • terraform_remote_state reads another state's outputs only
  • It does NOT give access to internal resources
  • The source state must have defined outputs
  • Requires read access to the remote backend

14.2 Backend Configuration

⚙️

Configuring State Storage

The backend block inside terraform { } tells Terraform where and how to store its state file. Without a backend configuration, Terraform defaults to local storage. With a backend, state goes to S3, Azure Blob, GCS, HCP Terraform, or another supported location.

Critical Constraint: Backend configuration cannot use variables, expressions, or interpolations. Everything must be hardcoded values (or provided via -backend-config at init time). This is because the backend must be configured before Terraform can interpret any configuration or variables.

Initialization Required: Every time you change backend settings, you must run terraform init again. For major changes (switching backend types or destinations), use terraform init -migrate-state to move your state to the new location without losing it.

📝

14.2.1 backend Block Syntax

Backend Block Structure 
terraform {
  # Backend configuration
  backend "<BACKEND_TYPE>" {
    # Backend-specific arguments
    <argument1> = <value1>
    <argument2> = <value2>
    # ...
  }
  
  # Other terraform settings
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

# Example: S3 backend
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-west-2"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}
⚠️

Backend Block Restrictions

  • Only ONE backend block is allowed per configuration
  • Backend configuration cannot use variables, locals, or functions
  • Backend type cannot be changed without state migration
  • Backend configuration is evaluated during terraform init
🚀

14.2.2 Backend Initialization

Initializing a Backend 
# First time initialization
$ terraform init

Initializing the backend...

Successfully configured the backend "s3"! Terraform will automatically
use this backend unless the backend configuration changes.

# Re-initialize after backend changes
$ terraform init -reconfigure

# Migrate existing state to new backend
$ terraform init -migrate-state

# Upgrade providers and modules
$ terraform init -upgrade

📊 terraform init Workflow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    terraform init WORKFLOW                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   terraform init                                                                 │
│        │                                                                         │
│        ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│        │                                                                      │  │
│        ▼                                                                      │  │
│   ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌────────────┐ │  │
│   │ 1. Parse    │────►│ 2. Backend  │────►│ 3. Provider │────►│ 4. Module  │ │  │
│   │    Config   │     │    Init     │     │    Install  │     │   Install  │ │  │
│   └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └────────────┘ │  │
│                             │                                                 │  │
│                             ▼                                                 │  │
│                    ┌─────────────────┐                                        │  │
│                    │ Backend exists? │                                        │  │
│                    └────────┬────────┘                                        │  │
│                             │                                                 │  │
│              ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐                                  │  │
│              │                              │                                  │  │
│              ▼                              ▼                                  │  │
│        First Init                  Config Changed                             │  │
│        ┌──────────┐              ┌──────────────┐                             │  │
│        │ Create   │              │ Migrate or   │                             │  │
│        │ workspace│              │ Reconfigure? │                             │  │
│        └──────────┘              └──────────────┘                             │  │
│                                                                               │  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧

14.2.3 Partial Configuration

🔧 Why Partial Configuration?

Partial backend configuration keeps sensitive values out of source code. Values can be provided via CLI flags, config files, or interactively during terraform init.

Partial Configuration in Code 
# main.tf - Partial backend (no sensitive values)
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    # These are provided at init time
    # bucket = provided via -backend-config
    # key    = provided via -backend-config
    # region = provided via -backend-config
    
    # These can be in code (not sensitive)
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}
📂

14.2.4 Backend Configuration Variables

⚠️

Variables NOT Allowed in Backend Blocks!

Backend configuration is evaluated before Terraform loads variables, so you cannot use var.*, local.*, or any functions in backend blocks. Use partial configuration instead.

❌ This Will NOT Work 
# ❌ INVALID - Variables cannot be used in backend blocks!
variable "bucket_name" {
  default = "my-state-bucket"
}

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = var.bucket_name  # ❌ ERROR!
    key    = "terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-west-2"
  }
}
✅ Correct Approach: Partial Config 
# Use -backend-config flag instead
$ terraform init \
    -backend-config="bucket=my-state-bucket" \
    -backend-config="key=prod/terraform.tfstate" \
    -backend-config="region=us-west-2"

# Or use a config file
$ terraform init -backend-config=backend.hcl

14.3 S3 Backend (AWS) - Deep Dive

🪣

The Most Popular Backend

The S3 backend is the gold standard for AWS shops—it stores your state file in an S3 bucket with optional encryption (KMS), versioning (for rollback), and locking (via DynamoDB). This section walks through the complete production-ready setup.

The Chicken-and-Egg Problem: You need an S3 bucket to store Terraform state, but the bucket itself should be provisioned by Terraform. The standard solution is a "bootstrap" project: a small Terraform config with local state that creates the bucket, then you migrate your main project's state into it. Some teams create the bucket manually via CLI first.

Key Components:
• S3 bucket — Stores the state file with versioning enabled (for recovery)
• DynamoDB table — Provides state locking (prevents concurrent applies)
• KMS key (optional) — Encrypts state at rest with customer-managed keys
• IAM policies — Control who can read/write state

🪣

14.3.1 S3 Bucket Configuration

Creating the S3 Bucket (Terraform) 
# bootstrap/main.tf - Create state bucket (run first!)
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "terraform_state" {
  bucket = "my-company-terraform-state-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "Terraform State"
    Environment = "management"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform-bootstrap"
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "terraform_state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"  # CRITICAL: Enable versioning!
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "terraform_state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id
  
  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.terraform_state.arn
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "terraform_state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}
🗄️

14.3.2 DynamoDB Table for Locking

📝

EXAM TIP: DynamoDB Locking

The exam frequently asks about S3 backend locking. Key points:

  • S3 alone does NOT provide state locking
  • DynamoDB is required for locking with S3 backend
  • The table must have a primary key named LockID (String)
  • Configure via dynamodb_table argument
Creating DynamoDB Lock Table 
# bootstrap/dynamodb.tf
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "terraform_locks" {
  name         = "terraform-state-locks"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"  # No capacity planning needed
  hash_key     = "LockID"           # MUST be "LockID"

  attribute {
    name = "LockID"
    type = "S"  # String type
  }

  tags = {
    Name        = "Terraform State Locks"
    Environment = "management"
  }
}

# Output for use in backend config
output "dynamodb_table_name" {
  value = aws_dynamodb_table.terraform_locks.name
}

📊 S3 + DynamoDB Architecture

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    S3 BACKEND WITH DYNAMODB LOCKING                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                        terraform apply                                           │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              ▼                                                   │
│        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│        │                1. Acquire Lock                       │                  │
│        └───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                │                                                 │
│                                ▼                                                 │
│               ┌────────────────────────────────┐                                │
│               │         DynamoDB Table         │                                │
│               │   ┌────────────────────────┐   │                                │
│               │   │ LockID: s3://bucket/   │   │                                │
│               │   │         key.tfstate    │   │                                │
│               │   │ Who: alice@company.com │   │                                │
│               │   │ Created: 2024-03-15... │   │                                │
│               │   └────────────────────────┘   │                                │
│               └────────────────────────────────┘                                │
│                                │                                                 │
│                                │ Lock acquired                                   │
│                                ▼                                                 │
│        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│        │           2. Read Current State                      │                  │
│        └───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                │                                                 │
│                                ▼                                                 │
│               ┌────────────────────────────────┐                                │
│               │           S3 Bucket            │                                │
│               │  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │                                │
│               │  │ project/terraform.tfstate│  │                                │
│               │  │ (encrypted with KMS)     │  │                                │
│               │  └──────────────────────────┘  │                                │
│               └────────────────────────────────┘                                │
│                                │                                                 │
│                                │ ... apply changes to infrastructure...          │
│                                │                                                 │
│                                ▼                                                 │
│        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│        │           3. Write Updated State                     │                  │
│        │           4. Release Lock                            │                  │
│        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔑

14.3.3 IAM Permissions Required

Minimum IAM Policy for S3 Backend 
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "S3StateAccess",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject",
        "s3:ListBucket"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-terraform-state",
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-terraform-state/*"
      ]
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DynamoDBLocking",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "dynamodb:GetItem",
        "dynamodb:PutItem",
        "dynamodb:DeleteItem"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:*:*:table/terraform-state-locks"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "KMSDecrypt",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "kms:Decrypt",
        "kms:GenerateDataKey"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:kms:*:*:key/YOUR_KMS_KEY_ID"
    }
  ]
}
🔐

14.3.4 Encryption Configuration

S3 Backend with KMS Encryption 
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-west-2"
    
    # Enable encryption (uses default aws/s3 key)
    encrypt        = true
    
    # Use custom KMS key (recommended for production)
    kms_key_id     = "arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:123456789:key/mrk-abc123..."
    
    # Locking
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-locks"
  }
}
📚

14.3.5 Versioning Best Practices

MUST
✅

Enable Versioning

Always enable S3 versioning to recover from corrupted or accidentally deleted state

RECOMMENDED
♻️

Lifecycle Rules

Configure lifecycle rules to expire old versions after 90+ days

OPTIONAL
🔄

Cross-Region Replication

Replicate state to another region for disaster recovery

ADVANCED
🔒

Object Lock

Enable object lock for compliance (prevents deletion)

🏢

14.3.6 Cross-Account State Access

Assume Role for Cross-Account Access 
# State bucket in Account A (123456789012)
# Terraform running in Account B (987654321098)

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "central-terraform-state"
    key            = "project-x/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-west-2"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    
    # Assume role in Account A to access state
    role_arn       = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformStateAccess"
    
    # Optional: External ID for additional security
    external_id    = "terraform-project-x"
  }
}
📋

14.3.7 Complete S3 Backend Example

Production-Ready S3 Backend 
# versions.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
  
  backend "s3" {
    # State storage
    bucket = "mycompany-terraform-state-us-west-2"
    key    = "prod/networking/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-west-2"
    
    # Security
    encrypt    = true
    kms_key_id = "alias/terraform-state-key"
    
    # Locking
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-locks"
    
    # Cross-account (if needed)
    # role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformAccess"
    
    # Workspace prefix (if using workspaces)
    workspace_key_prefix = "workspaces"
  }
  
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

14.4 Cloud Backend (HCP Terraform)

☁️

HashiCorp's Managed Solution

HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud) is HashiCorp's managed platform for Terraform. Instead of setting up your own S3 bucket, DynamoDB table, and encryption—HCP handles all of that for you, plus provides a web UI, team management, and remote execution.

Why Choose HCP Over S3?
• Zero infrastructure setup — No buckets, tables, or IAM policies to manage
• Built-in UI — View state, run history, and team activity in a dashboard
• Remote execution — Plans and applies run in HCP's infrastructure, not locally
• Policy as code — Sentinel/OPA policies can gate deployments
• Free tier available — Small teams can use it at no cost

The cloud Block: Modern versions of Terraform use the cloud block (instead of the older backend "remote"). It connects directly to HCP Terraform and supports workspace tags for dynamic workspace selection.

📝

14.4.1 cloud Block Syntax

Cloud Block Configuration 
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-organization"
    hostname     = "app.terraform.io"  # Default, can be omitted
    
    workspaces {
      name = "my-workspace"
    }
  }
  
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
}
💡

cloud vs backend "remote"

The cloud block (introduced Terraform 1.1) is the modern replacement for backend "remote". It offers better workspace management and is the recommended approach for HCP Terraform integration.

🏢

14.4.2 Organization and Workspace

📊 HCP Terraform Structure

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HCP TERRAFORM HIERARCHY                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │                        ORGANIZATION                                      │   │
│   │                        "my-company"                                      │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │   ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐        │   │
│   │   │   WORKSPACE     │  │   WORKSPACE     │  │   WORKSPACE     │        │   │
│   │   │   "app-dev"     │  │   "app-staging" │  │   "app-prod"    │        │   │
│   │   ├─────────────────┤  ├─────────────────┤  ├─────────────────┤        │   │
│   │   │ • State         │  │ • State         │  │ • State         │        │   │
│   │   │ • Variables     │  │ • Variables     │  │ • Variables     │        │   │
│   │   │ • Run History   │  │ • Run History   │  │ • Run History   │        │   │
│   │   │ • Team Access   │  │ • Team Access   │  │ • Team Access   │        │   │
│   │   └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘        │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │   ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐                              │   │
│   │   │   WORKSPACE     │  │   WORKSPACE     │                              │   │
│   │   │   "network-dev" │  │   "network-prod"│                              │   │
│   │   └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘                              │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🏷️

14.4.3 Tags-Based Workspace Selection

Dynamic Workspace Selection with Tags 
# Use tags instead of explicit name
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-company"
    
    workspaces {
      # Select workspaces by tags
      tags = ["app:web-api", "env:production"]
    }
  }
}

# Then use terraform workspace commands:
# terraform workspace list     - List matching workspaces
# terraform workspace select   - Choose a workspace
📝

EXAM TIP: Workspace Selection

You can use name for a single workspace OR tags for multiple workspaces, but not both. Tags enable the use of terraform workspace commands with HCP Terraform.

🔄

14.4.4 Migration from backend to cloud

Migrating to cloud Block 
# 1. Update configuration from:
# terraform {
#   backend "s3" { ... }
# }

# To:
# terraform {
#   cloud { ... }
# }

# 2. Run init with migration
$ terraform init -migrate-state

Initializing HCP Terraform...
Do you want to copy existing state to HCP Terraform?
  Enter "yes" to copy and "no" to start with empty state.

  Enter a value: yes

# State is now in HCP Terraform!

14.5 Other Backend Options

🗄️

Beyond S3 and HCP

Terraform supports many backend options for different cloud providers and use cases. Choose based on your infrastructure platform and requirements.

🔷

14.5.1 Azure Storage (azurerm)

Azure Blob Storage Backend 
terraform {
  backend "azurerm" {
    resource_group_name  = "terraform-state-rg"
    storage_account_name = "tfstateaccount"
    container_name       = "tfstate"
    key                  = "prod.terraform.tfstate"
    
    # Authentication options:
    # 1. Azure CLI (az login) - automatic
    # 2. Service Principal
    # client_id       = "..."
    # client_secret   = "..."
    # tenant_id       = "..."
    # subscription_id = "..."
    
    # 3. Managed Identity (on Azure VMs/AKS)
    # use_msi = true
  }
}
🟡

14.5.2 Google Cloud Storage (gcs)

GCS Backend 
terraform {
  backend "gcs" {
    bucket      = "my-terraform-state"
    prefix      = "prod"
    
    # Authentication:
    # 1. GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS env var
    # 2. User credentials (gcloud auth application-default login)
    # 3. Workload Identity (on GKE)
    
    # Optional encryption with customer-managed key
    # encryption_key = "..."
  }
}
🔗

14.5.3 Consul

HashiCorp Consul Backend 
terraform {
  backend "consul" {
    address = "consul.example.com:8500"
    scheme  = "https"
    path    = "terraform/state/myproject"
    
    # Authentication
    access_token = "..."  # Or use CONSUL_HTTP_TOKEN env var
    
    # TLS
    ca_file   = "/path/to/ca.crt"
    cert_file = "/path/to/client.crt"
    key_file  = "/path/to/client.key"
    
    # Locking (default: enabled via Consul sessions)
    lock = true
  }
}
🐘

14.5.4 PostgreSQL (pg)

PostgreSQL Backend 
terraform {
  backend "pg" {
    conn_str    = "postgres://user:pass@dbhost/terraform_state?sslmode=require"
    schema_name = "terraform_remote_state"
    
    # Or use DATABASE_URL env var
  }
}

# The pg backend automatically creates a table named "states"
# and supports locking via PostgreSQL advisory locks
☸️

14.5.5 Kubernetes Secret

Kubernetes Secret Backend 
terraform {
  backend "kubernetes" {
    secret_suffix    = "my-project"
    namespace        = "terraform"
    
    # Uses in-cluster config or kubeconfig file
    # config_path = "~/.kube/config"
    # config_context = "my-cluster"
    
    # Labels for the secret
    labels = {
      "app" = "terraform"
    }
  }
}

# State stored in Secret: tfstate-default-my-project
🌐

14.5.6 HTTP Backend

HTTP Backend (Custom Server) 
terraform {
  backend "http" {
    address        = "https://state.example.com/terraform/state"
    lock_address   = "https://state.example.com/terraform/lock"
    unlock_address = "https://state.example.com/terraform/unlock"
    
    # Authentication
    username = "terraform"
    password = "secret"  # Or use TF_HTTP_PASSWORD env var
    
    # Or use client certificates
    # client_certificate_pem = "..."
    # client_private_key_pem = "..."
    
    # Retry configuration
    retry_max      = 4
    retry_wait_min = 1
    retry_wait_max = 30
  }
}
💻

14.5.7 Local Backend (Enhanced)

Local Backend Configuration 
# Explicit local backend (optional, this is default)
terraform {
  backend "local" {
    path = "relative/path/to/terraform.tfstate"
    
    # Or use workspace_dir for multiple workspaces
    workspace_dir = ".terraform/workspace-states"
  }
}

# By default (without any backend block):
# - State: ./terraform.tfstate
# - Backup: ./terraform.tfstate.backup
# - Lock: ./.terraform.tfstate.lock.info
⚠️

Local Backend Limitations

  • No team collaboration (state on one machine)
  • No encryption at rest
  • Easy to lose (laptop theft/failure)
  • Only suitable for learning, not production

14.6 Backend Authentication

🔑

Secure Access to State

Your state file contains sensitive information—database passwords, API keys, and the complete blueprint of your infrastructure. This means backend authentication isn't just about making Terraform work—it's about security.

Authentication Methods Vary by Backend: Each backend supports different authentication mechanisms. S3 can use IAM roles, access keys, or assume-role. HCP Terraform uses API tokens. Azure uses service principals or managed identity. Understanding what works where—and what takes precedence—prevents frustrating authentication failures.

Best Practice: Never Hardcode Credentials. Use environment variables, IAM roles, or separate credential files. Backend blocks cannot use variables, so credentials often come from the environment or -backend-config flags during init.

🔐

14.6.1 Authentication Methods by Backend

🔑

Backend Authentication Methods

Backend Authentication Methods
s3 IAM Role, Access Keys, Instance Profile, SSO, Web Identity
azurerm Azure CLI, Service Principal, Managed Identity, OIDC
gcs Service Account, User (gcloud), Workload Identity
cloud API Token (TF_TOKEN_*), terraform login
consul ACL Token, TLS Client Certificate
http Basic Auth, Client Certificate
📊

14.6.2 Credential Precedence

📊 AWS Credential Precedence (S3 Backend)

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    AWS CREDENTIAL PRECEDENCE                                     │
│                    (Highest to Lowest Priority)                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   1. ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│      │ Backend Config (NOT recommended - hardcoded)                   │         │
│      │ access_key = "..." / secret_key = "..."                        │         │
│      └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              ▼                                                   │
│   2. ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│      │ Environment Variables                                          │         │
│      │ AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY                      │         │
│      └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              ▼                                                   │
│   3. ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│      │ Shared Credentials File                                        │         │
│      │ ~/.aws/credentials [default] or [profile]                      │         │
│      └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              ▼                                                   │
│   4. ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│      │ Shared Config File                                             │         │
│      │ ~/.aws/config (with role_arn for AssumeRole)                   │         │
│      └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              ▼                                                   │
│   5. ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│      │ EC2 Instance Metadata / ECS Task Role / Lambda Role            │   ✅    │
│      │ (Automatic on AWS compute - RECOMMENDED)                       │  BEST   │
│      └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🌍

14.6.3 Environment Variables

Common Backend Environment Variables 
# AWS (S3 Backend)
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIA..."
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="..."
export AWS_REGION="us-west-2"
export AWS_PROFILE="terraform"

# Azure (azurerm Backend)
export ARM_CLIENT_ID="..."
export ARM_CLIENT_SECRET="..."
export ARM_TENANT_ID="..."
export ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID="..."

# GCP (gcs Backend)
export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/key.json"
export GOOGLE_PROJECT="my-project"

# HCP Terraform (cloud block)
export TF_TOKEN_app_terraform_io="..."

# Consul
export CONSUL_HTTP_TOKEN="..."
export CONSUL_HTTP_ADDR="consul.example.com:8500"

# PostgreSQL
export DATABASE_URL="postgres://user:pass@host/db"

14.7 Migrating State Between Backends

🔄

Changing State Storage

Sooner or later, you'll need to move your state: from local to S3, from one S3 bucket to another, from S3 to HCP Terraform. This migration process must preserve your state data or you'll lose track of all your managed infrastructure.

The Critical Moment: When you change the backend block and run terraform init, Terraform detects the change and asks what to do. Your two options:
• -migrate-state — Copy existing state to the new backend (usually what you want)
• -reconfigure — Ignore existing state, start fresh (dangerous for existing infra!)

Safety Protocol: Always backup before migrating. Run terraform state pull > backup.tfstate so you have a local copy. After migration, verify with terraform state list and terraform plan (which should show "No changes").

🚀

14.7.1 terraform init -migrate-state

State Migration Command 
# After changing backend configuration, run:
$ terraform init -migrate-state

Initializing the backend...
Backend configuration changed!

Terraform has detected that the configuration specified for the backend
has changed. Terraform will now check for existing state in the backends.


Do you want to copy existing state to the new backend?
  Pre-existing state was found while migrating the previous "local" backend to the
  newly configured "s3" backend. No existing state was found in the newly
  configured "s3" backend. Do you want to copy this state to the new "s3"
  backend? Enter "yes" to copy and "no" to start with an empty state.

  Enter a value: yes

Successfully configured the backend "s3"! Terraform will automatically
use this backend unless the backend configuration changes.
📝

EXAM TIP: Migration Flags

  • -migrate-state - Copies existing state to new backend
  • -reconfigure - Ignores existing state (starts fresh)
  • You must choose one when backend config changes
📋

14.7.2 State Migration Workflow

📊 Safe Migration Workflow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATE MIGRATION WORKFLOW                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   1. PREPARATION                                                                 │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ • Ensure no one else is running Terraform                               │   │
│   │ • Backup current state: terraform state pull > backup.tfstate          │   │
│   │ • Verify current state: terraform state list                            │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                  │                                               │
│                                  ▼                                               │
│   2. UPDATE CONFIGURATION                                                        │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ # Change backend block in configuration                                 │   │
│   │ terraform {                                                              │   │
│   │   backend "s3" {  # Was "local"                                         │   │
│   │     bucket = "my-new-state-bucket"                                       │   │
│   │     ...                                                                  │   │
│   │   }                                                                      │   │
│   │ }                                                                        │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                  │                                               │
│                                  ▼                                               │
│   3. MIGRATE                                                                     │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ $ terraform init -migrate-state                                         │   │
│   │ Enter "yes" when prompted                                                │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                  │                                               │
│                                  ▼                                               │
│   4. VERIFY                                                                      │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ $ terraform state list    # Should show same resources                  │   │
│   │ $ terraform plan          # Should show "No changes"                    │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️

14.7.3 Handling Migration Errors

Common Migration Errors 
# Error: Backend configuration block has changed
# Solution: Use -migrate-state or -reconfigure
$ terraform init -migrate-state

# Error: Error loading state: AccessDenied
# Solution: Check IAM permissions to new backend
$ aws s3 ls s3://my-state-bucket  # Test access

# Error: Error acquiring the state lock
# Solution: Someone else is running Terraform, or stale lock
$ terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID  # Only if safe!

# Error: State snapshot was created by Terraform vX.X.X
# Solution: Upgrade Terraform or use state push
$ terraform state pull > state.json
# Edit version in state.json if needed
$ terraform state push state.json
↩️

14.7.4 Rolling Back Migration

Rollback Procedure 
# If migration failed or you need to revert:

# 1. Restore original backend configuration

# 2. If you have backup:
$ terraform state push backup.tfstate

# 3. If S3 versioning enabled:
#    - Go to S3 console
#    - Find terraform.tfstate
#    - Restore previous version

# 4. Reinitialize
$ terraform init -reconfigure
⚠️

Always Backup Before Migration!

Before any migration, always run terraform state pull > backup.tfstate. Keep this backup until you've verified the migration is successful and run a terraform plan showing no changes.

14.8 Partial Backend Configuration

🔧

Keeping Secrets Out of Code

Here's the security dilemma: backend configurations can't use variables, but you don't want to commit bucket names, account-specific paths, or credentials to Git. The solution is partial configuration—you define the backend type in code, but provide the actual values at terraform init time.

Two Approaches:
• -backend-config="key=value" flags — Pass individual values on the command line
• -backend-config=file.hcl — Load values from a file (keep these files out of Git!)

Why This Matters: Same Terraform code can target different environments (dev/staging/prod) just by initializing with different backend configs. CI/CD pipelines can inject values from secrets managers. No sensitive information ends up in version control.

🚩

14.8.1 -backend-config Flag

Using -backend-config with Key=Value 
# main.tf (partial config - no sensitive values)
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    # bucket, key, region provided at init
  }
}

# Initialize with values
$ terraform init \
    -backend-config="bucket=my-company-state" \
    -backend-config="key=prod/app/terraform.tfstate" \
    -backend-config="region=us-west-2"

# For different environments:
$ terraform init \
    -backend-config="bucket=my-company-state" \
    -backend-config="key=dev/app/terraform.tfstate" \
    -backend-config="region=us-west-2"
📄

14.8.2 Backend Config Files

Using .hcl Config Files 
# backend-prod.hcl
bucket = "my-company-terraform-state"
key    = "prod/app/terraform.tfstate"
region = "us-west-2"

# backend-dev.hcl
bucket = "my-company-terraform-state"
key    = "dev/app/terraform.tfstate"
region = "us-west-2"
Initialize with Config File 
# For production
$ terraform init -backend-config=backend-prod.hcl

# For development
$ terraform init -backend-config=backend-dev.hcl

# Combine file and flags
$ terraform init \
    -backend-config=backend-base.hcl \
    -backend-config="key=custom/path/terraform.tfstate"
🛡️

14.8.3 Security Benefits

SECURITY
🔐

No Secrets in Git

Bucket names, keys, and credentials stay out of version control

FLEXIBILITY
🔄

Environment Switching

Use same code for dev/staging/prod with different backends

CI/CD
🚀

Pipeline Integration

Inject values from CI/CD secrets or vaults

COMPLIANCE
✅

Audit Friendly

No sensitive data in repository history

📝

EXAM TIP: Partial Configuration

Remember: Backend configuration cannot use Terraform variables (var.*). Use partial configuration with -backend-config flags or files to parameterize backend settings. Environment variables work for credentials but not for arguments like bucket or key.

📋 Chapter 14 Summary: Remote State & Backends

☁️

Remote State

  • Enables team collaboration
  • Provides automatic locking
  • Supports encryption at rest
  • Enables disaster recovery
🪣

S3 Backend

  • Most popular for AWS environments
  • S3 bucket for state storage
  • DynamoDB table for locking
  • Enable versioning for recovery
🏢

HCP Terraform

  • Managed solution by HashiCorp
  • Built-in remote runs
  • Workspace management and RBAC
  • Use cloud block to configure
🔧

Partial Config

  • Keep secrets out of code
  • Use -backend-config flags
  • Load from files or env vars
  • terraform init -reconfigure
🔧 📝 🔄 📥
15

State Operations

Inspect, Modify, and Recover Terraform State

10 Sections
30+ Subsections
15+ Exam Tips

Chapter 15State Operations

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 List and inspect resources in Terraform state
2 Move resources between addresses and states
3 Remove resources from state without destroying them
4 Import existing infrastructure into Terraform management
5 Recover from lost or corrupted state files
6 Detect and manage configuration drift
7 Use pull/push for state backup and recovery

📑 In This Chapter

15.1 terraform state list 15.2 terraform state show 15.3 terraform state mv 📝 15.4 terraform state rm 📝 15.5 terraform state pull 15.6 terraform state push 15.7 replace-provider 15.8 Importing Resources 15.9 State Recovery 15.10 Drift Management

15.1 terraform state list

📋

Discovering Managed Resources

The terraform state list command displays all resources currently tracked in your state file. This is your primary tool for understanding what Terraform manages and for planning state operations. Think of it as running ls on your infrastructure—it shows you everything Terraform knows about.

Why this matters: Before performing any state operation (move, remove, import), you need to know the exact resource addresses. This command gives you that information. It's also invaluable for auditing—confirming which resources are under Terraform control and which might have been created manually.

📜

15.1.1 Listing All Resources

📋 Understanding the Output

Each line in the output represents one resource instance in your state. The format is resource_type.resource_name for root module resources, or module.module_name.resource_type.resource_name for resources inside modules. Resources with count or for_each show their index in brackets.

Basic State List 
$ terraform state list

aws_instance.web
aws_instance.api
aws_s3_bucket.logs
aws_security_group.web_sg
aws_vpc.main
module.database.aws_db_instance.primary
module.database.aws_security_group.db_sg
module.networking.aws_subnet.private[0]
module.networking.aws_subnet.private[1]
module.networking.aws_subnet.public[0]
🔍

15.1.2 Filtering with Address

Filtering Resources 
# List only aws_instance resources
$ terraform state list aws_instance
aws_instance.web
aws_instance.api

# List resources in a specific module
$ terraform state list module.database
module.database.aws_db_instance.primary
module.database.aws_security_group.db_sg

# List specific resource with wildcards (not regex)
$ terraform state list 'module.networking.aws_subnet.*'
module.networking.aws_subnet.private[0]
module.networking.aws_subnet.private[1]
module.networking.aws_subnet.public[0]
🆔

15.1.3 ID-Only Output

Getting Resource IDs 
# Show resource with its ID
$ terraform state list -id=i-0abc123def456
aws_instance.web

# Useful for finding which Terraform resource manages an AWS resource
$ terraform state list | xargs -I {} sh -c 'terraform state show {} | grep -q "i-0abc123" && echo {}'
aws_instance.web

15.2 terraform state show

🔎

Inspecting Resource Details

While state list shows you what resources exist, terraform state show reveals everything about a specific resource—every attribute, every computed value, every piece of metadata that Terraform has recorded. This is your deep-dive inspection tool.

When to use this: After importing a resource, use state show to see all the attributes you need to add to your configuration. When debugging why Terraform wants to change something, compare the state values with your configuration. When you need to reference a specific attribute value (like an ID or ARN), this command gives you the exact value.

📊

15.2.1 Showing Resource Details

State Show Output 
$ terraform state show aws_instance.web

# resource "aws_instance" "web" {
    ami                          = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
    arn                          = "arn:aws:ec2:us-west-2:123456789:instance/i-0abc123"
    associate_public_ip_address  = true
    availability_zone            = "us-west-2a"
    cpu_core_count               = 1
    cpu_threads_per_core         = 2
    id                           = "i-0abc123def456"
    instance_state               = "running"
    instance_type                = "t3.micro"
    private_dns                  = "ip-10-0-1-50.us-west-2.compute.internal"
    private_ip                   = "10.0.1.50"
    public_dns                   = "ec2-54-123-45-67.us-west-2.compute.amazonaws.com"
    public_ip                    = "54.123.45.67"
    subnet_id                    = "subnet-0abc123"
    tags                         = {
        "Environment" = "production"
        "Name"        = "web-server"
    }
    vpc_security_group_ids       = [
        "sg-0abc123",
    ]
    
    root_block_device {
        delete_on_termination = true
        device_name           = "/dev/xvda"
        volume_id             = "vol-0abc123"
        volume_size           = 20
        volume_type           = "gp3"
    }
# }
📄

15.2.2 Output Format

📄 Understanding the Output Format

The output is in HCL format—the same syntax used in your .tf configuration files. This is intentional: you can literally copy the output and paste it into your configuration with minor adjustments. The output shows both configured values (what you specified) and computed values (what AWS/Azure/GCP assigned, like IDs, ARNs, and IP addresses).

📊 Attribute Types in State

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ATTRIBUTE TYPES IN STATE                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  CONFIGURED (You Set These)         COMPUTED (Provider Assigns)  │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┤   ├────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │ ami = "ami-0c55b159..."     │   │ id = "i-0abc123def456"   │  │
│  │ instance_type = "t3.micro"│   │ arn = "arn:aws:ec2:..."  │  │
│  │ tags = { Name = "web" }   │   │ public_ip = "54.123..."  │  │
│  │ subnet_id = "subnet-..."  │   │ private_ip = "10.0.1.50" │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────┘   └────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                   │
│  ✔ You define these in config      ✔ Read-only, assigned by cloud   │
│  ✔ Changes here trigger updates    ✔ Can be referenced by other      │
│  ✔ Required for resource creation    resources via expressions       │
│                                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡

Tip: Use for Import Prep

After importing a resource, use terraform state show to see all the attributes you might want to include in your configuration to avoid drift.

15.3 terraform state mv 📝 EXAM TOPIC

📦

Moving Resources in State

The terraform state mv command is one of the most powerful state manipulation tools—it changes the address of a resource in state without affecting the actual infrastructure. Think of it as telling Terraform: "This resource hasn't changed, I've just reorganized my code to refer to it differently."

The Problem This Solves: When you rename a resource in your configuration (from aws_instance.web to aws_instance.frontend), Terraform sees one resource disappear and another appear—triggering a destroy-and-recreate. For production resources, this is devastating. state mv tells Terraform "these are the same resource, just with a new name" and prevents any infrastructure changes.

Common Use Cases: Renaming resources for clarity, refactoring monolithic configurations into modules, splitting large projects into smaller state files, and adopting better naming conventions without downtime.

🔄 How state mv Prevents Destruction

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WITHOUT state mv (Rename in Config Only)                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Config: resource "aws_instance" "web" → "frontend"                        │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌──────────────┐                                ┌──────────────┐           │
│  │   OLD NAME   │         terraform plan         │   NEW NAME   │           │
│  │   "web"      │ ──────────────────────────────>│  "frontend"  │           │
│  └──────────────┘          DESTROYS!             └──────────────┘           │
│        ↓                        ↓                                           │
│  [Your running                [Creates brand                                │
│   EC2 deleted!]                new EC2!]                                    │
│                                                                             │
│  ❌ Data loss! ❌ Downtime! ❌ IP changes!                                  │
│                                                                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                WITH state mv (Update State First)                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Step 1: terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.frontend         │
│  Step 2: Update config file with new name                                   │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌──────────────┐      State Updated      ┌──────────────┐                 │
│  │   OLD NAME   │  ───────────────────>  │   NEW NAME   │                 │
│  │   "web"      │    Same Instance ID     │  "frontend"  │                 │
│  └──────────────┘                         └──────────────┘                 │
│                                                                             │
│  terraform plan: "No changes. Infrastructure is up-to-date."               │
│                                                                             │
│  ✅ No destruction! ✅ Zero downtime! ✅ Same resource!                     │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✏️

15.3.1 Renaming Resources

📝 The Rename Workflow

The order matters! Run state mv before changing your configuration, or run it immediately after changing config but before running plan. Either way, the state and config must match when Terraform evaluates them.

Rename a Resource 
# Scenario: Rename aws_instance.web to aws_instance.frontend

# Step 1: Update configuration file
# Change: resource "aws_instance" "web" { ... }
# To:     resource "aws_instance" "frontend" { ... }

# Step 2: Move in state (before running plan!)
$ terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.frontend
Move "aws_instance.web" to "aws_instance.frontend"
Successfully moved 1 object(s).

# Step 3: Verify with plan (should show no changes)
$ terraform plan
No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration.
📝

EXAM TIP: Why Use state mv?

Without state mv, renaming a resource in configuration causes Terraform to destroy the old resource and create a new one. Using state mv prevents destruction by updating the state to match the new name.

📦

15.3.2 Moving to Different Module

Move Resource into a Module 
# Scenario: Refactor root module resource into a child module

# Before: aws_instance.web (in root module)
# After:  module.compute.aws_instance.web (in child module)

# Step 1: Create module and move configuration

# Step 2: Move in state
$ terraform state mv aws_instance.web module.compute.aws_instance.web
Move "aws_instance.web" to "module.compute.aws_instance.web"
Successfully moved 1 object(s).

# Moving out of a module
$ terraform state mv module.old.aws_instance.web aws_instance.web

# Moving between modules
$ terraform state mv module.old.aws_instance.web module.new.aws_instance.web
🔀

15.3.3 Moving Between States

Move Resource to Different State File 
# Scenario: Split a monolith into separate projects

# Move resource from current state to another state file
$ terraform state mv \
    -state=terraform.tfstate \
    -state-out=../networking/terraform.tfstate \
    aws_vpc.main \
    aws_vpc.main

Move "aws_vpc.main" to "aws_vpc.main"
Successfully moved 1 object(s).

# For remote backends, specify both backend configs
$ terraform state mv \
    -state-out="s3://bucket/networking/terraform.tfstate" \
    aws_vpc.main aws_vpc.main
⚠️

Moving Between States Warning

When moving resources between states, ensure both configurations reference the moved resource correctly. The resource must exist in the destination configuration before running terraform plan.

🧪

15.3.4 Dry Run (-dry-run)

Preview State Move 
# Preview the move without making changes
$ terraform state mv -dry-run aws_instance.web aws_instance.frontend
Would move "aws_instance.web" to "aws_instance.frontend"

# Also supports -lock=false for read-only operations
$ terraform state mv -dry-run -lock=false aws_instance.web aws_instance.frontend

15.4 terraform state rm 📝 EXAM TOPIC

🗑️

Removing Resources from State

The terraform state rm command tells Terraform to forget that a resource exists—without touching the actual infrastructure. The cloud resource continues running; Terraform just stops tracking it. This creates what's called an orphaned resource.

The Key Distinction: Unlike terraform destroy (which deletes real infrastructure), state rm is purely a bookkeeping operation. It removes the resource's entry from the state file, but your EC2 instance, S3 bucket, or database keeps running in the cloud. You'll need to manage it manually or with another tool.
