When You Need This: Handing off resources to another team's Terraform configuration, migrating resources to a different tool (like CloudFormation or Pulumi), splitting a monolithic state into smaller projects, or when you imported a resource by mistake and want to "undo" without destroying it.

⚠️

Critical Understanding: Orphaned ≠ Deleted

After state rm, the resource becomes invisible to Terraform but still incurs cloud costs. It won't appear in terraform plan, and running terraform destroy won't remove it. You must delete orphaned resources manually through the cloud console, CLI, or import them into another Terraform project.

✂️

15.4.1 Removing from State

📋 Before You Remove

Always use -dry-run first to preview what will be removed. Also remove the corresponding resource block from your configuration file—otherwise, the next terraform plan will try to recreate the resource!

Remove Resource from State 
# Remove a single resource
$ terraform state rm aws_instance.web
Removed aws_instance.web
Successfully removed 1 resource instance(s).

# Remove a module (all resources within)
$ terraform state rm module.networking
Removed module.networking.aws_vpc.main
Removed module.networking.aws_subnet.public[0]
Removed module.networking.aws_subnet.private[0]
Successfully removed 3 resource instance(s).

# Remove with dry-run first
$ terraform state rm -dry-run aws_instance.web
Would remove aws_instance.web
📝

EXAM TIP: state rm vs destroy

Key distinction:

  • terraform state rm - Removes from state only, resource continues to exist
  • terraform destroy - Destroys the actual resource

Use state rm when you want Terraform to "forget" a resource it manages.

❓

15.4.2 When to Use

USE CASE
🔄

State Split

When splitting a monolith into multiple states/projects

USE CASE
👋

Migration

Handing off resources to another team or tool

USE CASE
🚫

Stop Managing

Resource will be managed manually going forward

USE CASE
🔧

Fix Mistakes

Remove mistakenly imported or created resources

👻

15.4.3 Orphaned Resources

📊 State rm Creates Orphaned Resources

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    AFTER terraform state rm                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   BEFORE:                                                                        │
│   ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐    │
│   │   Configuration  │◄───────►│     State        │◄───────►│  AWS/Azure   │    │
│   │   aws_instance   │         │   aws_instance   │         │  Instance    │    │
│   └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘         └──────────────┘    │
│                                                                                  │
│   AFTER terraform state rm:                                                      │
│   ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐    │
│   │   Configuration  │         │     State        │         │  AWS/Azure   │    │
│   │   (EMPTY or      │         │   (EMPTY)        │         │  Instance    │    │
│   │    deleted)      │         │                  │         │  ORPHANED!   │    │
│   └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘         └──────────────┘    │
│                                                                                  │
│   The resource still exists but Terraform no longer knows about it!             │
│   • Won't appear in terraform plan                                               │
│   • Can't be destroyed by Terraform                                              │
│   • Must be deleted manually or re-imported                                      │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️

Remember: Also Remove from Configuration!

After terraform state rm, you should also remove the resource from your configuration files. Otherwise, the next terraform plan will show it as a new resource to create.

15.5 terraform state pull

📥

Downloading State

terraform state pull downloads the current state from your configured backend and outputs it as raw JSON to your terminal (stdout). This is the read half of the pull/push pair—it lets you inspect, backup, or process your state without modifying it.

Why JSON Output Matters: Since the output is formatted JSON, you can pipe it through tools like jq to query specific values, count resources, or extract attribute data. This is invaluable for scripting, auditing, and building dashboards.

Key Workflow: Before any risky operation (like manually editing state, changing backends, or running untested state mv commands), always run terraform state pull > backup.tfstate first. This gives you a rollback point.

⬇️

15.5.1 Downloading State

Pull State to File 
# Output state to stdout
$ terraform state pull

# Save to a backup file
$ terraform state pull > backup.tfstate

# Parse with jq
$ terraform state pull | jq '.resources[] | .type + "." + .name'
"aws_instance.web"
"aws_s3_bucket.logs"

# Count resources
$ terraform state pull | jq '.resources | length'
12
💡

15.5.2 Use Cases

ESSENTIAL
💾

Backup

Create backups before risky operations

DEBUG
🔍

Analysis

Inspect state structure and values

MIGRATION
🔄

Transfer

Move state between backends

AUDIT
📋

Compliance

Document managed resources

15.6 terraform state push

📤

Uploading State

terraform state push uploads a local state file to your configured backend, overwriting what's currently stored there. This is the write half of the pull/push pair—and it's the most dangerous state command if misused.

When You Need This: Recovering from a corrupted state by restoring a backup, manually migrating state between backends when terraform init -migrate-state can't be used, and advanced troubleshooting scenarios where you've made manual edits to state.

The Serial Number Safety Check: Terraform tracks a "serial" number in state that increments with each change. When you push, Terraform verifies your local serial isn't older than remote—preventing you from accidentally overwriting newer changes. The -force flag bypasses this check (use with extreme caution!).

⬆️

15.6.1 Pushing State to Backend

Push State File 
# Push a backup state file to the backend
$ terraform state push backup.tfstate

# Force push (overwrite serial number check)
$ terraform state push -force backup.tfstate

# Common workflow: Pull, modify, push
$ terraform state pull > modified.tfstate
$ # Edit modified.tfstate carefully
$ terraform state push modified.tfstate
⚠️

Extreme Caution Required!

terraform state push can overwrite your production state. Always ensure you're pushing the correct state and consider backups first. The -force flag bypasses safety checks and should rarely be used.

🔢

15.6.2 Serial Numbers

📊 State Serial Number Protection

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATE SERIAL NUMBER CHECK                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Remote State (serial: 42)                Local State (serial: 40)             │
│   ┌─────────────────────────┐              ┌─────────────────────────┐          │
│   │  {                      │              │  {                      │          │
│   │    "version": 4,        │              │    "version": 4,        │          │
│   │    "serial": 42,    ◄───┼──── NEWER    │    "serial": 40,        │          │
│   │    "resources": [...]   │              │    "resources": [...]   │          │
│   │  }                      │              │  }                      │          │
│   └─────────────────────────┘              └─────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                                  │
│   $ terraform state push local.tfstate                                          │
│   Error: Serial increased, would lose data!                                     │
│   Remote serial: 42, local serial: 40                                           │
│                                                                                  │
│   Use -force to override (DANGEROUS!)                                           │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧

15.6.3 Recovery Scenarios

State Recovery with Push 
# Scenario 1: Restore from S3 versioning
$ aws s3api get-object \
    --bucket my-terraform-state \
    --key production/terraform.tfstate \
    --version-id "previous-version-id" \
    restored.tfstate

$ terraform state push restored.tfstate

# Scenario 2: Migrate to new backend
$ terraform state pull > migration.tfstate

# Update backend configuration
# Then push to new backend
$ terraform init  # Configure new backend
$ terraform state push migration.tfstate

15.7 terraform state replace-provider

🔄

Switching Providers in State

terraform state replace-provider updates the provider "source address" recorded in your state file—without having to re-import every resource. This sounds obscure, but it's essential during Terraform version upgrades and provider migrations.

Why This Happens: When Terraform 0.13 introduced the full provider source syntax (registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws), existing state files still referenced the old short form (-/aws). Similarly, if you switch from a community provider to an official one, or move to a private registry, the state needs updating.

The Alternative: Without this command, you'd need to remove every resource from state and re-import them under the new provider address—a tedious and error-prone process for large projects.

🏷️

15.7.1 Updating Provider Addresses

Replace Provider Source 
# Common scenario: HashiCorp provider migration (Terraform 0.12 → 0.13+)
$ terraform state replace-provider \
    "registry.terraform.io/-/aws" \
    "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"

# Replace provider with auto-approval
$ terraform state replace-provider -auto-approve \
    "registry.terraform.io/-/aws" \
    "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"

Replacing provider:
  registry.terraform.io/-/aws
with:
  registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws

Successfully replaced provider for 15 resources.
📋

15.7.2 When to Use

Scenario From To
Terraform 0.12 → 0.13 upgrade -/aws hashicorp/aws
Provider fork hashicorp/kubernetes mycorp/kubernetes
Private registry registry.terraform.io/... private.registry.com/...
Community to official community/datadog DataDog/datadog

15.8 Importing Existing Resources

📥

Bringing Resources Under Terraform Management

The "adopt existing infrastructure" operation. Import takes a resource that already exists in your cloud account (created manually, by another tool, or by another Terraform project) and adds it to your current state—telling Terraform "start managing this resource from now on."

Two-Phase Process: Import only adds the resource to state—it doesn't generate configuration. You must also write the corresponding resource block in your .tf files. Terraform 1.5+ introduced import blocks for declarative imports that can generate configuration automatically.

Common Scenarios: Migrating manually-created infrastructure to Terraform, adopting resources from a decommissioned Terraform project, recovering from state loss where resources still exist in the cloud, and gradually terraforming an existing environment.

🎯 Finding Resource IDs

Each resource type requires a specific ID format for import. For AWS resources, this is typically the resource's ARN or ID (like i-0abc123 for EC2, vpc-0abc123 for VPCs). Check the provider documentation's "Import" section for each resource type—it documents the exact ID format required.

⌨️

15.8.1 terraform import Command

Basic Import Syntax 
# Syntax: terraform import [options] ADDRESS ID

# Import an EC2 instance
$ terraform import aws_instance.web i-0abc123def456789

# Import an S3 bucket
$ terraform import aws_s3_bucket.logs my-logging-bucket

# Import into a module
$ terraform import module.vpc.aws_vpc.main vpc-0abc123

# Import with count index
$ terraform import 'aws_instance.servers[0]' i-0abc123def456789

# Import with for_each key
$ terraform import 'aws_instance.servers["web"]' i-0abc123def456789
💡

Resource ID Format Varies

Each resource type has its own ID format. Check the provider documentation for the correct format. Common examples:

  • EC2: i-0abc123
  • S3: bucket-name
  • IAM Role: role-name
  • Security Group: sg-0abc123
📋

15.8.2 Import Process Steps

📊 Complete Import Workflow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAFORM IMPORT WORKFLOW                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 1: Write Empty Resource Block                                             │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  resource "aws_instance" "imported_server" {                             │  │
│   │    # Configuration will be filled after import                           │  │
│   │  }                                                                       │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 2: Run terraform import                                                   │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  $ terraform import aws_instance.imported_server i-0abc123               │  │
│   │  aws_instance.imported_server: Importing from ID "i-0abc123"...          │  │
│   │  aws_instance.imported_server: Import prepared!                          │  │
│   │  aws_instance.imported_server: Refreshing state...                       │  │
│   │  Import successful!                                                      │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 3: Run terraform state show to see attributes                            │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  $ terraform state show aws_instance.imported_server                     │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 4: Fill in configuration to match state                                  │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  resource "aws_instance" "imported_server" {                             │  │
│   │    ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"                               │  │
│   │    instance_type = "t3.micro"                                            │  │
│   │    tags = { Name = "imported" }                                          │  │
│   │  }                                                                       │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 5: Verify with terraform plan (should show no changes)                   │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  $ terraform plan                                                        │  │
│   │  No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration.              │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝

15.8.3 Import Block (Terraform 1.5+)

Declarative Import Block 
# imports.tf - Terraform 1.5+ declarative import
import {
  # Where to import (Terraform address)
  to = aws_instance.imported_server
  
  # What to import (provider resource ID)
  id = "i-0abc123def456789"
}

# The resource block must exist
resource "aws_instance" "imported_server" {
  # Terraform 1.5+ can generate this!
}

# Run: terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf
# This generates the configuration automatically!
📝

EXAM TIP: Import Block Benefits

The import block (Terraform 1.5+) is declarative and can be version controlled, unlike the imperative terraform import command. It also supports -generate-config-out to auto-generate configuration.

🔄

15.8.4 Generate Config on Import

Auto-Generate Configuration 
# Step 1: Create import block
cat > imports.tf <<'EOF'
import {
  to = aws_instance.web
  id = "i-0abc123def456789"
}

import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.logs  
  id = "my-logging-bucket"
}
EOF

# Step 2: Generate configuration
$ terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated_resources.tf

# Terraform will:
# 1. Import the resources into state
# 2. Generate HCL configuration 
# 3. Write to generated_resources.tf

# Step 3: Review and customize generated code
# The generated code may need adjustments for:
# - Removing computed attributes
# - Adding lifecycle rules
# - Organizing with locals/variables
⚙️

15.8.5 Import Limitations

LIMITATION
📄

No Auto-Config

Traditional import doesn't write configuration (use import block instead)

LIMITATION
🔗

No Dependencies

Import doesn't capture relationships between resources

LIMITATION
🚫

Not All Resources

Some provider resources don't support import

LIMITATION
🔐

Sensitive Data

Secrets may be stored in state as plain text

📚

15.8.6 Provider-Specific Import IDs

AWS Resource Import ID Format Example
aws_instance Instance ID i-0abc123def456
aws_s3_bucket Bucket name my-bucket-name
aws_iam_role Role name my-role
aws_security_group Security group ID sg-0abc123
aws_lb ARN arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...
aws_route53_record Zone_Record_Type Z123_www.example.com_A

15.9 State File Recovery

🔧

Recovering from State Problems

The nightmare scenario: Your state file is gone, corrupted, or hopelessly out of sync with reality. Your production infrastructure still exists in the cloud, but Terraform has lost its "map" of what it manages. This section covers how to rebuild.

Recovery Options Ranked by Preference:
1. Restore from S3 versioning — If you enabled versioning on your state bucket, just download a previous version and push it back.
2. Restore from backup — If you have manual backups (from terraform state pull), use them.
3. Re-import everything — Last resort: inventory your cloud resources and import them one by one back into a fresh state.

Prevention Is Key: Enable S3 versioning, enable state locking, take manual backups before risky operations, and maintain an external inventory of critical resource IDs. These practices can turn a disaster into a minor inconvenience.

💾

15.9.1 Recovery from Backups

S3 Versioning Recovery 
# List state file versions
$ aws s3api list-object-versions \
    --bucket my-terraform-state \
    --prefix production/terraform.tfstate \
    --max-items 10

# Download a previous version
$ aws s3api get-object \
    --bucket my-terraform-state \
    --key production/terraform.tfstate \
    --version-id "abc123-version-id" \
    recovered.tfstate

# Review the recovered state
$ cat recovered.tfstate | jq '.serial, .lineage'

# Push recovered state to backend
$ terraform state push recovered.tfstate
📥

15.9.2 Rebuilding from Import

📊 State Rebuild Strategy

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATE RECOVERY VIA IMPORT                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   When: State is completely lost and no backups exist                           │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 1: Inventory existing resources                                          │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  $ aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].{     │  │
│   │      ID:InstanceId, Name:Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]}'                  │  │
│   │  $ aws s3 ls                                                             │  │
│   │  $ aws rds describe-db-instances                                         │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 2: Create import blocks for each resource                                │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  import { to = aws_instance.web; id = "i-0abc123" }                      │  │
│   │  import { to = aws_s3_bucket.logs; id = "my-logs-bucket" }               │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 3: Generate configuration                                                │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  $ terraform plan -generate-config-out=recovered.tf                      │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Step 4: Verify and adjust configuration                                       │
│   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│   │  $ terraform plan  # Should show "No changes"                            │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🛡️

15.9.3 Prevention Best Practices

REQUIRED
📦

Enable Versioning

Always enable versioning on S3 state buckets

REQUIRED
🔒

State Locking

Enable DynamoDB locking to prevent conflicts

RECOMMENDED
💾

Regular Backups

terraform state pull before major operations

RECOMMENDED
📋

Document IDs

Maintain inventory of resource IDs externally

15.10 State Drift Management

⚡

Detecting and Handling Configuration Drift

Drift occurs when real infrastructure differs from what Terraform expects based on its state file. Someone changed a security group rule in the AWS console. An automated process scaled up an instance. A colleague used the CLI to modify a setting "just temporarily." Now Terraform's view of the world doesn't match reality.

Why Drift Matters: Drift creates unpredictable behavior. Your next terraform apply might undo someone's emergency fix, revert a production optimization, or cause unexpected outages by restoring old settings. Detecting drift early lets you make informed decisions about whether to accept the changes or revert them.

Detection Strategies:
• terraform plan — Shows drift as part of its normal refresh operation
• terraform refresh — Updates state without planning changes (deprecated in favor of -refresh-only)
• terraform plan -refresh-only — Explicitly check for drift without generating a full plan
• Run plan in CI pipelines to catch drift automatically

🔍

15.10.1 Detecting Drift

Drift Detection with Refresh 
# terraform plan automatically detects drift
$ terraform plan

# Example output showing drift:
aws_instance.web: Refreshing state... [id=i-0abc123]

Terraform detected the following changes made outside of Terraform:

  # aws_instance.web has been changed
  ~ resource "aws_instance" "web" {
        id            = "i-0abc123"
      ~ instance_type = "t3.micro" -> "t3.small"  # Changed manually!
        tags          = {
            "Name" = "web-server"
        }
    }

# Refresh-only mode (update state without changing resources)
$ terraform apply -refresh-only

# Output detailed drift report
$ terraform plan -detailed-exitcode
# Exit code 2 = drift detected
🎯

15.10.2 Handling Drift

📊 Drift Resolution Decision Tree

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         DRIFT RESOLUTION OPTIONS                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                        Drift Detected!                                           │
│                             │                                                    │
│             ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐                                   │
│             │               │               │                                    │
│             ▼               ▼               ▼                                    │
│   ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐                   │
│   │ OPTION A        │ │ OPTION B        │ │ OPTION C        │                   │
│   │ Revert to Code  │ │ Accept Reality  │ │ Ignore Drift    │                   │
│   ├─────────────────┤ ├─────────────────┤ ├─────────────────┤                   │
│   │                 │ │                 │ │                 │                   │
│   │ terraform apply │ │ 1. Update code  │ │ Use lifecycle { │                   │
│   │                 │ │ 2. refresh-only │ │   ignore_changes│                   │
│   │ Restores infra  │ │                 │ │ }               │                   │
│   │ to match config │ │ Code matches    │ │                 │                   │
│   │                 │ │ actual state    │ │ Ignores future  │                   │
│   │                 │ │                 │ │ drift on attr   │                   │
│   └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘                   │
│                                                                                  │
│   Common Choice:                                                                 │
│   • Production fixes → Option B (accept changes, update code)                   │
│   • Unauthorized changes → Option A (revert to code-defined state)              │
│   • Auto-updated attributes → Option C (ignore computed fields)                 │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔄

15.10.3 Refresh-Only Apply

Accept Drift into State 
# When you want to accept manual changes:

# Step 1: Preview what will be updated in state
$ terraform plan -refresh-only
Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_instance.web will be updated in-place
  ~ resource "aws_instance" "web" {
        id            = "i-0abc123"
      ~ instance_type = "t3.micro" -> "t3.small"
    }

# Step 2: Apply refresh to update state
$ terraform apply -refresh-only
Apply complete! Resources: 0 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.

# Step 3: Update your configuration to match
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  instance_type = "t3.small"  # Updated to match reality
  # ... other config
}
🚫

15.10.4 Ignoring Drift (ignore_changes)

Lifecycle ignore_changes 
# Ignore specific attributes that change outside Terraform
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  
  lifecycle {
    # Ignore changes to tags made by AWS
    ignore_changes = [
      tags,
      tags_all,
    ]
  }
}

# Ignore all attributes (rarely recommended)
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "example" {
  # ... config ...
  
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = all  # Ignores ALL drift
  }
}

# Common ignore scenarios:
# - Auto-updated AMIs
# - Tags managed by other tools
# - Instance sizes changed by auto-scaling policies
📝

EXAM TIP: ignore_changes

ignore_changes is a lifecycle meta-argument that tells Terraform to ignore changes to specific attributes during planning. It prevents drift detection for those attributes but doesn't prevent the resource from being managed.

📋 Chapter 15 Summary: State Operations

📋

Inspection Commands

  • terraform state list - List all managed resources
  • terraform state show - Show resource details
  • Filter with address patterns
📦

Modification Commands

  • state mv - Rename or move resources
  • state rm - Remove from state (not destroy)
  • state replace-provider - Update provider addresses
📥

Import & Recovery

  • terraform import - Bring existing resources under management
  • Import blocks (Terraform 1.5+) for declarative import
  • state pull/push for backup and recovery
⚡

Drift Management

  • terraform plan detects drift automatically
  • -refresh-only to accept manual changes
  • ignore_changes to skip specific attributes
📦 🧩 🔄
16

Introduction to Modules

Building Reusable Infrastructure Components

📑 5 Sections
⏱️ 45 min read
📝 Exam Topic

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Understand what Terraform modules are and why they matter
2 Distinguish between root modules and child modules
3 Recognize the benefits of modular infrastructure design
4 Learn standard module structure and organization
5 Master different module source types for the exam

📑 In This Chapter

16.1 What are Modules? 📝 16.2 Root Module vs Child Modules 16.3 Why Use Modules? 16.4 Module Structure and Organization 16.5 Module Sources 📝

16.1 What are Modules? 📝 EXAM TOPIC

📦

The Building Blocks of Terraform

A module is a container for multiple resources that are used together. Think of it like a function in programming—you define it once, then call it multiple times with different inputs to create similar infrastructure patterns.

The Key Insight: Every Terraform configuration is already a module! When you run terraform apply in a directory, you're executing the "root module." Modules you call from your root module are called "child modules." This hierarchy enables code reuse, abstraction, and team collaboration at scale.

Why This Matters for the Exam: Module concepts are heavily tested. You need to understand module definition, invocation, inputs (variables), outputs, and the different source types (local, registry, Git, etc.).

📖

16.1.1 Module Definition

📊 What is a Terraform Module?

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         TERRAFORM MODULE CONCEPT                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   A MODULE IS:                                                                   │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │    📁 A directory containing .tf files                                   │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │    my-vpc-module/                                                        │   │
│   │    ├── main.tf        ← Resource definitions                            │   │
│   │    ├── variables.tf   ← Input parameters                                │   │
│   │    ├── outputs.tf     ← Return values                                   │   │
│   │    └── versions.tf    ← Provider requirements                           │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                                  │
│   MODULES PROVIDE:                                                               │
│   ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │
│   │  📦 Packaging  │  │  🔄 Reuse      │  │  🎭 Abstraction│  │ 🔒 Encapsul. │ │
│   │   Group        │  │   Use same     │  │   Hide complex │  │  Control     │ │
│   │   resources    │  │   code many    │  │   details from │  │  what's      │ │
│   │   together     │  │   times        │  │   consumers    │  │  exposed     │ │
│   └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └──────────────┘ │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝

EXAM TIP: Module Definition

The exam will ask: "What is a module?" The official definition is: A module is a container for multiple resources that are used together. Any directory containing .tf files is a module—including your working directory (the root module).

💡

16.1.2 Module Philosophy

🧠 The DRY Principle in Infrastructure

DRY = Don't Repeat Yourself. Without modules, you'd copy-paste the same VPC configuration across 10 projects. With modules, you define the VPC pattern once and instantiate it 10 times with different parameters. Changes to the module automatically apply everywhere it's used.

Without Modules (Copy-Paste Anti-Pattern) 
# project-a/main.tf - VPC for Project A
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  tags = { Name = "project-a-vpc" }
}
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24"
}
# ... 50 more lines of networking code

# project-b/main.tf - COPY-PASTED! 😱
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"  # Only this changed
  tags = { Name = "project-b-vpc" }
}
# ... same 50 lines copied again

# Bug fix needed? Update ALL copies manually!
With Modules (DRY Pattern) 
# project-a/main.tf
module "vpc" {
  source     = "./modules/vpc"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  name       = "project-a"
}

# project-b/main.tf
module "vpc" {
  source     = "./modules/vpc"
  cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"
  name       = "project-b"
}

# Bug fix? Update the module ONCE, all projects get it!
✨

16.1.3 Module Benefits

BENEFIT
🔄

Reusability

Write once, use everywhere. Same module for dev, staging, and production with different inputs.

BENEFIT
📐

Consistency

Enforce organizational standards. All VPCs follow the same security and tagging patterns.

BENEFIT
🧪

Testability

Test modules in isolation before deploying. Catch bugs before they hit production.

BENEFIT
👥

Collaboration

Teams can own modules. Platform team provides VPC module; app teams consume it.

BENEFIT
📚

Documentation

Modules are self-documenting. Variables and outputs define the interface clearly.

BENEFIT
🔐

Encapsulation

Hide complexity. Users don't need to understand all 20 resources—just the inputs/outputs.

16.2 Root Module vs Child Modules

🌳

Understanding the Module Hierarchy

Terraform organizes configuration in a tree structure. At the top is your root module—the directory where you run terraform apply. Any modules called by the root (using module blocks) are child modules. Child modules can call other modules, creating a nested hierarchy.

Key Distinction: The root module is where execution happens. It's where you define your backend, configure providers, and orchestrate child modules. Child modules are reusable components that receive inputs and return outputs—they don't run independently.

🏠

16.2.1 Root Module Concept

📊 Root Module vs Child Modules

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         MODULE HIERARCHY                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                        ┌─────────────────────────┐                              │
│                        │      ROOT MODULE        │                              │
│                        │   (Working Directory)   │                              │
│                        │                         │                              │
│                        │  $ terraform apply      │ ← Execution entry point     │
│                        │                         │                              │
│                        │  • backend config       │                              │
│                        │  • provider config      │                              │
│                        │  • module calls         │                              │
│                        └───────────┬─────────────┘                              │
│                                    │                                             │
│                    ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐                            │
│                    │               │               │                            │
│                    ▼               ▼               ▼                            │
│           ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐                │
│           │ CHILD MODULE  │ │ CHILD MODULE  │ │ CHILD MODULE  │                │
│           │   "vpc"       │ │   "ec2"       │ │   "rds"       │                │
│           │               │ │               │ │               │                │
│           │ • receives    │ │ • receives    │ │ • receives    │                │
│           │   variables   │ │   variables   │ │   variables   │                │
│           │ • creates     │ │ • creates     │ │ • creates     │                │
│           │   resources   │ │   resources   │ │   resources   │                │
│           │ • returns     │ │ • returns     │ │ • returns     │                │
│           │   outputs     │ │   outputs     │ │   outputs     │                │
│           └───────────────┘ └───────────────┘ └───────────────┘                │
│                                    │                                             │
│                                    ▼                                             │
│                          ┌───────────────┐                                      │
│                          │ NESTED MODULE │  ← Child can call other modules    │
│                          │   "subnet"    │                                      │
│                          └───────────────┘                                      │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Root Module Example 
# This IS your root module - the directory where you run terraform
# infrastructure/
# ├── main.tf        ← You are here (ROOT MODULE)
# ├── variables.tf
# ├── outputs.tf
# ├── terraform.tfvars
# └── modules/
#     └── vpc/       ← Child module

# main.tf (ROOT MODULE)
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
  
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "my-terraform-state"
    key    = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-west-2"
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
}

# Calling a child module
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  # Passing variables to the child module
  cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr
  name       = var.environment
}
📞

16.2.2 Child Module Invocation

🔑 The module Block

You call a child module using a module block. The block requires a source argument (where to find the module) and any required input variables. The module's local name (e.g., "vpc") is how you reference its outputs.

Child Module Structure 
# modules/vpc/main.tf (CHILD MODULE)
# This module creates a VPC with public/private subnets

resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.cidr_block
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.name}-vpc"
    Environment = var.name
  }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = length(var.public_subnets)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = var.public_subnets[count.index]
  availability_zone = var.azs[count.index]
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.name}-public-${count.index + 1}"
  }
}

# modules/vpc/variables.tf
variable "cidr_block" {
  description = "The CIDR block for the VPC"
  type        = string
}

variable "name" {
  description = "Name prefix for resources"
  type        = string
}

variable "public_subnets" {
  description = "List of public subnet CIDRs"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = []
}

variable "azs" {
  description = "Availability zones"
  type        = list(string)
}

# modules/vpc/outputs.tf
output "vpc_id" {
  description = "The ID of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  description = "List of public subnet IDs"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}
🏗️

16.2.3 Module Hierarchy

Aspect Root Module Child Module
Definition Working directory where you run Terraform Module called via module block
Backend Config Yes - defines state storage No - inherits from root
Provider Config Yes - configures providers Inherits or receives via providers
Variables From .tfvars, CLI, or env vars From parent module's module block
Outputs Displayed after apply Accessed via module.name.output
Entry Point Yes - terraform apply No - always called by parent
📝

EXAM TIP: Module Terminology

Be precise with terminology:

  • Root module = Your working directory (always exists)
  • Child module = Any module called with a module block
  • Calling module = The module that contains the module block
  • Called module = The module being invoked

16.3 Why Use Modules?

🎯

The Case for Modular Infrastructure

Modules solve the fundamental challenges of infrastructure at scale: code duplication, inconsistency, and complexity. Without modules, a 50-person organization might have 50 different ways to create a VPC. With modules, they have one tested, approved pattern.

🔄

16.3.1 Code Reusability

📊 Module Reuse Across Environments

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ONE MODULE → MANY DEPLOYMENTS                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                         ┌─────────────────────────┐                             │
│                         │    VPC MODULE           │                             │
│                         │    (Single Definition)  │                             │
│                         │                         │                             │
│                         │  • CIDR calculation     │                             │
│                         │  • Subnet creation      │                             │
│                         │  • Route tables         │                             │
│                         │  • NAT gateways         │                             │
│                         │  • Flow logs            │                             │
│                         └───────────┬─────────────┘                             │
│                                     │                                            │
│           ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐                 │
│           │                         │                         │                 │
│           ▼                         ▼                         ▼                 │
│   ┌───────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐        │
│   │  Development  │         │    Staging    │         │  Production   │        │
│   │               │         │               │         │               │        │
│   │ cidr: 10.0.x  │         │ cidr: 10.1.x  │         │ cidr: 10.2.x  │        │
│   │ 2 AZs         │         │ 2 AZs         │         │ 3 AZs         │        │
│   │ 1 NAT         │         │ 1 NAT         │         │ 3 NATs (HA)   │        │
│   │               │         │               │         │               │        │
│   └───────────────┘         └───────────────┘         └───────────────┘        │
│                                                                                  │
│   Same module, different parameters → Consistent infrastructure everywhere!     │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📁

16.3.2 Organization

Organized Project Structure 
# Without modules: Flat, monolithic, hard to navigate
infrastructure/
├── main.tf           # 2000 lines of mixed resources 😱
├── variables.tf      # 100 variables, all mixed together
└── outputs.tf

# With modules: Organized, logical, maintainable
infrastructure/
├── main.tf           # Clean orchestration
├── variables.tf      # Only top-level variables
├── outputs.tf
├── modules/
│   ├── networking/   # All VPC/subnet logic
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   ├── compute/      # EC2, ASG, ALB
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   ├── database/     # RDS, ElastiCache
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   └── security/     # Security groups, IAM
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
└── environments/
    ├── dev/
    ├── staging/
    └── production/
📦

16.3.3 Encapsulation

🔐 Information Hiding

Encapsulation means hiding internal complexity. A VPC module might create 20 resources (VPC, subnets, route tables, NAT gateways, internet gateway, flow logs...), but consumers only see 5 inputs and 3 outputs. They don't need to understand the internals.

Encapsulation Example 
# Consumer sees this simple interface:
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  name               = "production"
  cidr               = "10.0.0.0/16"
  availability_zones = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = false  # One per AZ for HA
}

# Consumer DOESN'T need to know about:
# - aws_vpc resource configuration
# - aws_internet_gateway attachment
# - aws_eip allocation for NAT gateways
# - aws_nat_gateway creation in each AZ
# - aws_route_table creation for public/private
# - aws_route_table_association logic
# - aws_flow_log configuration
# - CIDR calculations for subnets
# ... and 15 more implementation details!

# Just use the outputs:
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  subnet_id = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids[0]
}
🎭

16.3.4 Abstraction

ABSTRACTION
🔧

Hide Complexity

100-line EKS cluster becomes a 10-line module call. Users focus on "what" not "how."

ABSTRACTION
📋

Standardize Patterns

Bake best practices into modules. Security, tagging, and compliance built-in.

ABSTRACTION
🚫

Prevent Mistakes

Hide dangerous options. Users can't accidentally create public S3 buckets.

ABSTRACTION
📈

Enable Self-Service

Developers deploy infrastructure without deep cloud expertise.

👥

16.3.5 Team Collaboration

📊 Module Ownership Model

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TEAM COLLABORATION WITH MODULES                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   PLATFORM TEAM (Module Producers)                                               │
│   ═══════════════════════════════                                               │
│   ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐           │
│   │ VPC Module  │  │ EKS Module  │  │ RDS Module  │  │ S3 Module   │           │
│   │             │  │             │  │             │  │             │           │
│   │ • Security  │  │ • IRSA      │  │ • Backups   │  │ • Encryption│           │
│   │ • Compliance│  │ • Logging   │  │ • HA        │  │ • Versioning│           │
│   │ • Standards │  │ • Monitoring│  │ • Encryption│  │ • Lifecycle │           │
│   └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘           │
│          │                │                │                │                   │
│          └────────────────┴────────────────┴────────────────┘                   │
│                                     │                                            │
│                          Published to Registry                                   │
│                                     │                                            │
│          ┌────────────────┬─────────┴─────────┬────────────────┐                │
│          │                │                   │                │                │
│          ▼                ▼                   ▼                ▼                │
│   ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐          │
│   │ App Team A  │  │ App Team B  │   │ App Team C  │  │ Data Team   │          │
│   │             │  │             │   │             │  │             │          │
│   │ Uses: VPC,  │  │ Uses: VPC,  │   │ Uses: VPC,  │  │ Uses: VPC,  │          │
│   │ EKS         │  │ RDS         │   │ EKS, RDS    │  │ S3, RDS     │          │
│   └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘   └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘          │
│                                                                                  │
│   APPLICATION TEAMS (Module Consumers)                                           │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

16.4 Module Structure and Organization

📂

Standard Module Layout

While Terraform doesn't enforce a specific file structure, the community has established conventions. Following the standard structure makes your modules easier for others to understand, use, and contribute to.

📁

16.4.1 Standard Module Structure

Recommended Module File Structure 
terraform-aws-vpc/              # Module root directory
├── main.tf                     # Primary resource definitions
├── variables.tf                # Input variable declarations
├── outputs.tf                  # Output value declarations
├── versions.tf                 # Terraform and provider version constraints
├── README.md                   # Module documentation
├── LICENSE                     # License file (MIT, Apache 2.0, etc.)
├── CHANGELOG.md                # Version history
│
├── modules/                    # Nested submodules (optional)
│   ├── vpc-flow-logs/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   └── vpc-endpoints/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
│
├── examples/                   # Example implementations
│   ├── simple/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── README.md
│   └── complete/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── outputs.tf
│       └── README.md
│
└── tests/                      # Test files (Terraform 1.6+)
    └── vpc.tftest.hcl
File Purpose Required?
main.tf Primary resource definitions and logic Yes (by convention)
variables.tf Input variable declarations with descriptions Yes (by convention)
outputs.tf Output value declarations Yes (by convention)
versions.tf Terraform and provider version constraints Recommended
README.md Documentation, usage examples, inputs/outputs Required for Registry
examples/ Working example implementations Required for Registry
🪆

16.4.2 Nested Modules

🏗️ Breaking Down Complexity

Large modules can be broken into smaller nested modules (submodules). The parent module orchestrates the submodules, while each submodule handles a specific concern. This improves maintainability and allows selective use of functionality.

Nested Module Example 
# modules/vpc/main.tf (Parent module calls submodules)
module "flow_logs" {
  source = "./modules/vpc-flow-logs"
  count  = var.enable_flow_logs ? 1 : 0
  
  vpc_id           = aws_vpc.main.id
  retention_days   = var.flow_log_retention
  traffic_type     = var.flow_log_traffic_type
}

module "endpoints" {
  source = "./modules/vpc-endpoints"
  count  = var.enable_vpc_endpoints ? 1 : 0
  
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  enabled_endpoints   = var.vpc_endpoint_services
}
🧱

16.4.3 Module Composition

📊 Composing Modules Together

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         MODULE COMPOSITION PATTERN                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ROOT MODULE (main.tf)                                                          │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │   module "vpc" {           ───►   ┌─────────────────┐                   │   │
│   │     source = "./modules/vpc"      │  VPC Module     │                   │   │
│   │   }                               │  outputs:       │                   │   │
│   │                                   │  - vpc_id       │                   │   │
│   │                              ┌────│  - subnet_ids   │                   │   │
│   │                              │    └─────────────────┘                   │   │
│   │                              │                                           │   │
│   │   module "security" {       │    ┌─────────────────┐                   │   │
│   │     source = "./modules/sg" │    │  Security Group │                   │   │
│   │     vpc_id = module.vpc.vpc_id   │  Module         │                   │   │
│   │   }       └──────────────────────│  outputs:       │                   │   │
│   │                              ┌───│  - sg_id        │                   │   │
│   │                              │   └─────────────────┘                   │   │
│   │                              │                                           │   │
│   │   module "ec2" {             │   ┌─────────────────┐                   │   │
│   │     source = "./modules/ec2" │   │  EC2 Module     │                   │   │
│   │     subnet_ids = module.vpc.subnet_ids            │                   │   │
│   │     security_groups = [module.security.sg_id]     │                   │   │
│   │   }                              └─────────────────┘                   │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                                  │
│   Modules connect through outputs → inputs relationships                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

16.5 Module Sources 📝 EXAM TOPIC

🔗

Where Modules Come From

The source argument tells Terraform where to find a module. Terraform supports many source types: local paths, the public Terraform Registry, GitHub, Bitbucket, generic Git repositories, S3 buckets, and more. Understanding source types is critical for the exam.

Important: Module sources are evaluated during terraform init, not during plan or apply. If you change a module source, you must run terraform init (or init -upgrade) to download it.

📁

16.5.1 Local Paths (./modules/vpc)

Local Path Sources 
# Relative path (most common)
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"  # Relative to current directory
}

# Parent directory
module "shared" {
  source = "../shared-modules/networking"
}

# Absolute path (not recommended - not portable)
module "legacy" {
  source = "/opt/terraform/modules/legacy-vpc"
}

# Local modules DON'T need terraform init -upgrade
# Changes are picked up immediately on next plan/apply
💡

Local Path Behavior

Local modules are not copied to .terraform/modules/. Terraform references them directly from your filesystem. Changes to local modules take effect immediately without re-running init.

🏪

16.5.2 Terraform Registry

🌐 The Public Module Registry

The Terraform Registry hosts thousands of community and verified modules. Registry modules use a special shorthand syntax and support version constraints. This is the recommended source for production use.

Terraform Registry Sources 
# Public Registry syntax: NAMESPACE/NAME/PROVIDER
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.4.0"  # Always pin versions!
  
  name = "my-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

# Version constraints
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"    # >= 5.0.0, < 6.0.0 (recommended)
}

module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = ">= 5.0, < 6.0"  # Explicit range
}

# Private Registry (HCP Terraform / Terraform Enterprise)
module "vpc" {
  source  = "app.terraform.io/my-org/vpc/aws"
  version = "1.2.0"
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Registry Module Syntax

Registry modules use the format: NAMESPACE/NAME/PROVIDER

  • NAMESPACE: Organization or user (e.g., terraform-aws-modules)
  • NAME: Module name (e.g., vpc)
  • PROVIDER: Target provider (e.g., aws)

The version argument is only supported for Registry modules!

🐙

16.5.3 GitHub (github.com/org/repo)

GitHub Module Sources 
# GitHub shorthand (HTTPS, public repos)
module "vpc" {
  source = "github.com/terraform-aws-modules/terraform-aws-vpc"
}

# With ref (branch, tag, or commit)
module "vpc" {
  source = "github.com/terraform-aws-modules/terraform-aws-vpc?ref=v5.4.0"
}

# Specific subdirectory
module "subnet" {
  source = "github.com/terraform-aws-modules/terraform-aws-vpc//modules/vpc-endpoints?ref=v5.4.0"
}

# SSH for private repos (requires SSH key)
module "internal" {
  source = "git@github.com:my-company/terraform-modules.git//vpc?ref=main"
}

# GitHub Enterprise
module "enterprise" {
  source = "github.example.com/org/terraform-modules//vpc?ref=v1.0.0"
}
🔗

16.5.4 Other Source Types

Additional Module Sources 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BITBUCKET
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
module "vpc" {
  source = "bitbucket.org/my-org/terraform-vpc?ref=v1.0.0"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GENERIC GIT (Any Git server)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
module "vpc" {
  source = "git::https://example.com/modules/vpc.git?ref=v1.0.0"
}

module "vpc" {
  source = "git::ssh://git@example.com/modules/vpc.git?ref=v1.0.0"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# HTTP/HTTPS URLs (zip/tar archives)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
module "vpc" {
  source = "https://example.com/modules/vpc.zip"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# S3 BUCKET
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
module "vpc" {
  source = "s3::https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/my-bucket/modules/vpc.zip"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GCS BUCKET
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
module "vpc" {
  source = "gcs::https://www.googleapis.com/storage/v1/my-bucket/modules/vpc.zip"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AZURE BLOB STORAGE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
module "vpc" {
  source = "https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/modules/vpc.zip"
}
🧭

16.5.10 Module Source Selection Guide

Source Type When to Use Version Control
Local Path Development, monorepo, tightly coupled modules Via surrounding Git repo
Terraform Registry Production, community modules, semantic versioning version argument ✅
GitHub Private modules, internal sharing ?ref= (tag/branch/commit)
Generic Git Self-hosted Git servers (GitLab, etc.) ?ref=
S3/GCS/Azure Air-gapped, highly secure environments Manual (archive naming)
HTTP URL Simple distribution, custom artifact servers Manual (URL naming)
⚠️

Version Argument Limitation

The version argument is only supported for Registry modules! For Git sources, use ?ref=TAG. For local paths, versioning comes from your overall repository. This is a common exam question!

📋 Chapter 16 Summary: Introduction to Modules

📦

Module Definition

  • A module is a container for multiple resources
  • Any directory with .tf files is a module
  • Enables code reuse and abstraction
🌳

Module Types

  • Root module: Working directory
  • Child module: Called via module block
  • Child modules receive inputs, return outputs
📂

Module Structure

  • main.tf - Resource definitions
  • variables.tf - Input declarations
  • outputs.tf - Return values
  • README.md - Documentation
🔗

Module Sources

  • Local: ./modules/vpc
  • Registry: namespace/name/provider
  • GitHub: github.com/org/repo
  • version only works with Registry!
🏗️ 📦 ⚙️
17

Creating Modules - Complete Guide

📑 8 Sections 📝 28 Subsections ⏱️ 55 min read

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Structure modules with proper file organization
2 Design effective input variables with validation
3 Expose outputs for inter-module communication
4 Implement semantic versioning for modules
5 Document modules for discoverability
6 Test modules with terraform test

📑 In This Chapter

17.1 Module File Structure 17.2 Module Input Variables 17.3 Module Output Values 17.4 Module Dependencies 17.5 Module Versioning 17.6 Module Documentation 17.7 Module Testing 17.8 Publishing Modules

17.1 Module File Structure

📂

Organizing Your Module

A well-organized module follows conventions that make it easy to understand, maintain, and share. While Terraform will execute any .tf files in a directory, following standard naming conventions helps users (including future you) quickly navigate the module's structure.

The Standard Convention: Most modules use three core files: main.tf (resources), variables.tf (inputs), and outputs.tf (outputs). This separation isn't required by Terraform, but it's universally expected and makes modules instantly understandable.

📄

17.1.1 Required Files (main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf)

📊 Standard Module Structure

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STANDARD MODULE FILE STRUCTURE                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   modules/vpc/                                                                   │
│   ├── main.tf           # Primary resources - VPC, Subnets, IGW, NAT            │
│   ├── variables.tf      # Input variables - CIDR blocks, tags, AZs              │
│   ├── outputs.tf        # Output values - VPC ID, subnet IDs, etc.              │
│   ├── versions.tf       # Provider and Terraform version constraints            │
│   ├── README.md         # Module documentation                                   │
│   ├── locals.tf         # (Optional) Local values for internal use              │
│   ├── data.tf           # (Optional) Data sources                               │
│   └── examples/         # (Optional) Example configurations                     │
│       ├── simple/                                                                │
│       │   └── main.tf                                                            │
│       └── complete/                                                              │
│           └── main.tf                                                            │
│                                                                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   FILE PURPOSES:                                                                 │
│                                                                                  │
│   main.tf        →  The "what" - actual infrastructure being created            │
│   variables.tf   →  The "inputs" - what callers can customize                   │
│   outputs.tf     →  The "exports" - what callers can reference                  │
│   versions.tf    →  The "requirements" - version constraints                    │
│   README.md      →  The "how to use" - documentation for users                  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
main.tf - Primary Resources 
# modules/vpc/main.tf
# Primary resources for the VPC module

resource "aws_vpc" "this" {
  cidr_block           = var.cidr_block
  enable_dns_hostnames = var.enable_dns_hostnames
  enable_dns_support   = var.enable_dns_support

  tags = merge(
    var.tags,
    {
      Name = var.name
    }
  )
}

resource "aws_internet_gateway" "this" {
  count = var.create_igw ? 1 : 0

  vpc_id = aws_vpc.this.id

  tags = merge(var.tags, { Name = "${var.name}-igw" })
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)

  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.this.id
  cidr_block              = var.public_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone       = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = merge(var.tags, { 
    Name = "${var.name}-public-${count.index + 1}"
    Tier = "public"
  })
}
variables.tf - Input Variables 
# modules/vpc/variables.tf
# Input variables for the VPC module

variable "name" {
  description = "Name to be used as identifier for all resources"
  type        = string
}

variable "cidr_block" {
  description = "The IPv4 CIDR block for the VPC"
  type        = string
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0))
    error_message = "Must be a valid IPv4 CIDR block."
  }
}

variable "public_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "List of CIDR blocks for public subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = []
}

variable "availability_zones" {
  description = "List of availability zones to use"
  type        = list(string)
}

variable "enable_dns_hostnames" {
  description = "Enable DNS hostnames in the VPC"
  type        = bool
  default     = true
}

variable "enable_dns_support" {
  description = "Enable DNS support in the VPC"
  type        = bool
  default     = true
}

variable "create_igw" {
  description = "Whether to create an Internet Gateway"
  type        = bool
  default     = true
}

variable "tags" {
  description = "A map of tags to add to all resources"
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
}
outputs.tf - Output Values 
# modules/vpc/outputs.tf
# Output values for the VPC module

output "vpc_id" {
  description = "The ID of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.this.id
}

output "vpc_cidr_block" {
  description = "The CIDR block of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.this.cidr_block
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  description = "List of IDs of public subnets"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}

output "internet_gateway_id" {
  description = "The ID of the Internet Gateway"
  value       = var.create_igw ? aws_internet_gateway.this[0].id : null
}
📝

17.1.2 Optional Files (README.md, versions.tf)

versions.tf - Version Constraints 
# modules/vpc/versions.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = ">= 5.0.0"
    }
  }
}

📋 README.md Template

Every published module should have a README.md with: description, usage examples, inputs table, outputs table, and requirements.

📚

17.1.3 Examples Directory

examples/simple/main.tf 
# examples/simple/main.tf
# Minimal example showing basic module usage

module "vpc" {
  source = "../../"
  
  name               = "simple-vpc"
  cidr_block         = "10.0.0.0/16"
  availability_zones = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
  
  public_subnet_cidrs = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  
  tags = {
    Environment = "development"
    Example     = "simple"
  }
}
🧪

17.1.4 Tests Directory

📊 Complete Module Directory Structure

terraform-aws-vpc/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── versions.tf
├── locals.tf
├── README.md
├── CHANGELOG.md
├── LICENSE
├── .gitignore
├── .terraform-docs.yml
│
├── examples/
│   ├── simple/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── README.md
│   └── complete/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── outputs.tf
│       └── README.md
│
└── tests/
    ├── simple.tftest.hcl
    └── complete.tftest.hcl

17.2 Module Input Variables

📥

Designing Module Inputs

Input variables are the module's API—they define what callers can customize. Good module design means carefully choosing which aspects should be configurable and providing sensible defaults for optional settings.

⭐

17.2.1 Required vs Optional Variables

REQUIRED
🔴

No Default Value

Caller MUST provide a value. Use for critical configuration.

variable "name" { type = string }
OPTIONAL
🟢

Has Default Value

Caller CAN override. Use for sensible defaults.

variable "port" { default = 80 }
Required vs Optional Pattern 
# REQUIRED - No default, must be provided
variable "vpc_id" {
  description = "The VPC ID where resources will be created"
  type        = string
  # No default = required!
}

# OPTIONAL - Has default, can be overridden
variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t3.micro"  # Sensible default
}

# OPTIONAL with null default - Conditionally use
variable "kms_key_id" {
  description = "KMS key ID for encryption. If null, uses AWS managed key"
  type        = string
  default     = null  # Optional encryption
}
🔤

17.2.2 Variable Types for Modules

Type Use Case Example
string Names, IDs, single values var.environment
number Counts, ports, sizes var.instance_count
bool Feature flags, toggles var.enable_monitoring
list(string) Multiple values of same type var.subnet_ids
map(string) Tags, labels var.tags
object({...}) Complex configuration var.database_config
✅

17.2.3 Variable Validation

Custom Validation Rules 
variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment"
  type        = string
  
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

variable "cidr_block" {
  description = "VPC CIDR block"
  type        = string
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0))
    error_message = "Must be a valid IPv4 CIDR block (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16)."
  }
  
  validation {
    condition     = tonumber(split("/", var.cidr_block)[1]) >= 16
    error_message = "CIDR block must be /16 or larger."
  }
}

variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t3.micro"
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^t[23]\\.", var.instance_type))
    error_message = "Only t2 and t3 instance types are allowed."
  }
}
📋

17.2.4 Variable Defaults

💡

Best Practices for Defaults

  • Provide defaults for optional configuration
  • Use null for truly optional resources
  • Empty lists/maps allow conditional creation
  • Document what happens with default values

17.3 Module Output Values

📤

Exposing Module Data

Outputs are how modules communicate information to their callers. They expose resource attributes that other parts of your configuration need—like IDs, ARNs, endpoints, and IP addresses.

🔗

17.3.1 Exposing Resource Attributes

Standard Output Patterns 
# outputs.tf

# Single resource attribute
output "vpc_id" {
  description = "The ID of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.this.id
}

# Multiple resources (list)
output "subnet_ids" {
  description = "List of subnet IDs"
  value       = aws_subnet.this[*].id
}

# Map of resources
output "subnet_id_by_az" {
  description = "Map of AZ to subnet ID"
  value       = { for s in aws_subnet.this : s.availability_zone => s.id }
}

# Entire resource (for advanced use)
output "vpc" {
  description = "All VPC attributes"
  value       = aws_vpc.this
}
🔢

17.3.2 Computed Outputs

Computed/Derived Outputs 
# Computed URL
output "endpoint_url" {
  description = "Full endpoint URL"
  value       = "https://${aws_lb.this.dns_name}:${var.port}"
}

# Conditional output
output "nat_gateway_ips" {
  description = "NAT Gateway public IPs (if created)"
  value       = var.enable_nat_gateway ? aws_eip.nat[*].public_ip : []
}

# Aggregated data
output "summary" {
  description = "Module deployment summary"
  value = {
    vpc_id         = aws_vpc.this.id
    vpc_cidr       = aws_vpc.this.cidr_block
    public_subnets = length(aws_subnet.public)
    private_subnets = length(aws_subnet.private)
  }
}
🔒

17.3.3 Sensitive Outputs

Marking Outputs as Sensitive 
# Database password - marked sensitive
output "db_password" {
  description = "The master password for the database"
  value       = random_password.db.result
  sensitive   = true  # Won't show in CLI output
}

# Connection string with embedded credentials
output "connection_string" {
  description = "Database connection string"
  value       = "postgresql://${var.db_username}:${random_password.db.result}@${aws_db_instance.this.endpoint}/${var.db_name}"
  sensitive   = true
}
⚠️

Sensitive Output Behavior

Marking an output as sensitive = true hides it in CLI output but does NOT encrypt it in state. State files still contain the plain value!

17.4 Module Dependencies

🔗

Managing Module Relationships

Modules can depend on each other, and Terraform automatically detects most dependencies through reference expressions. Understanding how dependencies work helps you design modules that compose well together.

🔄

17.4.1 Implicit Dependencies

Automatic Dependency Detection 
# VPC module runs first (no dependencies)
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  name       = "production"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

# EC2 module depends on VPC (reference creates dependency)
module "web_servers" {
  source = "./modules/ec2"
  
  # This reference creates implicit dependency:
  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id           # Wait for VPC
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.public_subnet_ids # Wait for subnets
  
  instance_count = 3
}
📌

17.4.2 depends_on in Modules

Explicit Module Dependencies 
# Sometimes dependencies aren't captured by references
module "app" {
  source = "./modules/app"
  
  name = "my-app"
  
  # Explicit dependency when there's no reference
  depends_on = [
    module.iam_roles,  # IAM must exist first
    module.secrets     # Secrets must be created first
  ]
}
🔀

17.4.3 Module-to-Module Dependencies

📊 Module Dependency Graph

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    MODULE DEPENDENCY FLOW                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                           ┌─────────────┐                                        │
│                           │   VPC       │                                        │
│                           │   Module    │                                        │
│                           └──────┬──────┘                                        │
│                                  │                                               │
│              ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐                          │
│              │                   │                   │                          │
│              ▼                   ▼                   ▼                          │
│       ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐                   │
│       │  Security    │   │   Subnets    │   │    IAM       │                   │
│       │  Groups      │   │              │   │   Roles      │                   │
│       └──────┬───────┘   └──────┬───────┘   └──────┬───────┘                   │
│              │                   │                   │                          │
│              └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘                          │
│                                  │                                               │
│                                  ▼                                               │
│                           ┌─────────────┐                                        │
│                           │   EC2       │                                        │
│                           │   Module    │                                        │
│                           └─────────────┘                                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

17.5 Module Versioning

🏷️

Version Control for Modules

Versioning modules allows you to evolve them over time while maintaining stability for existing users. Semantic versioning communicates the impact of changes and helps callers choose appropriate version constraints.

📊

17.5.1 Semantic Versioning

📊 Semantic Versioning Explained

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SEMANTIC VERSIONING (SemVer)                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                            v 2 . 1 . 4                                          │
│                              │   │   │                                          │
│                              │   │   └── PATCH: Bug fixes, no API changes       │
│                              │   │         → Safe to upgrade automatically      │
│                              │   │                                               │
│                              │   └────── MINOR: New features, backward compat   │
│                              │             → New inputs with defaults            │
│                              │             → New outputs                         │
│                              │             → Safe to upgrade (usually)           │
│                              │                                                   │
│                              └────────── MAJOR: Breaking changes                 │
│                                           → Removed/renamed variables            │
│                                           → Changed behavior                     │
│                                           → REQUIRES attention to upgrade        │
│                                                                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Examples:                                                                      │
