│   • 1.0.0 → 1.0.1  (patch)  Fixed bug in subnet calculation                    │
│   • 1.0.1 → 1.1.0  (minor)  Added optional NAT gateway support                 │
│   • 1.1.0 → 2.0.0  (major)  Changed 'subnets' from list to map                 │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📏

17.5.2 Version Constraints

Constraint Meaning Example Matches
= 1.2.3 Exact version only 1.2.3
>= 1.2.0 Minimum version 1.2.0, 1.3.0, 2.0.0
~> 1.2.0 Pessimistic (patch only) 1.2.0, 1.2.5 (not 1.3.0)
~> 1.2 Pessimistic (minor updates) 1.2, 1.9 (not 2.0)
>= 1.0, < 2.0 Range with upper bound 1.0.0 through 1.99.99
📝

EXAM TIP: ~> Constraint

The pessimistic constraint (~>) allows only the rightmost version component to increment. ~> 1.2.0 allows 1.2.x but not 1.3.0. This is the recommended constraint for production modules.

📌

17.5.3 Version Pinning Best Practices

Production Version Pinning 
# Production: Pin to specific or pessimistic
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.1.0"  # Allow 5.1.x patches only
}

# Development: More flexibility
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = ">= 5.0.0, < 6.0.0"  # Any 5.x version
}

# Experimentation: Latest (not for production!)
module "vpc" {
  source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  # No version = latest (DANGEROUS in prod)
}
🏷️

17.5.4 Git Tags as Versions

Using Git Tags for Versions 
# Reference specific Git tag
module "vpc" {
  source = "github.com/myorg/terraform-vpc?ref=v1.2.3"
}

# Reference branch (for development)
module "vpc" {
  source = "github.com/myorg/terraform-vpc?ref=main"
}

# Reference specific commit (most precise)
module "vpc" {
  source = "github.com/myorg/terraform-vpc?ref=abc1234"
}

17.6 Module Documentation

📚

Documenting for Users

Good documentation makes the difference between a module that gets adopted and one that gets abandoned. Every published module needs clear README, working examples, and kept-up-to-date input/output documentation.

📝

17.6.1 README.md Structure

Standard README Template 
# AWS VPC Terraform Module

Terraform module to create a production-ready VPC with public and 
private subnets, NAT gateways, and VPC endpoints.

## Features

- Multi-AZ deployment
- Public and private subnets
- Optional NAT Gateway (single or per-AZ)
- VPC Flow Logs support
- VPC Endpoints for AWS services

## Usage

```hcl
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.1.0"

  name = "production"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway = true
}
```

## Requirements

| Name | Version |
|------|---------|
| terraform | >= 1.5.0 |
| aws | >= 5.0 |

## Inputs

| Name | Description | Type | Default | Required |
|------|-------------|------|---------|:--------:|
| name | VPC name | `string` | n/a | yes |
| cidr | VPC CIDR block | `string` | n/a | yes |

## Outputs

| Name | Description |
|------|-------------|
| vpc_id | The ID of the VPC |
| private_subnet_ids | List of private subnet IDs |
🔧

17.6.2 terraform-docs Tool

Auto-Generate Documentation 
# Install terraform-docs
$ brew install terraform-docs   # macOS
$ choco install terraform-docs  # Windows

# Generate markdown documentation
$ terraform-docs markdown table . > README.md

# With custom template
$ terraform-docs markdown table --output-file README.md .

# Configuration file (.terraform-docs.yml)
formatter: markdown table
output:
  file: README.md
  mode: inject

content: |
  {{ .Header }}
  ## Usage
  {{ include "examples/simple/main.tf" }}
  {{ .Requirements }}
  {{ .Inputs }}
  {{ .Outputs }}
📖

17.6.3 Examples Documentation

EXAMPLE
📁

examples/simple/

Minimal configuration showing basic usage

EXAMPLE
📁

examples/complete/

Full-featured configuration with all options

📋

17.6.4 Changelog Maintenance

📋 CHANGELOG.md Format

Follow Keep a Changelog format: document Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, and Security changes for each version.

17.7 Module Testing

🧪

Validating Module Behavior

Testing modules ensures they work as expected before deployment. Terraform 1.6+ includes native testing support with terraform test, making it easier than ever to validate your modules.

📝

17.7.1 Example-Based Testing

Testing via Examples 
# Basic validation: Does the module plan successfully?
$ cd examples/simple
$ terraform init
$ terraform validate
$ terraform plan

# This catches:
# - Syntax errors
# - Missing required variables  
# - Invalid references
# - Type mismatches
🧪

17.7.3 terraform test (Native)

tests/vpc.tftest.hcl 
# tests/vpc.tftest.hcl
# Native Terraform testing (1.6+)

variables {
  name       = "test-vpc"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  azs        = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
}

run "vpc_creates_successfully" {
  command = plan
  
  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.this.cidr_block == "10.0.0.0/16"
    error_message = "VPC CIDR block mismatch"
  }
  
  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.this.enable_dns_hostnames == true
    error_message = "DNS hostnames should be enabled"
  }
}

run "outputs_are_valid" {
  command = plan
  
  assert {
    condition     = output.vpc_id != ""
    error_message = "VPC ID output should not be empty"
  }
}
Running Tests 
# Run all tests
$ terraform test

# Run specific test file
$ terraform test -filter=tests/vpc.tftest.hcl

# Verbose output
$ terraform test -verbose

17.8 Publishing Modules

🚀

Sharing Your Modules

Once your module is ready, you can share it via the public Terraform Registry, a private registry, or directly from version control. The Registry provides discoverability and version management.

📋

17.8.1 Terraform Registry Requirements

REQUIRED
📛

Repository Naming

terraform-<PROVIDER>-<NAME>

e.g., terraform-aws-vpc
REQUIRED
🏷️

Semantic Versions

Git tags as vX.Y.Z

e.g., v1.0.0, v2.1.3
REQUIRED
📄

README.md

Documentation at root

Usage, inputs, outputs
REQUIRED
📂

Standard Structure

main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf

At repository root
🐙

17.8.2 GitHub Integration

Publishing Workflow 
# 1. Create GitHub repository with correct naming
# Repository: terraform-aws-vpc

# 2. Push your module code
$ git init
$ git add .
$ git commit -m "Initial module release"
$ git remote add origin git@github.com:myorg/terraform-aws-vpc.git
$ git push -u origin main

# 3. Create a version tag
$ git tag v1.0.0
$ git push --tags

# 4. Sign in to registry.terraform.io with GitHub
# 5. Publish module (auto-detects from GitHub)

# Module address will be:
# myorg/vpc/aws
🔒

17.8.3 Private Registry Publishing

HCP Terraform Private Registry 
# Using private module from HCP Terraform
module "vpc" {
  source  = "app.terraform.io/my-org/vpc/aws"
  version = "1.0.0"
  
  # ... variables
}

📋 Chapter 17 Summary: Creating Modules

📂

Module Structure

  • main.tf - Primary resources
  • variables.tf - Input definitions
  • outputs.tf - Export values
  • versions.tf - Constraints
📥

Input Variables

  • Required: No default value
  • Optional: Has default
  • Use validation blocks
  • Type constraints for safety
🏷️

Versioning

  • Semantic: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
  • ~> 1.2 pessimistic constraint
  • Git tags for versions
  • Pin versions in production
🧪

Testing

  • terraform test native (1.6+)
  • .tftest.hcl test files
  • Assert conditions and outputs
  • Example-based validation
🔧 🔄 ⚡
18

Using Modules - Advanced

📑 6 Sections 📝 24 Subsections ⏱️ 45 min read

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Use count and for_each with modules
2 Pass providers to child modules
3 Master module composition patterns
4 Refactor configurations for modularity
5 Handle module-specific provider configurations
6 Debug module relationship issues

📑 In This Chapter

18.1 count with Modules 18.2 for_each with Modules 18.3 Module Composition Patterns 18.4 Refactoring with Modules 18.5 Module Providers ⭐ 18.6 Module Debugging

18.1 count with Modules

🔢

Creating Multiple Module Instances

Just like resources, modules can use count to create multiple instances. This is useful when you need identical copies of infrastructure (like multiple environments) or want to conditionally include/exclude a module.

📊

18.1.1 Basic Module count

Creating Multiple Module Instances 
# Create 3 identical VPCs
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  count  = 3
  
  name       = "vpc-${count.index}"
  cidr_block = "10.${count.index}.0.0/16"
}

# Reference outputs from counted modules
output "vpc_ids" {
  value = module.vpc[*].vpc_id
}

# Access specific instance
output "first_vpc_id" {
  value = module.vpc[0].vpc_id
}

📊 Module count Creates Indexed Instances

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    MODULE COUNT INDEXING                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   module "vpc" {                                                                │
│     count = 3                                                                    │
│   }                                                                              │
│                                                                                  │
│         Creates:                                                                 │
│                                                                                  │
│         module.vpc[0]  ──────►  VPC "vpc-0" (10.0.0.0/16)                       │
│         module.vpc[1]  ──────►  VPC "vpc-1" (10.1.0.0/16)                       │
│         module.vpc[2]  ──────►  VPC "vpc-2" (10.2.0.0/16)                       │
│                                                                                  │
│   Reference all:   module.vpc[*].vpc_id                                         │
│   Reference one:   module.vpc[0].vpc_id                                         │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔀

18.1.2 Conditional Module Creation

Using count for Conditional Modules 
# Conditionally create module based on variable
variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

module "monitoring" {
  source = "./modules/monitoring"
  count  = var.enable_monitoring ? 1 : 0
  
  vpc_id = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

# Safe reference to conditional module
output "monitoring_endpoint" {
  value = var.enable_monitoring ? module.monitoring[0].endpoint : null
}

# Using one() for cleaner conditional references (Terraform 1.3+)
output "monitoring_endpoint_v2" {
  value = one(module.monitoring[*].endpoint)
}
💡

The one() Function Pattern

Use one(module.name[*].output) to get a single value from a conditional module. Returns null if count is 0, or the value if count is 1.

⚠️

18.1.3 count Limitations

⚠️

Index-Based Problems

  • Removing item from middle causes all subsequent items to shift
  • Index 2 becomes index 1, forcing recreation
  • For dynamic collections, prefer for_each

18.2 for_each with Modules

🔄

Key-Based Module Instances

Using for_each with modules creates instances identified by keys rather than indices. This is the preferred approach for dynamic collections because adding/removing items doesn't affect other instances.

🗺️

18.2.1 Map-Based for_each

Module for_each with Map 
variable "environments" {
  type = map(object({
    cidr_block     = string
    instance_type  = string
    instance_count = number
  }))
  
  default = {
    dev = {
      cidr_block     = "10.0.0.0/16"
      instance_type  = "t3.micro"
      instance_count = 1
    }
    staging = {
      cidr_block     = "10.1.0.0/16"
      instance_type  = "t3.small"
      instance_count = 2
    }
    prod = {
      cidr_block     = "10.2.0.0/16"
      instance_type  = "t3.medium"
      instance_count = 3
    }
  }
}

module "environment" {
  source   = "./modules/environment"
  for_each = var.environments
  
  name           = each.key                # "dev", "staging", "prod"
  cidr_block     = each.value.cidr_block
  instance_type  = each.value.instance_type
  instance_count = each.value.instance_count
}

# Access by key
output "prod_vpc_id" {
  value = module.environment["prod"].vpc_id
}

# All VPC IDs as map
output "all_vpc_ids" {
  value = { for k, v in module.environment : k => v.vpc_id }
}
📋

18.2.2 Set-Based for_each

Module for_each with Set 
# Using toset() for simple lists
variable "regions" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-east-1", "us-west-2", "eu-west-1"]
}

module "regional_vpc" {
  source   = "./modules/vpc"
  for_each = toset(var.regions)
  
  providers = {
    aws = aws.regions[each.key]
  }
  
  name       = "vpc-${each.key}"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

# Access specific region
output "us_east_vpc" {
  value = module.regional_vpc["us-east-1"].vpc_id
}
📊

18.2.3 for_each vs count Comparison

Aspect count for_each
Identification Index (0, 1, 2...) Key ("dev", "prod"...)
Reference module.x[0] module.x["key"]
Removal Impact Shifts all subsequent Only affects that key
Best For Identical copies, conditionals Dynamic collections
Input Type Number Map or Set

18.3 Module Composition Patterns

🏗️

Building Complex Infrastructure

Real-world infrastructure combines multiple modules together. Understanding composition patterns helps you design flexible, reusable architectures that can be assembled in different ways.

📦

18.3.1 Flat Composition

📊 Flat Composition Pattern

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FLAT COMPOSITION                                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Root Module (main.tf)                                                          │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │                                                                       │     │
│   │   ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐ │     │
│   │   │   module    │  │   module    │  │   module    │  │   module    │ │     │
│   │   │   "vpc"     │──│   "ec2"     │──│   "rds"     │──│   "alb"     │ │     │
│   │   └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘ │     │
│   │                                                                       │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                                  │
│   Characteristics:                                                               │
│   • All modules at same level                                                    │
│   • Root orchestrates all connections                                            │
│   • Simple to understand                                                         │
│   • Good for smaller projects                                                    │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Flat Composition Example 
# Root module coordinates all modules
module "vpc" {
  source     = "./modules/vpc"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

module "security" {
  source = "./modules/security"
  vpc_id = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

module "database" {
  source            = "./modules/rds"
  vpc_id            = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids        = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_id = module.security.db_sg_id
}

module "application" {
  source            = "./modules/ec2"
  vpc_id            = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids        = module.vpc.public_subnet_ids
  security_group_id = module.security.app_sg_id
  db_endpoint       = module.database.endpoint
}
🏢

18.3.2 Nested Composition

📊 Nested Composition Pattern

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    NESTED COMPOSITION                                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Root Module                                                                    │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │                                                                       │     │
│   │   module "platform"                                                   │     │
│   │   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │     │
│   │   │                                                               │  │     │
│   │   │   module "network"                                            │  │     │
│   │   │   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │  │     │
│   │   │   │   module "vpc"     module "subnets"                 │    │  │     │
│   │   │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │  │     │
│   │   │                                                               │  │     │
│   │   │   module "compute"                                            │  │     │
│   │   │   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │  │     │
│   │   │   │   module "ec2"     module "asg"                     │    │  │     │
│   │   │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │  │     │
│   │   │                                                               │  │     │
│   │   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │     │
│   │                                                                       │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                                  │
│   Characteristics:                                                               │
│   • Modules contain other modules                                                │
│   • Creates logical groupings                                                    │
│   • Hides complexity from root                                                   │
│   • Good for large, complex systems                                              │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯

18.3.3 Facade Pattern

Facade Module - Simplified Interface 
# modules/complete-app/main.tf
# Facade module that hides complexity

module "vpc" {
  source     = "../vpc"
  cidr_block = var.cidr_block
}

module "rds" {
  source     = "../rds"
  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
}

module "ecs" {
  source      = "../ecs"
  vpc_id      = module.vpc.vpc_id
  db_endpoint = module.rds.endpoint
}

# User of facade just says:
module "my_app" {
  source     = "./modules/complete-app"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  # That's it! All complexity hidden
}

18.4 Refactoring with Modules

♻️

Restructuring Existing Code

Moving resources into modules (or between modules) requires careful handling to avoid destroying and recreating infrastructure. The moved block and terraform state mv are your tools for safe refactoring.

📦

18.4.1 Extracting Resources to Modules

Using moved Block (Terraform 1.1+) 
# BEFORE: Resource in root module
# resource "aws_vpc" "main" { ... }

# AFTER: Resource moved to child module
module "networking" {
  source = "./modules/networking"
}

# Tell Terraform about the move
moved {
  from = aws_vpc.main
  to   = module.networking.aws_vpc.this
}

# Terraform will update state without recreation
🔧

18.4.2 terraform state mv

Manual State Movement 
# Move resource into module
$ terraform state mv aws_vpc.main module.networking.aws_vpc.this

# Move entire module
$ terraform state mv module.old_name module.new_name

# Move resource between modules
$ terraform state mv module.a.aws_instance.this module.b.aws_instance.this

# Preview what would happen (dry-run)
$ terraform state mv -dry-run aws_vpc.main module.networking.aws_vpc.this
⚠️

Always Backup State First!

Before any state manipulation, create a backup with terraform state pull > backup.tfstate

↪️

18.4.3 Renaming Modules

Safe Module Renaming 
# Rename module "old_vpc" to module "network"
moved {
  from = module.old_vpc
  to   = module.network
}

module "network" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  # ...
}

18.5 Module Providers EXAM TOPIC

🔌

Provider Configuration in Modules

Modules inherit providers from their calling module by default. However, you can explicitly pass provider configurations to modules for multi-region or multi-account deployments. This is a common exam topic!

⬇️

18.5.1 Default Provider Inheritance

Automatic Provider Passing 
# Root module defines provider
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-west-2"
}

# Child module automatically inherits this provider
module "vpc" {
  source     = "./modules/vpc"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  # No 'providers' block = inherits default aws provider
}
📤

18.5.2 Explicit Provider Passing

Passing Aliased Providers to Modules 
# Root module - Multiple provider configurations
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "us_east"
  region = "us-east-1"
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "us_west"
  region = "us-west-2"
}

# Pass specific provider to module
module "vpc_east" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  providers = {
    aws = aws.us_east  # Use the us_east provider
  }
  
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

module "vpc_west" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  providers = {
    aws = aws.us_west  # Use the us_west provider
  }
  
  cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"
}

📊 Provider Flow to Modules

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    EXPLICIT PROVIDER PASSING                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   Root Module                                                                    │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │  provider "aws" { alias = "east", region = "us-east-1" }              │     │
│   │  provider "aws" { alias = "west", region = "us-west-2" }              │     │
│   │                                                                       │     │
│   │      ┌────────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐               │     │
│   │      │  providers = {     │     │  providers = {     │               │     │
│   │      │    aws = aws.east  │     │    aws = aws.west  │               │     │
│   │      │  }                 │     │  }                 │               │     │
│   │      └──────────┬─────────┘     └──────────┬─────────┘               │     │
│   │                 │                          │                          │     │
│   │                 ▼                          ▼                          │     │
│   │      ┌──────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐                 │     │
│   │      │  module.vpc_east │       │  module.vpc_west │                 │     │
│   │      │   (us-east-1)    │       │   (us-west-2)    │                 │     │
│   │      └──────────────────┘       └──────────────────┘                 │     │
│   │                                                                       │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📋

18.5.3 required_providers in Modules

Module Provider Requirements 
# modules/vpc/versions.tf
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = ">= 5.0"
      # Modules declare requirements, NOT configurations
    }
  }
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Provider Configurations

Child modules should NOT contain provider blocks with configuration. They should only have required_providers in the terraform block. Provider configurations belong in the root module.

🌐

18.5.4 Multi-Region Module Patterns

Multi-Region Deployment 
# Root module - providers for each region
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"  # Default provider
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "eu"
  region = "eu-west-1"
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "ap"
  region = "ap-southeast-1"
}

# Deploy same module to multiple regions
module "us_app" {
  source = "./modules/app"
  # Uses default provider (us-east-1)
}

module "eu_app" {
  source = "./modules/app"
  providers = { aws = aws.eu }
}

module "ap_app" {
  source = "./modules/app"
  providers = { aws = aws.ap }
}

18.6 Module Debugging

🔍

Troubleshooting Module Issues

When modules don't behave as expected, you need techniques to understand what's happening inside them. Terraform provides several tools for debugging module configurations.

🔧

18.6.1 Debug Outputs

Adding Debug Outputs 
# Temporary debug output in module
output "debug_subnet_calculation" {
  value = {
    input_cidrs   = var.subnet_cidrs
    calculated    = local.subnet_map
    resource_ids  = aws_subnet.this[*].id
  }
}

# Run plan to see values
# $ terraform plan
📊

18.6.2 terraform console for Modules

Interactive Module Debugging 
$ terraform console

# Access module outputs
> module.vpc.vpc_id
"vpc-abc123"

# Access all module outputs
> module.vpc
{
  "vpc_id" = "vpc-abc123"
  "subnet_ids" = ["subnet-1", "subnet-2"]
}

# Access counted/for_each modules
> module.environment["prod"].vpc_id
> module.vpc[0].vpc_id
📋

18.6.3 TF_LOG for Module Traces

Enabling Debug Logging 
# Enable detailed logging
$ export TF_LOG=DEBUG
$ terraform plan

# Log levels: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR

# Log to file
$ export TF_LOG_PATH="terraform.log"

# Windows PowerShell
$env:TF_LOG = "DEBUG"
$env:TF_LOG_PATH = "terraform.log"
❌

18.6.4 Common Module Errors

Error Cause Solution
"Module not found" Wrong source path Run terraform init, check path
"Required variable not set" Missing input Add the required variable in module block
"Output not found" Typo or missing output Check module's outputs.tf
"Provider configuration not present" Module needs provider Add providers block to module
"Cycle detected" Circular dependency Restructure module relationships

📋 Chapter 18 Summary: Using Modules - Advanced

🔢

count & for_each

  • count for identical copies
  • for_each for keyed instances
  • for_each preferred for collections
  • one() for conditional refs
🏗️

Composition

  • Flat: All modules at root level
  • Nested: Modules within modules
  • Facade: Simplified interface
  • Choose based on complexity
🔌

Providers

  • Default inheritance from parent
  • Explicit providers block
  • Multi-region via aliases
  • No configurations in child modules
♻️

Refactoring

  • moved block (1.1+)
  • terraform state mv
  • Always backup state first
  • Test with plan before apply
☁️ 📦 🔗
19

AWS Modules from Terraform Registry

📑 5 Sections 📝 20 Subsections ⏱️ 40 min read

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Navigate and search the Terraform Registry
2 Use verified AWS VPC modules
3 Deploy EC2 instances with registry modules
4 Create S3 buckets using best-practice modules
5 Combine multiple registry modules
6 Evaluate module quality and maintenance

📑 In This Chapter

19.1 Terraform Registry Overview 19.2 VPC Module Examples 19.3 EC2 Module Examples 19.4 S3 Module Examples 19.5 Combining Registry Modules

19.1 Terraform Registry Overview

🌐

Your Module Marketplace

The Terraform Registry at registry.terraform.io is the central repository for discovering modules and providers. It hosts thousands of community and verified modules that implement AWS best practices.

🔍

19.1.1 Browsing the Registry

📊 Registry Module Information

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAFORM REGISTRY MODULE PAGE                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws                                          │   │
│   │  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │  ✓ VERIFIED MODULE              v5.5.3           ⬇️ 45M Downloads       │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │   │
│   │  │  Provision Instructions │ Inputs │ Outputs │ Dependencies │ ... │   │   │
│   │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   │  module "vpc" {                                                         │   │
│   │    source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"                            │   │
│   │    version = "5.5.3"                                                    │   │
│   │    ...                                                                   │   │
│   │  }                                                                       │   │
│   │                                                                          │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                                  │
│   Key Information:                                                               │
│   • Source path for module block                                                 │
│   • Available versions                                                           │
│   • Required inputs                                                              │
│   • Available outputs                                                            │
│   • Usage examples                                                               │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅

19.1.2 Verified vs Community Modules

VERIFIED
✓

Verified Modules

  • Maintained by HashiCorp partners
  • Reviewed for quality
  • Regular updates
  • Recommended for production
COMMUNITY
👥

Community Modules

  • Created by community members
  • Varying quality levels
  • Check activity/stars
  • Review before production use
📋

19.1.3 Module Naming Convention

Registry Module Source Format 
# Registry module source format:
# <NAMESPACE>/<NAME>/<PROVIDER>

module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  #         └── namespace ──┘ └─┘ └─┘
  #                          name  provider
  version = "5.5.3"
}

# Popular AWS module namespaces:
# • terraform-aws-modules - Official AWS modules
# • cloudposse - Well-maintained community modules
# • hashicorp - HashiCorp's own modules
⚙️

19.1.4 Using Registry Modules

Basic Registry Module Usage 
# Step 1: Find module on registry.terraform.io
# Step 2: Copy the provision instructions

module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.5.3"
  
  # Step 3: Configure required inputs
  name = "my-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

# Step 4: Initialize to download
# $ terraform init

# Step 5: Use outputs from module
output "vpc_id" {
  value = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

19.2 VPC Module Examples

🌐

Production-Ready VPC

The terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws module is one of the most popular modules on the Registry. It creates a production-ready VPC with public/private subnets, NAT gateways, route tables, and more.

🚀

19.2.1 terraform-aws-modules/vpc

Basic VPC Module Usage 
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.5.3"

  name = "production-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  # Availability Zones
  azs             = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
  
  # Subnet CIDRs
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24", "10.0.103.0/24"]

  # NAT Gateway - single for cost savings in non-prod
  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = true

  # DNS
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = {
    Terraform   = "true"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}
🏗️

19.2.2 Complete VPC Configuration

Full-Featured VPC 
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.5.3"

  name = "complete-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs              = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"]
  private_subnets  = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
  public_subnets   = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24", "10.0.103.0/24"]
  database_subnets = ["10.0.201.0/24", "10.0.202.0/24", "10.0.203.0/24"]

  # One NAT gateway per AZ (production recommended)
  enable_nat_gateway     = true
  single_nat_gateway     = false
  one_nat_gateway_per_az = true

  # Database subnet group
  create_database_subnet_group = true
  create_database_subnet_route_table = true

  # VPC Flow Logs
  enable_flow_log                      = true
  create_flow_log_cloudwatch_log_group = true
  create_flow_log_cloudwatch_iam_role  = true

  # VPC Endpoints
  enable_s3_endpoint       = true
  enable_dynamodb_endpoint = true

  tags = {
    Terraform   = "true"
    Environment = "production"
  }

  # Subnet tags for Kubernetes
  public_subnet_tags = {
    "kubernetes.io/role/elb" = 1
  }
  private_subnet_tags = {
    "kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb" = 1
  }
}
📤

19.2.3 VPC Module Outputs

Common VPC Module Outputs 
# Access module outputs
output "vpc_id" {
  value = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value = module.vpc.private_subnets
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  value = module.vpc.public_subnets
}

output "database_subnet_group_name" {
  value = module.vpc.database_subnet_group_name
}

output "nat_gateway_ips" {
  value = module.vpc.nat_public_ips
}

19.3 EC2 Module Examples

🖥️

EC2 Instance Provisioning

The EC2 module simplifies instance creation with sensible defaults for security, monitoring, and IAM. It handles the complexity of instance creation while exposing all necessary customization options.

🚀

19.3.1 terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance

Basic EC2 Instance 
module "ec2_instance" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance/aws"
  version = "5.6.0"

  name = "web-server"

  instance_type          = "t3.micro"
  ami                    = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"  # Amazon Linux 2
  
  # Network configuration
  subnet_id              = module.vpc.private_subnets[0]
  vpc_security_group_ids = [module.security_group.security_group_id]

  # Storage
  root_block_device = [
    {
      volume_type = "gp3"
      volume_size = 20
      encrypted   = true
    }
  ]

  tags = {
    Environment = "production"
  }
}
🔢

19.3.2 Multiple Instances

Creating Multiple EC2 Instances 
module "ec2_cluster" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance/aws"
  version = "5.6.0"

  for_each = toset(["web-1", "web-2", "web-3"])

  name = each.key

  instance_type          = "t3.small"
  ami                    = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  subnet_id              = element(module.vpc.private_subnets, index(tolist(toset(["web-1", "web-2", "web-3"])), each.key))
  vpc_security_group_ids = [module.security_group.security_group_id]

  tags = {
    Environment = "production"
    Role        = "web-server"
  }
}

# Get latest Amazon Linux AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]
  }
}
📜

19.3.3 EC2 with User Data

EC2 with Bootstrap Script 
module "ec2_webserver" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance/aws"
  version = "5.6.0"

  name = "nginx-server"

  instance_type = "t3.small"
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  
  subnet_id                   = module.vpc.public_subnets[0]
  vpc_security_group_ids      = [module.web_sg.security_group_id]
  associate_public_ip_address = true

  # User data for bootstrapping
  user_data = <<-EOF
              #!/bin/bash
              yum update -y
              amazon-linux-extras install nginx1 -y
              systemctl start nginx
              systemctl enable nginx
              EOF

  user_data_replace_on_change = true

  tags = {
    Environment = "production"
  }
}

19.4 S3 Module Examples

🪣

Secure S3 Bucket Creation

The S3 module implements security best practices by default—encryption, versioning, access logging, and blocking public access. This helps you create compliant buckets without remembering all the security settings.

🚀

19.4.1 terraform-aws-modules/s3-bucket

Secure S3 Bucket 
module "s3_bucket" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/s3-bucket/aws"
  version = "4.1.0"

  bucket = "my-application-data-${random_id.suffix.hex}"

  # Block all public access (security best practice)
  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true

  # Versioning
  versioning = {
    enabled = true
  }

  # Server-side encryption
  server_side_encryption_configuration = {
    rule = {
      apply_server_side_encryption_by_default = {
        sse_algorithm = "AES256"
      }
    }
  }

  tags = {
    Name        = "application-data"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}

resource "random_id" "suffix" {
  byte_length = 4
}
🌐

19.4.2 S3 Static Website Hosting

S3 Website Bucket 
module "s3_website" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/s3-bucket/aws"
  version = "4.1.0"

  bucket = "my-static-website-${random_id.suffix.hex}"

  # Allow public read for website
  block_public_acls       = false
  block_public_policy     = false
  ignore_public_acls      = false
  restrict_public_buckets = false

  # Website configuration
  website = {
    index_document = "index.html"
    error_document = "error.html"
  }

  # Bucket policy for public read
  attach_policy = true
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid       = "PublicReadGetObject"
        Effect    = "Allow"
        Principal = "*"
        Action    = "s3:GetObject"
        Resource  = "arn:aws:s3:::my-static-website-${random_id.suffix.hex}/*"
      }
    ]
  })

  tags = {
    Purpose = "static-website"
  }
}
📋

19.4.3 S3 with Lifecycle Rules

S3 Bucket with Lifecycle Management 
module "s3_logs" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/s3-bucket/aws"
  version = "4.1.0"

  bucket = "application-logs-${random_id.suffix.hex}"

  # Lifecycle rules for cost optimization
  lifecycle_rule = [
    {
      id      = "log-retention"
      enabled = true

      transition = [
        {
          days          = 30
          storage_class = "STANDARD_IA"
        },
        {
          days          = 90
          storage_class = "GLACIER"
        }
      ]

      expiration = {
        days = 365
      }

      noncurrent_version_expiration = {
        days = 30
      }
    }
  ]

  tags = {
    Purpose = "logs"
  }
}

19.5 Combining Registry Modules

🔗

Building Complete Architectures

Real-world deployments combine multiple modules together. By passing outputs from one module as inputs to another, you create complete architectures from reusable building blocks.

🏗️

19.5.1 Complete AWS Environment

Multi-Module AWS Architecture 
# VPC Module
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.5.3"

  name = "production"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = true
}

# Security Group Module
module "web_sg" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/security-group/aws"
  version = "5.1.0"

  name        = "web-sg"
  description = "Security group for web servers"
  vpc_id      = module.vpc.vpc_id  # Output from VPC module

  ingress_cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  ingress_rules       = ["http-80-tcp", "https-443-tcp"]
  
  egress_rules = ["all-all"]
}

# EC2 Instances
module "ec2_instances" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance/aws"
  version = "5.6.0"

  for_each = toset(["web-1", "web-2"])

  name = each.key

  instance_type          = "t3.small"
  ami                    = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  subnet_id              = element(module.vpc.private_subnets, index(keys(toset(["web-1", "web-2"])), each.key) % length(module.vpc.private_subnets))
  vpc_security_group_ids = [module.web_sg.security_group_id]
}

# ALB Module
module "alb" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/alb/aws"
  version = "9.4.0"

  name    = "production-alb"
  vpc_id  = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnets = module.vpc.public_subnets

  security_groups = [module.web_sg.security_group_id]

  listeners = {
    http = {
      port     = 80
      protocol = "HTTP"
      forward = {
        target_group_key = "web"
      }
    }
  }

  target_groups = {
    web = {
      name_prefix       = "web-"
      protocol          = "HTTP"
      port              = 80
      target_type       = "instance"
      
      health_check = {
        enabled             = true
        path                = "/"
        healthy_threshold   = 2
        unhealthy_threshold = 3
      }
    }
  }
}
📊

19.5.2 Module Connection Diagram

📊 Module Dependencies Flow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    COMBINED REGISTRY MODULES                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│                        ┌──────────────────────┐                                 │
│                        │     VPC Module       │                                 │
│                        │  (vpc/aws v5.5.3)    │                                 │
│                        └──────────┬───────────┘                                 │
│                                   │                                              │
│              ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐                        │
│              │                    │                    │                        │
│              ▼                    ▼                    ▼                        │
│   ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐                │
│   │  Security Group │  │   EC2 Module    │  │   ALB Module    │                │
│   │  (sg v5.1.0)    │  │  (ec2 v5.6.0)   │  │  (alb v9.4.0)   │                │
│   └────────┬────────┘  └────────┬────────┘  └────────┬────────┘                │
│            │                    │                    │                          │
│            └────────────────────┼────────────────────┘                          │
│                                 │                                                │
│                                 ▼                                                │
│                    ┌─────────────────────────┐                                  │
│                    │   Working Application   │                                  │
│                    │   VPC + SG + EC2 + ALB  │                                  │
│                    └─────────────────────────┘                                  │
│                                                                                  │
│   Data Flow:                                                                     │
│   • vpc.vpc_id → sg, alb                                                        │
│   • vpc.private_subnets → ec2                                                   │
│   • vpc.public_subnets → alb                                                    │
│   • sg.security_group_id → ec2, alb                                             │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡

19.5.3 Best Practices for Registry Modules

💡

Registry Module Best Practices

  • Pin versions - Always specify version constraints
  • Prefer verified - Use verified modules when available
  • Check activity - Review recent commits and issues
  • Read documentation - Understand all inputs/outputs
  • Test in dev - Validate before production use
  • Lock versions - Use lock file for consistency

📋 Chapter 19 Summary: AWS Modules from Registry

🌐

Registry Basics

  • registry.terraform.io
  • NAMESPACE/NAME/PROVIDER format
  • Verified vs community modules
  • Always specify version
🌐

VPC Module

  • terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws
  • Multi-AZ, public/private subnets
  • NAT gateways, flow logs
  • VPC endpoints support
🖥️

EC2 Module

  • terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance
  • Single or multiple instances
  • User data support
  • Use for_each for clusters
🪣

S3 Module

  • terraform-aws-modules/s3-bucket
  • Secure by default
  • Lifecycle rules
  • Website hosting option
⚡ 🔄 🚀
20

Core Workflow Commands

📑 7 Sections 📝 28 Subsections ⏱️ 50 min read

🎯 Learning Objectives

1 Master the init-plan-apply workflow
2 Understand terraform init internals
3 Interpret terraform plan output
4 Execute terraform apply safely
5 Validate configurations pre-apply
6 Format and lint Terraform code

📑 In This Chapter

20.1 terraform init ⭐ 20.2 terraform validate 20.3 terraform fmt 20.4 terraform plan ⭐ 20.5 terraform apply ⭐ 20.6 terraform destroy 20.7 Workflow Best Practices

20.1 terraform init EXAM TOPIC

🚀

Initializing Your Configuration

terraform init is the first command you run in any Terraform configuration. It initializes the working directory by downloading providers, installing modules, and configuring backends. Without init, no other Terraform command will work.

❓

20.1.1 What init Does

📊 terraform init Process

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAFORM INIT PROCESS                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   $ terraform init                                                               │
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ STEP 1: Backend Initialization                                        │     │
│   │ • Configure state storage location                                    │     │
│   │ • Set up remote state if configured                                   │     │
│   │ • Initialize state locking mechanism                                  │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                          │                                                       │
│                          ▼                                                       │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ STEP 2: Provider Installation                                         │     │
│   │ • Download providers from registry                                    │     │
│   │ • Install into .terraform/providers/                                  │     │
│   │ • Verify checksums against lock file                                  │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                          │                                                       │
│                          ▼                                                       │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ STEP 3: Module Installation                                           │     │
│   │ • Download modules from sources                                       │     │
│   │ • Install into .terraform/modules/                                    │     │
│   │ • Create module manifest                                              │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                          │                                                       │
│                          ▼                                                       │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ STEP 4: Lock File                                                     │     │
│   │ • Generate/update .terraform.lock.hcl                                 │     │
│   │ • Record exact provider versions                                      │     │
│   │ • Store provider checksums                                            │     │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                                  │
│   Result: Directory ready for plan/apply                                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⬇️

20.1.2 Provider Installation

terraform init Output 
$ terraform init

Initializing the backend...

Initializing provider plugins...
- Finding hashicorp/aws versions matching ">= 5.0"...
- Finding hashicorp/random versions matching ">= 3.0"...
- Installing hashicorp/aws v5.31.0...
- Installed hashicorp/aws v5.31.0 (signed by HashiCorp)
- Installing hashicorp/random v3.6.0...
- Installed hashicorp/random v3.6.0 (signed by HashiCorp)

Terraform has created a lock file .terraform.lock.hcl to record the provider
selections it made above. Include this file in your version control repository
so that Terraform can guarantee to make the same selections by default when
you run "terraform init" in the future.

Terraform has been successfully initialized!
📦

20.1.3 Module Installation

Init with Modules 
$ terraform init

Initializing modules...
Downloading terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws 5.5.3 for vpc...
- vpc in .terraform/modules/vpc
Downloading terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance/aws 5.6.0 for ec2...
- ec2 in .terraform/modules/ec2

Initializing the backend...
Initializing provider plugins...
# ... providers downloaded

Terraform has been successfully initialized!
⚙️

20.1.4 init Options

Option Description Use Case
-upgrade Update providers/modules to latest Get new versions within constraints
-reconfigure Reconfigure backend, ignoring saved config Fix backend configuration issues
-migrate-state Migrate state to new backend Moving from local to remote state
-backend=false Skip backend configuration CI validation without backend access
-get=false Skip module installation Offline or cached modules
Common init Commands 
# Standard initialization
$ terraform init

# Upgrade providers to latest within constraints  
$ terraform init -upgrade

# Reconfigure backend (e.g., after changing backend config)
$ terraform init -reconfigure

# Migrate state to new backend
$ terraform init -migrate-state

# Initialize without configuring backend (for CI validation)
$ terraform init -backend=false
📝

EXAM TIP: When to Re-run init

  • After adding/changing provider requirements
  • After adding/changing module sources
  • After modifying backend configuration
  • When cloning existing configuration
  • After deleting .terraform directory

20.2 terraform validate

✅

Configuration Validation

terraform validate checks your configuration files for syntax errors and internal consistency without accessing remote services. It's fast and ideal for pre-commit hooks or CI pipelines.

🔍

20.2.1 What validate Checks

VALIDATES
✅

What It Checks

  • HCL syntax correctness
  • Attribute names and types
  • Required arguments present
  • Variable type consistency
  • Module references
DOES NOT CHECK
❌

What It Doesn't Check

  • Provider API availability
  • Resource existence in cloud
  • IAM permissions
  • State file access
  • Actual deployment feasibility
💻

20.2.2 Validate Output

Validation Examples 
# Successful validation
$ terraform validate
Success! The configuration is valid.

# Failed validation - missing required argument
$ terraform validate
╷
│ Error: Missing required argument
│ 
│   on main.tf line 1, in resource "aws_instance" "example":
│    1: resource "aws_instance" "example" {
│ 
│ The argument "ami" is required, but no definition was found.
╵

# JSON output for CI/CD
$ terraform validate -json
{
  "valid": true,
  "error_count": 0,
  "warning_count": 0,
  "diagnostics": []
}
🔄

20.2.3 CI/CD Integration

GitHub Actions Validation 
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml
name: Terraform Validation
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init -backend=false
        
      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate

20.3 terraform fmt

✨

Code Formatting

terraform fmt rewrites Terraform configuration files to follow the canonical format and style conventions. Consistent formatting makes code easier to read and reduces merge conflicts.

📝

20.3.1 Basic Formatting

terraform fmt Commands 
# Format current directory
$ terraform fmt
main.tf
variables.tf

# Format recursively (all subdirectories)
$ terraform fmt -recursive

# Check formatting without changing (for CI)
$ terraform fmt -check
# Exit code: 0 = formatted, 3 = needs formatting

# Show diff of changes
$ terraform fmt -diff
--- a/main.tf
+++ b/main.tf
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
-resource "aws_instance" "example" {
-    ami = "ami-123"
-    instance_type="t2.micro"
+resource "aws_instance" "example" {
+  ami           = "ami-123"
+  instance_type = "t2.micro"
 }
📋

20.3.2 Formatting Rules

🎨 What terraform fmt Does

  • Indentation: 2 spaces per nesting level
  • Alignment: Aligns equals signs within blocks
  • Spacing: Consistent whitespace
  • Line length: No hard limit, but readable
  • Block ordering: Preserves your order

20.4 terraform plan EXAM TOPIC

📋

Execution Preview

terraform plan creates an execution plan showing what Terraform will do when you run apply. It compares current state with desired configuration and calculates the necessary changes. Always review plans before applying!

🔄

20.4.1 Plan Process

📊 terraform plan Workflow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAFORM PLAN PROCESS                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────┐                      ┌─────────────────┐                  │
│   │  Configuration  │                      │   State File    │                  │
│   │    (.tf files)  │                      │ (terraform.tfstate) │              │
│   └────────┬────────┘                      └────────┬────────┘                  │
│            │                                        │                            │
│            └──────────────────┬─────────────────────┘                            │
│                               │                                                  │
│                               ▼                                                  │
│                    ┌─────────────────────┐                                      │
│                    │   terraform plan    │                                      │
│                    │   ───────────────   │                                      │
│                    │ 1. Read state       │                                      │
│                    │ 2. Read config      │                                      │
│                    │ 3. Refresh (query)  │                                      │
│                    │ 4. Calculate diff   │                                      │
│                    └──────────┬──────────┘                                      │
│                               │                                                  │
│                               ▼                                                  │
│                    ┌─────────────────────┐                                      │
│                    │   Execution Plan    │                                      │
│                    │   ───────────────   │                                      │
│                    │ + Create (green)    │                                      │
│                    │ ~ Update (yellow)   │                                      │
│                    │ - Destroy (red)     │                                      │
│                    │ -/+ Replace         │                                      │
│                    └─────────────────────┘                                      │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📊

20.4.2 Plan Output

Reading Plan Output 
$ terraform plan

Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_instance.web will be created
  + resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      + ami                    = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
      + instance_type          = "t3.micro"
      + id                     = (known after apply)
      + public_ip              = (known after apply)
      + tags                   = {
          + "Name" = "web-server"
        }
    }

  # aws_security_group.web will be updated in-place
  ~ resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
        id          = "sg-123456"
        name        = "web-sg"
      ~ description = "Web server SG" -> "Web server security group"
    }

  # aws_instance.old will be destroyed
  - resource "aws_instance" "old" {
      - ami           = "ami-old123" -> null
      - instance_type = "t2.micro" -> null
      - id            = "i-abc123" -> null
    }

Plan: 1 to add, 1 to change, 1 to destroy.
Symbol Meaning Description
+ Create Resource will be created
~ Update in-place Resource will be modified
- Destroy Resource will be deleted
-/+ Replace Resource destroyed and recreated
+/- Replace (create first) New created before old destroyed
⚙️

20.4.3 Plan Options

Useful Plan Options 
# Save plan to file (for later apply)
$ terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Plan with variable
$ terraform plan -var="instance_type=t3.small"

# Plan with variable file
$ terraform plan -var-file="production.tfvars"

# Plan destruction
$ terraform plan -destroy

# Target specific resource
$ terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web

# Refresh only mode (detect drift)
$ terraform plan -refresh-only

# Compact warnings (cleaner output)
$ terraform plan -compact-warnings
📝

EXAM TIP: Saved Plans

Using -out=tfplan saves the exact plan to a file. When you later run terraform apply tfplan, Terraform applies exactly that plan without re-calculating. This is crucial for CI/CD to ensure consistency between plan review and apply.

🎯

20.4.4 Plan Best Practices

📋 Plan Review Checklist

  • ✅ Review ALL resources being modified
  • ✅ Check for unexpected destroys (-)
  • ✅ Verify replace (-/+) operations are acceptable
  • ✅ Confirm resource counts at bottom match expectations
  • ✅ Watch for sensitive data in outputs

20.5 terraform apply EXAM TOPIC

🚀

Applying Changes

terraform apply executes the actions proposed in a plan to create, update, or destroy infrastructure. This is the command that actually makes changes to your cloud environment.

▶️

20.5.1 Apply Process

terraform apply Output 
$ terraform apply

Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_instance.web will be created
  + resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      + ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
      + instance_type = "t3.micro"
      ...
    }

Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

Do you want to perform these actions?
  Terraform will perform the actions described above.
  Only 'yes' will be accepted to approve.

  Enter a value: yes

aws_instance.web: Creating...
aws_instance.web: Still creating... [10s elapsed]
aws_instance.web: Creation complete after 35s [id=i-0abc123def456]

Apply complete! Resources: 1 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.

Outputs:

instance_ip = "54.123.45.67"
⚙️

20.5.2 Apply Options

Apply Command Options 
# Auto-approve (skip confirmation) - USE WITH CAUTION!
$ terraform apply -auto-approve

# Apply a saved plan file
$ terraform apply tfplan

# Apply with variables
$ terraform apply -var="instance_type=t3.small"

# Apply with var file
$ terraform apply -var-file="production.tfvars"

# Target specific resource
$ terraform apply -target=aws_instance.web

# Destroy mode
$ terraform apply -destroy

# Parallelism (default 10)
$ terraform apply -parallelism=5
⚠️

-auto-approve Warning

Using -auto-approve skips the confirmation prompt. Only use in automated pipelines where a human has already reviewed the plan, or for disposable dev environments.

💾

20.5.3 Apply Saved Plan

CI/CD Safe Apply Pattern 
# Step 1: Create and save plan
$ terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Step 2: Review plan (manual or automated)
$ terraform show tfplan

# Step 3: Apply the exact saved plan
$ terraform apply tfplan

# Note: When applying a saved plan:
# - No confirmation prompt is shown
# - The plan cannot be modified
# - Variables cannot be overridden
# - Terraform applies exactly what was planned
📝

EXAM TIP: Apply Without Arguments

Running terraform apply without a saved plan file will:
1. Calculate a new plan
2. Show the plan output
3. Prompt for confirmation
4. Execute on "yes"

20.6 terraform destroy

💥

Destroying Infrastructure

terraform destroy terminates all resources managed by the current configuration. It's essentially terraform apply -destroy and follows the same workflow: plan destruction, confirm, execute.

🗑️

20.6.1 Destroy Process

terraform destroy Output 
$ terraform destroy

aws_instance.web: Refreshing state... [id=i-0abc123]
aws_security_group.web: Refreshing state... [id=sg-456def]
aws_vpc.main: Refreshing state... [id=vpc-789ghi]

Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_instance.web will be destroyed
  - resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      - ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0" -> null
      - id            = "i-0abc123" -> null
      - instance_type = "t3.micro" -> null
    }

  # aws_security_group.web will be destroyed
  - resource "aws_security_group" "web" { ... }

  # aws_vpc.main will be destroyed
  - resource "aws_vpc" "main" { ... }

Plan: 0 to add, 0 to change, 3 to destroy.

Do you really want to destroy all resources?
  Only 'yes' will be accepted to confirm.

  Enter a value: yes

aws_instance.web: Destroying... [id=i-0abc123]
aws_instance.web: Destruction complete after 30s
aws_security_group.web: Destroying... [id=sg-456def]
aws_security_group.web: Destruction complete after 1s
aws_vpc.main: Destroying... [id=vpc-789ghi]
aws_vpc.main: Destruction complete after 1s

Destroy complete! Resources: 3 destroyed.
🎯

20.6.2 Targeted Destruction

Destroy Specific Resources 
# Destroy specific resource only
$ terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web

# Destroy multiple specific resources
$ terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web -target=aws_ebs_volume.data

# Destroy entire module
$ terraform destroy -target=module.vpc
⚠️

Target Warning

-target is for exceptional use only. It can leave your state inconsistent with your configuration. Always prefer removing resources from configuration and running apply instead.

20.7 Workflow Best Practices

📚

Recommended Workflows

A consistent workflow helps teams work safely with infrastructure. Whether working locally or in CI/CD, following these patterns reduces errors and improves collaboration.

🔄

20.7.1 Standard Workflow

📊 Recommended Terraform Workflow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STANDARD TERRAFORM WORKFLOW                                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   1. WRITE                                                                       │
│      ┌─────────────────┐                                                        │
│      │ Edit .tf files  │                                                        │
│      └────────┬────────┘                                                        │
│               │                                                                  │
│               ▼                                                                  │
│   2. FORMAT & VALIDATE                                                          │
│      ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐                                  │
│      │ terraform fmt   │─►│ terraform       │                                  │
│      │ -recursive      │  │ validate        │                                  │
│      └─────────────────┘  └────────┬────────┘                                  │
│                                    │                                            │
│               ▼                    │                                            │
│   3. INITIALIZE (if needed)        │                                            │
│      ┌─────────────────┐           │                                            │
│      │ terraform init  │ ◄─────────┘                                           │
│      └────────┬────────┘                                                        │
│               │                                                                  │
│               ▼                                                                  │
│   4. PLAN & REVIEW                                                              │
│      ┌─────────────────┐                                                        │
│      │ terraform plan  │  ◄── REVIEW CAREFULLY                                  │
│      │ -out=tfplan     │                                                        │
│      └────────┬────────┘                                                        │
│               │                                                                  │
│               ▼                                                                  │
│   5. APPLY                                                                       │
│      ┌─────────────────┐                                                        │
│      │ terraform apply │                                                        │
│      │ tfplan          │                                                        │
│      └─────────────────┘                                                        │
│                                                                                  │
│   6. COMMIT                                                                      │
│      git add . && git commit && git push                                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🤖

20.7.2 CI/CD Workflow

GitHub Actions Production Workflow 
name: Terraform
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      
      - name: Terraform Format
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
        
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init
        
      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate
        
      - name: Terraform Plan
        run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
        
      # Only apply on push to main (after PR merge)
      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
✅

20.7.3 Pre-commit Hooks

.pre-commit-config.yaml 
# Install: pip install pre-commit
# Setup: pre-commit install

repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.86.0
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
      - id: terraform_validate
      - id: terraform_docs
      - id: terraform_tflint

📋 Chapter 20 Summary: Core Workflow Commands

🚀

terraform init

  • Downloads providers & modules
  • Configures backend
  • Creates .terraform.lock.hcl
  • Run after any config change
📋

terraform plan

  • Creates execution plan
  • Shows +/~/- changes
  • Save with -out=tfplan
  • Always review before apply
🚀

terraform apply

  • Executes the plan
  • Creates/updates/destroys
  • Updates state file
  • -auto-approve for CI/CD
🔄

Workflow

  • Write → fmt → validate
  • init → plan → review
  • apply → commit
  • Use saved plans in CI/CD
📋 🔀 🏢 🌍 ⚙️ 📂
21

Workspaces

Environment Isolation and State Separation in Terraform

📚7 Sections
⏱️35 min read
📝EXAM TOPIC
🎯Essential

🎯 Learning Objectives

📋

Understand Workspaces

Learn the concept of workspaces and how they provide isolated state environments

🔀

Workspace Commands

Master all workspace CLI commands: new, list, select, show, and delete

⚙️

terraform.workspace Variable

Use the workspace variable for conditional logic and dynamic resource naming

🏢

Environment Patterns

Implement best practices for dev/staging/prod environment separation

21.1 What are Workspaces?
  • Workspace Concept
  • Use Cases
  • Limitations
21.2 Default Workspace
  • "default" Workspace
  • Cannot Delete Default
21.3 Workspace Commands
  • terraform workspace new
  • terraform workspace list
  • terraform workspace select
  • terraform workspace show
  • terraform workspace delete
21.4 terraform.workspace Variable
  • Accessing Current Workspace
  • Conditional Logic
  • Resource Naming
21.5 Workspace State Storage
  • Local Backend State
  • S3 Backend State
  • HCP Terraform Workspaces
21.6 Workspaces vs Directory Structure
  • When to Use Workspaces
  • When to Use Directories
  • Hybrid Approaches
21.7 Workspaces Best Practices
  • Environment Separation
  • Naming Conventions
  • CI/CD Integration
21.1

What are Workspaces?

EXAM TOPIC

Terraform workspaces provide a mechanism to manage multiple distinct sets of infrastructure resources using the same Terraform configuration. Each workspace has its own isolated state file, allowing you to deploy and manage separate environments (like dev, staging, and production) without duplicating your configuration code.

21.1.1 Workspace Concept

💡

What is a Workspace?

A workspace is a named container for Terraform state. Each workspace maintains its own independent state file, allowing the same configuration to manage multiple instances of infrastructure.

ascii-diagram Workspace Architecture 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        TERRAFORM CONFIGURATION                          │
│                         (main.tf, variables.tf)                         │
│                              Same Code                                  │
└───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                │
            ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────────┐
            │                   │                       │
            ▼                   ▼                       ▼
┌───────────────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐
│   WORKSPACE: dev  │ │ WORKSPACE: staging│ │  WORKSPACE: prod  │
├───────────────────┤ ├───────────────────┤ ├───────────────────┤
│  terraform.tfstate│ │  terraform.tfstate│ │  terraform.tfstate│
│      (dev)        │ │     (staging)     │ │      (prod)       │
├───────────────────┤ ├───────────────────┤ ├───────────────────┤
│  • dev resources  │ │ • staging resources│ │ • prod resources  │
│  • t2.micro       │ │ • t2.small        │ │ • t2.large        │
│  • 1 instance     │ │ • 2 instances     │ │ • 5 instances     │
└───────────────────┘ └───────────────────┘ └───────────────────┘
     Isolated              Isolated              Isolated
      State                 State                 State
📝

Key Concept

One configuration, multiple state files. Workspaces allow you to reuse the same Terraform code to manage separate infrastructure deployments, each tracked in its own state file.

hcl Single Configuration Supporting Multiple Workspaces 
# main.tf - Works across all workspaces
# The terraform.workspace variable determines environment-specific settings

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = local.instance_types[terraform.workspace]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-${terraform.workspace}-server"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
  }
}

# Local values to define workspace-specific configurations
locals {
  instance_types = {
    default = "t2.micro"
    dev     = "t2.micro"
    staging = "t2.small"
    prod    = "t2.large"
  }
  
  instance_counts = {
    default = 1
    dev     = 1
    staging = 2
    prod    = 5
  }
}

21.1.2 Use Cases

🌍

Environment Separation

Manage dev, staging, and production environments with isolated state while sharing the same configuration

dev → staging → prod
👥

Multi-Tenant Deployments

Deploy identical infrastructure for different customers or teams, each in their own workspace

customer-a, customer-b
🌐

Regional Deployments

Deploy the same infrastructure across multiple AWS regions with workspace per region

us-east-1, eu-west-1
🧪

Feature Testing

Create temporary workspaces to test infrastructure changes before merging to main environments

feature-x, experiment-1
hcl Multi-Region Workspace Pattern 
# variables.tf
variable "workspace_to_region" {
  description = "Map workspace names to AWS regions"
  type        = map(string)
  default = {
    default    = "us-east-1"
    us-east    = "us-east-1"
    us-west    = "us-west-2"
    eu-west    = "eu-west-1"
    ap-south   = "ap-south-1"
  }
}

# providers.tf
provider "aws" {
  region = lookup(
    var.workspace_to_region,
    terraform.workspace,
    "us-east-1"  # default fallback
  )
}

# main.tf
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  
  tags = {
    Name   = "vpc-${terraform.workspace}"
    Region = var.workspace_to_region[terraform.workspace]
  }
}

21.1.3 Limitations

⚠️

Important Workspace Limitations

Understanding these limitations helps you decide when workspaces are appropriate and when alternative approaches (like separate directories) are better.

Limitation Description Impact
Same Backend All workspaces share the same backend configuration Cannot use different backends per environment
Same Providers Provider versions are shared across workspaces Cannot test different provider versions
Same Variables terraform.tfvars applies to all workspaces Need conditional logic for different values
No Isolation Workspaces don't provide code isolation Code changes affect all workspaces
Visibility Current workspace not visible in directory Risk of applying to wrong workspace
ascii-diagram Workspace Limitations Visualization 
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │     SHARED ACROSS WORKSPACES        │
                    ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
                    │  ✓ Configuration Files (*.tf)       │
                    │  ✓ Backend Configuration            │
                    │  ✓ Provider Versions                │
                    │  ✓ Module Versions                  │
                    │  ✓ Variable Definitions             │
                    └─────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
        ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐
        │                           │                           │
        ▼                           ▼                           ▼
┌───────────────┐           ┌───────────────┐           ┌───────────────┐
│   ISOLATED    │           │   ISOLATED    │           │   ISOLATED    │
├───────────────┤           ├───────────────┤           ├───────────────┤
│ State File    │           │ State File    │           │ State File    │
│ (workspace    │           │ (workspace    │           │ (workspace    │
│  specific)    │           │  specific)    │           │  specific)    │
└───────────────┘           └───────────────┘           └───────────────┘<

⚠️ CAUTION: A bad configuration change affects ALL workspaces!
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Example: If you break main.tf, ALL environments are impacted
📝 EXAM TIP

Workspaces are NOT a replacement for:

  • Code versioning (use Git branches)
  • Access control (use separate backends with different IAM)
  • Provider version testing (use separate directories)

Workspaces are best for: Same code, different instances (environments, regions, tenants)

21.2

Default Workspace

21.2.1 "default" Workspace

📘

The Default Workspace

Every Terraform configuration starts with a workspace named default. If you've never used workspaces explicitly, you've been using the default workspace all along. This workspace is created automatically and always exists.

bash Checking the Current Workspace 
# Show current workspace
$ terraform workspace show
default

# List all workspaces - asterisk (*) marks current
$ terraform workspace list
* default

# The default workspace is automatically created
# You don't need to create it explicitly
ascii-diagram Default Workspace State Location 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   LOCAL BACKEND (Default)                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│   project/                                                      │
│   ├── main.tf                                                   │
│   ├── variables.tf                                              │
│   ├── terraform.tfstate          ◄── Default workspace state    │
│   └── terraform.tfstate.d/       ◄── Other workspace states     │
│       ├── dev/                                                  │
│       │   └── terraform.tfstate                                 │
│       ├── staging/                                              │
│       │   └── terraform.tfstate                                 │
│       └── prod/                                                 │
│           └── terraform.tfstate                                 │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Note: "default" workspace uses terraform.tfstate in root
      Other workspaces use terraform.tfstate.d//
💡 terraform.workspace Value

When using the default workspace, the terraform.workspace variable returns the string "default".

# In your configuration
output "current_workspace" {
