  value = terraform.workspace  # Returns "default"
}

21.2.2 Cannot Delete Default

⚠️

The Default Workspace Cannot Be Deleted

The default workspace is permanent. Terraform will return an error if you attempt to delete it. This ensures there's always at least one workspace available.

bash Attempting to Delete Default Workspace 
# This will FAIL
$ terraform workspace delete default

Error: Cannot delete default workspace

The "default" workspace cannot be deleted because Terraform requires
at least one workspace to be available.
📝 EXAM TIP

Key facts about the default workspace:

  • Always exists automatically
  • Cannot be deleted
  • Returns "default" for terraform.workspace
  • State stored in root terraform.tfstate (local backend)
21.3

Workspace Commands

EXAM TOPIC

Terraform provides a complete set of workspace management commands. These commands allow you to create, list, switch between, and delete workspaces from the command line.

21.3.1 terraform workspace new

📘

Creating New Workspaces

The terraform workspace new command creates a new workspace and automatically switches to it. This creates a fresh, empty state for the new workspace.

bash Creating New Workspaces 
# Create a new workspace named "dev"
$ terraform workspace new dev
Created and switched to workspace "dev"!

You're now on a new, empty workspace. Workspaces isolate their state,
so if you run "terraform plan" Terraform will not see any existing state
for this configuration.

# Create workspace named "staging"
$ terraform workspace new staging
Created and switched to workspace "staging"!

# Create workspace named "prod"
$ terraform workspace new prod
Created and switched to workspace "prod"!

# Verify current workspace
$ terraform workspace show
prod
bash Create Workspace from Existing State 
# Create workspace and copy state from another workspace
$ terraform workspace new -state=terraform.tfstate.d/dev/terraform.tfstate prod-copy

# This copies the state file content, useful for:
# - Creating a test copy of production
# - Migrating between workspace strategies

21.3.2 terraform workspace list

bash Listing All Workspaces 
# List all workspaces
$ terraform workspace list
  default
  dev
  staging
* prod

# The asterisk (*) indicates the currently selected workspace
# In this example, "prod" is the active workspace
💡 Understanding the Output
  • * marks the currently active workspace
  • Workspaces are listed alphabetically
  • default always appears in the list

21.3.3 terraform workspace select

bash Switching Between Workspaces 
# Switch to dev workspace
$ terraform workspace select dev
Switched to workspace "dev".

# Verify the switch
$ terraform workspace show
dev

# Switch to staging
$ terraform workspace select staging
Switched to workspace "staging".

# Attempt to select non-existent workspace
$ terraform workspace select test
Workspace "test" doesn't exist.

You can create this workspace with the "new" subcommand
or include the "-or-create" flag with the "select" subcommand.
bash Select or Create (Terraform 1.4+) 
# Select workspace, creating if it doesn't exist
$ terraform workspace select -or-create=true test
Created and switched to workspace "test"!

# Useful in CI/CD pipelines where workspace may not exist yet
# Avoids errors on first run
⚠️

Always Verify Before Apply

Always verify which workspace is active before running terraform apply. Applying to the wrong workspace can accidentally modify production infrastructure!

21.3.4 terraform workspace show

bash Showing Current Workspace 
# Display current workspace name
$ terraform workspace show
dev

# Useful in shell scripts
CURRENT_WS=$(terraform workspace show)
echo "Currently in workspace: $CURRENT_WS"

# Safety check before destructive operations
if [ "$(terraform workspace show)" = "prod" ]; then
    echo "⚠️  WARNING: You are in PRODUCTION workspace!"
    read -p "Are you sure you want to continue? (yes/no): " confirm
    if [ "$confirm" != "yes" ]; then
        exit 1
    fi
fi

21.3.5 terraform workspace delete

bash Deleting Workspaces 
# You cannot delete the current workspace
# First, switch to another workspace
$ terraform workspace select default
Switched to workspace "default".

# Delete an empty workspace
$ terraform workspace delete test
Deleted workspace "test"!

# Attempting to delete workspace with resources
$ terraform workspace delete dev
Error: Workspace "dev" is not empty!

Workspace "dev" currently contains 3 resources.
Running "terraform destroy" before deleting is recommended.
To delete anyway, use -force.

# Force delete workspace with resources (DANGEROUS!)
$ terraform workspace delete -force dev
Deleted workspace "dev"!
⚠️

Danger: Force Delete

Using -force deletes the workspace and its state file, but does NOT destroy the actual infrastructure. This leaves resources orphaned in the cloud!

Best practice: Always run terraform destroy before deleting a workspace.

ascii-diagram Workspace Commands Summary 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      WORKSPACE COMMAND REFERENCE                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace new <name>                                           │
│   └── Creates new workspace and switches to it                             │
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace list                                                  │
│   └── Lists all workspaces (* marks current)                               │
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace select <name>                                        │
│   └── Switches to specified workspace                                      │
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace select -or-create=true <name>                        │
│   └── Switches to workspace, creates if not exists (TF 1.4+)               │
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace show                                                  │
│   └── Displays current workspace name                                      │
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace delete <name>                                        │
│   └── Deletes empty workspace (must not be current)                        │
│                                                                             │
│   terraform workspace delete -force <name>                                 │
│   └── Force deletes workspace with resources (DANGEROUS!)                  │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 EXAM TIP

Remember these rules:

  • workspace new - Creates AND switches
  • workspace delete - Cannot delete current workspace
  • workspace delete - Cannot delete "default"
  • workspace delete -force - Deletes state, NOT resources
21.4

terraform.workspace Variable

EXAM TOPIC

The terraform.workspace variable is a built-in variable that returns the name of the currently selected workspace. This enables workspace-aware configurations that behave differently based on the active environment.

21.4.1 Accessing Current Workspace

hcl Using terraform.workspace 
# The terraform.workspace variable is built-in
# It returns the name of the current workspace as a string

# Output the current workspace
output "current_workspace" {
  description = "The currently selected Terraform workspace"
  value       = terraform.workspace
}

# Use in resource names
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  bucket = "my-company-data-${terraform.workspace}"
  # Results in:
  # - my-company-data-default (default workspace)
  # - my-company-data-dev (dev workspace)
  # - my-company-data-prod (prod workspace)
}

# Use in tags
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "app-server-${terraform.workspace}"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    Workspace   = terraform.workspace
  }
}
📘

terraform.workspace Rules

  • Returns a string value
  • Read-only (cannot be assigned)
  • Available in all configurations automatically
  • Works with any backend type

21.4.2 Conditional Logic

hcl Workspace-Based Conditional Logic 
# Different instance types per workspace
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  
  # Ternary conditional
  instance_type = terraform.workspace == "prod" ? "t2.large" : "t2.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "app-${terraform.workspace}"
  }
}

# Multiple conditions with nested ternary
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  
  instance_type = (
    terraform.workspace == "prod" ? "t2.xlarge" :
    terraform.workspace == "staging" ? "t2.large" :
    "t2.micro"  # default for dev and other workspaces
  )
  
  tags = {
    Name = "web-${terraform.workspace}"
  }
}

# Conditional resource creation
resource "aws_cloudwatch_alarm" "high_cpu" {
  # Only create alarms in production
  count = terraform.workspace == "prod" ? 1 : 0
  
  alarm_name          = "high-cpu-${terraform.workspace}"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  namespace           = "AWS/EC2"
  period              = 120
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = 80
  
  dimensions = {
    InstanceId = aws_instance.app.id
  }
}
hcl Using Local Values for Workspace Config 
# locals.tf - Define workspace-specific configurations
locals {
  # Environment configuration map
  env_config = {
    default = {
      instance_type  = "t2.micro"
      instance_count = 1
      enable_backups = false
      log_level      = "DEBUG"
    }
    dev = {
      instance_type  = "t2.micro"
      instance_count = 1
      enable_backups = false
      log_level      = "DEBUG"
    }
    staging = {
      instance_type  = "t2.small"
      instance_count = 2
      enable_backups = true
      log_level      = "INFO"
    }
    prod = {
      instance_type  = "t2.large"
      instance_count = 5
      enable_backups = true
      log_level      = "WARN"
    }
  }
  
  # Get current workspace config with fallback to default
  current_config = lookup(
    local.env_config,
    terraform.workspace,
    local.env_config["default"]
  )
}

# main.tf - Use local values
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  count         = local.current_config.instance_count
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = local.current_config.instance_type
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "app-${count.index}-${terraform.workspace}"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    LogLevel    = local.current_config.log_level
  }
}

resource "aws_backup_plan" "daily" {
  count = local.current_config.enable_backups ? 1 : 0
  name  = "daily-backup-${terraform.workspace}"
  
  rule {
    rule_name         = "daily-backup"
    target_vault_name = aws_backup_vault.main[0].name
    schedule          = "cron(0 5 ? * * *)"
  }
}

21.4.3 Resource Naming

hcl Best Practices for Workspace-Based Naming 
# Define naming convention in locals
locals {
  # Common prefix for all resources
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-${terraform.workspace}"
  
  # Common tags for all resources
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    Workspace   = terraform.workspace
  }
}

# Apply consistent naming across resources
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr
  
  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${local.name_prefix}-vpc"
  })
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  
  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${local.name_prefix}-public-${count.index + 1}"
    Type = "public"
  })
}

resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name        = "${local.name_prefix}-web-sg"
  description = "Security group for web servers in ${terraform.workspace}"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${local.name_prefix}-web-sg"
  })
}

# S3 bucket with workspace in name (ensures uniqueness)
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "assets" {
  # Global unique name using workspace
  bucket = "${var.project_name}-assets-${terraform.workspace}-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
  
  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${local.name_prefix}-assets"
  })
}
ascii-diagram Naming Convention Pattern 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 WORKSPACE-BASED NAMING PATTERN                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│    {project}-{workspace}-{resource-type}                        │
│                                                                 │
│    Examples:                                                    │
│    ├── myapp-dev-vpc                                           │
│    ├── myapp-dev-public-subnet-1                               │
│    ├── myapp-staging-web-sg                                    │
│    ├── myapp-prod-rds-primary                                  │
│    └── myapp-prod-assets-123456789012                          │
│                                                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│    Benefits:                                                    │
│    ✓ Clear environment identification                          │
│    ✓ Prevents naming conflicts                                 │
│    ✓ Easy filtering in AWS Console                             │
│    ✓ Consistent across all resources                           │
│    ✓ Facilitates cost allocation                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
21.5

Workspace State Storage

Each workspace maintains its own separate state file. The storage location depends on the backend type being used. Understanding how each backend stores workspace state helps you manage and debug your infrastructure effectively.

21.5.1 Local Backend State

ascii-diagram Local Backend Workspace State Structure 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    LOCAL BACKEND STATE STORAGE                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│   project/                                                      │
│   ├── main.tf                                                   │
│   ├── variables.tf                                              │
│   ├── outputs.tf                                                │
│   ├── .terraform/                   ◄── Provider plugins        │
│   │   └── providers/                                            │
│   │                                                             │
│   ├── terraform.tfstate             ◄── "default" workspace     │
│   │                                                             │
│   └── terraform.tfstate.d/          ◄── Other workspaces        │
│       ├── dev/                                                  │
│       │   └── terraform.tfstate     ◄── "dev" workspace state   │
│       ├── staging/                                              │
│       │   └── terraform.tfstate     ◄── "staging" workspace     │
│       └── prod/                                                 │
│           └── terraform.tfstate     ◄── "prod" workspace state  │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key points:
• "default" workspace: terraform.tfstate (in root)
• Other workspaces: terraform.tfstate.d/<workspace>/terraform.tfstate
• Each workspace has completely isolated state
bash Viewing Local Workspace State Files 
# Create workspaces and observe state file creation
$ terraform init
$ terraform workspace new dev
$ terraform apply -auto-approve

# List state files
$ ls -la terraform.tfstate.d/
drwxr-xr-x  dev/
drwxr-xr-x  staging/
drwxr-xr-x  prod/

$ ls -la terraform.tfstate.d/dev/
-rw-r--r--  terraform.tfstate

# View state file content
$ terraform state list
# Shows resources for current workspace only
⚠️

Local Backend Limitations

Local backend should only be used for learning and single-developer projects. For team collaboration or production use, always use a remote backend like S3 or HCP Terraform.

21.5.2 S3 Backend State

hcl S3 Backend Configuration 
# backend.tf
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key            = "myapp/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
    
    # Workspace state files are stored with workspace prefix
    # s3://bucket/env:/<workspace>/myapp/terraform.tfstate
  }
}
ascii-diagram S3 Backend Workspace State Structure 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    S3 BACKEND STATE STORAGE                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│   S3 Bucket: my-terraform-state-bucket                          │
│   │                                                             │
│   ├── myapp/                                                    │
│   │   └── terraform.tfstate        ◄── "default" workspace      │
│   │                                                             │
│   └── env:/                         ◄── Workspace prefix folder │
│       ├── dev/                                                  │
│       │   └── myapp/                                            │
│       │       └── terraform.tfstate  ◄── "dev" workspace        │
│       ├── staging/                                              │
│       │   └── myapp/                                            │
│       │       └── terraform.tfstate  ◄── "staging" workspace    │
│       └── prod/                                                 │
│           └── myapp/                                            │
│               └── terraform.tfstate  ◄── "prod" workspace       │
│                                                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   DynamoDB Table: terraform-state-lock                          │
│   └── Separate lock entry per workspace                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

S3 State Path Pattern:
• default:  s3://bucket/key
• other:    s3://bucket/env:/<workspace>/key
bash Viewing S3 Workspace State Files 
# List state files in S3 bucket
$ aws s3 ls s3://my-terraform-state-bucket/ --recursive
myapp/terraform.tfstate
env:/dev/myapp/terraform.tfstate
env:/staging/myapp/terraform.tfstate
env:/prod/myapp/terraform.tfstate

# Download state file for inspection
$ aws s3 cp s3://my-terraform-state-bucket/env:/prod/myapp/terraform.tfstate .

# View state through terraform
$ terraform workspace select prod
$ terraform state list
💡 Custom Workspace Key

You can customize the workspace state path using the workspace_key_prefix argument in the S3 backend configuration:

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket               = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key                  = "terraform.tfstate"
    region               = "us-east-1"
    workspace_key_prefix = "workspaces"  # Custom prefix
  }
}
# Results in: s3://bucket/workspaces/<workspace>/terraform.tfstate

21.5.3 HCP Terraform Workspaces

☁️

HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud)

HCP Terraform has its own concept of workspaces that's different from CLI workspaces. In HCP Terraform, workspaces are first-class organizational units with additional features like run history, variable storage, and team access controls.

hcl HCP Terraform Backend Configuration 
# Using HCP Terraform with single workspace
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-organization"
    
    workspaces {
      name = "my-app-prod"  # Single workspace
    }
  }
}

# Using HCP Terraform with workspace tags (multiple workspaces)
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-organization"
    
    workspaces {
      tags = ["app:myapp"]  # Match workspaces by tags
    }
  }
}

# Using HCP Terraform with workspace prefix pattern
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-organization"
    
    workspaces {
      project = "my-project"
      tags    = ["component:web"]
    }
  }
}
Feature CLI Workspaces HCP Terraform Workspaces
State Storage Same backend, different keys Each workspace = separate entity
Variables Shared .tfvars Per-workspace variable sets
Run History Not tracked Full history with logs
Access Control Backend IAM only Team-based permissions
Remote Operations Local execution Remote execution option
📝 EXAM TIP

CLI workspaces vs HCP Terraform workspaces:

  • CLI workspaces: Multiple state files, same configuration
  • HCP Terraform: Independent entities with their own settings, runs, and permissions
  • When using the cloud backend, terraform workspace commands interact with HCP Terraform workspaces
21.6

Workspaces vs Directory Structure

Workspaces are one approach to managing multiple environments, but using separate directories with different configurations is another common pattern. Understanding when to use each approach is crucial for effective Terraform organization.

21.6.1 When to Use Workspaces

✅

Identical Infrastructure

All environments have the same resources, just different sizes or counts

✅

Same Team Ownership

One team manages all environments with identical access requirements

✅

Rapid Provisioning

Need to quickly spin up new environments for testing or demos

✅

Multi-Tenant SaaS

Deploy identical infrastructure for multiple customers

hcl Good Use Case for Workspaces 
# Same infrastructure, different sizes
# Perfect for workspaces

locals {
  config = {
    dev     = { instance_type = "t2.micro",  count = 1 }
    staging = { instance_type = "t2.small",  count = 2 }
    prod    = { instance_type = "t2.large",  count = 5 }
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  count         = local.config[terraform.workspace].count
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = local.config[terraform.workspace].instance_type
}

# All environments have the same resources
# Only the configuration values differ

21.6.2 When to Use Directories

📁

Different Resources

Environments have different resources (e.g., prod has disaster recovery)

📁

Different Providers

Need different provider versions or configurations per environment

📁

Different Backends

Required to use separate backends for isolation or compliance

📁

Separate Teams

Different teams manage different environments with distinct access

ascii-diagram Directory-Based Environment Structure 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            DIRECTORY-BASED ENVIRONMENT STRUCTURE                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│   terraform/                                                    │
│   ├── modules/                 ◄── Shared modules               │
│   │   ├── vpc/                                                  │
│   │   ├── ec2/                                                  │
│   │   └── rds/                                                  │
│   │                                                             │
│   ├── environments/                                             │
│   │   ├── dev/                 ◄── Development                  │
│   │   │   ├── main.tf                                          │
│   │   │   ├── backend.tf      (different backend)               │
│   │   │   └── terraform.tfvars                                  │
│   │   │                                                         │
│   │   ├── staging/             ◄── Staging                      │
│   │   │   ├── main.tf                                          │
│   │   │   ├── backend.tf      (different backend)               │
│   │   │   └── terraform.tfvars                                  │
│   │   │                                                         │
│   │   └── prod/                ◄── Production                   │
│   │       ├── main.tf         (may have extra resources)        │
│   │       ├── dr.tf           (disaster recovery - prod only)   │
│   │       ├── backend.tf      (different backend + region)      │
│   │       └── terraform.tfvars                                  │
│   │                                                             │
│   └── global/                  ◄── Shared resources             │
│       ├── iam/                                                  │
│       └── dns/                                                  │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

21.6.3 Hybrid Approaches

ascii-diagram Hybrid: Directories + Workspaces 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HYBRID APPROACH                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│   Use DIRECTORIES for major environment categories:             │
│   ├── nonprod/          (dev + staging)                         │
│   └── prod/             (production + DR)                       │
│                                                                 │
│   Use WORKSPACES within each category:                          │
│                                                                 │
│   nonprod/                                                      │
│   ├── main.tf                                                   │
│   └── workspaces: [dev, staging, qa]                           │
│                                                                 │
│   prod/                                                         │
│   ├── main.tf           (includes DR resources)                 │
│   └── workspaces: [prod-us, prod-eu, prod-ap]                  │
│                                                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Benefits:                                                     │
│   ✓ Separate backends for prod vs nonprod                       │
│   ✓ Different provider configs per category                     │
│   ✓ Easy region/tenant replication within category              │
│   ✓ Code isolation where needed, reuse where possible           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Approach Best For Avoid When
Workspaces Only Identical environments, single team, same backend Different resources per env, compliance requirements
Directories Only Significantly different environments, separate teams High code duplication, identical resources
Hybrid Complex organizations, mixed requirements Simple projects, small teams
21.7

Workspaces Best Practices

21.7.1 Environment Separation

hcl Complete Environment Separation Pattern 
# locals.tf - Comprehensive environment configuration
locals {
  # Validate workspace name
  valid_workspaces = ["default", "dev", "staging", "prod"]
  
  validate_workspace = (
    contains(local.valid_workspaces, terraform.workspace)
    ? true
    : tobool("ERROR: Invalid workspace '${terraform.workspace}'. Valid: ${join(", ", local.valid_workspaces)}")
  )
  
  # Environment-specific settings
  environments = {
    default = {
      aws_region     = "us-east-1"
      instance_type  = "t2.micro"
      instance_count = 1
      enable_https   = false
      backup_enabled = false
      log_retention  = 7
      
      db_config = {
        instance_class    = "db.t3.micro"
        allocated_storage = 20
        multi_az          = false
      }
    }
    
    dev = {
      aws_region     = "us-east-1"
      instance_type  = "t2.micro"
      instance_count = 1
      enable_https   = false
      backup_enabled = false
      log_retention  = 7
      
      db_config = {
        instance_class    = "db.t3.micro"
        allocated_storage = 20
        multi_az          = false
      }
    }
    
    staging = {
      aws_region     = "us-east-1"
      instance_type  = "t2.small"
      instance_count = 2
      enable_https   = true
      backup_enabled = true
      log_retention  = 30
      
      db_config = {
        instance_class    = "db.t3.small"
        allocated_storage = 50
        multi_az          = false
      }
    }
    
    prod = {
      aws_region     = "us-east-1"
      instance_type  = "t2.large"
      instance_count = 5
      enable_https   = true
      backup_enabled = true
      log_retention  = 90
      
      db_config = {
        instance_class    = "db.t3.large"
        allocated_storage = 100
        multi_az          = true
      }
    }
  }
  
  # Current environment config
  env = local.environments[terraform.workspace]
}

21.7.2 Naming Conventions

hcl Standardized Naming Convention 
# naming.tf - Centralized naming convention
locals {
  # Organization abbreviation
  org = "acme"
  
  # Project/Application name
  project = "webapp"
  
  # Standard naming components
  naming = {
    # Full prefix: acme-webapp-dev
    prefix = "${local.org}-${local.project}-${terraform.workspace}"
    
    # Short prefix: webapp-dev
    short_prefix = "${local.project}-${terraform.workspace}"
    
    # For S3 buckets (add account ID for uniqueness)
    bucket_prefix = "${local.org}-${local.project}-${terraform.workspace}-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
  }
  
  # Standard tags for all resources
  default_tags = {
    Project     = local.project
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    Owner       = var.team_email
    CostCenter  = var.cost_center
  }
}

# Usage example
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count         = local.env.instance_count
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = local.env.instance_type
  
  tags = merge(local.default_tags, {
    Name = "${local.naming.prefix}-web-${count.index + 1}"
    Role = "web-server"
  })
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "assets" {
  bucket = "${local.naming.bucket_prefix}-assets"
  
  tags = merge(local.default_tags, {
    Name = "${local.naming.prefix}-assets"
  })
}
💡 Naming Best Practices
  • Always include environment/workspace in resource names
  • Use consistent prefixes across all resources
  • Include account ID in globally unique names (S3, etc.)
  • Tag all resources with workspace information
  • Use lowercase and hyphens for compatibility

21.7.3 CI/CD Integration

yaml GitHub Actions - Workspace-Aware Deployment 
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml
name: Terraform Deployment

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main        # Deploy to prod
      - develop     # Deploy to dev
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main        # Plan only for PRs to main

env:
  TF_IN_AUTOMATION: true
  AWS_REGION: us-east-1

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        
      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: 1.7.0
          
      - name: Configure AWS Credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          aws-secret-access-key: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
          aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}
          
      - name: Determine Workspace
        id: workspace
        run: |
          if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
            echo "name=prod" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          elif [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/develop" ]; then
            echo "name=dev" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          else
            echo "name=dev" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi
          
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init
        
      - name: Select Workspace
        run: terraform workspace select -or-create=true ${{ steps.workspace.outputs.name }}
        
      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -out=tfplan -no-color
        continue-on-error: true
        
      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' || github.ref == 'refs/heads/develop'
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
bash Shell Script for Safe Workspace Operations 
#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh - Safe workspace deployment script

set -e

WORKSPACE=${1:-dev}
ACTION=${2:-plan}

# Validate workspace
VALID_WORKSPACES="dev staging prod"
if ! echo "$VALID_WORKSPACES" | grep -qw "$WORKSPACE"; then
    echo "❌ Invalid workspace: $WORKSPACE"
    echo "Valid workspaces: $VALID_WORKSPACES"
    exit 1
fi

# Safety check for production
if [ "$WORKSPACE" == "prod" ] && [ "$ACTION" == "apply" ]; then
    echo "⚠️  WARNING: You are about to deploy to PRODUCTION!"
    read -p "Type 'yes-deploy-to-prod' to confirm: " confirm
    if [ "$confirm" != "yes-deploy-to-prod" ]; then
        echo "Deployment cancelled."
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Initialize and select workspace
terraform init
terraform workspace select -or-create=true "$WORKSPACE"

# Show current workspace
echo "📍 Current workspace: $(terraform workspace show)"

# Execute action
case $ACTION in
    plan)
        terraform plan -out="tfplan-$WORKSPACE"
        ;;
    apply)
        terraform apply "tfplan-$WORKSPACE"
        ;;
    destroy)
        terraform destroy
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Unknown action: $ACTION"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

📋 Chapter 21 Summary: Workspaces

📋

Workspace Concept

  • Named containers for state
  • Same config, multiple states
  • Environment isolation
  • Default workspace always exists
⌨️

Commands

  • new - Create and switch
  • list - Show all (*=current)
  • select - Switch workspace
  • show - Display current
  • delete - Remove workspace
⚙️

terraform.workspace

  • Returns workspace name string
  • Use in conditionals
  • Dynamic resource naming
  • Environment-specific config
💾

State Storage

  • Local: terraform.tfstate.d/
  • S3: env:/workspace/key
  • HCP: Separate entities
  • Each workspace isolated

🎯 Key Exam Points

  • Default workspace cannot be deleted and always exists
  • terraform.workspace returns the current workspace name as a string
  • workspace new creates AND switches to the new workspace
  • workspace delete -force removes state but NOT actual cloud resources
  • Workspaces share configuration, providers, and backend - only state is isolated
  • Use workspaces for same infrastructure with different sizes, not different architectures
⌨️ 🔧 📋 ⚙️ 🔐 🧪
22

Terraform CLI - Complete Reference

Environment Variables, Configuration Files, and Advanced CLI Features

📚9 Sections
⏱️50 min read
📝EXAM TOPIC
🎯Critical

🎯 Learning Objectives

🌍

Environment Variables

Master TF_LOG, TF_VAR, TF_CLI_ARGS and other critical environment variables

📄

CLI Configuration

Configure .terraformrc with credentials, plugin cache, and provider settings

🔧

Advanced Commands

Use console, force-unlock, providers, and the new terraform test command

💾

Plugin Management

Set up provider caching for faster operations and offline environments

  • -chdir

  • TF_LOG_PATH

  • Enabling Plugin Cache

  • terraform providers lock

  • Assertions
22.1

Global Options

Terraform provides several global options that can be used with any command. These options control how Terraform behaves regardless of the specific command being executed.

22.1.1 -chdir

📘

Change Directory Option

The -chdir option tells Terraform to change to a different directory before executing the command. This is useful for scripts and CI/CD pipelines where you want to run Terraform on a specific directory without changing the shell's current working directory.

bash Using -chdir Option 
# Run terraform in a different directory
$ terraform -chdir=environments/prod plan

# Equivalent to:
$ cd environments/prod && terraform plan

# Multiple commands with -chdir
$ terraform -chdir=environments/dev init
$ terraform -chdir=environments/dev plan
$ terraform -chdir=environments/dev apply

# Useful in CI/CD pipelines
$ for env in dev staging prod; do
    terraform -chdir=environments/$env plan -out=tfplan
  done

# Can be combined with other options
$ terraform -chdir=modules/vpc -json version
💡 When to Use -chdir
  • CI/CD pipelines managing multiple environments
  • Scripts that operate on multiple Terraform configurations
  • When you need to stay in the repository root
  • Avoiding cd commands in shell scripts

22.1.2 -help

bash Getting Help 
# General help - lists all commands
$ terraform -help

# Help for specific command
$ terraform plan -help
$ terraform apply -help
$ terraform state -help

# Subcommand help
$ terraform state mv -help
$ terraform workspace new -help

# Alternative syntax
$ terraform help plan
$ terraform help state mv

22.1.3 -version

bash Version Information 
# Show Terraform version
$ terraform -version
Terraform v1.7.4
on linux_amd64

+ provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws v5.40.0
+ provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random v3.6.0

# Short form
$ terraform version

# JSON output (useful for scripts)
$ terraform version -json
{
  "terraform_version": "1.7.4",
  "platform": "linux_amd64",
  "provider_selections": {
    "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws": "5.40.0",
    "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random": "3.6.0"
  },
  "outdated": false
}
22.2

Environment Variables

EXAM TOPIC

Terraform reads environment variables prefixed with TF_ to configure various behaviors. These are essential for debugging, automation, and customizing Terraform's operation.

22.2.1 TF_LOG (TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR)

📝 EXAM TOPIC

TF_LOG enables detailed logging for debugging. Know the log levels and when to use each one. This is frequently tested on the certification exam.

bash TF_LOG Environment Variable 
# Enable TRACE logging (most verbose)
$ export TF_LOG=TRACE
$ terraform plan

# Enable DEBUG logging
$ export TF_LOG=DEBUG
$ terraform apply

# Enable INFO logging
$ export TF_LOG=INFO
$ terraform init

# Enable WARN logging
$ export TF_LOG=WARN
$ terraform plan

# Enable ERROR logging (least verbose)
$ export TF_LOG=ERROR
$ terraform apply

# Disable logging
$ unset TF_LOG
# Or set to empty
$ export TF_LOG=
Level Verbosity Use Case
TRACE Most verbose Deep debugging, provider issues, API calls
DEBUG Very verbose Understanding Terraform internals, dependency resolution
INFO Moderate General operation information
WARN Limited Potential issues, deprecation warnings
ERROR Minimal Only critical errors
bash Provider-Specific Logging 
# Enable logging for specific components (Terraform 0.15+)
$ export TF_LOG_CORE=ERROR    # Terraform core logging
$ export TF_LOG_PROVIDER=DEBUG # Provider logging only

# This is useful when:
# - Debugging provider issues without noisy core logs
# - Investigating Terraform internals without provider noise

22.2.2 TF_LOG_PATH

bash Logging to File 
# Log to specific file
$ export TF_LOG=DEBUG
$ export TF_LOG_PATH=./terraform.log
$ terraform apply

# The log file will contain all debug information
$ cat terraform.log

# Common pattern: timestamp in filename
$ export TF_LOG_PATH="./logs/terraform-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log"
$ terraform plan

# In CI/CD pipelines
$ export TF_LOG=TRACE
$ export TF_LOG_PATH="${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform-debug.log"
$ terraform apply
# Upload terraform-debug.log as artifact on failure
⚠️

Log Files May Contain Secrets

Terraform log files at TRACE and DEBUG levels may contain sensitive information including provider credentials, API keys, and resource values. Never commit log files to version control!

22.2.3 TF_INPUT

bash Disabling Interactive Input 
# Disable interactive prompts
$ export TF_INPUT=0
$ terraform apply
# Without TF_INPUT=0, Terraform would prompt for missing variables
# With TF_INPUT=0, it will fail if required variables are missing

# Alternative: same as -input=false flag
$ terraform apply -input=false

# Use in automation/CI to ensure reproducible runs
$ export TF_INPUT=false
$ export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
$ terraform init
$ terraform apply -auto-approve

22.2.4 TF_VAR_name

📝 EXAM TOPIC

TF_VAR_ environment variables set Terraform input variables. The variable name follows the prefix: TF_VAR_<variable_name>.

hcl variables.tf 
# Define variables in your configuration
variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment"
  type        = string
}

variable "instance_count" {
  description = "Number of instances"
  type        = number
  default     = 1
}

variable "enable_monitoring" {
  description = "Enable CloudWatch monitoring"
  type        = bool
  default     = false
}

variable "tags" {
  description = "Resource tags"
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
}
bash Setting Variables via Environment 
# String variable
$ export TF_VAR_environment="production"

# Number variable
$ export TF_VAR_instance_count=5

# Boolean variable
$ export TF_VAR_enable_monitoring=true

# Complex types (using JSON)
$ export TF_VAR_tags='{"Project":"webapp","Team":"platform"}'

# Verify variables are set
$ env | grep TF_VAR

# Run terraform
$ terraform plan

# Common CI/CD pattern
$ export TF_VAR_environment="${CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME}"
$ export TF_VAR_image_tag="${CI_COMMIT_SHA:0:8}"
$ terraform apply -auto-approve
ascii-diagram Variable Precedence (Lowest to Highest) 
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VARIABLE PRECEDENCE (Lowest → Highest)                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│  1. default value in variable block    ← Lowest priority             │
│                    ↓                                                 │
│  2. Environment variables (TF_VAR_*)                                 │
│                    ↓                                                 │
│  3. terraform.tfvars file                                            │
│                    ↓                                                 │
│  4. terraform.tfvars.json file                                       │
│                    ↓                                                 │
│  5. *.auto.tfvars or *.auto.tfvars.json (alphabetical)              │
│                    ↓                                                 │
│  6. -var and -var-file command line    ← Highest priority            │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Example: If same variable is set in multiple places:
- TF_VAR_environment="dev" (env var)
- terraform.tfvars: environment = "staging"
- -var="environment=prod" (command line)

Result: environment = "prod" (command line wins)

22.2.5 TF_CLI_ARGS

bash Adding CLI Arguments via Environment 
# TF_CLI_ARGS applies to ALL commands
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS="-input=false"
$ terraform plan   # Same as: terraform plan -input=false
$ terraform apply  # Same as: terraform apply -input=false

# Multiple arguments
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS="-input=false -no-color"

22.2.6 TF_CLI_ARGS_command

bash Command-Specific CLI Arguments 
# Apply arguments only to specific commands
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-out=tfplan"
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-auto-approve"
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS_init="-upgrade"

# Now commands automatically include these flags
$ terraform init   # terraform init -upgrade
$ terraform plan   # terraform plan -out=tfplan
$ terraform apply  # terraform apply -auto-approve

# Common CI/CD setup
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS_init="-backend-config=backend.hcl"
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-var-file=prod.tfvars -out=tfplan"
$ export TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-auto-approve"

22.2.7 TF_DATA_DIR

bash Custom Data Directory 
# Change location of .terraform directory
$ export TF_DATA_DIR="/tmp/terraform-data"
$ terraform init

# Directory structure
# /tmp/terraform-data/
# └── providers/
#     └── registry.terraform.io/
#         └── hashicorp/
#             └── aws/

# Useful for:
# - Read-only source directories
# - Shared network locations
# - Debugging initialization issues

22.2.8 TF_WORKSPACE

bash Setting Workspace via Environment 
# Select workspace via environment variable
$ export TF_WORKSPACE="prod"
$ terraform plan  # Runs in prod workspace

# Overrides terraform workspace select
# Useful in CI/CD where workspace is determined by branch/tag

# CI/CD example
$ export TF_WORKSPACE="${CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME}"
$ terraform init
$ terraform plan

22.2.9 TF_IN_AUTOMATION

bash Automation Mode 
# Signal that Terraform is running in automation
$ export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
$ terraform plan

# Effects:
# - Suppresses some interactive hints
# - Adjusts output formatting
# - Changes some warning messages

# Always set in CI/CD pipelines
$ export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=1  # Any non-empty value works

22.2.10-13 Additional Environment Variables

Variable Purpose Example
TF_REGISTRY_CLIENT_TIMEOUT Timeout for registry requests (seconds) export TF_REGISTRY_CLIENT_TIMEOUT=30
TF_REGISTRY_DISCOVERY_RETRY Retry count for registry discovery export TF_REGISTRY_DISCOVERY_RETRY=3
TF_CLI_CONFIG_FILE Path to CLI config file export TF_CLI_CONFIG_FILE=~/.custom-terraformrc
TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR Provider plugin cache directory export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR=~/.terraform.d/plugin-cache
📝 EXAM TIP - Environment Variables

Key environment variables to remember:

  • TF_LOG - Logging levels: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR
  • TF_LOG_PATH - Write logs to file (may contain secrets!)
  • TF_VAR_name - Set input variables from environment
  • TF_INPUT=false - Disable interactive prompts
  • TF_IN_AUTOMATION - Indicate running in CI/CD
22.3

CLI Configuration File

The Terraform CLI configuration file configures per-user settings for the CLI. This includes credentials for remote backends, plugin cache directories, and provider installation methods.

22.3.1 .terraformrc / terraform.rc Location

ascii-diagram CLI Configuration File Locations 
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                CLI CONFIGURATION FILE LOCATIONS                      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   LINUX / macOS:                                                     │
│   └── ~/.terraformrc                                                │
│                                                                      │
│   WINDOWS:                                                           │
│   └── %APPDATA%\terraform.rc                                         │
│       (e.g., C:\Users\username\AppData\Roaming\terraform.rc)        │
│                                                                      │
│   CUSTOM LOCATION (any OS):                                          │
│   └── Set TF_CLI_CONFIG_FILE environment variable                    │
│       $ export TF_CLI_CONFIG_FILE="/path/to/custom.terraformrc"      │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
hcl Complete CLI Configuration Example 
# ~/.terraformrc (Linux/macOS) or %APPDATA%\terraform.rc (Windows)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CREDENTIALS FOR HCP TERRAFORM / TERRAFORM ENTERPRISE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
credentials "app.terraform.io" {
  token = "xxxxxxx.atlasv1.zzzzzzzzzzzzz"
}

# For Terraform Enterprise installations
credentials "terraform.mycompany.com" {
  token = "xxxxx"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PLUGIN CACHE DIRECTORY
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
plugin_cache_dir = "$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
# Windows: plugin_cache_dir = "C:/Users/username/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PROVIDER INSTALLATION SETTINGS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
provider_installation {
  # Use local mirror first
  filesystem_mirror {
    path    = "/usr/share/terraform/providers"
    include = ["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/*"]
  }
  
  # Fall back to direct download
  direct {
    exclude = []
  }
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DISABLE VERSION CHECKPOINT
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
disable_checkpoint = true

22.3.2 credentials Block

hcl Credentials Configuration 
# Store tokens for remote backends
credentials "app.terraform.io" {
  token = "xxxxxxx.atlasv1.zzzzzzzzzzzzz"
}

# Multiple hosts supported
credentials "terraform.mycompany.com" {
  token = "enterprise-token-here"
}

credentials "custom-registry.example.com" {
  token = "custom-registry-token"
}
⚠️

Security Warning

Never commit your .terraformrc file to version control! It contains sensitive credentials. Add it to .gitignore.

bash Alternative: terraform login 
# Interactive login to HCP Terraform
$ terraform login
# Opens browser for authentication
# Automatically creates credentials in .terraformrc

# Login to specific host
$ terraform login terraform.mycompany.com

# Logout (removes credentials)
$ terraform logout
$ terraform logout terraform.mycompany.com

22.3.3-6 Additional CLI Configuration

hcl credentials_helper Block 
# Use external credential helper program
credentials_helper "aws-secretmanager" {
  args = ["--region", "us-east-1"]
}

# The helper must:
# - Accept JSON input on stdin with {"hostname": "..."}
# - Return JSON on stdout with {"token": "..."}
hcl provider_installation Block 
# Control how providers are installed
provider_installation {
  # Local filesystem mirror
  filesystem_mirror {
    path    = "/opt/terraform/providers"
    include = ["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/*"]
  }
  
  # Network mirror
  network_mirror {
    url     = "https://terraform-mirror.example.com/providers/"
    include = ["registry.terraform.io/*/*"]
  }
  
  # Direct from registry (fallback)
  direct {
    exclude = ["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"]
  }
}

# Useful for:
# - Air-gapped environments
# - Corporate proxies
# - Faster local installs
22.4

Provider Plugin Cache

22.4.1 Enabling Plugin Cache

bash Setting Up Plugin Cache 
# Method 1: Environment variable
$ export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
$ mkdir -p "$TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR"
$ terraform init

# Method 2: CLI config file (~/.terraformrc)
plugin_cache_dir = "$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"

# Verify cache is being used
$ terraform init
# Output includes: "- Using previously-installed hashicorp/aws v5.40.0"
💡 Benefits of Plugin Caching
  • Speed: Avoids re-downloading providers for each project
  • Bandwidth: Reduces network usage, especially for large providers
  • Offline: Work offline with cached providers
  • CI/CD: Faster pipeline execution with shared cache

22.4.2 Cache Directory Structure

ascii-diagram Plugin Cache Directory Structure 
~/.terraform.d/plugin-cache/
├── registry.terraform.io/
│   └── hashicorp/
│       ├── aws/
│       │   ├── 5.40.0/
│       │   │   └── linux_amd64/
│       │   │       └── terraform-provider-aws_v5.40.0_x5
│       │   └── 5.39.0/
│       │       └── linux_amd64/
│       │           └── terraform-provider-aws_v5.39.0_x5
│       ├── random/
│       │   └── 3.6.0/
│       │       └── linux_amd64/
│       │           └── terraform-provider-random_v3.6.0_x5
│       └── null/
│           └── 3.2.2/
│               └── linux_amd64/
│                   └── terraform-provider-null_v3.2.2_x5
└── registry.example.com/
    └── custom/
        └── provider/
            └── 1.0.0/

22.4.3 CI/CD Caching

yaml GitHub Actions with Provider Cache 
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml
name: Terraform

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    env:
      TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR: ${{ github.workspace }}/.terraform-plugin-cache
    
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup Plugin Cache Directory
        run: mkdir -p $TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR
      
      - name: Cache Terraform Providers
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ${{ env.TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR }}
          key: terraform-providers-${{ hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
          restore-keys: |
            terraform-providers-
      
      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: 1.7.0
      
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init
        # Providers loaded from cache if available
22.5

terraform console

The terraform console command provides an interactive console for evaluating Terraform expressions. This is invaluable for testing functions, debugging complex expressions, and exploring your configuration's values.

22.5.1 Interactive Expression Evaluation

bash Using terraform console 
# Start the console (requires terraform init)
$ terraform console

# Evaluate expressions
> 1 + 2
3

> "Hello, ${"World"}"
"Hello, World"

# Access variables
> var.environment
"production"

# Access local values
> local.name_prefix
"myapp-prod"

# Access resource attributes (after apply)
> aws_instance.web.public_ip
"54.123.45.67"

# Exit console
> exit
# Or Ctrl+D

22.5.2 Testing Functions

bash Function Testing in Console 
$ terraform console

# String functions
> upper("hello")
"HELLO"

> format("instance-%03d", 5)
"instance-005"

> split(",", "a,b,c")
["a", "b", "c"]

> join("-", ["web", "server", "01"])
"web-server-01"

# Collection functions
> length([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
5

> flatten([[1, 2], [3, 4]])
[1, 2, 3, 4]

> merge({a = 1}, {b = 2})
{
  "a" = 1
  "b" = 2
}

# Network functions
> cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, 1)
"10.0.1.0/24"

> cidrhost("10.0.1.0/24", 5)
"10.0.1.5"

# Type conversion
> tolist(toset(["a", "b", "a"]))
["a", "b"]

> tonumber("42")
42

22.5.3 Debugging Expressions

bash Debugging Complex Expressions 
$ terraform console

# Test for_each keys
> { for k, v in var.instances : k => v.instance_type }
{
  "web" = "t2.micro"
  "api" = "t2.small"
}

# Test conditional expressions
> var.environment == "prod" ? "t2.large" : "t2.micro"
"t2.large"

# Test lookup with defaults
> lookup(var.instance_types, "staging", "t2.micro")
"t2.small"

# Test complex data transformations
> [for s in var.subnets : {
    cidr = s.cidr
    az   = s.availability_zone
    type = s.public ? "public" : "private"
  }]

# Non-interactive mode (pipe expression)
$ echo "1 + 5" | terraform console
6

$ echo "var.environment" | terraform console
"production"
22.6

terraform force-unlock

State locking prevents multiple Terraform operations from running simultaneously. Sometimes locks become orphaned and need manual removal. The force-unlock command handles this situation.

22.6.1 When to Use

bash Lock Error Scenario 
# When you encounter a lock error:
$ terraform apply

Error: Error acquiring the state lock

Error message: ConditionalCheckFailedException: The conditional request
failed

Lock Info:
  ID:        a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
  Path:      my-terraform-bucket/env:/prod/terraform.tfstate
  Operation: OperationTypeApply
  Who:       user@hostname
  Version:   1.7.4
  Created:   2024-03-15 10:30:45.123456 +0000 UTC
  Info:

Terraform acquires a state lock to protect against modifying the state
during apply operations.
⚠️

Only Use When Necessary

Before using force-unlock:

  • Verify no other Terraform process is actually running
  • Check if the user/process listed in "Who" is still active
  • Wait a few minutes - the lock may release automatically

22.6.2 Lock ID

bash Using force-unlock 
# Extract the Lock ID from the error message
# ID: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

# Force unlock with the Lock ID
$ terraform force-unlock a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

Do you really want to force-unlock?
  Terraform will remove the lock on the remote state.
  This will allow local Terraform commands to modify this state, even though
  it may still be in use. Only 'yes' will be accepted to confirm.

  Enter a value: yes

Terraform state has been successfully unlocked!

# Skip confirmation prompt (for automation)
$ terraform force-unlock -force a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

22.6.3 Risks

🚨

Force-Unlock Risks

  • State Corruption: If another process is actually running, you may cause state corruption
  • Concurrent Modifications: Two applies could run simultaneously
  • Lost Updates: Changes from the locked process may be overwritten
📝 EXAM TIP

force-unlock - Key points:

  • Requires the Lock ID from the error message
  • Only use when you're certain no other process is running
  • Most common cause: CI/CD pipeline interrupted/crashed
  • Does NOT affect the state itself, only releases the lock
22.7

terraform providers

The terraform providers command and its subcommands help you manage provider dependencies, create local mirrors, and inspect provider schemas.

22.7.1 terraform providers (list)

bash Listing Required Providers 
# List all providers required by configuration
$ terraform providers

Providers required by configuration:
.
├── provider[registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws] ~> 5.0
├── provider[registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random] ~> 3.0
└── module.vpc
    └── provider[registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws] ~> 5.0

# Shows providers needed by root module and all child modules
# Does NOT require terraform init

22.7.2 terraform providers lock

bash Generating Lock File for Multiple Platforms 
# Generate/update .terraform.lock.hcl for multiple platforms
$ terraform providers lock \
    -platform=linux_amd64 \
    -platform=darwin_amd64 \
    -platform=darwin_arm64 \
    -platform=windows_amd64

# Output shows checksums being fetched
- Fetching hashicorp/aws 5.40.0 for linux_amd64...
- Fetching hashicorp/aws 5.40.0 for darwin_amd64...
- Fetching hashicorp/aws 5.40.0 for darwin_arm64...
- Fetching hashicorp/aws 5.40.0 for windows_amd64...
- Obtained hashicorp/aws checksums for linux_amd64; ...

Success! Terraform has updated the lock file.

# This is essential for teams working across different OS
# Without this, terraform init might fail on other platforms
💡 Why Use providers lock?

By default, terraform init only records checksums for your current platform. Use providers lock to pre-populate checksums for all platforms your team uses. Commit the updated .terraform.lock.hcl to version control.

22.7.3 terraform providers mirror

bash Creating a Local Provider Mirror 
# Create local mirror directory for air-gapped environments
$ terraform providers mirror /path/to/mirror

# Mirror for specific platforms
$ terraform providers mirror \
    -platform=linux_amd64 \
    -platform=linux_arm64 \
    /opt/terraform-providers/

# Result directory structure:
/opt/terraform-providers/
├── registry.terraform.io/
│   └── hashicorp/
│       ├── aws/
│       │   ├── 5.40.0.json
│       │   ├── index.json
│       │   └── terraform-provider-aws_5.40.0_linux_amd64.zip
│       └── random/
│           ├── 3.6.0.json
│           ├── index.json
│           └── terraform-provider-random_3.6.0_linux_amd64.zip

# Then configure CLI to use the mirror
# ~/.terraformrc
provider_installation {
  filesystem_mirror {
    path = "/opt/terraform-providers/"
  }
}

22.7.4 terraform providers schema

bash Exporting Provider Schemas 
# Export full provider schemas as JSON (requires init)
$ terraform providers schema -json > schemas.json

# The output is large - filter with jq
$ terraform providers schema -json | jq '.provider_schemas["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"].resource_schemas["aws_instance"]'

{
  "version": 1,
  "block": {
    "attributes": {
      "ami": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "AMI to use for the instance.",
        "required": true
      },
      "instance_type": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "Instance type to use for the instance.",
        "required": true
      },
      ...
    }
  }
}

# Useful for:
# - Generating documentation
# - Building custom tooling
# - IDE integration
22.8

terraform version

22.8.1 Version Information

bash terraform version Output 
# Basic version info
$ terraform version
Terraform v1.7.4
on linux_amd64

# After terraform init - includes provider versions
$ terraform version
Terraform v1.7.4
on linux_amd64
+ provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws v5.40.0
+ provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random v3.6.0

# Check if newer version available (if checkpoint not disabled)
Your version of Terraform is out of date! The latest version
is 1.7.5. You can update by downloading from https://www.terraform.io/downloads.html

22.8.2 JSON Output

bash Machine-Readable Version Output 
# JSON output for automation
$ terraform version -json
{
  "terraform_version": "1.7.4",
  "platform": "linux_amd64",
  "provider_selections": {
    "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws": "5.40.0",
    "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random": "3.6.0"
  },
  "terraform_outdated": true
}

# Extract specific values with jq
$ terraform version -json | jq -r '.terraform_version'
1.7.4

$ terraform version -json | jq -r '.provider_selections["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"]'
5.40.0
22.9

terraform test (Terraform 1.6+)

Terraform 1.6 introduced native testing support with terraform test. This allows you to write automated tests for your Terraform modules using a declarative syntax.

22.9.1 .tftest.hcl Files

ascii-diagram Test File Structure 
my-module/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── tests/
│   ├── basic.tftest.hcl      # Test files use .tftest.hcl extension
│   ├── advanced.tftest.hcl
│   └── setup/
│       └── main.tf           # Helper module for tests
└── examples/
    └── complete/
        └── main.tf

22.9.2 run Blocks

hcl tests/basic.tftest.hcl 
# tests/basic.tftest.hcl

# Variables for the test
variables {
  environment = "test"
  instance_count = 2
}

# First run block - plan only
run "validate_plan" {
  command = plan
  
  assert {
    condition     = length(aws_instance.web) == 2
    error_message = "Expected 2 instances to be created"
  }
}

# Second run block - actual apply
run "create_resources" {
  command = apply
  
  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.web[0].instance_type == "t2.micro"
    error_message = "Instance type should be t2.micro"
  }
  
  assert {
    condition     = output.instance_count == 2
    error_message = "Output should show 2 instances"
  }
}

22.9.3 Assertions

hcl Assertion Patterns 
# tests/assertions.tftest.hcl

run "test_outputs" {
  command = apply
  
  # String comparison
  assert {
    condition     = output.environment == "test"
    error_message = "Environment output mismatch"
  }
  
  # Numeric comparison
  assert {
    condition     = output.instance_count > 0
    error_message = "Must have at least one instance"
  }
  
  # Contains check
  assert {
    condition     = contains(output.availability_zones, "us-east-1a")
    error_message = "Should include us-east-1a AZ"
  }
  
  # Regex match
  assert {
    condition     = can(regex("^vpc-", aws_vpc.main.id))
    error_message = "VPC ID should start with 'vpc-'"
  }
  
  # Not null check
  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.web.public_ip != null
    error_message = "Instance should have a public IP"
  }
}

22.9.4 Mock Providers

hcl Using Mocks for Faster Tests 
# tests/mock_test.tftest.hcl

# Mock the AWS provider to avoid real API calls
mock_provider "aws" {
  # Provide mock data for data sources
  mock_data "aws_availability_zones" {
    defaults = {
      names = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
    }
  }
  
  # Mock resource behavior
  mock_resource "aws_instance" {
    defaults = {
      id = "i-mock12345"
      public_ip = "1.2.3.4"
      private_ip = "10.0.0.5"
    }
  }
}

run "test_with_mocks" {
  command = plan  # Fast - no real resources
  
  assert {
    condition     = length(aws_instance.web) == var.instance_count
    error_message = "Instance count mismatch"
  }
}

22.9.5 Running Tests

bash terraform test Commands 
# Run all tests
$ terraform test
tests/basic.tftest.hcl... in progress
  run "validate_plan"... pass
  run "create_resources"... pass
tests/basic.tftest.hcl... tearing down
tests/basic.tftest.hcl... pass

Success! 2 passed, 0 failed.

# Run specific test file
$ terraform test -filter=tests/basic.tftest.hcl

# Verbose output
$ terraform test -verbose

# JSON output for CI/CD
$ terraform test -json

# Don't destroy resources after test (debugging)
$ terraform test -no-color 2>&1 | tee test-output.log
📝 EXAM TIP

terraform test - Key points:

  • Introduced in Terraform 1.6
  • Test files use .tftest.hcl extension
  • Tests are placed in tests/ directory
  • By default, resources are destroyed after tests
  • command = plan for fast validation without creating resources

Chapter 22 Summary

Key Takeaways

  • Global Options: -chdir changes working directory, -help and -version are informational
  • Environment Variables: TF_LOG controls debugging (TRACE/DEBUG/INFO/WARN/ERROR), TF_VAR_name sets variables
  • CLI Config: ~/.terraformrc stores credentials and plugin cache settings
  • Plugin Cache: Use plugin_cache_dir or TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR for faster init
  • terraform console: Interactive REPL for testing expressions and functions
  • force-unlock: Manually release orphaned state locks with Lock ID
  • providers lock: Generate checksums for multiple platforms
  • providers mirror: Create local mirror for air-gapped environments
  • terraform test: Native testing with .tftest.hcl files (Terraform 1.6+)

CLI Quick Reference

Command Purpose Common Use
terraform -chdir=DIR CMD Run in different directory CI/CD pipelines
terraform console Interactive expression testing Debug complex expressions
terraform force-unlock ID Release orphaned state lock After failed CI run
terraform providers lock Multi-platform lock file Team collaboration
terraform providers mirror Create local provider mirror Air-gapped environments
terraform test Run module tests CI/CD validation
Chapter 23

AWS Networking Resources

Building Production-Ready Network Infrastructure with Terraform

12 Sections
Part 8: AWS Resources
60 Minutes

Learning Objectives

🌐

VPC Architecture

Design and implement VPCs with public/private subnets, multi-AZ deployment

🔒

Network Security

Configure Security Groups and NACLs for defense in depth

🔗

Connectivity

Implement VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, and VPN connections

🚪

Private Access

Set up VPC Endpoints for private access to AWS services

23.1

VPC (aws_vpc)

Amazon VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is the foundation of your AWS network infrastructure. It provides an isolated virtual network where you launch AWS resources.

23.1.1 CIDR Block Configuration

hcl Basic VPC with CIDR Block 
# Basic VPC
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"  # 65,536 IP addresses
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "main-vpc"
    Environment = var.environment
  }
}

# VPC with secondary CIDR blocks (IPv4)
resource "aws_vpc" "extended" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

resource "aws_vpc_ipv4_cidr_block_association" "secondary" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.extended.id
  cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"
}

# VPC with IPv6
resource "aws_vpc" "dual_stack" {
  cidr_block                       = "10.0.0.0/16"
  assign_generated_ipv6_cidr_block = true
  
  tags = {
    Name = "dual-stack-vpc"
  }
}
ascii-diagram Common CIDR Block Sizes 
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     VPC CIDR BLOCK SIZES                           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                    │
│   CIDR Range      │  IP Addresses  │  Common Use Case              │
│   ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│   10.0.0.0/16     │  65,536        │  Large production VPCs        │
│   10.0.0.0/20     │  4,096         │  Medium production VPCs       │
│   10.0.0.0/24     │  256           │  Small VPCs, dev environments │
│                                                                    │
│   AWS LIMITS:                                                      │
│   ├── Minimum: /28 (16 IPs)                                        │
│   ├── Maximum: /16 (65,536 IPs)                                    │
│   └── Reserved per subnet: 5 IPs (first 4 + last 1)               │
│                                                                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

23.1.2 DNS Support and Hostnames

hcl VPC DNS Settings 
# VPC with DNS support enabled
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  
  # Enable DNS resolution (queries to AWS DNS server)
  enable_dns_support = true   # Default: true
  
  # Enable DNS hostnames for instances
  enable_dns_hostnames = true  # Default: false
  
  # Instance tenancy (default, dedicated, host)
  instance_tenancy = "default"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "main-vpc"
  }
}

# When enable_dns_hostnames = true:
# - EC2 instances get public DNS names
# - Required for:
#   - Private hosted zones
#   - VPC interface endpoints with private DNS
#   - Service discovery
📝 EXAM TIP

enable_dns_hostnames must be set to true for:

  • EC2 instances to receive public DNS names
  • VPC endpoints with private DNS to work properly
  • Route 53 private hosted zones associated with the VPC

23.1.3 Default vs Custom VPCs

Feature Default VPC Custom VPC
Created automatically Yes (per region) No (Terraform creates)
CIDR block 172.31.0.0/16 You define
Internet Gateway Attached automatically Must create
Subnets One per AZ (public) You define
Production use Not recommended Recommended

23.1.4 VPC Tags

hcl VPC with Standard Tags 
# VPC with comprehensive tagging
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_support   = true
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  
  tags = merge(
    var.common_tags,
    {
      Name        = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-vpc"
      Environment = var.environment
      Project     = var.project
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  )
}

# Variables for flexible VPC creation
variable "vpc_cidr" {
  description = "CIDR block for the VPC"
  type        = string
  default     = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

variable "common_tags" {
  description = "Common tags applied to all resources"
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
}
23.2

Subnets (aws_subnet)

23.2.1 Public Subnets

hcl Public Subnet Configuration 
# Data source for availability zones
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# Public subnets across multiple AZs
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)
  
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block              = var.public_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone       = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true  # Auto-assign public IPs
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-public-${count.index + 1}"
    Type = "public"
  }
}

variable "public_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "CIDR blocks for public subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
}

23.2.2 Private Subnets

hcl Private Subnet Configuration 
# Private subnets (no public IPs)
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count = length(var.private_subnet_cidrs)
  
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = var.private_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  
  # No public IPs for private subnets
  map_public_ip_on_launch = false
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-private-${count.index + 1}"
    Type = "private"
    # Kubernetes tags for EKS
    "kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb" = "1"
  }
}

variable "private_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "CIDR blocks for private subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["10.0.11.0/24", "10.0.12.0/24", "10.0.13.0/24"]
}

23.2.3 Availability Zone Distribution

ascii-diagram Multi-AZ Subnet Architecture 
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          VPC: 10.0.0.0/16                              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                        │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│   │                      Internet Gateway                           │  │
│   └───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                               │                                        │
│   ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┐    │
│   │                           │                                   │    │
│   │  us-east-1a    us-east-1b    us-east-1c                       │    │
│   │  ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐                      │    │
│   │  │ Public  │   │ Public  │   │ Public  │  ← map_public_ip=true│    │
│   │  │10.0.1.0 │   │10.0.2.0 │   │10.0.3.0 │                      │    │
│   │  │  /24    │   │  /24    │   │  /24    │                      │    │
│   │  └────┬────┘   └────┬────┘   └────┬────┘                      │    │
│   │       │             │             │                           │    │
│   │       ▼             ▼             ▼                           │    │
│   │  ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐                      │    │
│   │  │  NAT    │   │  NAT    │   │  NAT    │  ← For HA           │    │
│   │  │ Gateway │   │ Gateway │   │ Gateway │                      │    │
│   │  └────┬────┘   └────┬────┘   └────┬────┘                      │    │
│   │       │             │             │                           │    │
│   │       ▼             ▼             ▼                           │    │
│   │  ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐                      │    │
│   │  │ Private │   │ Private │   │ Private │  ← No public IPs     │    │
│   │  │10.0.11.0│   │10.0.12.0│   │10.0.13.0│                      │    │
│   │  │  /24    │   │  /24    │   │  /24    │                      │    │
│   │  └─────────┘   └─────────┘   └─────────┘                      │    │
│   │                                                               │    │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

23.2.4 map_public_ip_on_launch

💡 Public vs Private Subnet Definition

A subnet is "public" when:

  • Its route table has a route to an Internet Gateway
  • map_public_ip_on_launch = true (optional but common)

A subnet is "private" when:

  • No direct route to Internet Gateway
  • Uses NAT Gateway for outbound internet access
23.3

Internet Gateway (aws_internet_gateway)

23.3.1 IGW Attachment

hcl Internet Gateway Creation 
# Create and attach Internet Gateway
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-igw"
  }
}

# Alternative: Create first, then attach
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "detached" {
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-igw"
  }
}

resource "aws_internet_gateway_attachment" "attach" {
  internet_gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.detached.id
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
}

23.3.2 Route Configuration

hcl IGW Route for Public Subnets 
# Route table for public subnets
resource "aws_route_table" "public" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  # Route all internet traffic through IGW
  route {
    cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
    gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.main.id
  }
  
  # IPv6 route (if enabled)
  route {
    ipv6_cidr_block = "::/0"
    gateway_id      = aws_internet_gateway.main.id
  }
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-public-rt"
  }
}

# Associate route table with public subnets
resource "aws_route_table_association" "public" {
  count = length(aws_subnet.public)
  
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.public.id
}
23.4

NAT Gateway (aws_nat_gateway)

23.4.1 NAT Gateway vs NAT Instance

Feature NAT Gateway NAT Instance
Managed by AWS (managed service) You (EC2 instance)
Availability Highly available in AZ Single point of failure
Bandwidth Up to 100 Gbps Based on instance type
Maintenance None required Patching, updates
Cost Hourly + data processing Instance + data transfer
Security Groups Not supported Supported

23.4.2 Elastic IP Allocation

hcl NAT Gateway with Elastic IP 
# Elastic IP for NAT Gateway
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  count = var.enable_nat_gateway ? 1 : 0
  
  domain = "vpc"  # Replaces deprecated 'vpc = true'
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-nat-eip"
  }
  
  # Ensure IGW exists before creating EIP
  depends_on = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
}

# NAT Gateway in public subnet
resource "aws_nat_gateway" "main" {
  count = var.enable_nat_gateway ? 1 : 0
  
  allocation_id = aws_eip.nat[0].id
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[0].id  # Must be in public subnet
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-nat"
  }
  
  depends_on = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
}

# Route table for private subnets
resource "aws_route_table" "private" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  route {
    cidr_block     = "0.0.0.0/0"
    nat_gateway_id = aws_nat_gateway.main[0].id
  }
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-private-rt"
  }
}

23.4.3 High Availability Pattern

hcl Multi-AZ NAT Gateway Setup 
# One NAT Gateway per AZ for high availability
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  count = var.single_nat_gateway ? 1 : length(var.azs)
  
  domain = "vpc"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-nat-eip-${count.index + 1}"
  }
}

resource "aws_nat_gateway" "main" {
  count = var.single_nat_gateway ? 1 : length(var.azs)
  
  allocation_id = aws_eip.nat[count.index].id
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-nat-${count.index + 1}"
  }
}

# Route table per private subnet (for HA)
resource "aws_route_table" "private" {
  count  = length(var.private_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  route {
    cidr_block     = "0.0.0.0/0"
    nat_gateway_id = var.single_nat_gateway ? aws_nat_gateway.main[0].id : aws_nat_gateway.main[count.index].id
  }
  
  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-private-rt-${count.index + 1}"
  }
}

resource "aws_route_table_association" "private" {
  count = length(var.private_subnet_cidrs)
  
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private[count.index].id
}
💰 Cost vs Availability Trade-off
  • Single NAT Gateway: Lower cost, single point of failure
  • NAT per AZ: Higher cost, eliminates cross-AZ traffic charges, HA

Use var.single_nat_gateway to toggle between modes based on environment.

23.5

Route Tables (aws_route_table)
